The World Bank Group Environmental, Health, and Safety (EHS) Guidelines are technical
reference documents that define Good International Industry Practice (GIIP) for development
projects. They comprise General EHS Guidelines covering universal issues such as air
emissions, wastewater, occupational health, and community safety, alongside over 60 sector-
specific guidelines. For World Bank-financed projects, these guidelines are applied as
mandatory performance standards. Where host country regulations are less stringent than the
EHS Guidelines, the more protective standard applies.
3.3. International Obligations (including OP 7.50 on International Waterways)
This policy defines what comprises an international waterway and the types of activities that
trigger policy application. The core objective of OP 7.50 is to ensure that WB-financed projects
will neither affect the efficient utilization and protection of international waterway, nor
adversely affect relations between the Bank and its Borrowers, and between riparian states.
Notifications to the riparian countries along the international waterway will be required when
WB-financed has the potential to cause impacts and risks on international waterway.
Exemptions of Notification Requirements are applicable to projects in a tributary wholly in
lowest downstream riparian. The MERIT project includes construction of sluice gates and
dredging at canals/river branches that are hydrologically connected to the Mekong River, an
international waterway. The locations of the proposed physical investments and the extent of
impacts and risks will be assessed during environmental and social assessment for assessing the
applicability of OP7.50.

      CHAPTER 4: NATURAL, ENVIRONMENTAL, AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC
                            CONDITIONS
4.1. Overview of the Mekong Delta
4.1.1. Natural Conditions
a. Geographical Location
The Mekong Delta is a large alluvial plain within the Mekong River system. It borders the
Kingdom of Cambodia to the north, Ho Chi Minh City to the northeast, the Western Sea (Gulf
of Thailand) to the west, and the East Sea (South China Sea) to the south and southeast. The
Mekong Delta has a land area of approximately 4 million hectares (accounting for 93.9 percent)
and about 245,000 hectares of water surface including rivers, canals, and ponds (accounting for
6.1 percent), representing around 5.1 percent of the total Mekong River basin area. The region
also has an extensive coastal area and is located near major maritime shipping routes connecting
to international trade networks. At present, the Mekong Delta comprises six provincial-level
administrative units, including Can Tho City and the provinces of Tay Ninh, Dong Thap, Vinh
Long, An Giang, and Ca Mau




            Figure 4. 6: Administrative of the Mekong Delta after the merger
The Merit Project will be implemented in 29 communes/wards across 4 provinces/cities,
including Vinh Long Province, Tay Ninh Province, Dong Thap Province, and Can Tho City.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            53
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)




                         Figure 4. 7: Map the Project Components
b. Topographical conditions
The Mekong Delta is formed by alluvial deposits of the Mekong River. It has a relatively flat
terrain with an average elevation ranging from 0.5 to 1.2 m above mean sea level, with many
areas below 0.5 m. The western area, particularly the Ca Mau Peninsula, is the lowest-lying
region. The northern area along the border with Cambodia is relatively higher; however, due to
the coastal embankment forming a raised belt, this northern area often becomes a flood-prone
depression during the rainy season. Riverbank and coastal areas generally exhibit elevation
differences compared to surrounding areas, resulting from ongoing alluvial deposition from
rivers and sediment transport from the sea. Coastal zones feature sand dunes interspersed with
low-lying tidal depressions, which are commonly found in Ben Tre, Tra Vinh, and Soc Trang
provinces. The Dong Thap Muoi area is a low-lying floodplain that is frequently inundated
during the flood season. The downstream areas of the Cai Lon–Cai Be river basin and the U
Minh Thuong and U Minh Ha regions are among the lowest-lying areas, with elevations ranging
from 0.1 to 0.7 m, and are regularly flooded during high tides, heavy rainfall, and upstream
flood flows. Some areas in An Giang and Kien Giang near the Cambodian border also include
mountainous terrain with relatively higher elevations.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                           54
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


c. Geological Conditions
Delta sediments fall into four geotechnical units relevant to foundation design: recent fluvial
deposits (clay and sandy silt along the Tiền and Hậu rivers, variable bearing capacity); recent
swamp deposits (highly compressible organic mud in low-lying areas, poor bearing capacity);
marine-swamp deposits (weak muddy clay along the coast, high settlement potential); and
fluvio-marine mixed deposits (muddy clay and clayey silt, low bearing capacity, widespread
across the delta plain). Soft, compressible soils are ubiquitous across all subproject areas and
ground improvement is a standard foundation design requirement.
d. Climate
The delta has a tropical monsoon climate with two seasons. The rainy season (May–November)
delivers 80–90% of annual rainfall, concentrated in September–October, with annual totals
ranging from 1,300 mm (inland) to 2,500 mm (Tây Ninh). The dry season (December–April)
sees near-zero precipitation in many months. Mean annual temperatures range from 27.3°C to
28.5°C, peaking in April–May, with a slight warming trend observed over 2022–2024. Relative
humidity averages 78–85%, exceeding 90% during the wet season. Wind speeds are low (1.0–
2.0 m/s, higher at coastal stations). Annual sunshine duration is 2,000–2,500 hours, peaking
February–March.
e. Hydrology, Tidal Regime, and Water Resources
Delta hydrology is governed by upstream Mekong flows, East Sea tides, and Gulf of Thailand
tidal influences. During the low-flow season (January–June), discharge falls to 2,000–2,400
m³/s, tidal influence dominates, and saline intrusion penetrates 30–40 km inland. During the
flood season (May–November), peak discharges reach 38,000–40,000 m³/s, inundating 1.2–1.9
million hectares to depths of 0.5–4.5 m. The project area experiences irregular semidiurnal tides
with ranges typically exceeding 2 m and reaching 3.5–4.2 m during peak Metonic cycle
conditions.
Annual Mekong inflow is estimated at 410–475 billion m³, of which 80–85% occurs during the
flood season. The Tiền and Hậu rivers discharge through the Cổ Chiên, Hàm Luông, Định An,
and Trần Đề estuaries, with channel depths of 10–20 m (up to 45 m locally). A dense canal
network (2–4 km/km²) serves irrigation, navigation, and drainage. Regional rainfall adds
approximately 66 billion m³ annually. Groundwater comprises eight aquifers at depths of 10–
500 m; deep fossil aquifers are non-renewable and at risk of depletion, with shallow aquifers
already depleted across much of the delta.
f. Mineral Resources
Mineral resources relevant to construction material supply include river sand and gravel (~9–
10 million m³ reserves from the Vàm Cỏ, Tiền, and Hậu rivers) and clay (~30–40 million m³).
Other regional resources include limestone in An Giang (~440 million tonnes), andesite and
granite in An Giang (~450 million tonnes), and peat (~370 million tonnes in U Minh and Long
Xuyên areas). Offshore oil and gas fields in the Nam Con Sơn basin support an estimated 5
billion m³/year production capacity. Emelite ore has been identified along the Hậu River estuary
but reserves are not yet quantified.
g. Soils and Land Resources
Delta soils are alluvial in origin and vary significantly across ecological zones. Acid sulfate
soils are the dominant type, covering 42.7% of the delta (~1.6 million ha), concentrated in Đồng
Tháp Mười and the Long Xuyên Quadrangle — the primary driver of the dredging chemical
risk in SP 2.2. Alluvial soils (31.7%, ~1.19 million ha) are the most fertile and productive
agricultural soils, annually replenished by fluvial deposition. Saline soils (19.9%, ~744,000 ha)


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             55
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


line the coast and are better suited to brackish aquaculture than crop cultivation. Remaining
soils include peat (U Minh area), grey soils on old alluvium, and eroded soils in the northwest.
                        Table 4. 1: Soil Types in the Mekong Delta
    No.                   Soil group                      Area (ha)                 Rate (%)
    1.       Sandy land                                     43,318                    1.16
    2.       Salty soil                                    744,547                   19.87
    3.       Acid sulfate soil                            1,600,263                  42.70

Acid sulfate soils (WRB - FAO: Ortho - Thionic Gleysols) are about 1,600,263ha, accounting
for nearly 41.1% of the Mekong Delta. Acid sulfate soils are most concentrated in Dong Thap
Muoi and Long Xuyen Quadrangle, while saline sulfate soils are concentrated in the central
region of Ca Mau peninsula
Alluvial soil (Fluvisols), 1,189,396 ha (31.7%): Concentrated in the central region of the
Mekong Delta. This soil type is highly fertile and well-suited for diverse agricultural
development. Most parent materials are relatively rich, resulting in high-quality, productive
soil, although the heavy clay texture makes tilling more difficult. Notably, as this is deltaic soil,
annual alluvial deposition helps maintain its fertility at a fairly stable level.
Saline soil (Salic Gleysols), 744,547 ha (10.87%): Distributed along the coastal areas, from
Tien Giang to Ca Mau. These soils have a high sand content and low fertility, making them
less suitable for crop cultivation. However, they are more appropriate for brackish and
saltwater aquaculture.
Other soil types (0.35 million ha): Including peat soil (Histosols) in the U Minh area, grey soil
on old alluvium (Plintosols) in the northmost part of the Mekong Delta, and eroded, gravelly
soil (Leptosols) in the northwest region of the Mekong Delta.
h. Biodiversity
   ✓ Species diversity
The Greater Mekong region in Southeast Asia – which includes Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar,
Thailand and Vietnam – is part of the Indo-Burma biodiversity hotspot. It is home to globally
iconic and endangered species, including tigers, Asian elephants, Sunda pangolins and giant
freshwater stingrays. New species are constantly being discovered here as researchers continue
to explore ancient natural habitats and sift through specimens preserved decades ago in natural
history museums around the world. The discoveries in 2021 and 2022 bring the total number of
higher plants, fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals scientifically described in the
region since 1997 to 3,389.
Over the past two years, researchers have brought several new species to the world’s attention,
including a thick-toed bat; a new species of venomous sea snake named after a snake goddess
in Chinese mythology; a new species of saw-throated eel, the third described in its genus; and
a ginger-like plant dispersed by ants. These new species, identified and described by expert
naturalists and taxonomists, and compiled by WWF-Greater Mekong, demonstrate that the



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 56
              Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


region remains a fertile ground for scientific discovery and a hotspot for species diversity
            1
(WWF 2023) .
According to the Mekong Delta Master Plan, the fauna includes 23 mammal species, 386 bird
species and subspecies, 35 reptile species and 6 amphibian species. Aquatic species in the
Mekong Delta are very rich and diverse with 347 recorded species, including 187 phytoplankton
                                                        2
species, 100 zooplankton species and 60 benthic species. More than 850 species of marine fish
have been recorded, and if estuarine and coastal fish species are included, the number of fish
species in the region reaches 1,100 species.
The Mekong River system is also home to 87 endemic species found nowhere else. There are
at least 460 known fish species in the Mekong Delta – about half of all fish species in the
                      3
Mekong River basin. However, biodiversity in the Mekong Delta has been seriously degraded
due to habitat loss, water pollution and hydrological disturbances.
    ✓ Conservation areas
Important protected areas include (i) 04 Ramsar Sites including Tram Chim National Park
(Dong Thap province), Ca Mau Cape National Park (Ca Mau province), and Ca Mau Nature
Reserve, Lang Sen Wetland conservation area (Tay Ninh province) and U Minh Thuong
National Park (An Giang Province); (ii) 02 World Biosphere Reserves (An Giang and Ca Mau
Cape); and (iii) ASEAN Heritage Park, U Minh Thuong National Park (An Giang Province).

i. Climate Change and Challenges in the Mekong Delta
In the context of increasingly severe climate change, the Mekong Delta in general, and coastal
provinces such as Vinh Long, etc., are facing a growing number of extreme meteorological and
hydrological events in terms of both frequency and intensity. Natural factors such as river flow,
rainfall patterns, water levels, and coastal dynamic processes are undergoing significant
changes, leading to increased disaster risks and adverse impacts on the environment,
infrastructure, and local livelihoods. Notable phenomena include abnormal flooding, droughts
and saltwater intrusion, riverbank and coastal erosion, as well as the impacts of storms and

storm-related circulation systems. These risks should be fully considered and assessed during

Saline Intrusion
Saline intrusion has increasingly become a serious risk to food security, livelihoods, and
freshwater supply in the Mekong Delta. The periods 2015–2016 and 2019–2020 are considered
historic drought and salinity events, during which several coastal provinces declared provincial-
level natural disasters. In 2020 alone, saline intrusion caused widespread impacts, damaging
approximately 117,634 ha of agricultural production, including 63,637 ha of rice, 37,826 ha of
fruit trees, 1,129 ha of vegetables, and more than 15,000 ha of aquaculture. Ben Tre was the
most severely affected province, with over 42,700 ha impacted, followed by Vinh Long and Ca
Mau. At the same time, approximately 94,050 households experienced shortages of domestic
water, with Cần Tho, Ca Mau, and Đong Thap recording the highest numbers of affected
households.
Saline intrusion is driven not only by climate change and sea level rise but also by reduced dry-
season flows due to upstream hydropower development on the Mekong River, together with
declining freshwater storage within the river and canal systems. These factors underscore the
need for an integrated, proactive, and adaptive water resources management strategy to respond
to climate variability and changes in flow regimes.

Riverbank and Coastal Erosion
Land erosion in the Mekong Delta is a serious issue and is increasing in both frequency and
scale. According to survey data from the Southern Institute of Water Resources Research, the
entire region has recorded 604 riverbank and coastal erosion sites, with a total length of over
891 km. Of these, riverbank erosion accounts for 578 km (corresponding to 534 sites), while
coastal erosion comprises 70 sites with a total length of 313 km. Notably, 111 sites (totaling
298 km) are classified as highly dangerous, causing rapid land loss, damaging transport
infrastructure, dikes, and housing, and posing direct threats to residential areas. Provinces with
the highest number of erosion sites include An Giang (78 sites), Đong Thap (77 sites), Ca Mau
(94 sites), and Vinh Long (55 sites), accounting for nearly half of the total number of erosion
sites in the region. Coastal erosion is particularly severe in Ca Mau, where 25 erosion areas
have a total length of 109.3 km, equivalent to 43% of the province’s coastline.
Highly dangerous erosion sites tend to expand, posing significant risks to mangrove forests and
critical coastal dike systems. The primary causes are associated with sediment imbalance from
upstream, excessive sand mining, wave-induction erosion combined with sea level rise, and the
lack of comprehensive and sustainable investment in erosion prevention measures.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              59
              Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)

Storms
Historically, the Mekong Delta has recorded several storms causing significant damage. Over
more than 60 years (1950–2011), only about eight storms made direct landfall in the region;
however, years such as 1934, 1937, 1961, 1978, 1997, 2006, and 2018 experienced considerable
impacts. Typhoon Linda (1997) is considered the most devastating event, severely affecting Ca
Mau and An Giang, with wind speeds of Beaufort scale 10–11, rainfall of 400–600 mm, and
storm surge of approximately 1.5 m. The storm resulted in more than 3,000 deaths or missing
persons, caused damages exceeding VND 3 trillion, and destroyed large areas of rice fields and
fishing vessels. Typhoon Durian (2006) caused severe damage in Ben Tre and Tra Vinh (now
Vinh Long), with wind speeds of Beaufort scale 9–10, significantly affecting aquaculture
activities. Storm No. 9 (Usagi, 2018) brought winds of Beaufort scale 8 and caused widespread
flooding in Ca Mau and Bac Lieu. In addition, storm circulation can intensify the southwest
monsoon, leading to heavy rainfall and storm surges, thereby contributing to early flooding in
the middle and lower Mekong River basin.
In the context of climate change, storms tend to become more intense, with potential southward
shifts in their tracks, longer storm seasons, and an increased likelihood of storms reaching
Beaufort scale 10 or higher, thereby elevating disaster risks across the region. In particular, sea
level rise combined with storm events poses significant threats to agricultural land, coastal
ecosystems, and the agriculture- and aquaculture-based livelihoods of communities in the
Mekong Delta.

4.1.2. Socio-economic Conditions
(i) Characteristics of Population - Ethnicity
Population size and density: As of 2025, the total population of the region is estimated at
approximately 23.59 million people (accounting 23% of the national population). The average
population density is relatively high (524.78 people/km², compared to the national average of
308.42 people/km²). However, the population is unevenly distributed: densely concentrated in
Dong Thap (735.87 people/km²), Vinh Long, and Can Tho; but sparse in Ca Mau (328.94
people/km²) and coastal areas, creating challenges in the distribution of infrastructure and
public services (Source: Vietnam statical year book, 2025).
Ethnic composition: The region has a diverse population structure with 4 main ethnic groups.
The Kinh people make up the majority. The Khmer are the largest minority group (over 1
million people, accounting for 8% of the regional population), concentrated mainly in Can Tho,
Vinh Long, and An Giang. The Hoa (Chinese) people (about 250,000 people) live mainly in
urban areas, and the Cham people (about 13,000 people) are concentrated in An Giang. This
diversity creates a great advantage for cultural tourism but also requires equitable social security
policies (Source: The results of the 2024 survey of 53 ethnic groups).



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                      61
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Currently, the Mekong Delta is experiencing a very large wave of mechanical movement. The
young labor force tends to leave rural areas to move to the Southeast region and Ho Chi Minh
City, leading to population aging and a severe shortage of local labor.
(ii) Characteristics of Economic Development
General growth (2024): The region's GRDP growth rate reached 7.31% (higher than the
national average of 7%), ranking 3rd out of 6 economic regions. The GRDP scale reached
1,409.28 trillion VND, and the average per capita income was at 80.7 million VND/person/year.
Agriculture (Key sector): The Mekong Delta continues to affirm its role as the "rice bowl, fish
basket, and fruit basket" of Vietnam, contributing about 35% of the national agricultural GDP
and 60 - 70% of the total export turnover of agricultural and aquatic products (rice, shrimp,
pangasius, vegetables, and fruits). Agriculture is shifting strongly toward modern, high-quality
production, forming specialized ecological sub-regions (freshwater areas along the Tien and
Hau rivers specialized in rice and upland crops).
Industry: The industrial sector is gradually growing but still relies heavily on agriculture. The
agricultural and aquatic product processing industry accounts for up to 70% of the entire sector's
production value. Most industry and handicrafts are still small-scale and dispersed. Some
localities such as Can Tho, Tay Ninh, and Dong Thap have formed large-scale industrial zones
attracting the garment, mechanical, footwear, and leather industries, but the industrial added
value of the entire region remains modest compared to the Southeast region.
Trade, Services, and Investment: Retail and service activities grew fairly well (9%). Total
regional exports in 2024 reached 25.7 billion USD. Cumulative FDI attraction reached over
36.7 billion USD with more than 2,080 active projects (Source: Mekong Delta Regional
Planning).
Livelihood characteristics: People's livelihoods are closely linked to water resources and
wetland ecosystems. Agriculture (rice, fruit trees) and aquaculture/fishing are the main pillars
(accounting 35% of the national agricultural GDP). Currently, changes in flow regimes (loss of
floods, sediment deficit), along with drought and deep saltwater intrusion, are directly
threatening traditional agricultural production chains.
Land use rights: In the Mekong Delta, agricultural production land is usually on a household
scale, fragmented and small. The greatest risk today is the loss of physical land. Every year, the
region loses 300 - 500 ha of land due to riverbank and coastal erosion, leaving tens of thousands
of households at risk of losing their means of production and housing.
Poverty: The rate of households according to the multidimensional poverty line is currently
only 4.15% (lower than the national average of 5.71%). However, the risk of falling back into
poverty is very high due to natural disasters and precarious livelihoods. Localized poverty still
exists in areas with large ethnic minority populations, coastal areas, and among households
without production land.
(iii) Infrastructure characteristics
Transport and Logistics Infrastructure: The transport system is incomplete and does not fully
meet the needs of goods transportation, leading to high logistics costs and reduced
competitiveness. The region has about 22,800 km of roads (National highways, provincial roads,
rural roads) and the advantage of 28,600 km of rivers and canals (of which 13,000 km can be
exploited for inland waterway transport).
Irrigation Infrastructure: Extensive and dense to serve production, with over 15,000 km of
main/level I canals; nearly 27,000 km of level II canals; about 50,000 km of on-farm canals,
along with tens of thousands of sluices, dams, and pumping stations of all types.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              62
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Flood and Salinity Control Infrastructure: A giant dike system 13,000 km long has been formed
for flood protection (of which 7,000 km protects the Summer-Autumn rice crop). Coastal areas
have built 450 km of sea dikes, 1,290 km of river dikes, and 7,000 km of internal embankments
to prevent high tides and saltwater intrusion.
Domestic Water Supply: Most urban areas have clean water from centralized plants, but in rural
areas, only 40% of the population has access to hygienic water. Water supply infrastructure is
under great pressure and frequently interrupted during the dry season due to drought, deep
saltwater intrusion, and surface water pollution. Water quality monitoring work is still mostly
manual, lacking early warning systems.
Solid waste management: The total solid waste generation in the region is approximately 4,200
tons per day. Of this, around 3,200 tons per day are collected from cities and towns, achieving
a collection rate of 70–80 percent, while the remaining portion is not properly managed. At
present, most landfills in the Mekong Delta are unsanitary, with waste largely disposed of
through uncontrolled burial without proper technical procedures, and without leachate
collection or treatment systems. This situation contributes to air pollution (odor emissions) and
directly and indirectly contaminates canals, waterways, groundwater, agricultural land, and
aquaculture areas.
Power supply: The regional power system includes Tra Noc thermal power plant (193.5 MW),
O Mon power complex (1,200 MW), Ca Mau power complex (1,500 MW), Duyen Hai III
thermal power plant (1,244 MW), Bac Lieu wind power plant (99 MW), and Duong Dong (Phu
Quoc) power plant (12 MW). The transmission network includes 500 kV substations (My Tho,
O Mon, Duyen Hai) and 220 kV substations (Long An, My Tho, Cai Lay, Ben Tre, Vinh Long,
Tra Vinh, etc.). The grid system consists of 500 kV transmission lines and 220 kV lines
interconnected with 220 kV substations.
4.2. Natural environmental and socio-economic conditions of the subprojects
4.2.1. Subproject 2.1
4.2.1.1. Natural conditions
Subproject 2.1 (SP 2.1) is located in four coastal communes/wards of Vinh Long Province,
including: Thoi Thuan Commune, Tan Thuy Commune, Dong Hai Commune and Truong Long
Hoa Ward.




                   Figure 4. 10: Location of Sub-project 2.1 components

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             63
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


a. Geographical Location: The project area is situated along the coastal strip of the lower
Mekong Delta, directly bordering the East Sea. The wave-reduction embankments run along
the coastline through Vinh Long Province. The implementation scope of the project
components encompasses Thoi Thuan, Tan Thuy, and Dong Hai communes, and Truong Long
Hoa Ward in Vinh Long Province.
b. Topography: Typical coastal plain terrain—relatively flat with arc-shaped sand dunes
running parallel to the shore. Elevations in Thoi Thuan and Tan Thuy range from -2.0m to
+3.5m. In Dong Hai and Truong Long Hoa, the terrain is quite gentle with elevations ranging
from -0.75m to +0.00m.
c. Geology - Soil:
   -   Geology: The coastal stratigraphic structure consists of two main layers: a top layer of
       grey-blue clean sand, medium density (4–6.4m thick), and an underlying layer of grey-
       blue medium-plastic clay/mud, occasionally mixed with silty sand (16–21m thick).
   -   Soil: Characterized by highly fertile, loose freshwater alluvial soil deposited by the Tien
       and Hau rivers. This area has a very low ratio of acid sulfate soil, with the sulfidic layer
       located deep underground.
d. Hydrological/Oceanographic Conditions
Hydrology in the project areas are Strongly governed by upstream Mekong water and East Sea
tidal regimes which is irregular semi-diurnal tides (two highs, two lows daily) with a high
amplitude of 2-4m. Coastal water levels peak during the trade wind period (Dec-Jan). In
summer, waves move West/Southwest with northward coastal currents. In winter, waves are
East/Northeast (80-87%), with southward currents. Flow velocity is generally below 0.3 m/s.
e. Natural/Biological Resources
Water & Minerals: Surface water is primarily saline; freshwater is scarce, but groundwater is
abundant (extracted at 80-120m) and serves as the primary source for domestic and production
use. Minerals include riverbed leveling sand (~130 million m³) and clay (~200 million m³), plus
Emilite ore and natural mineral water.
4.2.1.2. Baseline environmental conditions
The baseline environmental quality at the subproject sites was evaluated based on the
monitoring results of environmental components across two phases (Phase 1 was in April 2025
during the pre-FS stage by the Preliminary EIA Consultant; Phase 2 was in February 2026
during the FS stage by the ESIA/EIA Consultant) (see detailed analysis results in Appendix 4).
Ambient air quality and noise: Key parameters included dust (TSP), PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and
noise (Phase 1), and TSP, NO₂, SO₂, CO (Phase 2). Results indicate that all measured
parameters at the subproject construction sites were within the permissible limits of QCVN
05:2023/BTNMT (1-hour average) and QCVN 26:2025/BTNMT for noise.
Surface water quality: Monitoring at six locations (April 2025, pre-feasibility stage) shows that
pH, turbidity, and salinity are within QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT, Column B limits. However, DO
exceeds permissible levels at 4/6 locations, likely due to high salinity and/or organic matter,
where decomposition reduces dissolved oxygen.
Coastal seawater quality: Monitoring was conducted at four representative locations along the
proposed embankment. Two samples (Tan Thuy and Dong Hai) were collected and analyzed in
February 2026 during ESIA preparation (pH, DO, BOD₅, Coliform), and two samples (Thoi
Thuan and Truong Long Hoa) were obtained at the pre-feasibility stage in April 2025 (pH, DO,
salinity, turbidity). All results comply with QCVN 10:2023/BTNMT on seawater quality.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               64
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Sediment quality: Six sediment (30 -50cm depth) samples were collected in Thoi Thuan
Commune and Truong Long Hoa Ward during the pre-feasibility stage (April 2025).
Concentrations of metals (Cu, Zn) and heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr) are well below QCVN
43:2025/BNNMT limits. Fe exceeds the permissible limit, while pesticide residues are largely
undetectable.
Aquatic characteristics: Six samples were collected during the pre-feasibility stage (April 2025)
including three in Thoi Thuan Commune (TS2–TS4) and three in Truong Long Hoa Ward
(TS5–TS7). Results (Appendix 4) indicate very low benthic diversity across all sites (1–4
species/site), with no species listed in the Vietnam Red Data Book (2025) or IUCN Red List
(2020). Benthic densities vary widely, from 50–180 individuals/m² at low-density sites (TS4,
TS5, TS2) to high concentrations at TS3 (1,400 individuals/m²) and TS6 (1,210 individuals/m²).
4.2.1.3. Socio-economic Conditions
a. Population and Ethnicity
Subproject 2.1 covers an area of approximately 286.65 km2 with a population of about 96,866
people, accounting for about 2.28% of the provincial population. The average density is
approximately 337.9 people/km2.
Regarding ethnic composition, the Kinh people make up the majority, living alongside other
ethnic minority communities, the most numerous being Khmer and Hoa, along with a small
number of H'Mong and Nung households.
         Table 4. 6: Population and Ethnic Characteristics of the Subprojects 2.1

b. Economic Development
The 4 localities within the Subproject 2.1 area all share the characteristics of a coastal economic
zone.
Economic growth rate: All 4 localities recorded moderate to very high economic growth rates.
Typically, Truong Long Hoa Ward reached a growth rate of 14.47% in 2025 compared to 2024,
while Dong Hai Commune increased its production value by 479.13 billion VND (reaching
108.6% of the 2025 annual plan). People's living standards have improved significantly, with a
high average per capita income (for instance, exceeding 80.8 million VND/person/year in Thoi
Thuan).
Economic restructuring trends: The economic structure of the entire region is shifting toward
gradually reducing the share of Agriculture - Forestry - Fisheries while rapidly increasing the
share of Industry - Construction and Trade - Services.
Agriculture - Forestry - Fisheries Sector: This remains the "backbone" of the regional economy,
with fisheries playing a core role. In Dong Hai, this sector dominates at 50.74% and in Truong
Long Hoa, it is 28.43%. The general trend of the region is promoting offshore fishing (with
thousands of fishing vessels in Tan Thuy and Dong Hai) and developing aquaculture toward


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                       65
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


intensive farming and high-tech applications (shrimp, clam, and blood cockle farming in Thoi
Thuan and Truong Long Hoa). Crop agriculture mainly adapts to sandy ridge soil and focuses
on developing OCOP products. Although the absolute production value of this sector continues
to rise, its contribution to the total GRDP is decreasing compared to industry and services.
Industry - Construction Sector (Breakthrough growth driver): This sector is rising strongly,
contributing an increasingly large share (accounting for 42.74% in Truong Long Hoa and 36%
in Dong Hai). The largest development driver for the entire region is the renewable energy
industry, with the presence of a series of wind power plant projects operating stably and
expanding. Along with that, the seafood processing industry is recovering and developing well,
exemplified by the large-scale processing craft village in Dong Hai which provides jobs for
thousands of workers, or the dried fish and shrimp processing facilities in Tan Thuy.
Trade - Service Sector (Impressive growth with high potential): In Dong Hai, although trade
and services only account for 13.26% of the structure, the growth rate reached 175% of the
2025 plan (an increase of 230 billion VND). In Truong Long Hoa, this sector accounts for 28.82%
and grew by over 18%. The advantage of a long coastline is being fully exploited by localities
to develop eco-tourism, beach tourism, and spiritual tourism (Ba Dong beach resort, Truc Lam
Zen Monastery, or the "coastal flavors" model in Thoi Thuan). Nevertheless, the scale of trade
and services generally still consists of many small-scale individual business households and
lacks many high-added-value products.
c. Labor force, employment, and income
Regarding Labor Force and Vocational Training: Vocational training to improve the quality of
human resources is a priority for local authorities, with the rate of trained laborers reaching a
fair level:
Truong Long Hoa Ward: The rate of trained laborers in 2025 accounted for 88.07% of the total
labor force, in which laborers with degrees or certificates accounted for 53.09%. The
unemployment insurance participation rate reached 19.38% of the working-age labor force.
Thoi Thuan Commune: The rate of trained laborers reached 74.83%, in which trained laborers
with vocational degrees or certificates reached 28.4%.
Regarding Job Creation and Labor Export: In general, the creation of new jobs and labor export
in communes/wards all met and exceeded 2025 targets. However, communes still face seasonal
underemployment after harvests, especially unskilled labor.
Regarding Income: People's income is highly dependent on the stability of agricultural and
aquatic product prices and livelihood policies. The average per capita income in the project area
communes is over 80 million VND/person/year.
Poverty Reduction Efforts: Localities have recorded significant efforts in reducing the poverty
rate through social security policies. Typically, in Tan Thuy Commune, the number of poor
households at the beginning of 2025 was 486 (4.65%), but by the year-end review, it had
dropped to 191 households (1.83%). In Dong Hai Commune, poverty reduction reached 250%
of the plan, with only 25 poor households and 69 near-poor households remaining. Notably, the
task of "eliminating temporary and dilapidated houses" was implemented drastically: Tan Thuy
built and repaired 94 houses, Dong Hai handed over 63, Thoi Thuan built and repaired 34, and
Truong Long Hoa actively constructed Great Solidarity houses. Livelihood projects such as
loans and support for breeding cows (Truong Long Hoa) also helped the poor have more
opportunities to rise.
Although the poverty rate has decreased, the economic foundation of the poor is not yet truly
solid. In Tan Thuy Commune, "local re-poverty" still occurs due to the impact of diseases on


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             66
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


crops and livestock and price fluctuations. Inequality in access to employment is also evident
as unskilled laborers frequently face seasonal underemployment after harvests.
In these communes, ethnic minority communities live interspersed (Truong Long Hoa has 77
Khmer households, Dong Hai has 90 Khmer households, and Tan Thuy has Hoa, Khmer,
H'Mong, and Nung people). Despite receiving state support, these remaining groups require
continuous auxiliary policies.
d. Culture, religion, and beliefs
The system of religious establishments and folk beliefs in this area is highly diverse, serving
the spiritual lives of multiple ethnic communities (Kinh, Khmer, Hoa):
Tan Thuy Commune: This locality has the densest religious system with 4 main religions
(Buddhism, Catholicism, Cao Dai, Protestantism) and 21 folk belief establishments, including
01 Lang Ong (Whale Temple), 01 Chua Ong (Pagoda), 03 communal houses (Dinh), and 17
shrines (Mieu). A notable example is the Ba Tiem Tom Shrine, which frequently organizes
traditional performing arts activities.
Truong Long Hoa Ward: Prominent for the Truc Lam Zen Monastery, a major spiritual tourism
destination. Combined with the Ba Dong Beach Resort, it attracts tens of thousands of visitors
(nearly 37,540 visitors visited the monastery during the Tet holidays alone). The ward currently
has 05 active religious establishments.
e. Infrastructure
Transportation: With its central location in the Mekong Delta, Vinh Long Province is favorable
for both road and waterway transportation, ensuring smooth internal, regional, and national
connectivity.
Road transportation: The province has 09 national highways passing through, including NH.1,
NH.53 (including the Phan Van Dang road extension), NH.53B, NH.54, NH.57, NH.57B,
NH.57C, NH.60, and NH.80 with a total length of 716.699 km, of which 629.644 km are
managed by the Provincial People's Committee; 01 expressway (My Thuan - Can Tho
Expressway) measuring 13.376 km; and 29 provincial roads currently in operation and
management totaling 737.014 km.
Waterway transportation: The province has the Tien, Hau, Co Chien, and Mang Thit rivers
flowing through, with 06 major river mouths flowing into the East Sea including: Cua Dai, Cua
Ba Lai, Cua Ham Luong, Cua Co Chien, Cua Cung Hau, and Cua Dinh An. The total length of
inland waterways across the province is approximately 2,117 km, allowing 10,000-ton vessels
and 2,400-ton barges to operate.
Energy: 100% of the population is connected to the national power grid
Water supply system: The entire province has 87 water treatment plants and over 237 water
supply stations with a total capacity of about 342,800 m3/day-night, meeting domestic and
production needs.
The proportion of households with access to clean water from centralized water supply systems
(piped water) is relatively high (Truong Long Hoa: 87.39%; Dong Hai: 80.85%; Thoi Thuan:
70.1%; Tan Thuy: 60.0%). The remaining households use hygienic water, mainly sourced from
groundwater wells.
The construction area of the wave-dissipating embankment is located 100–150 meters from the
coastline and does not affect any water intake points of households or water treatment facilities.
Health facilities: Each commune has standardized health stations. The grassroots health system
is maintained stably, ensuring 24/24 shifts to serve primary healthcare and disease prevention.

4.2.2. Subproject 2.2
4.2.2.1. Natural conditions
a. Geography
Sub-project 2.2 is implemented across Dong Thap and Tay Ninh provinces. Specifically, the
components involving the dredging of the Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal and the construction of
associated embankments and sluice structures are located within An Phuoc, Phu Cuong, Truong
Xuan, and Thap Muoi communes of Dong Thap Province, as well as Vinh Chau and Vinh
Thanh communes of Tay Ninh Province. Additionally, the dredging of the Cho Bung canal,
along with the construction of sluices, upgrading of ring dikes, and embankment works, is
situated within Chau Thanh, Hung Thanh, Tan Huong, Tan Phuoc 3, and Long Hung communes,
and Trung An Ward in Dong Thap Province




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  68
              Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)




         Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal                                   Cho Bung canal

                     Figure 4. 8: Location of Sub-project 2.2 components
b. Topography
The Dong Thap Muoi area has ground elevations ranging from 0.3 to 4.0 m, with the majority
between 0.5 and 1.5 m (accounting for about 75 percent of the total area). The highest
elevations, reaching 3–4 m, are found along the Vietnam–Cambodia border, typically in gently
sloping ridges such as Don Phuc, Lang Xang, and Go Chua hills, which account for
approximately 7 percent of the total area. The lowest areas are located in the Bac Dong and
central Bo Bo zones, with elevations ranging from 0.4 to 0.7 m.
The Dong Thap Muoi area exhibits a peripheral highland and central depression topography.
The northern part consists of an interspersed plain and low hills of ancient alluvial origin,
extending from southern Cambodia, with typical elevations of 2–4 m. The western and
southwestern parts are elevated strips along the Tien River, located 1–2 km and in some areas
5–7 km from the riverbank, with elevations of 1.5–2.0 m. The southern part consists of sand
dune formations running nearly parallel to the coastline, representing remnants of ancient
coastal ridges in the southeastern communes of Dong Thap Province (formerly in Chau Thanh
and Cai Lay districts of the former Tien Giang Province).
c. Geology
From the bottom up, the strata can be generalized as follows4:
- Pliocene sediments (N2): Distributed from a depth of about 160 or more, mainly sand and gray
silt, the upper part of silt clay is dominant with yellow-brown color.
- Pleistocene sediments (Q1): Distributed from 20 - 30 m depth to 150-170 m. Sediment consists
of sand, silt clay, gray-white, and patchy.


4
  Nguyen Huy Dung. 2015. “Investigate and Assess Modern Geodynamics to Complete Climate Change Scenarios
and Propose Adaptation Solutions in the Mekong Delta.” https://diamoitruong.com/2015/12/21/canh-quan-dong-
thap-muoi/

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                      69
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


- Middle Holocene sediments (mQ22): Distributed from depths of 5 – 10 m to 30 m. Sediments
of marine origin, mainly clay, blue-gray silt clay.
- Late Holocene sediments (abQ23): distributed from the surface to a 5-10 m depth. Sediment
composition includes clay powder, clay, peat, and plant remains.
d. Hydrographic/oceanographic conditions
Surface water: Dong Thap Muoi has an interlaced network of rivers and canals, facilitating the
exchange of goods with neighboring areas and an environment for aquaculture and fishing. The
river system consists of three main rivers: Tien River, Hau River, and Vam Co River. River
water is used for domestic purposes, irrigation, navigation, and drainage for the area.
The Tien River in Dong Thap has a total length of 120km, of which the section from the border
with Dong Thap province to the beginning of Tau islet (Cu lao Tau) (where it distributes into
two rivers, Cua Tieu and Cua Dai) is 77.4 km long. The widest part of the river (2,100 m) is on
Tau islet, and the narrowest place (300 m) is 600m west of Tra Lot canal (Hoa Khanh commune,
Cai Be district).
The depth of the Tien river varies depending on each section: from the beginning of the Tau
islet to the Ky Hon canal, the depth is 9-11m, and the section from Ky Hon through My Tho
city to the Nguyen Tan Thanh canal, the is depth is 7-9m; from Nguyen Tan Thanh canal to My
Thuan bridge with an average riverbed depth of 12 - 15m above the natural ground - in this
section, the river section from My Thuan bridge back to the west has a depth of 27m, the
topography of the river bed is shallow towards Tien Giang, and the slope of the bank is light”.
Tien river in Tien Giang has a water flow of 563 - 1,900 m3/s; In the flood season (September),
the average discharge is from 10,406 to 16,300m3/s. At My Tho station, the highest water level
is +1.91 m, and the lowest is 2.01 m.
In the flood season, a part of the water from the Tien River flows into Dong Thap Muoi and
drains into the sea through the Vam Co Tay River. Still, this river's ability to discharge floods
is inferior because too many meandering sections exist. In the dry season, almost the entire
Vam Co River is dominated by the semi-diurnal tide of the East Sea; the sea water quickly
encroaches further upstream. Inundation depth and duration vary from place to place. When the
flood returns due to being blocked by the N1 road, the flooding time is earlier in the So Ha and
So Thuong areas to Tan Chau. In the field, generally over 2.5m. Around the beginning or the
end of December, the water recedes, and the flooding time lasts for more than four months.
Vam Co Tay and Vam Co Dong rivers both originate from Prey Veng province (Cambodia)
and confluence at Ban Quy confluence (Tan Tru).
Vam Co Tay river originates from Prey Veng province - Cambodia and flows in the direction
of Northwest - Southeast through Tay Ninh and Long An provinces and the confluence with
Vam Co Dong River to form Vam Co RIver, which flows to Soai Rap estuary. The length of
Vam Co Tay River is 150 km. According to the data of the Hydrometeorological Center of
Long An province, the Vam Co Tay River has the following characteristics: Max water level:
+ 1.72m; Min water level: - 2.16m; Speed in flood season: 1.5 m/s; Maximum flow (flood
season): 3,210 m3/s; Velocity in the dry season: 0.53 m/s; Minimum flow (dry season): 763
m3/s; Maximum flow (dry season): 1,080 m3/s.
Vam Co Dong River originates from Prey Veng province - Cambodia and flows in the direction
of Northwest - Southeast through Tay Ninh and Long An provinces and the confluence with
Vam Co Tay River to form Vam Co River, which flows to Soai Rap estuary. The length of Vam
Co Dong River is 218 km, the basin area is 12,800 km2, the average annual water volume is
4.47 km3, and the yearly flow modulus is 11 l/s.km2. According to data from Ben Luc



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             70
                Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


hydrological station, Vam Co Dong River has the following characteristics 5: The highest water
level: +1,846 m (Hon Dau system) corresponding to the frequency of 1.0%; The lowest water
level: -0.59m (Hon Dau system), corresponding to a frequency of 1.0%; Speed in flood season:
2.5 m/s; Maximum flow (flood season): 6,750 m3/s; Speed in the dry season: 0.58 m/s;
Minimum flow (dry season): 1,160 m3/s; Maximum flow (dry season): 1,600 m3/s.
The two canals affected by dredging under SP2.2 are characterized by a semi-diurnal tidal
regime with continuous flow reversal driven by flood and ebb tides. Hydrodynamic conditions
are generally low-energy, with flow velocities typically ranging from 0.05–0.2 m/s, indicating
limited advective transport and relatively low dispersion of suspended materials. Water
movement in both canals is strongly governed by tidal oscillation, which controls flow direction
and sediment transport processes.
Dong Thap Muoi has abundant groundwater potential (accounting for 27.4% of total
groundwater reserves in the Mekong Delta) and large exploitation capacity.
According to data on groundwater resources of the Southern Institute of Irrigation Planning, in
the Dong Thap Muoi region, the reserve of fresh water (M<1g/L) is estimated at 10.6 million
m3/day, brackish water (1<M<3g) /L) is 6.0 million m3/day, low salt water (3<M<10g/L) is 5.2
million m3/day and very salty water (M>10g/L) is 1.7 million m3/day6.
Hydrological features of the area play a decisive role in the design and construction of canal
dredging works:
-      Seasonal flood regimes and large water level fluctuations require appropriate calculation
       of canal bank elevations and dredged material disposal sites to avoid flooding and erosion;
-      High flow discharge during the flood season may increase the spread of suspended
       sediments during dredging operations;
-      The risks of ground subsidence due to geological–hydrological conditions need to be
       assessed when locating dredged material disposal sites.
f. Natural/Biological resources
According to the overall screening results for the entire Project and field surveys conducted for
Subproject 2.2 in February 2026, there are three protected areas located near the canal dredging
subproject in the Dong Thap Muoi area, as follows:
-      Tram Chim National Park (Ramsar Site): The shortest distance to Phuoc Xuyen Canal,
       measured by straight-line distance and via inland waterways (canal network), is
       approximately 7 km to the west.
-      Lang Sen Wetland Reserve (Ramsar Site): The shortest distance from the Phuoc Xuyen
       canal dredging area to the Ramsar site is approximately 2 km in a straight line.
-      Go Thap Muoi Nature Reserve: The shortest straight-line distance from the Phuoc Xuyen
       – Hai Tam canal dredging area to the nature reserve is approximately 0.6 km.
-      For Cho Bung Canal, there are no protected areas within a 5 km radius.
The Project’s area of influence is located outside the boundaries and buffer zones of the
protected areas.
4.2.2.2. Baseline Environmental Conditions


5
    Hydrometeorological Center of Long An Province, 2022
6
 JV Royal Haskoning DHV - GIZ. 2021. “Strategic Environmental Assessment for Mekong Delta Integrated
Regional Planning for the Period 2021-2030, with a Vision to 2050

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                71
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Baseline environmental quality was assessed across two monitoring phases: Phase 1 (April
2025, pre-feasibility stage, by the Preliminary EIA Consultant) and Phase 2 (February 2026,
feasibility stage, by the ESIA/EIA Consultant). Full monitoring data and sampling locations are
provided in Appendix 4.
Ambient Air and Noise: Parameters monitored included TSP, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and noise
levels. All values comply with QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT (1-hour average) and QCVN
26:2025/BNNMT at all construction sites, indicating acceptable pre-construction air and noise
baselines.
Surface Water Quality: Water quality in the Phước Xuyên–Hải Tâm and Chợ Bưng canals
generally complies with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column B) (see detail in the Appendix 4),
however localized exceedances indicate a system under stress. COD exceeds the 15 mg/L
standard at monitoring point NM02 (25.75 mg/L), with BOD₅ also exceeding the 6 mg/L limit
(8.28 mg/L) at the same location. Dissolved oxygen depletion has been recorded at multiple
sites, including Trường Xuân and the eastern Phước Xuyên embankment. Elevated NH₄⁺ at the
Thanh Lợi embankment area indicates localized organic-nutrient enrichment from adjacent
residential and agricultural activities.
Especially, analytical results of 05 surface water samples on February 2026 (dry season) show
that TSS concentrations ranged from 20 to 47 mg/L across five sampling locations, including
26 mg/L in surface water at the dredged canal section at the head of Hai Tam Canal (junction
with Nguyen Van Tiep Canal), 47 mg/L at the location of Vinh Chau A Cultural House on
Phuoc Xuyen–Hai Tam Canal, 38 mg/L in Cho Bung Canal at Long Hung Commune, 35 mg/L
in Cho Bung Canal at Tan Huong Commune, and 20 mg/L at the Thanh Loi embankment
construction area on Phuoc Xuyen–Hai Tam Canal. All measured concentrations were
substantially below the applicable limit of 100 mg/L under QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column
B). These baseline conditions are directly relevant to the assessment of dredging impacts: the
low flow velocities and limited dilution capacity during the dry season mean that TSS plumes
generated by dredging will persist longer and extend further than under wet-season conditions,
with corresponding risks to aquatic life, irrigation intakes, and the Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant
downstream.
Collectively, these results indicate that the canal system has moderate but diminished
assimilative capacity and is already showing early-stage eutrophication, making it particularly
vulnerable to additional pollutant loading during construction. Priority risks include: (i) TSS
increases from excavation and dredging; (ii) BOD/COD loading from construction worker
wastewater; and (iii) ammonium generation from unmanaged organic waste. The project must
implement strict wastewater collection and treatment prior to discharge, and conduct periodic
construction-phase monitoring of TSS, DO, NH₄⁺, and COD.
Sediment Quality: Concentrations of heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr, Cu, Zn) across all
monitoring sites are well below QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT permissible limits, and pesticide
residues are effectively undetectable, confirming no toxic accumulation in canal sediments.
Dredged material is therefore suitable for reuse in land filling and embankment construction.
However, iron (Fe) concentrations exceed permissible limits and represent a specific dredging
risk: mobilisation of Fe²⁺ into the water column during dredging can cause localised DO
depletion through oxidation reactions and the formation of iron hydroxide precipitates (visible
as yellow-brown discolouration), which can degrade aquatic habitat quality. Given the pre-
existing DO stress and elevated ammonium levels recorded at baseline, Fe remobilisation
represents a compounding redox pressure that requires targeted real-time monitoring and
mitigation during dredging operations.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             72
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Soil Quality: Annual heavy metal monitoring across Đồng Tháp province (12 sites across rice
fields, cropland, orchards, and gardens; latest data 2021) confirms all parameters within
permissible limits, with no evidence of dioxin, toxin, or chemical contamination in surface soils.
Complementary multi-depth sampling from the MD-ICRSL Project (May 2017; 15 sites; three
depth layers: 0–20 cm, 50–70 cm, and 1.3–1.5 m) identifies predominantly acid sulfate soils
with acidity ranging from acidic to very acidic throughout the soil profile. This is the defining
geotechnical and geochemical risk for SP 2.2: excavation and embankment works that expose
deeper potential acid sulfate layers to oxygen will trigger acidification and metal leaching
requiring active management under the Dredged Material Management Plan.
Aquatic Ecology: The aquatic environment within the SP 2.2 area shows clear indicators of
organic pollution and eutrophication across all biological assemblage layers. Surface-layer
phytoplankton is dominated by Euglena — a pollution-tolerant taxon indicative of high organic
loading. Zooplankton in the mid-layer is dominated by rotifers, consistent with eutrophic
conditions. Benthic fauna is characterised by pollution-tolerant oligochaete worms, indicating
chronic sediment oxygen depletion. Overall aquatic ecological quality is low across the
subproject area, with the TS12 monitoring site recording the poorest benthic and overall
ecological conditions. No rare, endangered, or protected species listed in the IUCN Red List or
Vietnam Red Data Book were recorded within the construction footprint.
4.2.2.3. Socio – Economic Conditions
a. Population and Ethnicity
Population Distribution and Density: Following administrative consolidation, the project area
spans multiple communes with varying population densities. The Phuoc Xuyen Canal area in
Dong Thap features communes ranging from 21,122 people (Phu Cuong, 234.9 persons/km²)
to 44,427 people (Thap Muoi, 631.1 persons/km²). In Tay Ninh, Vinh Thanh and Vinh Chau
communes report populations of 12,610 and 18,046 (or 13,022 post-merger) respectively. The
Cho Bung Canal area spans 6 communes with a substantial total population of 350,357 people.
Settlement patterns are typically open, concentrated along major transport routes, canals, and
riverine garden ecosystems.
         Table 4. 9: Population and Ethnic Characteristics of the Subprojects 2.2

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  73
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


The Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam Canal is characterized by uneven population distribution and signs
of encroachment on waterway safety corridors in several segments. Low to Medium Population
Density Area (K0 - K26+500): Residents living along the banks are mainly concentrated right
at the water's edge (Right bank) or 5–20m from the riverbank (Left bank). Civil structures
include houses, local ferry terminals, and boat houses (10 structures). In the segment K22+900
– K26+500, erosion has been recorded on the Right bank, directly threatening the safety of
households living near the water. High Population Density Area and Trade Hub (K26+500 -
K44+000): From the An Phong My Hoa canal intersection, population density spikes (e.g.,
segment K37+750 - K40+850 has 224 houses on the left bank). Infrastructure is more complex,
featuring markets (Truong Xuan market area), schools, administrative buildings, and
embankments built by residents or local authorities. Notably, serious encroachment into the
water flow occurs from K32+700 to K40+850, narrowing the canal width from 50m to 34–37m,
posing risks of waterway accidents and obstructing flow.
Compared to Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal, the Cho Bung canal has a sparser population and is
characterized by industrial activities and construction material businesses. Riparian residents
are scattered, mostly set back 5–20m from the bank or interspersed within fruit orchards
(K13+650 - K17+000). Cross-sectional transport infrastructure is quite dense, with many local
footbridges, suspension bridges, highway bridges, and reinforced concrete bridges. The end of
the route (K13+650 - K17+000) contains socially sensitive receptors, including one secondary
school and one religious facility (Pagoda), requiring strict environmental management
measures (noise and dust control) during the construction phase.
Ethnic Composition: The population is predominantly Kinh, who have resided in the area
extensively. Ethnic minority groups—primarily Khmer and Hoa—are present but represent a
very small proportion of the total population (ranging from 0.003% to 0.5% depending on the
specific aggregate). For example, the Cho Bung area has interspersed minority populations in
all communes except Long Hung, while Vinh Thạnh has no ethnic minorities.
b. Economic Development
Agricultural Dominance: The economic structure across the subproject area is heavily
dominated by agriculture. Phuoc Xuyen and Tay Ninh communes focus on high-quality and
high-tech rice production, with cultivated areas per commune reaching up to 27,930 ha and
yields averaging 6.0–6.8 tons/ha. Conversely, the Cho Bung Canal economy is strongly
orchard-based, focusing on high-value fruits (pineapple, pomelo, wax apple) with large
cultivation zones like Hung Thanh (3,741.4 ha) and Long Hung (1,566.3 ha). Agriculture is the
primary livelihood for people in the upper and middle reaches of the Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam
canal (K0 - K22+900), mainly rice cultivation (Left bank). At Cho Bung canal, the end of the
route (K9+600 - K17+000) is characterized by orchard-based economies.
Aquaculture: Dozens of household-scale fish ponds were recorded along the Left bank of the
Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal (from K0 to K22+900). This group is highly vulnerable and
requires a careful assessment of water pollution risks to their ponds during dredging operations.
Economic Transition and Value Addition: Localities are actively promoting production-
consumption linkages, mechanization, and the development of 3-to-4-star OCOP products (e.g.,
pineapple coconut jam, braised fish paste) to increase value addition.
Industrial and Commercial Growth: The economic structure is diversifying positively toward
trade, services, and small-scale industries. This transition is particularly evident along the Cho
Bung Canal, which hosts significant industrial activity, including 1,497 facilities in Chau Thanh,
the Tan Huong Industrial Park, and the Long Giang Industrial Park (housing 58 enterprises).
Construction Material Yards and Workshops: This is a prominent economic activity on both
canals. Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam has 16 scattered material yards, while Cho Bung canal has many

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              74
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


large-scale yards and production facilities concentrated on the Left bank (K5+00 - K6+00),
notably the Ticoll concrete pile/beam casting company with its wharves and moored barges.
Dredging activities may cause water turbidity, directly affecting the water source for 34 fish
ponds (Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam). Along the Cho Bung canal, there are no households engaged
in aquaculture.
c. Labor Force, Employment, and Income
Labor Force Profile: The region benefits from an abundant, working-age labor force. While
predominantly agrarian, there is a distinct labor shift toward industry, garment manufacturing,
processing, and out-moving.
Labor Quality: The proportion of trained workers is notably high, reaching 79–85% in the Dong
Thap communes, 76% in Vinh Thanh, and 65% in Vinh Chau. 80% in Chợ Bưng canal
Income Levels: Successful job creation and overseas employment programs have diversified
household income. The average per capita income in the Phuoc Xuyen area is estimated at a
robust VND 72–77 million/person/year, Cho Bung area is estimated at a robust VND 73-75
million/person/year for 2025, with expectations for continued growth driven by the expanding
industrial and service sectors.
d. Cultural Resources
Community and Intangible Culture: The "All people unite to build a cultural life" movement is
highly effective, with 100% of hamlets recognized as Cultural Hamlets and 96–99% of
households designated as Cultural Families. Regular cultural, artistic, and sports activities
foster strong community cohesion.
Physical Cultural Resources: Thap Muoi Commune is home to the Go Thap Relic Site, an
archaeological complex of immense historical and cultural significance in the Dong Thap Muoi
region. Furthermore, the Cho Bung Canal alignment includes religious sites (a Pagoda) situated
near the canal banks.
Cultural Economy: Community-based tourism tied to the local wetland ecosystems and lotus
landscapes is gradually developing, generating alternative livelihoods and improving the
spiritual well-being of the local population.
e. Infrastructure Systems
Transport and Irrigation: The infrastructure network is relatively well-developed but faces
significant climate and anthropogenic pressures. Transport infrastructure relies heavily on
provincial roads (e.g., DT.865, DT.867) and upgraded ring dikes that double as flood-resistant
rural roads. Irrigation networks (canals, sluices, pumping stations) are comprehensive and vital
for flood control, though many require climate-resilient upgrades.
Encroachment and Spatial Constraints: Along the Phuoc Xuyen 28 Canal, urbanization and
human impacts increase downstream. The section from K26+500 to K44+000 is highly dense,
featuring complex infrastructure (markets, schools) and severe canal encroachment, which has
narrowed the waterway from 50m to 34–37m, impeding flow and posing marine traffic risks.
Industrial and Sensitive Receptors: The Cho Bung Canal area (K5+00 to K6+00) features dense
industrial infrastructure, including construction material yards and concrete casting plants with
moored barges. Furthermore, the downstream section (K13+650 to K17+000) contains highly
sensitive social receptors, including a secondary school and a pagoda, requiring strict
construction-phase E&S management.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             75
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Basic Services: Environmental infrastructure is strong, with over 95% of households accessing
clean/hygienic water and solid waste collection. Electricity and telecommunications are stable
maintained.

4.2.3. Subproject 2.3
4.2.3.1. Natural condition
a. Geography
The Subproject 2.3 area falls within the communes of Truong Khanh, Dai Ngai, Ngoc To, and
Thanh Thoi An of Can Tho city.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                77
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)




                   Figure 4. 11: Location of Sub-project 2.3 components
b. Topography
The SP 2.3 project area is low-lying and flat, with elevations of 0.2–2.0 m above mean sea level
(inland areas 0.5–1.0 m), characterised by alluvial plains interspersed with depressions and low
sandy mounds along river and coastal margins. The terrain slopes gently inland from the Hậu
River and from the East Sea/Quan Lộ Canal. The flat topography favours gravity irrigation
during high tides but creates persistent flood risk in coastal aquaculture areas that sit below
high-tide levels, and the highly dissected canal network increases infrastructure costs.
c. Geology
The subproject area overlies a Mesozoic bedrock foundation at depths ranging from near-
surface in the northern delta to approximately 1,000 m near the coast. The overlying
sedimentary sequence comprises four principal strata: Holocene deposits (clay and fine-to-
medium sand at surface); Pleistocene deposits (sand and gravel mixed with marine clay and
silt); Pliocene deposits (clay and medium sand); and Miocene deposits (clay and medium sand).
Holocene soft clays dominate the near-surface profile across all construction sites and govern
foundation design and ground improvement requirements.
d. Hydrographic and Oceanographic Conditions
The hydrological regime is governed by East Sea tides, local rainfall, and flows from the Hậu
and Mỹ Thanh rivers, with a pronounced seasonal differentiation. The area experiences an
irregular semi-diurnal tidal regime (two tidal cycles per day). Tidal ranges at Đại Ngãi average
1.84–2.18 m (October–March), with the lowest trough in June (–103 cm) and highest in
November (–24 cm). During the rainy season (July–November), elevated upstream Hậu River
discharge suppresses saline intrusion. During the dry season, reduced upstream flows allow
salinity to penetrate deep into the canal network, affecting agricultural production in all
communes not protected by salinity-control sluices.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            78
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


The canal network in Sóc Trăng has a density of approximately 2.5–3.0 km/km². Key
waterways include the Hậu River (width 1,000–1,500 m; average dry-season discharge 2,400
m³/s, flood-season peak 41,500 m³/s), the Mỹ Thanh River (length ~25 km, average width ~200
m, average depth 11.5–14.0 m), and the Quan Lộ–Phụng Hiệp Canal, together with associated
primary canal systems (Saintard, Cái Trăm, Rạch Vọp, 30/4, and Tiếp Nhật canals). Most canal
flows are bidirectional throughout the year under tidal influence, enabling gravity irrigation and
drainage across much of the area. Water quality in the canal network is generally poor due to
saline intrusion, acid sulfate contamination, and domestic and agricultural waste discharge.
The Đại Ngãi River connecting the Xang Canal to Mỹ Xuyên is 60–80 m wide and 4–6 m deep,
and is directly relevant to the SP 2.3 lock and sluice construction area.
e. Natural Disasters
Key natural hazard risks in the project area include: saline intrusion (severe and worsening
during dry season); riverbank and coastal erosion; storm surges (storms are rare but have caused
significant historical damage, notably in 1977); and localised tidal flooding in low-lying areas,
particularly where approximately 60,000 ha along the Quan Lộ–Phụng Hiệp Canal experience
inundation depths of 0.6–1.0 m. Mean annual temperature is 26.8°C, with a recorded maximum
of 37.8°C (May) and minimum of 16.2°C (January).
4.2.3.2. Baseline Environmental Conditions
Ambient Air and Noise: All monitored parameters (TSP, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and noise)
comply with QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT (1-hour average) and QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT at all
construction sites, confirming an acceptable pre-construction baseline.
Surface Water Quality: Most parameters comply with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column B).
However, BOD₅ exceeds the permissible limit by 1.6 times at monitoring point NM6, and COD
exceeds the limit by 2.0 times (NM6) and 1.14 times (NM7), indicating elevated organic
loading at localised sites. Dissolved oxygen consumption is high across the system due to
significant pollutant inputs. Water is suitable for industrial and agricultural use after appropriate
treatment but not for direct domestic supply without treatment. These baseline exceedances
indicate limited assimilative capacity and heightened sensitivity to additional construction-
phase organic and suspended sediment loading.
Sediment Quality: Phase 1 monitoring (April 2025) found heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr,
Cu, Zn) well below QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT limits, with Fe exceeding the standard at some
locations. Phase 2 monitoring (February 2026) confirmed all parameters, including Fe, within
permissible limits. Pesticide residues were effectively undetectable in both phases. Overall,
sediments show no evidence of heavy metal pollution or toxic accumulation and are suitable
for reuse in backfilling and embankment construction.
Aquatic Ecology: The aquatic ecosystem exhibits estuarine/brackish water characteristics with
degraded ecological quality. Species diversity is low across all biological assemblage groups.
Blue-green algal dominance in the phytoplankton community, combined with the presence of
pollution-tolerant benthic indicator species, indicates chronic eutrophication driven by excess
organic and nutrient loading. Natural habitat quality for sensitive native aquatic species is poor
across the subproject area.
4.2.3.3. Socio-Economic Conditions
a. Population and Ethnicity
The SP 2.3 construction area spans several communes with medium-to-high population
densities (300–450 people/km²). Key communes include Đại Ngãi (5,462 households; ~16,154
people), Trường Khánh (5,557 households), Ngọc Tố (~47,259 people), and Thanh Thới An


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 79
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


(6,701 households; ~18,320 people). Settlement is concentrated along National Highway Nam
Sông Hậu, provincial roads, and main canal banks. Ethnic minority communities — principally
Khmer and Hoa — are present in Trường Khánh Commune and require targeted engagement
and livelihood assessment under ESS7.
        Table 4. 11: Population and Ethnic Characteristics of the Subprojects 2.3
