area in 2025
Age and Gender Structure: The population structure is entering a stage of local "aging." The
main labor force (18 - 40 years old) is largely absent. The female ratio is usually slightly higher
than the male ratio locally because young men tend to migrate more to find employment.
b. Economic Development
Growth Rate: The economy in the subproject implementation area maintains a fair and stable
growth rate, making an important contribution to the total GRDP of Can Tho City.
Economic Structure: Agriculture – Fisheries play a leading role, but there is a clear
differentiation between two areas: Dai Ngai and Truong Khanh Area: Located along the Hau
River, the economic structure leans toward freshwater agriculture (specialty rice, upland crops,
fruit trees) and is strongly developing riverside trade - services and logistics. Truong Khanh
Commune has a rice cultivation area of 8,261.45 ha. This locality focuses on developing
fragrant and specialty rice and has established 06 OCOP products reaching 3-star standards.
Dai Ngai has a smaller rice cultivation scale (3,268.11 ha) but achieves very high yields,
estimated at 6.8 tons/ha. The commune also focuses on high-quality rice varieties and specialty
fragrant rice. Livestock mainly consists of cattle and poultry. Income in this area comes from
rice, upland crops, fruit orchards, and small to medium-sized service businesses. Survey results
in the area planned for the construction of the Dai Ngai ship lock show no households engaged
in aquaculture or fishing on the Saintard River.
Ngoc To and Thanh Thoi An Area: Characterized by a coastal economic model and a brackish-
freshwater transition zone. The economic pillars here are brackish water aquaculture and the
"shrimp - rice" rotation model. Thanh Thoi An Commune is a large-scale rice bowl with a total
planting area of up to 12,134 ha. The commune pays special attention to fragrant and specialty
rice (accounting 91.26% of the area). Ngoc To Commune has a smaller rice area (6,491 ha) but
is very well-developed in fisheries with 4,495 ha of shrimp farming (black tiger shrimp and
white-leg shrimp). Income here mainly comes from brackish water shrimp farming, the shrimp
- rice rotation model, and inland fishing. Survey results in the area planned for the construction
of the My Xuyen ship lock show no households engaged in aquaculture or fishing on the Bay
Xao River.
Industry - Services: The industrial proportion mainly revolves around agricultural and aquatic
product processing (export shrimp processing, rice milling) and handicrafts. Ngoc To shows
more vibrant commercial development with total retail sales of goods and services reaching
2,183 billion VND and an industrial - handicraft production value of 511 billion VND. This
locality has up to 11 3-star OCOP products. Thanh Thoi An has total retail sales of consumer

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                       80
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


services reaching 983 billion VND and an industrial - handicraft production value of 144.34
billion VND. The commune currently has 03 products reaching 3-star OCOP standards. In Dai
Ngai, there is 01 stably operating passenger bus station and 02 announced inland waterway
wharves. In Truong Khanh, industry has not yet developed, while trade and services are
gradually growing to serve the needs of the people.
c. Labor Force, Employment, and Income
Labor Scale and Distribution: The labor force is abundant, concentrated primarily in rural areas
and working in the agriculture-forestry-fishery sector (accounting for over 60% of total
laborers).
Employment and Unemployment: The official unemployment rate is low, but
underemployment during the agricultural off-season is common. This has led to a trend of
young laborers migrating to Ho Chi Minh City, Binh Duong, and Dong Nai to work in industrial
zones, leaving behind an increasingly aging agricultural workforce.
Average Income: Income is gradually improving but remains lower than the national average.
People's income depends heavily on fluctuations in input prices (shrimp feed, fertilizer) and the
purchasing prices of agricultural and aquatic products. The average income of communes in the
area ranges from 67 – 73 million VND/year (Political Report of the Ward/Commune Party
Executive Committee, 2025).
d. Cultural Resources
Customs and Traditions: The cultural intersection of the Kinh, Khmer, and Hoa groups is very
distinct. The Khmer community strongly preserves traditional customs with major festivals
such as Chol Chnam Thmay (New Year), Sene Dolta (Ancestors' Day), and Ok Om Bok (Moon
Worship and Ngo boat racing).
Cultural Relics: The area has many religious establishments, particularly Khmer Theravada
Pagodas with unique architecture, which serve as centers for the cultural, educational, and
spiritual lives of the local people.
e. Infrastructure Systems
Transportation: The area is well-connected by a dense waterway system and important road
routes. The Dai Ngai area benefits directly from the South Hau River National Highway
(NH.91B) and the future Dai Ngai Bridge project connecting Soc Trang with Tra Vinh. Rural
transportation in Ngoc To and Thanh Thoi An has been significantly concretized under the New
Rural Development program. Inland waterway activities are developed on rivers throughout the
region. The Saintard and Bai Xau rivers are Grade III national inland waterway routes with high
volumes of vessels and barges. This infrastructural characteristic is a decisive factor, requiring
the project not only to build saltwater prevention sluices but also to design additional ship locks
(lock chambers 15m wide and 100m long) to operate in parallel. Furthermore, the road
infrastructure (National Highway 91B, Dai Ngai 2 Bridge currently under construction) is very
favorable for mobilizing heavy equipment and construction materials such as Larsen sheet piles
and pile drivers.
Power Supply and Telecommunications: 100% of the communes and towns in the project area
have access to the national power grid and telecommunications coverage, including broadband
internet, effectively meeting production and domestic needs.
Water Supply: The rate of hygienic water use reaches 83.3 – 89.5%, and the rate of clean water
use from centralized systems reaches 73.5 – 91.97%. However, the locality still faces situations
where a portion of the population experiences localized shortages of domestic water.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               81
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Industrial Zones/Clusters: The Dai Ngai area is forming and developing the Dai Ngai Industrial
Cluster associated with river ports, oriented toward attracting processing, energy, and logistics
industries.
Education System: The adult literacy rate is relatively high. All communes are fully equipped
with kindergartens, primary schools, and junior high schools. The primary school enrollment
rate reaches nearly 100%. However, dropout rates at the junior and senior high school levels
still occur sporadically due to families facing economic difficulties or students leaving school
to work and support their families.
Health Facilities: Each commune/town has a national standard Health Station, providing first
aid and vaccinations. Common health issues include gastrointestinal diseases (due to using
unhygienic water during the drought and saltwater season), dengue fever, respiratory diseases,
and musculoskeletal disorders among elderly agricultural laborers.

4.2.4. Subproject 2.4
4.2.4.1. Natural condition
a. Geography
The South Mang Thit area is geographically bounded by the Mang Thit River to the northwest,
the Co Chien River to the northeast, Provincial Road 914 in Dong Thap Province to the

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                    82
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


southeast, and the Hau River to the southwest. The construction works are situated within nine
communes of Vinh Long Province, namely Trung Hiep, Hoa Binh, Tra Con, Hieu Phung, Trung
Ngai, Cang Long, Nhi Long, Phong Thanh Phu, and An Phu Tan




                           Figure 4. 12: Location of Subproject 2.4
a. Topography
The SP 2.4 project area occupies the downstream end of a river island between the Tiền and
Hậu Rivers, with generally flat terrain at approximately 1 m above mean sea level. Crescent-
shaped sand ridges run parallel to the coastline, becoming higher and wider toward the sea,
creating an alternating pattern of elevated ridges and low-lying depressions fragmented by an
extensive road and canal network. Southern areas are lower (0.5–0.8 m), experiencing seasonal
saline intrusion (0.4–0.8‰) for 3–5 months annually. The topography supports diverse land use
— fruit trees and upland crops on ridges, rice at low-to-medium elevations, and aquaculture in
low-lying areas outside the dikes — but constrains freshwater supply development and
contributes to frequent waterlogging in depressions. Lower-level sluices have limited hydraulic
capacity and primarily function to exclude salinity and store freshwater; upper-level sluices
have comparatively better hydraulic performance.
b. Geology
Foundation conditions vary significantly across SP 2.4 construction sites and represent a key
geotechnical risk. At the Say Đón, Trường Bụng, Cái Cá, Mương Khái Lớn, and Sa Rài sluice
locations, the weak soil layer is very thick and the load-bearing stratum lies at depths exceeding
50 m, making construction costly and creating risks of sliding, erosion, and settlement cracking.
At the Trà Ngoa sluice location, soil layer No. 2 provides a viable load-bearing foundation, but
an overlying weak layer of 11.0–17.0 m depth requires careful design and ground improvement
measures. Foundation strengthening is feasible, using melaleuca timbers for this purpose is
common in the Mekong Delta.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              83
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


c. Hydrographic and Oceanographic Conditions
The SP 2.4 area is drained by two major river systems. The Cổ Chiên River — a principal Tiền
River distributary, approximately 45 km long within Vĩnh Long Province — has average
discharges of 12,000–19,000 m³/s, with flow velocities of 0.5–1.5 m/s in narrow sections
causing active riverbank erosion and sediment concentrations of 100–500 g/m³. Key tributaries
include the Mang Thít River (~47 km, connecting Cổ Chiên to the Hậu River) and the Cái Hóp–
An Trường River (~25 km). The Hậu River flows through Vĩnh Long Province for
approximately 65 km in a northwest-southeast direction, with average discharges of 20,000–
30,000 m³/s and sediment concentrations of 200–600 g/m³. Key tributaries include the Bồng
Bót–Ba Nghe–Tống Tơn river system (~30 km total) and the Trà Cú–Vàm Bướn River (~18
km). The Trà Ngoa Canal (~26 km) is a major inland canal influenced by both the Cổ Chiên
and Hậu rivers via the Mang Thít River, serving freshwater supply functions across the
subproject area. Canal water in the lower reaches is saline for approximately 5–6 months
annually.
4.2.4.3. Baseline Environmental Conditions
Ambient Air and Noise: All monitored parameters (TSP, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, and noise) are
within allowable limits set in national standards (QCVN) QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT (1-hour
average) and QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT at all construction sites, confirming an acceptable pre-
construction baseline.
Surface Water Quality: pH values comply with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column B) at all
monitoring points in both monitoring rounds. However, dissolved oxygen is a key concern:
April 2025 monitoring recorded low DO values at three sites — NM24 at the Trà Ngoa sluice
location (3.78 mg/L), NM23 at Cái Hóp (below standard), and NM19 at Cái Cá (4.01–4.21
mg/L) — indicating organic matter decomposition reducing oxygen availability for aquatic
organisms. By February 2026, DO values had recovered to 1.1–1.14 times above permissible
limits. Turbidity increased significantly between monitoring phases: 12.2–12.7 NTU (moderate)
in April 2025, rising to 42–110 NTU (high) in February 2026, warranting close monitoring
during construction to prevent further deterioration of aquatic habitat conditions. Dry-season
salinity was high (11.2–11.7‰) in April 2025, consistent with the reduced upstream Mekong
inflows during this period. BOD₅, COD, TSS, NH₄⁺, Cl⁻, As, total phosphorus, oil/grease, and
total coliforms all complied with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT (Column B) in the February 2026
campaign.
Sediment Quality: Sediment pH ranged from neutral (7.12–7.27) in April 2025 to slightly
acidic (5.95–6.32) in February 2026, indicating dynamic geochemical conditions. Sediment
salinity levels ranging from 0.384 to 0.474 g/kg indicate that surface runoff from material
stored at disposal sites (totaling 114,000 m³ from 6 sluice sites) must be monitored to
prevent saline drainage from affecting adjacent agricultural and aquaculture areas. Iron (Fe)
exceeded QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT limits; all other heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr, Cu, Zn)
were within permissible limits in both monitoring campaigns, with pesticide residues
effectively undetectable.
Aquatic Ecology: The aquatic ecosystem in SP 2.4 is under severe stress, exhibiting clear
indicators of organic pollution and eutrophication throughout the water column. Surface layers
show cyanobacterial blooms and abundant zooplankton driven by excess nutrient loading,
creating a high risk of harmful algal blooms and toxin production. Bottom layers suffer from
oxygen depletion and pollutant accumulation, resulting in impoverished benthic fauna. This
stratified degradation pattern poses significant risk to native aquatic species of both ecological
and economic value, and represents a sensitive baseline condition that construction activities
must not further exacerbate.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              84
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


4.2.4.4. Socio – economic conditions
a. Population and Ethnicity
The population of Vinh Long province is approximately 4,257,581 people. Vinh Long has
become one of the most populous provinces in the region (ranking 3rd in the Mekong Delta),
providing an abundant human resource for socio-economic development. The project area has
a population density ranging from medium to relatively high as a result of recent administrative
unit mergers. Settlements are mainly concentrated along major transportation routes and
primary canals.
The project implementation area includes the communes of Trung Hiep, Hieu Phung, Hoa Binh,
Tra Con, Trung Ngai, Cang Long, Nhi Long, An Phu Tan, and Phong Thanh. There is a
significant population disparity between localities, ranging from approximately 1,111 people in
Phong Thanh commune to 71,244 people in Phu Tan commune due to the merger process.
The inhabitants are predominantly Kinh people. However, in certain communes (Tra Con, Cang
Long, Nhi Long, Hieu Phung), there is a presence of Khmer and Hoa ethnic minority
communities. This intermingling requires the project to apply culturally appropriate
consultation measures and an Ethnic Minority Development Plan (EMDP).
        Table 4. 13: Population and Ethnic Characteristics of the Subprojects 2.4
                        Natural       Total                          Khmer       Hoa         Other

   2    Tra Con            59.37         45,778         771.1          231        0            0
   3    Hoa Binh           64.46         27,270         423.1          132        0            0

   5    Hieu Phung         42.46         28,418         669.3          520        80           0

 (Source: Political Reports of the Party Executive Committees of the communes in the project
                                                                                area, 2025)
b. Economic Development
Growth Rate: The Vinh Long area maintains a fairly stable GRDP growth rate. Despite being
affected by saltwater intrusion periods, the economy is showing recovery and positive structural
shifts.
Agriculture: Continues to play a leading role in the region. A highlight of the South Mang Thit
area is the strong transition from rice cultivation to high-value fruit crops, such as VietGAP
standard rice, specialized fruit orchards (king orange, Nam Roi pomelo, rambutan, durian), and
freshwater aquaculture (pangasius along the rivers).
Industry: Developing with sectors such as agricultural and food processing, textiles, and
particularly the traditional ceramic brick production industry (along the Mang Thit River). The
system of traditional markets and retail commercial services along national highways
contributes significantly to local GRDP.
Services: Growing thanks to intra-regional trade, waterway logistics, and garden eco-tourism.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  85
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


c. Labor Force, Employment, and Income
Scale and Distribution: The labor force is abundant, but the majority still operates within the
informal agricultural sector. The working-age population rate is high (about 62%); however,
the rate of laborers with professional training remains limited (under 50%).
Employment and Moving Rates: Like many places in the Mekong Delta, the South Mang Thit
area faces high mechanical Movement. Young people tend to leave their hometowns for Ho
Chi Minh City or Southeast provinces to work as factory laborers. Consequently, the local
agricultural workforce is aging rapidly, causing localized labor shortages during harvest
seasons.
Average Income: Livelihoods still primarily depend on subsistence or household-scale
agriculture. Local handicraft establishments and commercial services have begun to create
stable non-agricultural jobs (average income of 6.5 - 7 million VND/month), helping to
diversify income sources for the people.
d. Poverty Levels, Resource Dependency, and Economic Vulnerabilities
Poverty and Inequality: The overall poverty rate is low (mostly under 1%), yet vulnerable
groups remain, with precarious livelihoods due to a lack of productive land. The poverty rate
in the communes is very low, indicating an improved quality of life. For example, the poverty
rate in Cang Long is only 0.18%, Hieu Phung is 0.26%, and Trung Hiep is 0.27%.
Resource Dependency: The economy of South Mang Thit depends absolutely on freshwater
sources. The ecosystem of fruit orchards and pangasius ponds is extremely sensitive to salinity.
Core Economic Vulnerabilities: The regional economy is entirely dependent on the freshwater
hydrological system. The greatest weakness of this area is the risk of massive economic loss
due to saltwater intrusion. Unlike rice (where a loss only costs one 3-month crop), if durian,
orange, or pomelo orchards are affected by salinity, the roots will die, and it takes 4-5 years of
replanting before they bear fruit again, leading to a very high risk of re-poverty for orchard
owners.
e. Infrastructure and Social Systems
Transportation: This area has a special advantage in waterway transport, with the Mang Thit
River serving as a vital trade route connecting the Tien and Hau rivers, forming a national inland
waterway transport corridor. Road transport is also developed with National Highways 53, 54,
and 57 passing through.
Power Supply and Telecommunications: The rate of households with access to the national grid
and internet/telecommunications reaches nearly 100%, comprehensively meeting domestic
needs and the application of technology (smart agriculture) for the people.
Water Supply: Rural tap water infrastructure is relatively good. However, a serious challenge
arises during the dry season when saltwater from the sea follows river mouths (Co Chien, Hau)
deep into the Mang Thit River, rendering freshwater pumping stations ineffective. The
percentage of households using hygienic clean water from supply stations reaches about 95%.
Domestic and medical waste collection efforts achieve good rates (86% - 100%).
Industrial Zones/Clusters: The area features key industrial zones such as Hoa Phu and Binh
Minh (Vinh Long) and Co Chien (Tra Vinh), attracting a large volume of local labor.
Health and Education: Social welfare is ensured, as the health insurance participation rate in
most communes reaches approximately or exactly 100%. The education system effectively
meets learning needs with high school attendance rates and many facilities meeting national
standards.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              86
             Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)


Environmental Protection: The collection of domestic waste and the treatment of medical and
solid waste are carried out regularly and synchronously, achieving very high rates across
localities.

                      CHAPTER 5: ANALYSIS OF ALTERNATIVES
The Analysis of Alternatives (AoA) is a mandatory component of the ESIA under ESS1,
requiring systematic comparison of feasible project options — including location, technology,
design, scale, and operating procedures — against a "no-project" baseline. Alternatives are
evaluated across environmental, social, technical, and economic criteria, with the objective of
identifying the option that minimises adverse impacts while remaining technically and
financially feasible. The AoA was conducted during the feasibility study stage to allow findings
to meaningfully inform project design.

5.1. "With Project" vs. "Without Project" Scenario

The Mekong Delta's irrigation system — comprising thousands of kilometres of canals, dykes,
and sluice gates — underpins agricultural production across the region. In key subregions
including Đồng Tháp Mười and the Southwest Hậu River area, this infrastructure has severely
deteriorated: canals are heavily silted, low-ring dykes are eroded, and regulating sluices are
non-functional, substantially reducing water management effectiveness.
The delta is simultaneously facing compounding climate and anthropogenic stressors. Mean
temperatures have risen by 0.5–1.0°C above the historical baseline. Annual rainfall fluctuates
between 2,187–2,543 mm with an increasingly adverse seasonal distribution: higher-intensity
rainfall concentrated in shorter rainy-season periods, and a prolonged dry season with reduced
precipitation, increased evaporation, and growing water deficits. Upstream flow regulation by
the Mekong mainstream reservoir system has further altered the natural hydrological regime,
disrupting agricultural production cycles and depleting biodiversity. Specific acute
manifestations include deep inundation and infrastructure destruction in Đồng Tháp Mười
during major flood events, severe freshwater depletion on the Cà Mau Peninsula during
extended droughts, and accelerating riverbank and coastal erosion in Cần Thơ, An Giang, and
along both coasts due to sediment starvation.
Under Vietnam's updated climate scenarios (aligned with IPCC AR6), sea levels in the delta
are projected to rise by approximately 13 cm by 2030, 30–50 cm by 2050, and 72–75 cm by
2100 under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 pathways. Average temperatures are expected to increase by
1–2°C, dry-season salinity intrusion is projected to extend 50–70 km inland, and an estimated
30–50% of agricultural land in coastal provinces faces varying degrees of impact without
proactive adaptation.
Without the project, these trends will continue unchecked: deteriorating irrigation infrastructure
will lose further functionality, saline intrusion will increasingly compromise freshwater
agricultural zones, coastal erosion will accelerate in the absence of sediment management, and
vulnerable farming and fishing communities will face growing economic displacement with no
adaptive infrastructure support.
The MERIT Project is designed as a strategic, integrated response to this trajectory, combining
infrastructure investment, institutional strengthening, and livelihood transition support to
stabilise the delta's hydrology and protect the livelihoods of its rural population. The table below
provides a structured comparison of key environmental and social outcomes under both
scenarios.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                89
            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)

                      localized settlement causing a loss of aesthetic appeal. Alternative 2 offers good construction quality and maintains aesthetic value, but the

 CHAPTER 6: ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

This chapter assesses MERIT's environmental and social risks and impacts by component: an
overall assessment for Components 1 and 3, and a phased assessment (pre-construction,
construction, operation) for Component 2. The assessment identifies, predicts, and evaluates direct,
indirect, and cumulative impacts arising from MERIT's physical works and directly induced
changes, providing the basis for mitigation measures to avoid, minimize, mitigate, or compensate
for adverse impacts. It complies with applicable national legislation, ESS1 and other relevant ESSs,
and follows WBG EHS Guidelines and GIIP. Impact magnitude is classified using the criteria set
out in Table 6.1, consistent with Directive 2011/92/EU (as amended by 2014/52/EU) and the
approach applied across other World Bank-financed projects in Vietnam.
This ESIA does not replicate the strategic-level analysis already undertaken for the Mekong Delta
under Decisions 284/QD-TTg, 847/QD-TTg, and 2307/QD-TTg, which addressed the regional and
cumulative implications of the broader development trajectory within MERIT area. This ESIA
relies on those findings for strategic context — conditionally on the SEAs having been conducted
to a standard sufficient to capture MERIT-type interventions and remaining consistent with ESF
requirements — and addresses any project-specific gaps in full.
6.1. Positive Environmental and Social Impact and Cumulative Benefits
Strategic Policy Alignment: The MERIT project operationalizes the Government of Vietnam's
critical policy instruments, notably Resolution 120/NQ-TW and the Mekong Delta Regional
Master Plan (MDRMP). Rather than merely listing these policies, the project actively applies their
mandates by shifting the region from a purely defensive flood-control approach to an integrated,
data-driven framework. It embraces hydro-ecological transition zones (e.g., adapting to brackish
water rather than rigidly fighting it) to build long-term climate resilience and sustainably transform
the agricultural sector.
Component 1: Enhancing Institutions and Information Systems: provides the foundational data
architecture for the entire project. By upgrading disaster forecasting, analytical tools, and regional
monitoring networks including the establishment and operationalization of the Mekong Delta
Center, the project will benefit hundreds of thousands of residents by providing robust early
warning systems for salinity intrusion and extreme weather. This ensures that local communities
and provincial agencies can make proactive, data-driven decisions regarding planting schedules,
water use, and disaster preparedness.
Component 2: Investing in Climate-Resilient Infrastructure: delivers the physical hydraulic control
required to stabilize the environment. The construction of regional sluice gates, ship locks, and
embankments will directly secure freshwater resources and manage localized flooding.
Salinity Boundary Control: Hydrodynamic modeling demonstrates that upon operation, the project
will actively push back the 4 g/l salinity boundary on the Hậu River by approximately 0.9 km, and
the 1 g/l boundary by 1.2 km.
Duration of Salinity Intrusion: Compared to the "without-project" baseline, where unmitigated
salinity severely damages agricultural yields and isolates freshwater supplies, the MERIT
infrastructure will actively regulate saltwater exposure. With the project in place, the area affected
by the 1 g/l boundary for more than 3 months is stabilized at 1.305 million ha, and the 4 g/l
boundary at 1.154 million ha, securing crucial freshwater source for agricultural production.
2030 Climate Change Adaptation: Under the 2030 climate change scenario, sea-level rise will
inevitably increase regional salinity pressure. However, the MERIT infrastructure will actively
prevent an additional 43,000 hectares from being lost to saline intrusion, successfully buffering
the project area against severe climate deterioration.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)   102
               Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)

Component 3: Promoting Diversified Livelihoods and a Climate-Smart Rural Economy:
Leveraging the data from Component 1 and the physical security from Component 2, Component
3 supports farmers in transitioning away from intensive triple-rice cropping toward climate-
resilient, high-value livelihood models (such as shrimp-mangrove, rice-lotus, and eco-shrimp
systems).
Inclusion of Vulnerable Groups: The livelihood benefits are explicitly targeted to uplift
marginalized populations. The project specifically integrates vulnerable households, landless
smallholders, women (targeting at least 40% participation), rural youth, and ethnic minorities
(such as the Khmer in Vĩnh Long), where the project targets a minimum 30% ethnic minority
inclusion rate). These highly sensitive groups will receive prioritized access to vocational training,
microfinance, and agricultural extension services to ensure inclusive economic empowerment.
Environmental Prerequisites for Success: The economic success and sustainability of these
livelihood models are fundamentally dependent on strict environmental management. Increased
income from freshwater and brackish aquaculture can only be realized if robust sustainable
climate-smart agricultural practices, and rigorous water quality monitoring are enforced to prevent
intra-field pollution, agricultural run-off, and disease outbreaks.
Cumulative Synergistic Benefits: Ultimately, the MERIT project components do not operate in
isolation; they interact to produce cumulative benefits far greater than the sum of their parts.
Component 1’s early warning data informs the precise operation of Component 2’s sluice gates,
which in turn creates the stable hydro-ecological conditions required for Component 3’s vulnerable
farmers to safely invest in high-value, climate-smart agriculture. This integrated approach ensures
proactive climate adaptation, livelihood protection, and sustainable socio-economic development
across the Mekong Delta region.
6.2. Classification of negative impacts and risks
Potential negative environmental and social impacts and risks are classified across four levels —
Substantial, Moderate, Low, and No Impact — based on three interrelated dimensions: the duration
and geographic scale of the impact, the severity of effects on the physical and biological
environment, and the degree of harm to communities and human health. Risk levels are derived
from a combination of baseline data collection, field surveys across the project area, and
consultations with government officials and affected stakeholders. These inputs are assessed
against the nature and footprint of the proposed civil works under Component 2 — including
dredging, embankment construction, wave breakers, and sluice/ship-lock installation — and the
particular sensitivities of the Mekong Delta context, notably its dynamic estuarine and canal
ecosystems, acid-sulfate and saline soils, canal-dependent livelihoods, presence of Khmer ethnic
minority communities, and exposure to UXO hazards. The resulting classification reflects the
project's overall Substantial ESF risk rating and informs the mitigation hierarchy and management
measures detailed in the ESMP and associated instruments.

Indirect: An impact caused by the project but occurring later in time or further in distance than a
direct impact, and reasonably foreseeable, but excluding induced impacts.
Cumulative: The incremental impact of the project when added to impacts from other past, present,
and reasonably foreseeable developments, including unplanned but predictable activities enabled
by the project.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)   104
               Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)

Residual: The negative environmental, social, or economic effect that remains after all feasible
mitigation measures have been implemented — the unavoidable impact that persists despite efforts
to reduce harm.
Induced: Secondary consequences beyond the project's direct footprint, typically driven by
growth-enabling effects such as new infrastructure, population influx, or land-use change, which
generate new environmental pressures not initially planned but stemming from the project.
The environmental and social impact assessment is carried out by project component — covering
pre-construction, construction, and operation phases for Component 2, and an overall assessment
for Components 1 and 3. The assessment identifies, predicts, and evaluates direct, indirect, and
cumulative impacts, with findings used to recommend appropriate mitigation measures and
management plans to avoid, minimize, mitigate, or compensate for adverse impacts and risks. The
assessment complies with national legislation, ESS1 and applicable ESSs, and the WBG EHS
Guidelines and GIIPs.

- Temporary land acquisition

specific construction duration in accordance with the Project’s policy. In addition, upon completion of construction activities, the contractor shall be
responsible for reinstating the affected land to its original condition.
During the construction phase, unforeseen impacts such as subsidence, flooding, Erosion, and Sedimentation may occur. When such impacts arise, the
project follows mitigation hierarchy specified in the ESF: avoid minimizing – reduce – compensate/offset. Only compensate if still affected after all
other approaches had been trialed.

Impacts on trees
The total number of affected trees is 14,966, including banana, coconut, tamarind, jackfruit,
areca, mango, and bougainvillea of which subproject 2.1 does not impact on trees and crops,
subproject 2.2 impacted on 3,916 trees, subproject 2.2 impacted on 8,763 and subproject 2.4
impacted on 2,287 trees. In general, these trees do not have high economic value.
Social and livelihood impacts

The subproject will involve land acquisition affecting 141 households; however, the majority
of the acquired land is agricultural land with a relatively small acquisition rate (less than 30%).
Across all four subprojects, only 11 households will lose between 30% and 70% of their
productive land. For households losing less than 30% of their land, their main livelihoods are
not dependent on the affected land area; therefore, the project is not expected to significantly
impact their income or livelihoods. For households losing between 30% and 70% of their
productive land, most of the affected land is abandoned or saline land with low productivity;
thus, the land acquisition is also not expected to adversely affect their primary livelihoods.

In addition to land impacts, the project will affect assets on land, including houses, structures,
trees, and crops. The impacts on structures and tree and crops are generally minor. The affected
trees do not have high economic value. Regarding housing impacts, 34 households will need to
relocate their homes, of which 31 households have built houses on encroached land originally
designated for transport or irrigation and managed by the Commune People’s Committee. All
34 households will receive compensation and assistance for relocation. All of them prefer cash
compensation and self-relocation: one household can resettle in situ by rebuilding on the


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                      109
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

remaining land, while the other 33 households intend to use compensation funds to resettle on
other land plots they already own locally.

In addition to affected households, four clam-farming cooperatives will have 24 hectares of
leased water surface area acquired. However, cooperative members cultivate clams in dispersed,
non-concentrated plots. The construction sites do not overlap with any household clam farming
plots, which are located approximately 2 km away from the construction areas. Therefore, land
acquisition will not affect clam farming activities. The cooperatives will receive compensation
for investments made on the land, along with assistance for production and business
stabilization.

Considering the scope of land acquisition impacts and the characteristics of household
livelihoods and production activities, the overall impact on social conditions and livelihoods is
assessed to be Moderate.

6.3.1.2. Risks and Impacts Related to Unexploded Ordnance (UXO)
The project area — including Đồng Tháp Mười, Nam Mang Thít, and major river corridors —
was heavily affected during the Vietnam War, with extensive use of bombs, artillery, cluster
munitions, and anti-personnel mines. Residual UXO is likely embedded in both soils and
riverbeds across a significant portion of the project footprint, creating a high-magnitude latent
safety hazard for construction workers engaged in excavation, dredging, and deep foundation
works. Although no UXO incidents have been reported in the project area in the past ten years,
the consequence of accidental detonation — fatality, serious injury, equipment damage, and
construction disruption — is severe enough to require mandatory pre-construction clearance
across all affected areas before any civil works commence.
UXO clearance will be conducted by licensed specialist combat engineering contractors in
coordination with the Ministry of National Defence, following three sequential steps: (i)
preliminary survey using historical battle maps and local knowledge to identify high-risk zones;
(ii) detection using magnetic detectors and ground-penetrating radar; and (iii) neutralisation and
removal by qualified technicians in compliance with safety and environmental standards. The
entire process must comply with Circular 121/2021/TT-BQP (Ministry of National Defence).
Civil works shall only proceed in areas formally certified as UXO-free by the competent
military authority. This risk and impact are considered as High without mitigation and
monitoring measures.
            Table 6. 6: Estimated UXO clearance costs for specific subprojects
              Subproject                                     Amount (VND)
             Subproject 2.1                                   8,814,600,000
             Subproject 2.2                                  40,963,100,994
             Subproject 2.3                                   1,706,905,000
             Subproject 2.4                                   5,495,615,808
                Total                                        56,980,221,802

6.3.2. Impacts and Risks in Construction Phase
6.3.2.1. Dust and Emissions During Construction
Three primary dust and emission sources are associated with construction across all subprojects:
-   Excavation and earthworks: Excavation for construction of the embankments and sluices
    under SP2.2 generate dusts.
-   Materials and waste transport: Heavy dump trucks (10–15 tons, diesel) operating over
    distances of 20–40 km for road-based logistics, and waterway barges for dredged spoil and

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                110
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

     bulk materials under SP 2.2 (works at embankments and Cho Bung canal) and SP 2.4
     (utilizing the Mang Thít, Cổ Chiên, and Hậu river networks). Waterway transport is
     prioritized to minimize road vehicle movements, reducing road-based truck trips to
     approximately 1–5 per day per subproject for localized waste clearance.
- Materials handling: Mechanical loading and unloading of dry granular materials (crushed
     stone, sand, cement) at on-site storage yards, generating localized point-source particulate
     emissions and wind-driven dust from temporary stockpiles
- Mechanical concrete mixing during the construction of the administration house at the
     Sluice gates/ship locks
Dispersion Modelling Results
Dispersion modelling confirms that dust and exhaust gas concentrations (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, CO, SO₂,
NO₂) across all subproject components comply with QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT limits at
distances beyond 10 m from emission sources. Key results include:
   -  Material transport at Đại Ngãi lock (SP 2.3): at 10 m from source, dust concentration is
      1.78 µg/m³, NO₂ is 4.97 µg/m³, and CO is 821.1 µg/m³ — all far below national
      permissible limits (300, 200, and 30,000 µg/m³ respectively).
Key Sensitive Receptors
The residential clusters, schools, medical stations, markets, tourist destinations,
historical/cultural sites and public buildings located along the material transportation route
and/or adjacent to the sites being excavated, or open load of loos dry materials.
6.3.2.2. Impacts from Noise and Vibration
a. Noise Impacts
Primary Sources
Four main noise source categories are associated with MERIT construction activities:
   -   Dredging operations (SP 2.2): Suction dredgers (89–95 dBA), bucket excavators (72–
       84 dBA), floating cranes, and high-capacity pump boats operating continuously during
       canal rehabilitation.
   - Piling and heavy construction: Hydraulic pile-driving hammers) for erosion protection
       embankment construction (SP 2.2), Larsen sheet pile cofferdams, bored sluice
       foundations (SP 2.3 and 2.4), and coastal wave-breaker piles (SP2.1) generate peak
       sound pressure level of 140-180 dB though at a reference distance of 10 metres, reported
       levels commonly fall between 100–120 dBA. Ancillary stationary sources include
       concrete mixers (75–88 dBA), diesel compressors, and backup generators (30–50 kW).
   - Road transport: 10–15 tons diesel dump trucks generate transient noise of 82–94 dBA
       along rural residential corridors.
   - Waterway transport: 200–800 tons barges pushed by 150–360 CV tugboats generate
       continuous engine and propeller noise of 85–89 dBA on main river axes.
Acoustic Dispersion Modelling
                           Table 6. 7: Noise Dispersion Modelling
                                                  Allowable limit (QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT, Area B:
 Distance from Source   Cumulative Noise Level
                                                  55 dBA daytime)
        1.5 m                  101.1 dBA                              Exceed
         20 m                  78.6 dBA                              Exceed
         50 m                  70.7 dBA                              Exceed
        100 m                  64.7 dBA                              Exceed
        150 m                  51.1 dBA                              Exceed



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 111
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

The 150 m radius is established as the community noise impact boundary for pile driving
operations using vibration hammers, which generate high instantaneous noise levels. Within
this boundary, prolonged or repeated exposure to elevated noise levels can result in sleep
disruption, stress-related health effects, and reduced cognitive function — particularly for
elderly residents, students, and patients in healthcare facilities who are most physiologically
and functionally vulnerable. Nighttime and early morning exposure carries the greatest
consequence for rest and recovery, potentially compounding health impacts over the
construction period. Intermittent high-intensity noise events, characteristic of pile driving, are
associated with heightened startle responses and sustained anxiety, which can affect
productivity, learning outcomes, and general wellbeing in nearby communities. Where sensitive
receptors such as schools or clinics fall within the impact boundary, temporary disruption to
core functions — teaching, patient care, and rest — represents a tangible social consequence
requiring targeted scheduling controls and community engagement measures. The noise impact
from other sources is assessed as Moderate for three reasons. First, noise sources are
predominantly mobile and linear (dredgers, barges, excavators traversing 44 km of Phước
Xuyên–Hải Tâm and 17 km of Chợ Bưng canals), limiting continuous exposure at any single
receptor to short-term, intermittent periods. Second, the worst-case 101.1 dBA model assumes
simultaneous peak operation of all machinery, which will not occur in practice due to phased
construction sequencing. Third, the most severe acoustic impacts (within 50 m) constitute
primarily an OHS risk to construction workers rather than a community health impact. Within
the immediate work zone, prolonged worker exposure is required mandatory PPE and
engineering controls.
Underwater Noise (ESS6): Pile-driving generates high-intensity underwater acoustic
shockwaves that cause behavioral disruption and startle responses in fish and sensory
interference in low-mobility benthic organisms (polychaetae worms, clams). However, baseline
surveys confirm that coastal habitats within the construction footprint are already severely
degraded by historical erosion, and active clam and blood oyster farming has ceased in the
direct footprint. Given the pre-degraded baseline and the strictly temporary nature of piling
activities, the underwater noise impact on aquatic ecology is assessed as Moderate.
Sensitive receptors potentially affected by noise generated from subproject construction
activities include:
Noise — Schools (Hòa Bình Primary, Secondary and Kindergarten; Thanh Lợi Primary; Vĩnh
Long An Primary/Secondary; Vĩnh Châu Kindergarten; Nguyễn Văn Thời Primary; Xuân Hiệp
Kindergarten and Primary), health stations (Thanh Lợi Health Station), places of worship (Chợ
Bưng Church, Mỹ Hòa Temple, Bà Chúa Xứ Temple, Bà Thiên Hậu Pagoda, Hieu Nhon Church,
Xuan Hiep Church, Hòa Bình Parish), residential households, and ferry terminals are sensitive
to construction noise due to the functional disruption it causes to learning, worship, rest, and
daily activity.
b. Vibration Impacts
Primary Sources
   -   Terrestrial piling and compaction: Hydraulic and vibratory pile-driving hammers
       (Larsen sheet piles, bored sluice foundation piles, coastal wave-breaker piles) and heavy
       soil compaction rollers are the dominant vibration sources. Movement of 10–15 tonne
       trucks on weak rural road foundations generates secondary ground-transmitted
       vibration.
   -   Underwater propagation: Low-frequency vibrations from riverbed and seabed pile
       driving propagate powerfully through saturated sediment, affecting aquatic organisms
       throughout the water column.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                112
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Vibration propagation from pile driving is a complex, site-specific process influenced primarily
by geotechnical conditions, the driving energy source, distance from the pile, and pile-soil
interaction. The primary measure of vibration intensity is the Peak Particle Velocity (PPV),
which decreases as the distance from the source increases.
Key factors affecting vibration propagation include:
a. Geotechnical and Geological Conditions
    - Soil Type and Density: Vibrations generally propagate further and have higher
         amplitudes in saturated, dense, or stiff soils (like clay, dense sand, or gravel) compared
         to soft, loose soils, which act as better damping agents.
    - Groundwater Level: Saturated soils enhance vibration transmission. In loose, saturated
         sand or silt, pile driving can generate high pour water pressure, leading to soil
         liquefaction and reduced shear strength.
    - Soil Layering: The presence of hard layers or rock can cause vibrations to reflect or
         refract, potentially guiding energy over longer distances. Conversely, soft soil layers
         can act as efficient attenuator
b. Pile Installation Method and Energy
    - Driving Method: Impact hammers generate high-intensity, transient, short-duration
         (0.2-0.3 s) pulses that radiate from the pile toe or shaft. Vibratory hammers produce
         lower, continuous vibration levels, which may be less harmful to structures but more
         annoying to people.
    - Driving Energy: A higher hammer energy (impact force) generally results in higher
         ground vibration levels. However, if the pile penetrates easily (soft soil), less energy is
         transferred to the ground compared to hard driving.
    - Pile Type and Size: The stiffness (impedance) of the pile affects the stress wave; stiffer,
         larger-diameter piles may transmit vibrations more efficiently to the soil.
c. Source-to-Receiver Distance
    - Attenuation with Distance: The amplitude of vibrations decays as they move away from
       the pile. This reduction is due to geometric spreading (wavefront enlargement) and
       material damping (soil friction/energy loss).
    - Near-Field vs. Far-Field: Near the pile (within roughly 4 times the wavelength), both
       body waves (longitudinal) and surface waves (Rayleigh waves) occur. In the far-field,
       vibrations are mainly dominated by surface waves, which attenuate slower
d. Pile-Soil Interaction
    - Skin Friction and Toe Resistance: Vibrations are generated along the pile shaft (skin
        friction) and at the pile tip (toe resistance). If the pile is driven through a stiff layer,
        vibrations are higher. If most energy is dissipated at the toe (end-bearing pile), different
        waves are produced compared to friction piles.
    - Pile Penetration Depth: Vibrations are often highest in the initial phase of driving (near
        the surface) or when hitting hard strata.
    - Critical Depth: Research indicates that the maximum PPV often occurs when the pile
        has penetrated to a "critical depth," often around half its total length, where the vibration
        source is optimized for surface wave generation
e. Other Factors
    - Pre-augering: Pre-drilling the hole for the pile can significantly reduce vibration levels
       by reducing the soil displacement, especially when driving through hard surface layers.
    - Obstructions: Obstructions like boulders can cause erratic, high-intensity vibration
       peaks



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                  113
    Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    Compliance Distance
    Propagation modelling confirms that vibration from heavy machinery (pile drivers, rollers)
    attenuates to within the daytime permissible limit of QCVN 27:2025/BNNMT (<60 dB, Area
    B — sensitive residential areas) at distances beyond 12 m from the source.
                                       Table 6. 8: Impact rating
