    Key Sensitive Receptors at Structural Risk
-   Hiếu Nhơn Parish Church and a livestock feed factory: Located approximately 40 m from
    the Say Đón and Cái Cá sluice piling zones — within the structural risk threshold for vibration-
    induced cracking.
-   Residential structures adjacent to sluice sites: Approximately 25 households near the
    Mỹ Xuyên sluice and 6 households near the Đại Ngãi sluice are situated within a few
    metres of active piling areas. These temporary (Level 4) and brick structures with
    shallow foundations are at elevated risk of wall cracking and differential settlement.
    Pre-construction structural condition surveys of all at-risk buildings are required, with real-time
    vibration monitoring during piling operations and a mandatory suspension protocol if threshold
    levels are exceeded.
    Structural sensitivity should be assessed separately for older or traditionally constructed
    religious and community buildings (temples, pagodas, churches) and residential structures in
    close proximity to pile driving. Schools and health stations would only be considered vibration-
    sensitive if structural vulnerability is confirmed.
    6.3.2.3. Generation of Wastewater
        -   Three wastewater streams are generated during the construction phase. While individual
            volumes are minor, their discharge into the heavily utilized canal networks of the
            Mekong Delta requires targeted management given the already stressed baseline water
            quality.
        -   Domestic Wastewater Generated by construction workforces of 18–128 personnel per
            site, producing 1.73–6.14 m³/day per site. Pollutant concentrations (BOD₅, TSS,
            coliform) in septic tank effluent exceed QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT (Column B) and
            require either secondary treatment or collection by licensed environmental service
            providers. Impact is assessed as Low, given the negligible volumes and intermittent
            discharge. To reduce domestic wastewater generation, contractors will prioritize
            recruitment of local workers and sourcing of local accommodation where feasible.
        -   Construction Wastewater Effluents from vehicle and equipment washing and on-site
            concrete batching/mortar mixing operations. Volumes range from 6.8 m³/day at SP 2.1
            coastal sites to 12.28 m³/day at Mỹ Xuyên ship lock and 19.92 m³/day at Đại Ngãi sluice
            during peak transport operations. Wastewater contains elevated TSS and high pH.


    Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                      114
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

        Impact is assessed as Low; effluents are manageable through standard on-site settling
        ponds and oil-water separators prior to any environmental discharge.
    -   Stormwater Runoff and Dredge Decant Water The highest-risk wastewater stream.
        Hydraulic suction dredging of approximately 2.5 million m³ of sediment from the Phước
        Xuyên–Hải Tâm and Chợ Bưng canals produces a sludge-water slurry with 70–80%
        water content. When pumped into the 30 designated disposal sites, this generates large
        volumes of decant water and leachate that continuously return to the canal network. If
        containment systems (perimeter dikes, settling ponds) fail or are inadequately
        maintained, this return flow will carry heavy sediment loads, remobilized organic
        matter, and — critically in the Đồng Tháp Mười region — acidic leachate from oxidized
        Potential Acid Sulfate Soils. Remobilization of Fe²⁺ and Al³⁺ lowers pH, generates iron
        hydroxide precipitates, and rapidly depletes dissolved oxygen, causing acute stress and
        mortality in fish and benthic organisms. Stormwater washing over active earthworks
        carry the same risk pathways. Impact is assessed as Moderate, contingent on the
        integrity of disposal site containment and active leachate neutralization. Mandatory
        mitigation includes continuous pH and TSS monitoring at disposal site outlets, lime
        (CaCO₃) neutralization of acidic leachate, and a suspension protocol triggered by
        exceedance of discharge thresholds.
6.3.2.4. Solid Waste and Hazardous Waste
During the construction phase, solid waste will be generated from three main sources: domestic
activities, construction activities, and hazardous materials. The overall volumes are relatively
small and localized. The amount of waste is generated and the level of impact are assessed as
follows:
Primary Sources, Generation Ranges, and Impact Characterization
-   Domestic Solid Waste: Generated by local worker (ranging from 18 to 128 workers per
    site), producing approximately 0.5 kg/person/day. This results in a combined daily
    generation range of approximately 9 to 64 kg/day per site. The impact is assessed as Low,
    as the volume is small and easily manageable. Domestic waste will be collected in lidded
    bins and routinely transported to approved local landfills by licensed provincial urban
    environment companies (URENCO) or licensed local sanitation groups in alignment with
    current local collection capacities.
-   Construction Solid Waste: This stream consists primarily of debris from demolished
    structures, excess excavated soil, cleared vegetation, and packaging materials. Because
    material loss rates are carefully controlled (0.005–0.01%), the generated quantities of true
    waste are negligible. Most excavated soil will be reused for site leveling, while the
    remaining debris will be collected and transported to approved disposal sites. The impact
    is assessed as Moderate. However, the aquatic environments targeted for project
    interventions (such as the canals slated for dredging and embankment works) are already
    highly stressed and polluted with localized domestic waste. Dredging operations will likely
    unearth significant amounts of this historical plastic and household debris, requiring the
    Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP) to account for sorting inorganic waste from
    the dredged sludge. The management of massive dredged canal sediments, which poses
    distinct acid sulfate risks, is mentioned in this DMMP.
-   Hazardous Waste: Hazardous waste is generated in small quantities (estimated at 10–30
    kg/month per site). Sources include used lubricating oil, oily rags, empty asphalt/paint
    containers, and discarded welding rods (which account for 5-7% of total welding rod
    weight during sluice gate steel framing). While the volume is small, the inherent toxicity



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                               115
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    of these materials poses a Moderate risk if improperly handled, requiring strict regulatory
    compliance beyond routine collection.
-   Hazardous waste requires licensed disposal and strict containment in compliance with
    ESS3, the specific protocols for which are detailed in the Contractor’s Environmental and
    Social Management Plan (C-ESMP).
6.3.2.5. Excavated and Dredged Materials
The MERIT project will generate among the largest volumes of excavated soil and
hydraulically dredged sediment of any active World Bank-financed infrastructure project in the
region. The management, transportation, and disposal of approximately 2.56 million m³ of
material — of which 339,000 m3 is very likely under conditions of demonstrated Potential Acid
Sulfate Soil (PASS) geology, proximate sensitive receptors, and a 30-site simultaneous disposal
operation — constitutes the single most consequential environmental and social risk cluster of
the construction phase. Strict compliance with the Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP)
and the binding directives set out below are conditions precedent to commencement of dredging
activities.
Volumes
Excavated Soil: Construction of the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen navigation locks, combined with
canal embankment works, will generate significant earthworks volumes. While the majority of
excavated clay will be beneficially reused for localized backfilling within the construction
footprint, an estimated surplus of 63,834 m³ will require permanent disposal at designated sites.
Dredged Material: Approximately 2.5 million m³ of materials will be generated. This
comprises 2.15 million m³ from the 44 km Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam Canal and 339,523.59 m³
from the 17 km Cho Bung Canal, using a combination of hydraulic suction dredgers (for soft
sediment) and grab/clamshell dredgers in sections adjacent to sensitive receptors.
Impact 1: Acid Sulfate Soil (ASS) Mobilization and Leachate Generation at Disposal Sites
Classification: Substantial
SP 2.2 traverses the Plains of Reeds, one of the most extensive active Acid Sulfate Soil zones
in Southeast Asia. Sediment sample analysis across the subproject area confirms that
concentrations of metals (Cu, Zn) and heavy metals (As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr) are significantly below
the allowable limits of QCVN 43:2025/BTNMT. Residues of agrochemicals were almost
entirely undetected in sediment samples collected across multiple monitoring periods,
indicating no current signs of heavy metal accumulation or pesticide contamination. In situ pH
in the dredged channels ranges from 6.5–7.5. However, this characterization must not be
conflated with post-disposal safety. The critical geochemical hazard is not the sediment's
current reduced state, but the transformation that occurs upon oxidation.
When anaerobic, iron-rich sediment (Fe concentrations ranging from 32,200–39,730 mg/kg) is
hydraulically pumped into open disposal sites and exposed to atmospheric oxygen, the
oxidation of pyrite and other reduced sulfide compounds can generate sulfuric acid. Experience
from comparable disposal operations in the broader Mekong Delta indicates that pH in exposed
PASS materials may decline to between 3.8 and 5.5 within weeks of oxidation onset under
unfavorable conditions. At these pH levels, iron and aluminum can be remobilized in soluble
forms with potential phytotoxic and aquatically toxic effects if leachate is not adequately
managed.Should containment embankments fail, drainage outlets become blocked, or decant
management be inadequately supervised, this acidic, iron-laden leachate will discharge directly
into the inland canal network — causing severe, persistent water quality degradation,
suppression of dissolved oxygen (DO), mortality in aquaculture operations (shrimp and clam
farms being acutely vulnerable to "acid shock" events), and irreversible impacts on agricultural


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                116
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

soils. The duration and spatial extent of such contamination would extend well beyond the
operational phase without active remediation, qualifying this as a long-term, large-scale impact
on shared water resources.
Required mitigation: Disposal sites associated with SP 2.2 dredging materials must be
upgraded from standard two-cell designs to a three-compartment system: (i) receiving cell for
raw dredged material; (ii) active lime (CaCO₃) neutralization cell with continuous pH
monitoring; and (iii) final settling and verification cell with quality confirmation prior to any
decant water return to the canal. Contingency bunding and lined overflow channels must be
installed prior to pumping commencing at any site.
Impact 2: Cumulative Water Quality Degradation from Simultaneous Leachate
Discharge
Classification: Substantial
ESIA under the ESF is explicitly prospective, and the scale of disposal activity — 2.5 million
m³ of dredged material across 30 sites along 44 km of interconnected canal banks — creates a
credible cumulative risk pathway consistent with the precautionary principle. The Phuoc Xuyen
– Hai Tam and Cho Bung canal system is a connected hydraulic network with substantially
reduced dry season assimilative capacity; leachate from upstream sites is transported through
successive reaches and cannot be assumed to be fully neutralized before integrating with
downstream flow. The degree of hydrological convergence at specific receptors will be further
refined through connectivity mapping required under the C-ESMP as an early implementation
priority.
The 30 disposal sites collectively function as hydraulic settling basins, with decant water and
processed leachate returned to the canal network on an ongoing basis throughout dredging
operations. The ESIA's baseline hydrology data confirms that dry season flows in the Phuoc
Xuyen – Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals are substantially reduced relative to wet season
conditions, significantly diminishing the system's dilution and buffering capacity.
The simultaneous discharge of turbid, potentially acidic, and iron-rich decant water from up to
30 sites into the same interconnected canal network during low-flow dry season conditions
presents a severe cumulative water quality impact risk. The combined suspended solids,
biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and acid loading from concurrent discharges can exceed
the canal system's ambient assimilative capacity, suppressing DO below the 4 mg/L survival
threshold for aquatic fauna and triggering widespread aquatic mortality across a canal network
that supports both commercial and subsistence fisheries. The Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant —
serving approximately 8,000 households and drawing directly from the affected canal —
represents a critical, high-sensitivity receptor for this cumulative impact.
Required mitigation: The C-ESMP and DMMP must mandate staggered discharge schedules
across all 30 disposal sites during dry season months (November–April). A discharge
sequencing plan, coordinated by the PMU Environmental Specialist, must ensure that no more
than a defined subset of sites discharge simultaneously, and that cumulative TSS and pH
loading at designated monitoring points remain within approved thresholds at all times.
Impact 3: Turbidity and Suspended Solids (TSS) from Active Dredging Operations
Classification: Moderate
Active hydraulic and mechanical dredging will generate localized turbidity plumes extending
an estimated 350–450 m upstream and downstream of the dredge head. The resuspension of
fine, organic-rich sediment increases TSS concentrations and BOD in the water column,
temporarily reducing light penetration, suppressing aquatic photosynthesis, and placing
physiological stress on benthic invertebrates and demersal fish species. Under normal dredging


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                117
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

conditions, these effects are spatially bounded and ecologically reversible once dredging
concludes, given adequate flushing by seasonal flows.
However, poorly managed dredging operations — particularly those without silt curtains or
operating at excessive pump speeds — have the potential to extend plume extents significantly
and affect sensitive downstream receptors including the Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant intake.
Cumulative effects across long canal reaches also require active management.
Required mitigation: A real-time Operational Turbidity and TSS Management Protocol must
be incorporated into the DMMP, implementing a three-tier alert system: Tier 1 (80% of
permitted TSS threshold) — notification of PMU and reduction of dredger pump speed; Tier 2
(100% of threshold) — immediate halt of dredging and deployment of double silt curtain
barriers; Tier 3 (120% of threshold) — absolute suspension of dredging activities, formal
incident notification, and investigation.
Impact 4: Proximity of Disposal Sites to Sensitive Receptors
Classification: Substantial
Site-specific risk assessments of the 30 designated disposal sites identify several locations
presenting elevated or unacceptable risks to adjacent communities and cultural heritage assets.
Disposal Site No. 8 (adjacent to Hoa Binh Kindergarten): This site is located immediately
adjacent to a functioning primary school facility. The pumping of anaerobic sludge slurry to
this site creates acute life-safety hazards including the risk of embankment failure resulting in
slurry inundation of school grounds, drowning hazards for children, toxic gas (H₂S, NH₃)
exposure, and sustained noise and dust impacts during the school operating day. This site must
be subject to an immediate pre-construction review, with either mandatory relocation or the
imposition of absolute operational restrictions (no pumping during school hours, full perimeter
physical barrier with sealed access, dedicated safety warden).
Doc Binh Kieu Commune Disposal Site (600–700 m from Go Thap Archaeological Relic
Area): Go Thap is a nationally recognized cultural heritage site with significant archaeological
sensitivity. The disposal site introduces risks of landscape degradation, vibration from heavy
transport vehicles, and uncontrolled visual impact within the buffer zone of this protected site.
A Cultural Heritage Management Protocol specific to this site — including transport route
restrictions, vibration monitoring, and visual screening — must be prepared prior to disposal
operations commencing.
Impact 5: Landscape Degradation, Odor, and Atmospheric Emissions from Disposal Sites
Classification: Moderate
The rapid placement of over 2.5 million m³ of dark, anaerobic sludge across 112.6 hectares of
disposal area will produce immediate and highly visible landscape transformation at all 30 sites.
While the baseline ecological value of these sites is confirmed as low (public unused land or
borrow pits under Commune People's Committee management), the visual and atmospheric
impacts on adjacent communities are material. Anaerobic decomposition of the high-organic-
content sludge will release H₂S, NH₃, and CH₄, generating sustained offensive odors at nearby
residences and community facilities during the operational period, which may extend across
multiple construction seasons.
These impacts are localized, reversible upon site closure and revegetation, and manageable
through engineered controls, but require active monitoring and a community grievance
mechanism given the density of nearby settlements.
Required mitigation: Application of deodorizing agents or lime surface treatment to exposed
sludge within 48 hours of placement; perimeter vegetation screening where feasible; and a
community-accessible odor complaint and response mechanism linked to the project GRM.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                118
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Impact 6: Salinity Contamination Risk from Reuse of Brackish-Zone Dredged Material
Classification: Moderate
In tidal and brackish-influenced canal reaches, dredged sediment carries measurably elevated
salinity concentrations. Sediment analysis from the 2025 dredging area records salinity ranging
from 0.296 to 0.479 g/kg. While these values may appear modest in isolation, unrestricted
community reuse of saline-contaminated dredged material for agricultural backfill or
freshwater aquaculture pond preparation could cause progressive soil salinization, vegetation
die-back, and aquaculture stock loss — with particular relevance for smallholder farmers in
low-lying areas where the distinction between saline and freshwater zones is not always visually
apparent to end-users.
Required mitigation: The blanket authorization for unrestricted community reuse of dredged
material is hereby revoked. All reuse is strictly conditional upon pre-use chemical testing
(verifying pH > 5.5 and salinity within acceptable agricultural or aquaculture thresholds),
formal documentation of origin zone salinity classification, and written PMU clearance. Saline-
classified material may only be applied to embankment construction within confirmed saline-
affected zones.
Impact 7: Dike Failure and Sludge Spill Risk During the Rainy Season
Classification: Substantial
The 30 disposal sites rely on perimeter containment dikes to retain hydraulically pumped sludge
while decant water is managed and returned to the canal network. During the rainy season
(May–October), elevated rainfall significantly increases hydrostatic pressure on these
containment structures. Where dike design fails to meet technical standards — in particular
where leachate collection and drainage systems are absent or undersized — the risk of structural
failure is material. A breach event would result in the uncontrolled release of liquefied
anaerobic sludge onto adjacent agricultural land, directly destroying crops, inundating paddy
fields, and potentially reaching aquaculture ponds and residential areas.
This risk is compounded by the relatively short construction procurement timeline, which
creates pressure on contractors to establish disposal sites rapidly and may lead to under-
engineered bunding. Given that the 30 sites will be operational simultaneously across a
geographically dispersed network, the capacity for real-time inspection and response to early
signs of dike distress is inherently limited without dedicated supervision resources.
Required mitigation: All disposal site containment dikes must be designed and certified by a
qualified geotechnical engineer prior to commencement of pumping. Designs must include: (i)
engineered leachate collection and drainage systems with controlled discharge outlets; (ii)
emergency spillway capacity rated for a 1-in-25-year rainfall event; and (iii) mandatory pre-
rainy season structural inspection by the CPO Environmental and Civil Supervision team. A
Dike Integrity Monitoring Protocol — including visual inspection logs and piezometric
monitoring at high-risk sites — must be included in the DMMP.
Impact 8: Community Traffic Safety Risk from Sludge Transport Operations
Classification: Moderate
If dredged material is transported overland by truck rather than via water-based methods, the
movement of heavy, slurry-laden vehicles along narrow rural roads presents serious community
traffic safety risks. Rural road surfaces in the project area are typically narrow, underbed, and
in variable condition; slurry spillage from poorly sealed truck loads would create severely
slippery road surfaces, increasing the risk of vehicle accidents, pedestrian injury, and road
infrastructure damage. Canal-adjacent communities — including school children, cyclists, and
pedestrians — are the most exposed population groups.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                119
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

The project has mandated water-based construction methods as the primary mode of dredged
material transport, utilizing direct suction-pumping vessels and grab barges. However, the final
transfer of settled material from disposal sites to reuse locations, and any overland transport
that may be authorized by the PMU, requires explicit safety management.
Required mitigation: Water-based construction methods (suction-pumping vessels and grab
barges) must be maintained as the mandatory primary transport mode for all dredged material
movement along canals. Where any overland truck transport is authorized by the PMU on a
case-by-case basis, trucks must be fully sealed with certified load covers, and routes must avoid
school zones and market areas during peak hours. Road condition inspections must be
conducted before and after transport operations, with the contractor liable for reinstatement of
any road damage caused.
Impact 9: Social Conflicts, Long-term Land Sterilization, and Community Grievances
Classification: Substantial
In the absence of a fully implemented Beneficial Use Plan for dredged material, the long-term
fate of millions of cubic metres of dried sludge across 1,148,642 m² of permanently acquired
land presents a significant social risk. Agricultural communities whose land has been
permanently acquired for disposal sites face the prospect of that land remaining unproductive,
visually degraded, and economically inert for years beyond the construction phase —
effectively converting productive agricultural land into long-term barren sites.
This risk is particularly acute for Khmer ethnic minority communities and other canal-
dependent livelihood groups in Soc Trang and Dong Thap provinces, for whom agricultural
land is both an economic and cultural asset. The failure to plan for and execute site rehabilitation,
or to deliver a credible and community-endorsed Beneficial Use framework, is likely to
generate sustained and intensive community grievances, with the potential for escalation to
formal complaints under the project GRM and, ultimately, to the World Bank's Accountability
Mechanism. Community consultation records from the ESIA preparation phase confirm that
host communities have already expressed concerns regarding land sterilization and the long-
term use of disposal sites.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                  120
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

             Operational Turbidity and Suspended Solids (TSS) Management Protocol
 To comply with the WBG General EHS Guidelines and safeguard critical water users during
 dredging operations, contractors are strictly prohibited from relying on subjective visual assessments
 of water quality. The PMU and Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) shall enforce the
 following numerical thresholds and three-tier escalation protocol.
 1. Numerical Action Levels Differentiated by Receptor Type Based on baseline water quality data
 and National Technical Regulations (QCVN), the absolute maximum permissible thresholds for
 Total Suspended Solids (TSS) at the boundary of the designated dredging protection zones (e.g.,
 200m downstream of the dredger) are established as follows:
 - Hoa Binh Domestic Water Intake Protection Zone: TSS ≤ 30 mg/L (or strictly <20% above the
 established daily ambient background). Note: This is the strictest standard to prevent overwhelming
 the plant's filtration systems.
 - Aquaculture and Shrimp Farming Zones: TSS ≤ 100 mg/L (in accordance with Vietnamese
 Standards for brackish water shrimp farming).
 - General Canal/Ecological Zones: TSS ≤ 50 mg/L (in compliance with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT,
 Column B1).
 2. Three-Tier Alert, Action, and Suspension Protocol. The Contractor must integrate this three-tier
 protocol into their daily operations. Enforcement will be overseen by the CSC:
 - TIER 1: ALERT LEVEL (Trigger: TSS reaches 80% of the Receptor Threshold)
 - Trigger: Daily portable monitoring indicates TSS is climbing and has reached 80% of the
 specified limit (e.g., 24 mg/L at the Hoa Binh intake boundary).
 - Mandatory Action: The Contractor’s ESHS Officer must immediately notify the CSC. The
 dredging operator must reduce the cutter-suction head speed/production rate by 30%. Silt curtains
 and netting enclosures must be physically inspected for tears or bypasses and immediately repaired.
 - TIER 2: ACTION LEVEL (Trigger: TSS reaches 100% of the Receptor Threshold)
 - Trigger: TSS officially breaches the designated numerical threshold (e.g., >100 mg/L near
 aquaculture ponds).
 - Mandatory Action: Dredging operations must temporarily halt. The Contractor must deploy
 secondary/double silt curtains (which can reduce turbidity by up to 75%) and wait for the suspended
 sediment to settle. Dredging may only resume at a maximum of 50% capacity during favorable tidal
 flows, and monitoring frequency must increase two hourly.
 - TIER 3: SUSPENSION LEVEL (Trigger: TSS exceeds 120% of Threshold OR Receptor
 Complaint)
 - Trigger: TSS exceeds 120% of the limit, OR the Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant operator officially
 reports filter stress/intake impacts.
 - Mandatory Action: Absolute suspension of all dredging activities in the canal section. The
 Contractor will face financial penalties under the ESMP framework. The PMU and CSC will conduct
 a formal incident investigation. Operations cannot resume until a revised Contractor Dredging
 Management Plan (C-DMP) with upgraded technological interventions is formally cleared by the
 PMU and World Bank.
 3. Formal Daily In-Water Monitoring Mandate (ESMP Integration) To operationalize this protocol,
 the following mandates are legally bound to the Contractor's ESMP budget:
 - Daily Instrumental Monitoring: The Contractor’s dedicated ESHS personnel must conduct daily
 in-water monitoring using calibrated, portable Turbidity/TSS meters.
 - Locations: Measurements must be taken daily at three locations: (1) 50m upstream (to establish
 the daily ambient baseline), (2) 50m downstream of the dredger, and (3) at the boundary of the
 nearest sensitive receptor (e.g., the 500m protection boundary of the Hoa Binh Intake).
 - Reporting: Results must be logged in the daily site diary and submitted to the CSC. The CSC
 will conduct unannounced verification sampling twice a week.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                   121
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

6.3.2.6. Traffic Disruption and Increased Risk of Traffic Accidents
Impacts on waterway traffic arise from: (i) construction activities of sluices combined with
navigation locks and dredging along canal routes; (ii) transportation of materials from
temporary wharves to construction sites of Project works along the nearshore areas of Tan Thuy,
Thoi Thuan, Truong Long Hoa, and Dong Hai; and (iii) operations of dredging vessels along
canals and waterways, including material transport.
Based on the baseline waterway traffic survey conducted in February 2026, the current traffic
volume is as follows: (i) approximately 30-50 vessel movements through the Nam Mang Thit
River area, Co Chien River, and canals within the Nam Mang Thit region; (ii) around 45-50
trips through the Staind River and 35 trips through the Bay Xao River, respectively; and (iii)
roughly 100 trips through the Phuoc Xuyen - Hai Tam Canal and 5 trips through the Cho Bung
Canal. The transport fleet primarily consists of traditional boats, self-propelled vessels with a
capacity of approximately 100 DWT, and small-tonnage barges (50-100T)

Construction and transport activities involve the use of diesel hammers (of appropriate weight)

have a direct impact on waterway traffic within the project area.
Construction and transport activities, often involving barges, speedboats, suction dredgers,
tugboats, and other construction machinery, directly affect waterway traffic in the Project area.
These activities along waterways can cause daily navigation obstructions and increase the risk
of accidents due to collisions between vessels and construction equipment.

For dredging activities along the Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals and the
construction of sluices/locks (e.g., Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop), waterway traffic will be
affected to some extent during the 24-month construction period, including temporary
navigation restrictions and narrowing of waterway transport widths. Although vessel traffic on
these rivers and canals is relatively high, the direct impact is assessed as Low due to: (i) the
wide river/canal channels;; (ii) the sluice construction methods on the existing rivers/canals will
only reduce the current waterway cross-section by 40-50% (based on the design where the
sluice aperture accounts for 50-60% of the existing state); and (iii) the use of cofferdams without
completely blocking the flow, ensuring that vessels can still navigate safely and (iii) short
durations of navigation disruption, typically a few hours. These impacts can be mitigated
through construction methods appropriate to each specific work. The Project owner and
contractors will coordinate with local waterway management authorities to provide necessary
information on alternative navigation routes for vessels to minimize impacts.
Therefore, the magnitude of impact is considered Moderate, short-term, and intermittent during
partial construction of embankments, bridges, sluices, and canal/drain dredging, and can be
avoided or mitigated through appropriate technical and management measures.




            Figure 6. 1: Current Status of Waterway Traffic at Subproject 2.4
For road traffic:
Most sluices, navigation locks, and canal dredging activities will be implemented on existing
canals/rivers, with project materials transported primarily via waterways, thereby causing Low
impacts on road traffic and adjacent areas. Specifically for the Cai Hop sluice, it will be
constructed on the earthen dam at the former canal site. This location currently features an
asphalt road (Width = 6m), which serves as the main transport axis along the Co Chien River
dyke, connecting the Cai Hop sluice to Provincial Road 907 (PR907). Consequently, the
construction of the new Cai Hop navigation lock will result in the following specific impacts
on road traffic:
-   During the transportation of materials, spillage onto the roads can hinder mobility and
    increase the risk of traffic accidents for both transport vehicle operators and local residents.
-   Overloaded transport vehicles exceeding permitted weight and traffic volume can damage
    road surfaces, causing subsidence and cracking. Such road damage creates difficulties for
    road users, increases the likelihood of accidents, and affects public health and local
    business activities.
-   During peak hours, without proper traffic management and diversion plans, congestion may
    occur due to increased traffic density, making travel difficult for local residents and directly
    impacting the Project’s construction progress.
-   According to the Feasibility Study (FS) report, the total volume of materials required to
    construct the Cai Hop sluice is 35,382 tons over a 24-month construction period, equating
    to an average of approximately 6 trips per day using 10-ton trucks. Additionally,
    consultations with residents in Nhi Long Commune indicate that the traffic density along
    the route is relatively low, mainly comprising motorcycles, tricycles, and light trucks; local


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 123
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

        residents predominantly utilize boats/sampans to transport agricultural products. Therefore,
        the impact magnitude on roadway traffic is considered as Low due to: (i) the project site's
        proximity to National Highways, Provincial Roads, and the Co Chien River, which
        facilitates highly convenient waterway and road transportation; and (ii) the contractor's
        implementation of appropriate traffic management measures during construction, such as
        installing construction warning signs, providing nighttime illumination, and deploying
        flagmen/traffic controllers during peak construction periods (particularly during the dry
        season from December to April of the following year, when river flows are low)
6.3.2.7. Risks Related to Labor Influx
Construction activities will require a certain number of contract workers (abou 1,234 workers).
Most workers are expected to come from different regions of the country depending on demand
and the skills required for the work. The presence of construction workers may create potential
social and environmental risks, including: potential conflicts between construction workers and
local residents due to social disruption associated with the concentration of workers from other
regions with different cultures and lifestyles (For example, workers from the Mekong Delta
region typically prefer sweeter tastes, in contrast to those from Central Viet Nam who tend to
favor spicier flavors. The region is characterized by cultural diversity, with the presence of
multiple ethnic groups (Kinh, Hoa, Khmer, Cham), as well as differing practices in pagoda
worship. In other regions, there is generally a higher degree of uniformity in traditional belief
systems and Buddhist practices) impacts on employment opportunities and income; ( and
social-environmental impacts and risks (To minimize the generation of domestic solid waste,
reduce pressure on local infrastructure, and limit social disturbances, contractors will prioritize
the recruitment of local labor).Potential social impacts also include increased pressure on shared
public services and goods, the spread of infectious diseases, or worker involvement in “social
vices” such as alcohol abuse, prostitution, drugs, and gambling and GBV/SH/SEA related risks.
The risks associated with labor influx are considered low for the following reasons:
    -   A social welfare management system is established and operating effectively. Local
        authorities closely monitor residents’ entry and exit through an on-site registration system.
        Residents from other areas, including laborers as visitors, must register with local
        authorities upon arrival.
    -   Social order in the project area is stable under existing institutional arrangements, with
        local police assigned to each commune/ward to monitor community clusters, and many
        communities also apply self-management mechanisms.
    -   The number of workers present at each construction site is limited, except for construction
        sites of sluices, navigation locks, and possibly embankment casting sites during peak
        periods.
    -   Current national legislation, such as the Environmental Protection Law, Construction Law,
        and Occupational Safety and Health Law, requires contractors to comply with regulations
        on environmental protection, health, safety, and construction supervision, including OHS
        (Occupational Health and Safety) and EHS (Environmental, Health, and Safety)
        monitoring.
Child Labor or Forced Labor
In some cases, contractors may hire people from the local community as labor, especially for
simple and physically demanding tasks such as site leveling, dismantling construction materials,
loading and unloading excavated materials, etc. This may create a risk of child labor7 or forced


7
    For more details, see World Bank ESF ESS2, paragraphs 17, 18, and 19.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                              124
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

labor. In Vietnam, workers aged between 15 and 18 may be hired to perform non-hazardous
and non-dangerous tasks, provided that such work does not interfere with their education or
adversely affect their comprehensive development (physical, mental, and moral). However, in
order to mitigate any risk of children dropping out of school to support their families, the project
adopts a minimum age of 18 years to work in the project as stipulated in the Labor Management
Procedures (LMP).
The scale and level of potential impacts and risks are considered Moderate. The Project will
comply with all legal requirements for labor management and apply a minimum working age
of 18 as detailed in the LMP. Contractors must make efforts to ensure their employees meet
these minimum age requirements. Under applicable law, contractors must register workers from
other localities with local authorities or police. Registration includes key information such as
full name, place and date of birth, gender, national ID number and validity, marital status, and
other relevant details.
6.3.2.8. Risks to Workers and Community Safety and Health
Construction activities across Subprojects 2.2 and 2.3 will mobilize a substantial fleet of heavy
equipment and waterborne vessels across an extended 24-month period, operating within a
densely settled rural landscape characterized by narrow road networks, active waterways, and
multiple sensitive community receptors including schools, religious sites, and water supply
infrastructure. The intersection of intensive construction operations with existing community
life generates a distinct and multi-pathway risk profile spanning traffic safety, occupational
hazards, public health, and the protection of vulnerable groups. These risks are assessed below
by impact type, with classification applied against the established criteria.
Impact 1: Road and Waterway Traffic Safety Risks
Classification: Substantial
The construction phase will require the sustained mobilization of heavy transport vehicles —
including 5-ton, 7-ton, and 10-ton trucks — on rural road networks that are typically narrow,
underbed, and structurally insufficient for sustained heavy load traffic. The concurrent
movement of construction vehicles alongside mixed local traffic (motorcycles, bicycles,
pedestrians, school commuters) on roads passing through residential and urban areas creates a
materially elevated collision risk throughout the construction period.
The waterway traffic risk is equally significant in scale. Subproject 2.2 alone will deploy 24
excavators, 36 barges, and 24 tugboats operating concurrently along the dredged canals over 24
months. Subproject 2.3 adds 8 barges, 6 piling vessels, 6 tugboats, 6 motorboats, and 4 concrete
mixers. This construction fleet will operate within a canal network that currently records
approximately 100 vessel movements per day on the Phuoc Xuyen canal, 45–50 movements
per day on the Saind Tard river/canal, and 35 movements per day on the Bay Xao canal. The
superimposition of a large construction fleet onto these existing vessel movement volumes
creates significant potential for waterway collision events, particularly at canal intersections,
lock approaches, and dredge operating zones where navigation channels are temporarily
narrowed or obstructed.
Falling materials during transport, unsecured loads, and partial obstruction of road or waterway
lanes by stored construction materials represent additional secondary hazards. The combination
of road and waterway risks across a 24-month period qualifies this as a long-duration, large-
scale impact on community safety.
Required mitigation: A Traffic Management Plan (TMP) — covering both road and waterway
operations — must be prepared prior to mobilization, designating approved haulage routes,
speed restrictions, mandatory escort protocols near sensitive receptors, and defined vessel
exclusion zones around active dredge heads. Waterway safety signage, navigation lights, and a

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 125
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

vessel scheduling system must be established in coordination with local maritime authorities.
Strict prohibition on overland transport of materials past school gates during school arrival and
dismissal hours must be enforced through the C-ESMP.
Impact 2: Health and Safety Risks to Sensitive Receptors
Classification: Substantial
Several schools, religious facilities, and community gathering points are located in immediate
proximity to active construction zones, presenting a concentrated and serious risk to vulnerable
groups — particularly children.
Schools identified as directly exposed include Thanh Loi Primary School, Hoa Binh Primary
School, and Long An Primary and Secondary School. Religious facilities include Cho Bung
Church, Hoa Binh Parish, Ba Chua Xu Pagoda, and My Hoa Pagoda. The risks to these
receptors are multiple and simultaneous: heavy truck movements past school gates elevate
collision risk for schoolchildren during arrival and dismissal; the operation of air compressors,
compaction equipment, and transport vehicles in proximity to school buildings generates noise
levels that directly disrupt teaching and learning activities and may cause cumulative hearing
and respiratory health impacts among students; and open excavations near school perimeters
create acute drowning hazards, particularly during the rainy season when water-filled pits
present a fatal hazard to children who may access unfenced construction areas.
The proximity of construction operations to active religious facilities creates further risks of
vibration damage to structures, noise disruption to services, and access restriction for
congregants, particularly the elderly. These impacts are direct, localized, and of sufficient
severity to warrant classification as High given the vulnerability of the affected population
groups and the potential for irreversible harm (drowning, collision fatality).
Required mitigation: Site-specific Community Safety Management Plans must be prepared
for each sensitive receptor location, with details provided in Chapter 7 of the ESIA. As
minimum requirements: all excavations within 100 m of school perimeters must be fully fenced
with physical barriers, inspected daily, and pumped clear of accumulated water during the rainy
season. Heavy vehicle movements past school gates must be prohibited during school hours
(07:00–11:30 and 13:30–17:00). Noise-generating equipment must maintain a minimum
operational distance from school buildings, with decibel monitoring conducted during school
hours. A dedicated community safety liaison officer must be assigned to each sensitive receptor
cluster.
Impact 3: Excavation, Ground Disturbance, and Site Access Safety
Classification: Moderate
Deep excavations at sluice gate and lock foundation sites — reaching -5.0 m to -5.5 m — and
canal dredging operations create open ground hazards across an extended construction footprint.
Workers and community members accessing or passing near inadequately secured sites face
risks of fall, entrapment, or drowning. The risk is compounded during the rainy season when
excavations accumulate water rapidly and the ground surface adjacent to open pits becomes
unstable. Insufficient site lighting and improperly installed perimeter barriers create additional
hazards for residents navigating around construction zones at night. Contact with inadequately
protected electrical wiring — particularly in proximity to waterlogged conditions — presents
an additional electrocution risk.
These impacts are localized to the construction footprint and of limited duration, but require
active management given the density of adjacent settlement.
Required mitigation: All excavation sites must be enclosed with robust physical perimeter
barriers, warning lighting, and clear signage in Vietnamese. Daily site inspections must confirm


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                126
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

barrier integrity, particularly after rainfall events. Temporary electrical installations must
comply with Vietnamese electrical safety standards and be inspected weekly. Night-time
lighting at all active sites must meet minimum illumination standards for construction zones
adjacent to public access areas.
Impact 4: Occupational Health and Safety Risks to Construction Workers
Classification: Moderate
The range of construction activities across Subprojects 2.2 and 2.3 exposes workers to a broad
and overlapping set of occupational health and safety hazards. The principal OHS risk
categories are:
Drowning and waterway hazards: Workers engaged in canal dredging, sluice construction,
lock construction, river embankment works, and bridge pile driving operate continuously in or
over open water. The risk of drowning is elevated by fatigue during extended work shifts, non-
compliance with life jacket requirements, and the absence of adequate man-overboard recovery
protocols on multi-vessel construction sites.
Heat stress: Outdoor construction during Vietnamese summer months exposes workers to
sustained ambient temperatures of 38–40°C, with additional radiant heat from equipment and
reflective water surfaces. Heat exhaustion and heat stroke are realistic acute health risks for
workers on dredging vessels, open embankment sites, and unsheltered lock construction zones.
Machinery and equipment hazards: The concurrent operation of excavators, cranes, piling
vessels, concrete mixers, and grab dredgers on confined worksites creates hazards from moving
parts, swinging loads, unsecured materials at height, and equipment interactions. Landslide and
embankment collapse risks are present at deep excavation sites and bridge abutment
foundations during earthworks.
Flammable and hazardous materials: The storage and use of petroleum fuels, compressed
gases, paints, and solvents across dispersed construction sites and worker camps introduces
risks of fire, explosion, and chemical exposure. Electrical systems on vessels and in temporary
site facilities present electrocution risk if not properly installed and maintained.
Vector-borne and environmental health hazards: Land clearance operations expose workers
to insect bites, snakebite risk, and contact with sharp vegetation debris.
These impacts are assessed as direct and of medium significance, as they are spatially confined
to the construction footprint, of finite duration, and substantially controllable through standard
OHS management measures — provided contractor compliance is actively enforced.
Required mitigation: A project-specific Occupational Health and Safety Plan (OHSP) must
be enforced as a binding contractual requirement for all contractors and subcontractors.
Mandatory provisions include: personal protective equipment (PPE) for all task categories; life
jackets and man-overboard protocols for all water-based operations; work scheduling to avoid
outdoor tasks during peak heat hours (11:00–14:00); fuel and hazardous material storage
located a minimum of 50 m from worker accommodations with weekly fire safety inspections;
and mandatory OHS induction training before site access is granted. Incident reporting must be
logged in the project Safety Register and reported to the PMU within 24 hours of any lost-time
injury event.
Impact 5: Community Public Health Risks — Communicable and Vector-borne Disease
Classification: Moderate
The influx of construction workers — particularly where workers are recruited from outside the
local area and housed in temporary labor camps adjacent to communities — introduces
identifiable public health risks. Canal excavation and embankment earthworks disturb standing


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                127
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

water habitats and create new stagnant water accumulations in borrow pits and excavation
depressions, expanding vector breeding sites and increasing the risk of mosquito-borne disease
transmission (dengue fever, malaria) for both workers and adjacent communities.
Wastewater and solid waste from worker camps, if inadequately managed, introduces pathogen
loading into the canal network — a critical risk given that rural communities in the Mekong
Delta rely on canal water for domestic, agricultural, and aquaculture uses. The introduction of
a large, mobile workforce also raises the risk of transmission of sexually transmitted infections
(STIs), including HIV, through interactions between incoming laborers and local community
members. This risk is recognized as a standard labor influx impact under the World Bank's ESF
and requires structured prevention measures proportionate to workforce size.
Required mitigation: Labor Management Procedures prepared for the project must be
implemented, including: minimum sanitation standards for worker camps (potable water,
hygienic latrines at a ratio compliant with IFC performance standards, solid waste collection);
mandatory HIV/AIDS and STI awareness sessions for all workers during induction; vector
control measures (elimination of standing water, use of larvicides in unavoidable water
accumulations) on a weekly maintenance schedule; and prohibition of camp wastewater
discharge directly into canals without treatment. Community health awareness sessions should
be conducted in villages adjacent to worker camp locations at project commencement. The LMP
also includes Codes of Conduct for workers and commitment to GBV/SH/SEA prevention.
6.3.2.9. Potential Project Impacts on Landscape
Landscape areas will be affected by the implementation of project components, including the
construction of 16.6 km of coastal embankments in Vinh Long. The use of spun concrete piles
and rock revetments will transform the coastline from a natural state into an artificial structure.
This may result in the loss of the natural character of alluvial mudflats; however, it will create
a robust protection system against coastal erosion.
Large-scale dredging activities (at the Phuoc Xuyen and Cho Bung canals) and the
construction of wide sluice structures ranging from 22 m to 50 m (within the Nam Mang Thit
system), as well as the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluice and navigation lock structures, will
alter the cross-sectional profiles of river systems due to the introduction of large concrete
structures and steel gate systems. Construction of embankments and dredging works will
require site clearance, including the removal of green belts, riparian vegetation, and fruit
orchards along riverbanks. This will reduce the diversity of colors, textures, and visual
elements characteristic of traditional rural landscapes. The construction of infrastructure
components, including the Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, and Cai Hop sluice structures, the Nam Mang
Thit system, as well as river and coastal embankments and the design of disposal sites, will
result in temporary or permanent changes in land use at construction sites (e.g., agricultural
land being acquired for sluice construction or temporarily occupied during the construction
period). These activities will convert residential or agricultural land into construction sites,
affecting the landscape ecology of the Mang Thit River, Co Chien River, Bay Sao River,
Santard River, and surrounding areas. Additionally, excavation, dredging, leveling, and
material stockpiling will have negative visual impacts on the river/canal landscape within the
project area. However, the landscape impacts of sluice construction are considered minor
because the construction period is limited to approximately 18–24 months, implemented in a
phased (rolling) approach. After construction, the contractor will implement site restoration
measures, and the operational areas of the sluices will be rehabilitated to a condition better
than before construction. The impact magnitude is considered as Moderate.
6.3.2.10. Specific Risks and Impacts of Subprojects
(1) Subproject 2.1: Reducing Coastal Erosion Risks in the Mekong Delta


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 128
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

a. Resource demand
The MERIT project requires substantial volumes of construction materials sourced from
regional suppliers across the Mekong Delta, transported primarily by waterway over distances
of 70–130 km. Total material demand is estimated at approximately 413,522 tons, with the
dominant material being riprap stone (366,721 m³), followed by cement (5,626,607 kg), crushed
stone (13,843 m³), and coarse sand (8,398 m³). Freshwater demand for construction and
workforce use is modest relative to regional supply capacity, though seasonal constraints
introduce localized risks. The key environmental and social risks associated with resource
demand are not from direct project extraction — all materials will be procured from licensed
third-party suppliers — but from the indirect supply chain pressures generated by the project's
procurement volumes operating within an already heavily stressed regional resource
environment.
                  Table 6. 10: Demand and Sources of Essential Materials
                                                                Information

                                             Quantity        Location

  1    Coarse sand (Yellow sand)              8,398 m3      Vinh Long      70-80       by seaway
  2    D350mm Pre-cast concrete piles          748 m        Dong Thap     120-130      by seaway
  3    1x2 Crushed stone                     13,843 m3      Vinh Long      70-80       by seaway
  4    Riprap (Quarry stone)                366,721 m3      Vinh Long      70-80       by seaway

  9    Cement                              5,626,607 kg     Vinh Long      70-80       by seaway
All materials are to be procured from licensed regional suppliers. Vinh Long Province operates
nine licensed sand mines with total reserves of approximately 3.4 million m³ along the Tien and
Hau Rivers. Additional licensed sand and stone sources are available under auction in Dong
Thap Province and from licensed quarries in An Giang Province. Precast concrete piles will be
sourced from established regional suppliers including TICCO Company and Thai Duong
Company.
Impact 1: Supply Chain Pressure on Sand and Stone Resources — Cumulative and
Indirect Risk
Classification: Moderate
Although the project does not directly undertake any extraction activity, its procurement
demand — particularly riprap stone and sand — will be placed on a regional supply system that
is simultaneously under exceptional stress. The Mekong Delta is concurrently implementing 11
expressway projects totaling 434.7 km, along with major bridge and airport upgrade works
(Rach Mieu 2, My Thuan 2, Can Tho Airport). This creates a regionally significant cumulative
demand on licensed quarry and sand mining capacity that predates the MERIT project's
requirements.
Within this context, the project's procurement volumes — while dominated by riprap stone
rather than fill sand, which reduces its direct competition for the scarcest resource — carry a
risk of indirectly incentivizing the exceedance of licensed extraction capacities at supplier sites,
or the displacement of demand toward unlicensed or informal mining operations if licensed
supply becomes constrained. River sand extraction in the Mekong Delta is well-documented as
a high-risk activity associated with accelerated riverbank erosion, land loss, disruption of
benthic habitats, and livelihood impacts on riparian communities. Any project-induced increase
in extraction pressure — even through legitimate supply chains — contributes to these
cumulative effects.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 129
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

The risk is assessed as Medium given that: the project's sand demand is relatively modest (8,398
m³ of coarse sand against confirmed regional reserves of 3.4 million m³); riprap stone — the
dominant material — is sourced from quarries rather than riverbeds and carries lower erosion
risk; and mitigation through supply chain due diligence is feasible and manageable. However,
the cumulative regional demand context elevates this above a Low classification.
Required mitigation: All procurement contracts must require suppliers to provide
documentation verifying licensed extraction status, current reserve levels against licensed
capacity, and compliance with Vietnamese environmental regulations. Material traceability
records must be maintained in the project Environmental Register for the full duration of
construction. The PMU Procurement Officer, in coordination with the Environmental Specialist,
must conduct at least one pre-contract due diligence site visit to each primary supplier, with
findings documented and disclosed to the World Bank Task Team. Long-term supply
agreements must be established prior to mobilization to reduce pressure-driven substitution
toward unlicensed sources.
Impact 2: Reputational and Compliance Risk from Supply Chain Non-Compliance
Classification: Moderate
Given the scale of concurrent regional infrastructure demand and the known prevalence of
unlicensed sand mining in the Mekong Delta, there is a material risk that suppliers — under
production pressure — may blend unlicensed material with licensed stock, exceed permitted
extraction volumes, or subcontract to informal operators without the PMU's knowledge. If this
occurs and is subsequently identified through third-party audit, media reporting, or community
complaint, the project is exposed to significant reputational and compliance risk under the
World Bank ESF, specifically ESS1 (Environmental and Social Assessment) and ESS6
(Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources).
Required mitigation: The project must implement a Supply Chain Environmental and Social
Due Diligence Protocol, requiring quarterly verification of supplier compliance through
document review, site inspection, or third-party audit. Any supplier found to be in non-
compliance must be placed on corrective action notice, with termination of supply contract if
corrective actions are not implemented within a defined timeframe. A supplier non-compliance
log must be maintained and included in quarterly progress reports to the World Bank.
Impact 3: Freshwater Demand — Seasonal Scarcity and Groundwater Dependency
Classification: Low
Freshwater requirements for construction operations and worker welfare are modest. With a
workforce of 60–80 workers per construction site, domestic water demand is estimated at 3–4
m³/day/site, and construction water use (concrete mixing, equipment washing) adds
approximately 7 m³/day/site — a combined demand of around 10–11 m³/day/site that is well
within the capacity of local water treatment plant supply under normal seasonal conditions.
Vinh Long Province operates 237 rural water supply stations with a combined capacity of
342,800 m³/day, providing structural adequacy under baseline conditions. However, the dry
season months of March to May introduce meaningful seasonal risk. During this period, drought
conditions and saline intrusion — particularly in former Ben Tre Province areas — constrain
surface water availability at treatment plants and increase reliance on groundwater abstraction.
Incremental construction-phase groundwater demand, if sourced from shallow aquifers already
under stress, has the potential to marginally exacerbate land subsidence and saline intrusion
processes — risks that are regionally significant in the Mekong Delta context even if
individually small in scale.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                               130
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

The adoption of pre-mixed concrete supply — which the ESIA notes could reduce on-site water
demand by 40–50% — is a straightforward demand-reduction measure that should be mandated
rather than presented as optional.
Required mitigation: Pre-mixed concrete supply should be adopted as the default procurement
approach across all construction sites. Where on-site mixing is necessary, water must be
sourced from licensed municipal supply rather than direct groundwater abstraction, unless the
PMU provides specific written authorization following assessment of local aquifer conditions.
During dry season months (March–May), each site must submit a daily water use log to the
PMU to confirm supply adequacy and detect any reliance on groundwater. If seasonal
constraints at the municipal supply level are identified, the site-specific construction schedule
must be adjusted to defer high-water-demand activities to the wet season.
Impact 4: Cement and Steel Demand — Resource Efficiency and Waste
Classification: Low
Cement demand of 5,626,607 kg and steel demand of 319,750 kg are substantial in absolute
terms but represent standard construction material consumption proportionate to the scope of
sluice gate, navigation lock, and embankment works. These materials are procured from
established commercial suppliers and do not involve direct resource extraction by the project.
The primary environmental considerations are: (i) the embodied carbon associated with cement
production, which is relevant to the project's climate resilience objectives but falls outside the
scope of this impact assessment; and (ii) the generation of construction waste from off-cuts,
cement bag disposal, and steel scrap, which must be managed through designated waste streams
to avoid uncontrolled disposal into canal systems.
Required mitigation: Cement and steel waste must be managed under the project's
Construction Waste Management Plan, with steel scrap directed to licensed recyclers and
cement bag waste disposed of at approved solid waste facilities. Storage of cement must comply
with manufacturer requirements to prevent premature hardening and associated material
wastage.
b. Coastal Marine Environment
Construction of Subproject 2.1 involves the installation of 16.6 km of coastal breakwaters,
comprising offshore piling operations and large-volume riprap placement in the nearshore
marine environment. These activities represent the project's primary interface with the coastal
and estuarine ecosystem, introducing sediment disturbance, hydrodynamic modification, and
pollution risk pathways that must be assessed against a baseline marine environment already
subject to natural turbidity variability and seasonal salinity fluctuation characteristic of the
Mekong Delta coastline.
Impact 1: Seabed Sediment Disturbance, Turbidity, and Suspended Solids from Piling
and Riprap Placement
Classification: Moderate
Piling operations and riprap placement along the 16.6 km breakwater alignment will physically
disturb nearshore seabed sediments, generating turbidity plumes that elevate Total Suspended
Solids (TSS) and reduce light penetration in the affected water column. Baseline turbidity in
the project area ranges from 12.3 to 18.4 NTU, reflecting the naturally high sediment load
characteristic of the Mekong Delta coastal zone. Construction-phase exceedances above this
baseline will temporarily suppress phytoplankton photosynthesis, reducing primary
productivity in the immediate construction zone and, to a lesser degree, in the downstream drift
of suspended material. These impacts are spatially confined — plume dispersion is expected
within a few hundred meters of the active work front — and are temporally bounded by the
construction schedule. Given the naturally turbid baseline conditions, the incremental

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                131
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

ecological stress from construction turbidity, while real, is of lower relative magnitude than an
equivalent disturbance in a clear-water ecosystem. Benthic communities within the immediate
riprap footprint will experience permanent substrate change, but this is a designed and accepted
consequence of the breakwater structure rather than an unmanaged impact. Mitigation requires
a real-time turbidity monitoring protocol at defined stations along the breakwater alignment,
with threshold-triggered construction suspension provisions. Construction scheduling should,
where practicable, avoid peak spawning or nursery seasons for locally significant coastal
species, and silt curtains should be deployed at sections where nearshore fisheries or
aquaculture operations lie within the plume dispersion zone.
Impact 2: Nearshore Hydrodynamic Modification
Classification: Moderate
The installation of 16.6 km of breakwater structures will permanently alter nearshore wave
energy, current patterns, and sediment transport dynamics along the affected coastline. During
the construction phase, partially completed breakwater sections will create transitional
hydrodynamic conditions — including localized areas of accelerated current, modified wave
refraction, and transient changes in sediment deposition and erosion — that differ from both
the pre-construction baseline and the designed post-construction equilibrium. These transitional
conditions may temporarily affect sediment accretion or erosion patterns at adjacent
unprotected shoreline sections during the construction period. The long-term hydrodynamic
modification is the intended design outcome of the breakwater system and is assessed and
accepted within the project's coastal engineering design; the construction-phase transitional
impact is assessed as Medium due to its localized extent and the reversibility of any transient
morphological effects as the structure reaches completion. Coastal morphological monitoring
— using bathymetric survey and satellite shoreline change analysis — must be conducted at
baseline and at defined intervals during construction to detect any unanticipated adverse effects
on adjacent unprotected shorelines, with findings reviewed by the project's coastal engineering
consultant and reported to the PMU quarterly.
Impact 3: Marine Water Quality — Fuel and Grease Contamination from Construction
Vessels
Classification: Moderate
The sustained operation of barges, tugboats, piling vessels, and heavy equipment along a 16.6
km offshore construction corridor introduces a diffuse but persistent risk of hydrocarbon
contamination from fuel and lubricant leakage. Oil films on the water surface inhibit the air-
water oxygen exchange interface, reducing surface re-aeration rates. In combination with the
suppression of phytoplankton photosynthesis caused by elevated turbidity, this has the potential
to cause measurable reduction of dissolved oxygen (DO) from current baseline levels of 5.8–
6.83 mg/L in localized areas adjacent to active construction vessels. While the spatial extent of
any individual leakage event is inherently limited, the sustained multi-vessel operation over the
full construction period increases the cumulative probability of contamination incidents. A
significant acute spill event — arising from vessel collision, barge grounding, or fuel transfer
accident — would represent a discrete pollution incident of potentially High significance,
requiring immediate containment response; under routine operating conditions, however, the
risk is assessed as Medium given its localized, reversible nature and the feasibility of effective
prevention through mechanical integrity standards and onboard spill containment. All
construction vessels must hold valid oil pollution prevention certifications and carry onboard
spill response kits as a condition of site access; fuel transfers must be conducted using closed-
system equipment with drip trays; and a marine spill response protocol, with a designated
offshore spill response coordinator, must be included in the C-ESMP. DO and hydrocarbon



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                132
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

monitoring must be conducted at defined nearshore stations at minimum fortnightly frequency
during active construction.
c. Impacts on aquatic life
Construction-phase water quality changes — elevated turbidity and TSS from piling and riprap
placement, potential DO suppression, and hydrocarbon contamination risk from vessel
operations — will generate localized, temporary impacts on both natural aquatic ecology and
aquaculture operations in the project area.
For natural aquatic communities, phytoplankton productivity will be temporarily reduced in
areas of elevated turbidity, with diatom-dominated assemblages at monitoring stations TS3
(Ben Tre) and TS6 (Tra Vinh) the most exposed. Benthic fauna and any seagrass present within
the construction footprint face smothering from sediment deposition and localized hypoxia; the
existing low diversity of benthic communities (1–4 species, predominantly Mactra and Donax)
means that even modest disturbance represents a meaningful proportional impact on local
assemblage structure. Fish and larger mobile fauna are expected to avoid active construction
zones in response to increased vessel traffic and underwater noise, with minor risk of physical
harm; spawning and feeding behavior may be temporarily disrupted within a few hundred
meters of the work front. These impacts are spatially confined, of short duration, and subject to
natural recovery once construction concludes. Mobile species will self-relocate, and benthic
recolonization is expected within one to two post-construction seasons given the dynamic
sediment environment of the Mekong Delta coast.
Aquaculture operations present a more directly quantifiable social and economic exposure.
Clam farming cooperatives in Ben Tre (An Thuy, Tan Thuy) and Tra Vinh (Phuong Dong,
Truong Long Hoa) operate in close proximity to the breakwater alignment; elevated turbidity
and hydrodynamic disturbance from construction may affect clam habitat conditions and
harvesting access. Economic losses to these cooperatives must be assessed and addressed
through the project's Resettlement Plan. Shrimp pond operators in Truong Long Hoa Ward face
a lower-order risk, as turbidity-affected seawater intake periods can be managed through
temporary storage and scheduled intake avoidance during peak construction activity.
Overall, aquatic ecology impacts are classified as Moderate — localized in extent, reversible in
nature, and manageable through construction scheduling and monitoring — but the direct
exposure of active clam farming cooperatives introduces a social and livelihood dimension that
requires formal assessment and compensation commitments under the Resettlement Plan.
                                 Impact / Risk                                 Classification
Phytoplankton productivity and benthic community disturbance                     Moderate
Fish and mobile fauna — habitat disturbance and behavioral disruption              Low
Clam farming cooperatives — habitat and harvesting impacts                       Moderate
Shrimp pond operations — seawater intake quality                                   Low
d. Impacts on waterway traffic
Construction of Subproject 2.1 generates two distinct waterway traffic impact contexts that
require separate assessment. On the primary transport routes — the Class I Co Chien and Hau
rivers, with navigable widths of 120–150 m and existing traffic of 200–400 vessel movements
per day — the project's demand of 4–6 barge trips per day represents approximately 1.5–3% of
current traffic volume. At this level, congestion risk is negligible and no material disruption to
commercial or passenger navigation on these arterial waterways is anticipated.
The more significant risk arises at the construction and staging areas located 100–150 m
offshore between the Co Chien and Hau estuaries. This zone supports approximately 80–120
small fishing vessels (under 15 m in length) operating without fixed routes, with peak activity


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                      133
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

concentrated between 04:00–06:00 and 15:00–17:00. The simultaneous presence of 15–20
construction vessels — crane barges, tugboats, and material barges — with mooring lines
extending 30–50 m into the navigable area, combined with tidal constraints that confine
operations to approximately six hours per day (H ≥ +1.0 m), will create periods of elevated
localized traffic density at river mouths and nearshore sites. The interaction between
unscheduled small fishing vessels and a constrained construction fleet during tidal operating
windows presents a meaningful collision risk, particularly during low-visibility pre-dawn
fishing activity.
These impacts are temporary, spatially confined to the offshore construction corridor, and fully
manageable through navigation control, vessel scheduling, and signaling measures.
Classification as Medium reflects the real but controllable risk to small fishing vessel operators
during active construction windows, rather than any systemic disruption to regional waterway
traffic.

                                       Impact / Risk                                    Classification

Traffic congestion on primary transport routes (Co Chien, Hau rivers)                       Low
Collision risk with small fishing vessels at nearshore construction and staging areas    Moderate
e. Impacts on livelihoods and wind power infrastructure
Impacts on aquaculture livelihoods
The subproject will affect 04 aquaculture cooperatives (An Thuy, Tan Thuy in Tan Thuy
Commune; Phuong Dong, Truong Long Hoa in Truong Long Hoa Ward) with a total of 13 ha
of clam farming area. Compared to the total leased water surface of 1,007.5 ha, this represents
only 1.29%. As farming areas are dispersed (typically 150–300 m from shore, extending ~2 km
offshore), impacts are limited. In addition, lease expiry (e.g., Tan Thuy) and potential
reallocation of plots allow relocation of farming activities outside construction zones. Detailed
impacts and compensation will be addressed in the Resettlement Plan (RP), and overall impact
significance is considered as Low.
Impacts and risks on wind power infrastructure
All four communes/wards host nearshore and offshore wind power plants. While turbines are
located 1,000–2,000 m from the embankments and are not directly affected, submarine cables
buried at 2–3 m below the seabed may be at risk. Construction of 16.6 km of breakwaters (100–
150 m offshore), involving 10–12 m piles driven to –5 to –7.5 m, rock filling, and barge mooring,
may pose risks of cable damage or displacement, particularly in areas such as Dong Hai and
Truong Long Hoa. With effective coordination, cable mapping, and safety controls, these risks
can be minimized, and the overall impact magnitude is considered Low.
(2) Subproject 2.2: Upgrading Climate-Resilient Infrastructure and Integrated
Transformation of the Dong Thap Muoi Region
A. Canal dredging
Impact 1: Generation of dredged material
According to the Feasibility Study Report, dredging of the Phuoc Xuyen–Hai Tam and Cho
Bung canals will generate approximately 2.5 million m³ of material (see table 2.6, chapter 2),
mainly soft mud, clayey silt, and fine sediments. The material will be disposed at 30 sites along
the canals, covering about 1,126,000 m² (942,000 m² for Phuoc Xuyen–Hai Tam and 184,000
m² for Cho Bung), with an estimated average disposal thickness of 2.2–2.3 m.
The total volume of dredged material is calculated using the following formula:

-        V (m³): Total volume of dredged material
