(Note: 1 m³ of dredged mud ≈ 1.2 tons) (Source: Feasibility Study Report, March 2026)
Analysis results of sediment quality at sampling locations within the subproject area (samples
taken at 30–50 cm depth below the canal bed, see Chapter 4 for details) show pH values ranging
from 6.04 – 7.53, within the neutral to slightly acidic range, with no signs of active acid sulfate
soils. Heavy metal concentrations, including As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr, Cu, and Zn, were all low and
below the regulatory limits specified in QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT. Additionally, organochlorine
pesticide (OCP) compounds were not detected in any sediment samples. These results suggest
that sediments in the study area (up to 50 cm depth) show no clear evidence of acid sulfate
contamination, heavy metal pollution, or significant accumulation of agricultural chemical
residues.
Although sediment quality results indicate generally low levels of contamination within the
sampled depth (30–50 cm), this represents a significant limitation, as dredging will likely
extend to depths of approximately 1 m or more. As a result, deeper sediment layers remain
insufficiently characterized, creating uncertainty regarding potential contaminants, including
acid sulfate soils (ASS) and associated environmental risks during dredging.
As indicated in the Baseline Chapter, the subproject area is located within an acid sulfate soil
zone, where deeper sediment layers may contain ASS and elevated pyrite concentrations
capable of generating acidic leachate upon exposure during dredging. Baseline surface water
quality further indicates localized environmental stress, with reduced dissolved oxygen (DO)
and exceedances of COD, BOD₅, and NH4⁺, reflecting limited assimilative capacity in certain
canal sections. Disturbance of deeper, insufficiently characterized sediments may therefore
exacerbate water quality impacts, including increased turbidity, oxygen depletion, and localized
acidification, particularly in areas supporting aquaculture activities.
Given the large scale of the Subproject, involving the dredging of approximately 2.5 million m³
of sediment, the environmental risks and impacts are considered as Substantial. This rating is
driven by the high probability of disturbing deep-seated ASS and pyrite-rich layers that remain
insufficiently characterized below the 0.5m depth. The potential for large-scale acidification,
oxygen depletion, and heavy metal mobilization coupled with the challenges of managing such
a massive volume of dredged material poses a credible threat to the sensitive aquatic ecosystem
and environment. Therefore, detailed sampling at 1.5 – 2.0m depths is mandatory during the



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                              135
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

detailed design stage, and before commencing dredging activities to ensure mitigation strategies
are commensurate with this Substantial impact.
Impact 2: Pollution Risks related to acid sulfate soils
The Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam and Cho Bung canal subproject areas lie within the Dong Thap
Muoi region, one of the most extensive active and potential acid sulfate soil (AASS/PASS)
zones in Southeast Asia. The geochemical hazard is well-characterized: iron pyrite (FeS₂)
present in these soils oxidizes upon exposure to air and water, generating sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)
and mobilizing dissolved iron (Fe) and aluminum (Al). Under natural conditions in this region,
affected soils exhibit pH of 3.5–5.5, total Fe of 5–30 mg/L, and Al of 1–10 mg/L —
concentrations that are acutely toxic to aquatic fauna, inhibit agricultural productivity, and
render water unsuitable for domestic use without treatment.
Canal dredging to approximately 1 m below the existing bed, combined with embankment
excavation works, carries a direct risk of disturbing or exposing underlying PASS strata. Under
typical Dong Thap Muoi conditions, the upper sediment column consists of soft organic mud
0.5–1.5 m thick overlying acid sulfate deposits; dredging at the design depth will therefore
primarily remove the mud layer and in many areas will not directly reach the PASS horizon.
However, the FS report indicated that PASS do exist in the Cho Bung area and excavation for
embankment construction will take place along the Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam canal and there is
high probability that materials from such activities would be PASS. There are also spatial
variability in mud layer thickness across the canal network means that areas with thinner
surficial sediment — or where near-surface acid sulfate deposits occur — cannot be excluded
from the dredging footprint. This uncertainty is material: available baseline data do not provide
sufficient spatial resolution to confirm PASS depth across the full canal alignment, and the
consequence of unmanaged exposure is severe and potentially irreversible within the
construction timeframe. Additional samplings for soil pH/acidity will be required during detail
design and construction phases so as the design of the disposal sites, control measures as part
of the Dredging Management Plan can be updated accordingly.
Where PASS is exposed at disposal sites — whether through drainage of decant water, surface
desiccation, or embankment failure — the PASS will become active acid sulphate soil from the
oxidation of mobilized pyrite-bearing material which generate acidic, metal-rich leachate that.
if strong acidic effluent is leaked into aquaculture ponds, it may harm or even cause death to
aquatic species. Leaking of acidic water into crop/agriculture land would affect the grow which
affect productivity, damage to crops and/or trees. . This pollution risks from oxidised dredging
and excavation materials is assessed as the highest-consequence environmental risk chain in
the subproject, qualifying the overall impact as Substantial. Effective management through the
DMMP, including deep pre-construction soil sampling according to applicable standard, careful
dredging practice, and engineered disposal site design, is capable of reducing residual risk, but
cannot eliminate it given the scale of material movement and the spatial variability of PASS
occurrence.
There is a total of 24 storage sites is proposed along the Phuoc Xuyen Canal in Dong Thap
Province (15 sites) and Tay Ninh Province (9 sites) and 6 storage sites for Cho Bung canal.
These storage sites are mainly on public land managed by the commune People's Committee
which are the orrow pits formed from past land leveling projects or Vacant, low-lying land
planned for public facilities such as bus stations, commercial centers, etc.
A risk screening and assessment has been conducted for all 30 proposed disposal sites based on
the following formula:
                        Risk = Likelihood x Severity
Of which,

                                                136
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Likelihood (Probability): Based on historical data from similar projects in the Mekong Delta,
site-specific geological conditions (soft soil, acid sulfate soils, contaminated water leakage,
overflow and site instability, including embankment failure and uncontrolled runoff).
Severity (Consequence): Based on the presence and proximity of sensitive receptors, such as
schools, historical/cultural heritage sites, and local aquaculture households, water bodies and
sensibility of surrounding landscape.

Based on this screening, six sites (Nos. 8, 10, 13, 14, 20, and 22) are classified as Substantial.
These sites exhibit a high likelihood of hazards—such as contaminated water leakage, overflow,
and instability due to disposal heights exceeding 5m - coupled with significant severity because
of their proximity to sensitive receptors, particularly kindergartens in Phu Cuong and Vinh
Chau communes. Another six sites are classified as Moderate risk; while their severity is
moderate due to proximity to residential areas (Sites 23 and 24 in Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam, and
Site 2 in Cho Bung), they are notable for their proximity to schools (approx. 200m for Site 22),
acid sulfate conditions, and disposal heights of 3–4m (Sites 2, 9, 11, and 21). The remaining 18
sites are classified as Low risk, characterized by lower sensitivity (vacant land with shrubs) and
a reduced likelihood of leakage, overflow, or instability. This classification provides a
consistent basis for prioritizing mitigation measures related to containment, drainage control,
and site management across all disposal sites, as detailed in the DMMP.
If embankments, drainage systems, and settling ponds are properly designed, and transport and
operations are strictly controlled, the proposed disposal sites can be temporarily used to receive
dredged materials without causing serious environmental impacts. However, specific
environmental management measures should be applied to minimize risks to water resources,
residential areas, and sensitive sites.
Overall assessment: Impacts from the disposal sites are mainly localized and temporary, but the
impact magnitude may be considered Substantial if embankment and drainage systems are not
properly controlled.
Impact 3: Risk of Leachate and Effluent Management
Over a 2-year construction period, the 30 disposal sites (totaling 112.6 hectares) must manage
an estimated water volume of 7.17 million m³. This volume comprises:
   -   Stormwater runoff: Approximately 1.69 million m³, based on an average annual rainfall
       of 1,500 mm in the Mekong Delta (runoff coefficient of 0.5).
   -   Decant water and leachate: Approximately 5.48 million m³, originating from the
       hydraulic dredging process (30% solids to 70% transport water ratio). The quantitative
       flow rate assessment for discharge from disposal site shall be done during detailed
       design.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                   137
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

While many disposal sites utilize borrow pits or low-lying terrain, their proximity to the Phuoc
Xuyen Hai Tam or Cho Bung canals (90 – 300m) creates a critical pathway for contaminated
discharge. In the event of containment system failure or inadequate management, the following
potential impacts are anticipated:
-   Water Quality Degradation: Uncontrolled discharge of decant water and acidic leachate
    will lead to increased turbidity and TSS content, organic loading, and nutrient enrichment
    in adjacent canals. This may cause localized "acid shocks" and oxygen depletion, severely
    threatening aquatic life and domestic water sources for local communities..
-   Sediment and subsurface contamination: Pollutants accumulated in deeper sediment layers
    (below the 0.5m sampling depth) may be mobilized and spread into the surrounding
    environment through subsurface seepage, undermining the long-term fertility of
    agricultural land.
-   Acid Sulfate Contamination and Iron Mobilization: The primary environmental risk
    involves the oxidation of potential acid sulfate soils (PASS) within the dredged sediments.
    Based on the sediment quality analysis conducted by Pre-EIA Consultant in Phase 1 (April
    2025) and and EIA/ESIA Consultant Phase 2 (February 2026), the following baseline
    conditions have been identified:
(i) Elevated Iron (Fe) concentrations: Analysis results show that Iron concentrations
significantly exceed permissible limits. In Phase 1, Fe levels ranged from 32,2 mg/kg to 39,730
mg/kg, and in Phase 2, levels reached up to 28,839 mg/kg, all of which surpass the QCVN
43:2025/BNNMT threshold of 20,000 g/kg;
(ii) Acidic potential: Sediment pH values were recorded between 6.04 and 7.53. The
combination of high Iron content and slightly acidic pH indicates the presence of acid sulfate
soil conditions;
(iii) Ecological impact: Once placed in disposal sites, exposure to air triggers the oxidation of
these sediments, generating acidic leachate and remobilizing Fe²⁺. This process leads to the
formation of iron hydroxide precipitates and a localized reduction in dissolved oxygen (DO),
which can severely degrade aquatic habitats and water quality in adjacent canals used for rice
paddies and aquaculture.
Overall, the magnitude of impact is considered as Substantial.
Impact 4: Risk of Overtopping and Embankment Instability
Some storage sites are planned with fill heights of up to 3 – 4 m, while the natural terrain in
many places is about 1 m lower than the embankment. This creates a risk of water overflow
during heavy rain, embankment failure, and runoff onto surrounding rice fields. This risk is
particularly notable at the sites with filling height of 4-5m such as Site No. 20 (5m high) and
Site No. 24 (4m high), borrow pits with weak embankments, and sites without embankments.
Environmental Impacts of Surface Runoff and Acidic Leachate:
The interaction between surface runoff and Potential Acid Sulfate Soils (PASS) within the
disturbed sediments poses a significant geochemical threat to the Mekong Delta’s sensitive
agricultural landscape. The specialized impacts are categorized as follows:
- Impact on Rice and Crop Ecosystems:
Rice crops are the primary sensitive receptors in the project vicinity. The uncontrolled seepage
or leakage of leachate - characterized by low pH and high concentrations of phytotoxic ions
(Al3+, Fe2+) leads to:



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                138
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

•   Aluminum Toxicity: Excess Al3+ ions interfere with root elongation and nutrient uptake
    mechanisms, resulting in stunted growth, leaf chlorosis (yellowing), and significant yield
    loss.
•    Iron Toxicity ("Bronzing"): Elevated Fe2+ levels in the soil solution induce "iron bronzing"
    in rice leaves, disrupting cellular photosynthesis and potentially leading to localized crop
    failure.
•   Edaphic Soil Degradation: Chronic seepage of acidic effluent causes irreversible alterations
    to the soil's physical and chemical structure, leading to long-term loss of agricultural
    productivity in adjacent paddy fields.
- Impact on Aquatic Ecosystems:
The discharge of untreated acidic water into the Phuoc Xuyen canal and secondary drainage
systems triggers acute "acid shocks", resulting in:
• Acute Mortality: Sudden drops in pH (below 5.0) cause immediate lethality in sensitive fish
  species and macroinvertebrates through ion-regulatory failure.
• Physiological Stress: Sub-lethal acidity impairs metabolic functions and diminishes the
  reproductive success of aquatic organisms, undermining biodiversity.
• Heavy Metal Mobilization: The low-pH environment enhances the solubility and
  bioavailability of heavy metals potentially sequestered in sediments, facilitating
  bioaccumulation within the aquatic food web.
- Physicochemical Alterations of Receiving Water:
• Iron Flocculation: As iron-rich, acidic leachate mixes with neutral canal water, iron
  precipitates as ferric hydroxide (Fe(OH)3), forming "orange-red flocs." This precipitate
  increases turbidity, coats fish gills, and smothers benthic habitats.
• Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Depletion: The rapid chemical oxidation of ferrous iron (Fe2+) to
  ferric iron (Fe3+) exerts a significant Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), leading to hypoxic
  conditions that further stress aquatic life.
Overall, the magnitude of impact is considered as Substantial.
Impact 5: Risk of impacts on residential areas and sensitive structures
Some storage sites are located relatively close to residential areas and sensitive structures such
as Hoa Binh and Vinh Chau A Kindergartens (adjacent to or at a proximity of 50-70m), or areas
with houses and traffic routes. Transportation and reception of dredged material at these sites
may generate impacts such as dust and soil spillage during transport, noise from vehicles and
construction equipment, traffic safety risks, and odor from organic sludge during decomposition
and drying.
Potentially affected groups include students, residents near the storage sites, and road users,
including vulnerable groups such as children, the elderly, and people with respiratory conditions.
Therefore, site selection, design, and operational management of storage sites need to be strictly
implemented to minimize adverse impacts on these sensitive groups.
The above impacts mainly occur during the construction and transportation of dredged materials
and are localized and temporary. However, if not properly controlled, the impact is Substantial.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                139
    Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    Impact 6: Impacts and risks at the disposal sites
    The Site No.20 is a borrow pit in Doc Binh Kieu commune is located near the Go Thap relic
    cluster (about 600–700 m in a straight line). This site is 3 km from the Phuoc Xuyen canal.
    Dredged material is planned to be pumped directly into this site. However, given the relatively
    long distance, the bulldozer pumping method is considered cost-ineffective. In the case of road
    transport, attention must be given to the transport route passing through the Go Thap relic area.
    Potential risks include increased truck traffic in the relic area, vibration and dust, and impacts
    on cultural landscape. Therefore, transport routes and timing need to be controlled. This impact
    is considered Moderate.

    Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                           140
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

b. Water quality degradation
According to the subproject’s construction plan, dredging of Phuoc Xuyen and Cho Bung canals
will be carried out using a suction dredger (hydraulic dredging), where the bottom sediment is
sucked as a sludge–water mixture and pumped directly through a pipeline system to the dredged
material storage sites. During construction, several risks and impacts on water quality may arise
as follows:
(i) Baseline Environmental Settings
The pre-dredging water quality analysis (Detailed analysis results in the Annex 2) reveals a
highly sensitive aquatic environment characterized by existing organic and nutrient stress:
- Organic Loading and Oxygen Deficiency: Several monitoring points (NM10, NM20, NM25)
  exhibit Dissolved Oxygen (DO) levels between 3.78 and 4.04 mg/L, falling below the QCVN
  08:2023/BTNMT (Column B) threshold of 5 mg/L.
•   Nutrient Surplus: A critical baseline exceedance was observed at sampling point NM05, with
    Ammonium (NH+) concentrations reaching 5.13 mg/L—more than 17 times the allowable
    limit of 0.3 mg/L.
•   Physical Parameters: Baseline Total Suspended Solids (TSS) range from 20 to 35 mg/L, with
    turbidity peaks reaching 92 NTU (NM04). The system exhibits high natural variability
    driven by tidal cycles, with TSS fluctuating between 60 and 85 mg/L during peak flows.
(ii) Modeling Results and Contaminant Transport
The required input data parameters for the simulation include:
-    Hydrological network: The comprehensive river and canal network of the Mekong Delta
     region.
-    Water quality boundary conditions: Sectoral discharge data (the volume of wastewater
     effluents discharged into the canal system from various socio-economic development
     activities).
-    Hydrodynamic (HD) boundary conditions: Upstream discharge (Q) and water levels at
     coastal stations.
-    Topographic and bathymetric data: Surveyed elevation data of the canal bed and banks at
     designated cross-sections along the project route.
-    Time-series data: Hourly measured data (specifically for the February 2016 scenario) at
     key hydrological control stations located at the inflows and outflows of the simulated
     network.
-    Manning's roughness coefficient: A parameter characterizing the roughness of the channel
     bed, which governs the flow velocity. With flow velocities ranging from 0.2 to 0.5 m/s,
     depending on the specific location.
-    TSS concentrations at AD boundaries: Time-series data of baseline Total Suspended Solids
     (TSS) concentrations at the model's boundary inflows.
-    Dispersion Coefficient: A defined parameter representing the degree of mixing and
     longitudinal dispersion of suspended solids along the flow path.
-    Settling velocity: The rate at which suspended sediments fall to the canal bed under the
     influence of gravity (m/s): In the Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal area, the clay (< 0.002 mm)
     and silt (0.002 - 0.05 mm) fractions are dominant (typically exceeding 70-80%), with the
     settling velocity ranging from 0.0001 to 0.0005 m/s
-    Point source coordinates: The specific geographical coordinates of the excavator/dredger
     at the modeled location.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                141
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

-   Incremental TSS load induced by dredging: Calculated based on: the capacity of the
    dredging equipment; the spill rate associated with the selected construction technology
    (ranging from 1% to 5%); and the bulk density of the bottom sediments.
-   Operational schedule: The designated working shifts of the dredging equipment.
Modeling Approach utilizing MIKE 11 AD: The Hydrodynamics (HD) module was executed
to compute the baseline flow regime and the Advection-Dispersion (AD) module was run to
simulate the pollutant transport. The Contaminant Transport Model developed by the FS for the
Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals provides a quantitative forecast of dredging
impacts:




    Figure 6. 2: Changes in TSS concentration at Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal at the
                     dredging location compared to baseline condition
The MIKE 11AD hydrodynamic modeling results utilized the year 2016 as the baseline for
comparison due to the following rationale:
The year 2016 represents the historical "Worst-Case" scenario, allowing for the assessment of
the environmental carrying capacity under the most extreme and adverse conditions.
Specifically, 2016 experienced an extreme El Nino event, resulting in historic drought and
severe saline intrusion across the Mekong Delta. During this period, the water level of the
Mekong River plummeted to its lowest point in nearly a century. Consequently, the record-low
baseline flow of 2016 equates to the weakest hydrodynamic forces, thereby minimizing the
water body's natural dilution and self-purification capacities.
The modeling results indicate that both the baseline scenario (black line) and the project
scenario (blue line) exhibit continuous and rhythmic sinusoidal fluctuations. The Total
Suspended Solids (TSS) concentration reaches its minimum at approximately 58 mg/l (during
slack water) and peaks at around 85 - 86 mg/l (during peak tide/maximum flow velocity). This
demonstrates that the natural aquatic environment in this area already experiences significant
diurnal variations in turbidity (a differential of nearly 28 mg/l between the lowest and highest
turbidity phases). This phenomenon is characteristic of regions governed by an irregular semi-
diurnal tidal regime, where strong currents naturally resuspend the bottom sediment layer.
Furthermore, the simulation demonstrates that under the project scenario (blue line), TSS levels
are consistently, albeit marginally, higher than the baseline scenario (black line) throughout the
entire modeled period. However, the differential between the two scenarios is extremely narrow,
with the induced increment ranging only from 0.5 to slightly over 1 mg/l.
Therefore, the anticipated increase in TSS concentration attributable to the dredging activities
(approx 1 mg/l) is negligible when compared to the natural background fluctuation amplitude
of the Phuoc Xuyen Canal (nearly 28 mg/l). This marginal increase will not fundamentally alter
the baseline water quality profile of the area. Even at the projected maximum peak
concentration of approximately 86 mg/l during project implementation, the water quality
remains well within the permissible limits set by the National Technical Regulation on Surface

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                142
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Water Quality (QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT) for Class B (Class B: Average water quality.
The aquatic ecosystem consumes significant dissolved oxygen due to a considerable pollutant
load. The water is suitable for industrial and agricultural production purposes following the
application of appropriate treatment measures)




                                                                Dredging
                                                                location
    Figure 6.3: TSS propagation during dredging at Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal
- TSS Increment: The model predicts a localized increase in TSS concentrations of only 0.4 –
  0.6 mg/L above the baseline. Compared to the ambient TSS range, this represents a marginal
  increase of <1%.
- Spatial Influence (Mixing Zone): Due to the influence of the semi-diurnal tidal regime, the
  hydrodynamic flow is not unidirectional but is subject to continuous flow reversal (with
  flood tides driving water upstream and ebb tides drawing it downstream). Consequently, the
  suspended sediments generated at the dredging face are dispersed back and forth by these
  alternating currents, forming a localized turbidity plume that oscillates around the active
  construction site. The plume of increased turbidity is projected to extend 350 – 450 meters
  upstream and downstream from the active dredging face. The spatial footprint of this
  elevated turbidity is therefore highly localized, confined strictly to a radius of less than 500
  meters surrounding the dredging equipment, and this zone of influence will migrate in
  tandem with the progression of the dredger. Therefore, it can be conclusively stated that
  sensitive receptors—such as aquaculture zones, domestic water intake stations, and
  ecological conservation areas—located 1 kilometer or more from the active dredging
  operations will remain entirely unaffected by the suspended sediment dispersion
c. Impacts and risks to aquatic life and biodiversity
The dredging of approximately 2.5 million m³ of sediment from Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and
Cho Bung canals is expected to result in significant ecological consequences. Based on
baseline surveys conducted in April 2025, the following assessment addresses both direct and
indirect impacts:
Direct Impacts and Population Decline
Dredging operations will cause immediate physical disruption to the aquatic community,
which currently consists of common Mekong Delta species with no recorded rare or high-
conservation-value taxa:
•   Habitat destruction: The removal of the benthic sediment layer will eliminate the primary
    habitat and food sources for the local benthic community.
•   Direct mortality: Benthic organisms with limited mobility, such as polychaetes, mollusks,
    and aquatic insect larvae (recorded at densities of 110–1,960 individuals/m²), will be killed
    or buried during construction.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                143
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

•   Trophic level disruption: The decline in primary producers (phytoplankton at 1,304–5,587
    cells/L) and primary consumers (zooplankton at 10,000 – 131,117 individuals/m²) due to
    environmental disturbance will create a cascading effect throughout the food web.
Water Quality and Potential Acid Sulfate Soil Risks
Given the slow-flow characteristics of the canal system (velocities of 0.05–0.2 m/s), the claims
of rapid water quality recovery are deemed implausible. The following risks are identified:
- Persistent turbidity: Subject to confirmation by modeling, Total Suspended Solids (TSS)
  may rise up to 60.6 – 85.6 mg/L at 350 – 450 m distance from the dredging activities. In a
  slow-moving system, these fine particles will remain in the water column longer, adhering
  to the gills of fish and shrimp and severely hindering respiration.
- PASS activation: The disturbance of PASS at bottom sediments poses a significant risk of
  activating acidity. Oxidation of sulfide-bearing sediments can lead to localized acidification
  (pH drop) and the subsequent release of heavy metals and toxic contaminants.
• Toxic gas release: Dredging will trigger the release of trapped methane (CH4) and hydrogen
  sulfide (H2S), which can cause acute respiratory distress or mortality in localized aquatic
  populations.
Indirect Impacts and Secondary Pollution
•   Photosynthetic inhibition: Elevated turbidity reduces light penetration, limiting the growth
    and primary productivity of phytoplankton.
•   Noise and behavioral disturbance: Noise from dredging machinery causes stress-induced
    flight responses in fish and can mask environmental cues necessary for juveniles to detect
    predators.
•   Chemical contamination: Any accidental spill of oil or grease from construction equipment
    acts as an acute toxin and may lead to long-term bioaccumulation within the food chain.
Adaptability and Recovery Timeline
•   Organism mobility: Highly mobile species, such as adult fish and larger crustaceans, may
    avoid the immediate construction zone, leading to temporary local density reductions.
•   Ecological recovery: While benthic communities are expected to begin recolonizing the
    canal bed once dredging ceases, the combined effects of slow flow and PASS risks suggest
    that full recovery of biodiversity and community structure may require at least one year or
    longer.




                Figure 6. 4: Impacts of dredging on large aquatic animals
                                                               (Source: J.M. Miró et. al., 2022)
d. Impacts on fishing and inner canal aquaculture

Field surveys confirm no inner canal aquaculture occurs directly within the Phuoc Xuyen Hai
Tam and Cho Bung canals. Only 55 households extract canal water for inland aquaculture
(freshwater fish farms) raising (K0+000–K22+900, left bank, Tay Ninh), while no households
abstract water from the Cho Bung canal.
 TSS tolerance range of fish:
 Low to moderate concentrations 0-500 mg/L: limited impact though prolonged exposure can lead to
 physiological stress
 High concentration: (500-1000+ mg/L): High TSS (500 mg/L and higher) significantly reduces the
 growth rate and survival of juvenile fish, particularly when combined with high temperature and low
 pH
 Extreme concentrations (>1000 mg/L): such high turbidity even are known to cause severe impact,
 damage or kill fish over time

                                (Source: Google search (will be replaced with scientific source))
Fishing activities are minimal, involving approximately five households who engage in fishing
during their agricultural off-season to supplement their daily meals. Consequently, impacts are
considered negligible. Overall, impacts on aquaculture and fishing are assessed as moderate
with recovery expected post-construction.
e. Disruption to inland waterway traffic and traffic safety risks
Phuoc Xuyen Canal is an important inland waterway in the region, serving both freight transport
and local passenger traffic. Field surveys show a daily traffic volume of approximately 70
vessels, including boats, small ships, and barges with a maximum load of about 1,000 tons. In
addition, there are currently five informal ferry points along the canal serving local travel
between the two banks in Dong Thap and Tay Ninh provinces. Traffic on this canal occurs
regularly and tends to increase during the agricultural harvest season.




     Figure 6. 5: Intersection of Phuoc Xuyen Canal and Nguyen Van Tiep, Dong Thap
                                          Province

  Figure 6. 6: Waterway traffic at Truong           Figure 6. 7: Waterway traffic on the east
  Xuyen embankment – Phuoc Xuyen Hai                    bank of Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam Canal
              Tam Canal
       Source: Survey photos by the environmental consulting team, taken in February 2025


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                  145
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

During the construction phase, dredging and the building of embankments and sluices will use
specialized equipment such as dredgers, barges carrying dredged material, and embankment
construction materials, which may occupy part of the canal’s cross-section. The presence of
this equipment can temporarily narrow the navigation channel, especially in sections with
narrow cross-sections or high traffic density. This can increase the risk of localized congestion
and collisions between construction vessels and transport vessels.
This risk is particularly notable at intersection areas and canal sections with high traffic density,
such as the section from Hong Ngu Canal to Nguyen Van Tiep Canal and the intersection
between Phuoc Xuyen Canal and Nguyen Van Tiep Canal, where waterway traffic lanes cross
and large vessels and barges frequently pass. Additionally, in canal sections near densely
populated areas such as Thạnh Loi embankment, and the east and west banks of Phuoc Xuyen
Canal, the frequent activity of small local vessels (boats, sampans) increases the risk of traffic
conflicts when construction vessels are present.
Risks related to small informal ferry landing: As results of field survey conducted in February
2026, there are two small scale informal ferry landing points at Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal
(one ferry landing is at the Km18+400 and another is at Km35+750). Two these ferries cross
the canal about 5-6 rounds per day with about 6 - 8 persons per trip. Combined with construction
vessel activity, the risk of traffic conflicts between longitudinal-moving vessels and cross-
channel vessels may increase, especially during peak hours or when visibility is limited. This
can raise the risk of waterway accidents if appropriate warning and traffic management
measures are not implemented.




           Figure 6. 8: Small scale ferry landing in Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal
Risks related to steel ramp bridges:
Temporary steel ramp bridges (approximately 6 crossings) will be installed where dredge
discharge pipelines intersect existing roads to maintain traffic flow. These crossings may cause
localized disruption, requiring vehicles to slow down and posing safety risks, particularly for
motorcycles and under low-visibility conditions if gradients and signage are inadequate.
Impact magnitude varies by location, with higher risks at busy sections (e.g., Road 865 in An
Phuoc and Truong Xuan communes, canal junctions, and access to residential areas or ferry
points), and minor impacts in low-traffic areas. Impacts are temporary and limited to the
construction period; traffic conditions will return to normal after pipeline removal. With
appropriate, site-specific traffic management measures, overall impact magnitude is considered
as Moderate.
f. Impacts on sensitive receptors
Sensitive receptors include water supply stations, schools, markets, religious facilities, riverside
residential areas, and the Go Thap heritage site, generally located 20–100 m from the canal,
with some households directly adjacent. Construction activities (dredging, embankment works,
and material transport) may increase turbidity, noise, dust, and traffic.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                  146
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Impact levels vary by source, pathway, distance, and receptor sensitivity (Table 6.11), with the
highest sensitivity observed for Hoa Binh Private Water Supply Plant and aquaculture raisers
and riverside communities due to their direct exposure to water quality and safety risks.

 Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant in Phu Cuong
 commune, Dong Thap province, abstracts raw
 water daily from the Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam
 canal. The plant has a design capacity of 5,000
 m3/day, serving approximately 8,000
 households across three communes: Phu
 Cuong, Tram Chim, and Tam Nong. Notably,
 the plant is equipped with a backup reservoir
 of 25,000 m3, providing a strategic storage        Hoa Binh Domestic Water Supply Plant
 capacity equivalent to 5 days of normal
 operation.

 Dredging activities in the vicinity of the plant’s intake pose several risks:
 - Water Quality Degradation: Increased Total Suspended Solids (TSS) during dredging can
   exceed the plant's filtration capacity, leading to higher operational costs (chemical usage)
   or potential membrane clogging.
 • Release of Acid Sulfate and Pollutants: Disturbance of canal bed sediments may release
   accumulated acidity (lowering pH) and heavy metals, which are critical parameters for
   domestic water safety.
 • Physical Risks: Risk of dredged mud or debris entering the pump intake, and potential
   oil/fuel leaks from dredging vessels.
 Impact Assessment and Mitigation:
 Given the plant’s daily extraction requirement, the raw water quality is highly sensitive to
 localized dredging activities. However, the existing 25,000 m3 backup reservoir serves as a
 critical climate and operational resilience factor. The overall pre-mitigation impact is
 assessed as Moderate.
 Consultation with the Plant Management Board (Mr. Huynh Cong Tran – Deputy Director)
 on 10March, 2026. Some recommendations are given as follows: A Coordinated Water
 Management Protocol will be implemented: 1. The Plant will be notified 48 hours prior to
 dredging within a 500m radius of the intake to fully charge the backup reservoir. 2. Selective
 Extraction: During the actual dredging period passing the intake, the Plant can temporarily
 suspend raw water abstraction and rely on the 5-day backup supply to bypass the peak
 turbidity plume. 3. Real-time Monitoring: Monitoring of pH and turbidity at the intake during
 dredging to determine the safe window for resuming daily extraction. With these measures,
 the residual impact is expected to be Moderate.



  Impacts on Aquaculture Ponds along Phuoc Xuyen Canal
  There are 55 aquaculture ponds located along the left bank (Tay Ninh province). The
  primary species cultured are hardy, air-breathing fish such as Snakehead fish (Channa
  striata) and Climbing perch (Anabas testudineus). Due to the high tolerance of these
  species, the water abstraction frequency is relatively low, typically occurring once every 1
  to 2 weeks.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 147
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

  Dredging may temporarily affect water quality through increased turbidity and TSS,
  particularly for ponds within ~500 m of dredging areas that rely on direct intake. Potential
  effects include”
       • Reduced water quality: Dredging may cause temporary spikes in turbidity, TSS,
       and a drop in pH due to the disturbance of acid sulfate sediments.
       • Fish Health: Although the species are hardy, sudden exposure to high sediment
       loads can cause gill clogging or secondary infections.
       • Physical Risk: Potential for dredged material or accidental oil spills to enter pond
       intakes during extraction.
  With sequential dredging (~50m/day), each location may be affected for approximately 10
  days if no mitigation measures are applied. Given that aquaculture water intake is seasonal
  (1–2 weeks/cycle) and water quality is expected to recover after dredging, impacts are
  localized, temporary, and manageable, with negligible effects beyond 500 m.
  As results of consultation meetings, local communities’ recommendations include: (i) Pre-
  construction Notification: Households must be notified 3–5 days before dredging starts in
  their area. (ii) Strategic Water Intake: Households can postpone their intake schedule to
  avoid the dredging window. (iii) Sectional Execution: Dredging will be implemented in
  segments to ensure that water quality at any given intake point recovers within a few days.
  Overall Assessment: With the existing low-frequency water intake pattern and proper
  notification, the residual impact is assessed as Moderate.
g. Disturbance, damages of structure and public services
Source of impact: dredging of Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals, and transport of
disposal materials and vibrations from pile driving in embankment construction for river bank
erosion protection.
When dredging small canals and waterways, some small or old bridges (namely Ben Chua, Cho
Bung, Lo Lang, Kenh Năng, DR.39) may need to be removed before being reconstructed later.
The disruption in bridge use increases travel time, causing longer transport durations and higher
costs for businesses and local residents. Some existing infrastructure such as power grids,
telecom cables, water supply and/or drainage pipes, etc., may also need temporary removal or
relocation. Disruption of electricity, telecommunications, and water supply can cause
inconvenience and even losses for some businesses.
Dredging of Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal may affect several sensitive points along the canal,
such as Truong Xuan fuel depot, My Hoa Holy Temple, some public service facilities, Thanh
Loi 1 Primary School, and Vinh Chau A Kindergarten. The impact magnitude is considered
Moderate.
Weak houses and structures will be affected by vibrations from pile driving using vibration
hammers. The scope of impacts depends on the intensity of driving, the distance to the vibrating
hammer, geological conditions thus should be identified in more detail during detail design,
and pile driving test during construction phase. The status of existing houses and structures
would be surveyed and recorded during such phases. Relocations (either temporary or
permanently) would be required where high safety risks is identified. This risk exists in piple
driving under all subprojects.
h. Socio-economic impacts
Dredging may affect canal-side communities, including ethnic minority households
(approximately 165 Khmer and 9 Hoa households along Phuoc Xuyen canal). These households
are dispersed and generally located several hundred meters to a few kilometers from the canal;

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                148
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

therefore, direct exposure to construction activities is limited and impacts are negligible.
Occasional worker–community conflicts may also arise during construction.




           Figure 6. 9: Houses along the banks of Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam canal
    Source: Survey photos by the environmental consulting team, taken in February 2025

i. Specific impacts and risks at the subproject dredging sites
Common incidents during dredging activities are primarily related to occupational safety and
waterway traffic, with risk levels influenced by the specific operational context of the
subproject.
- Occupational health and safety (OHS) risks: During dredging work, workers may be exposed
to risks such as falls from barges or floating platforms, electric shocks, and entanglement in
mooring lines or winches. These risks are heightened under the specific working conditions of
the subproject, including continuous or extended working hours (potentially 24-hour
operations), night-time activities, and prolonged exposure to hot and humid conditions typical
of the project area. Working on open water further increases vulnerability to slips, falls, and
fatigue-related incidents. As a result, occupational risks are site- and operation-specific, rather
than generic, and primarily affect on-site workers.
- Traffic accidents: Waterway traffic incidents may occur due to interactions between dredging
vessels and local boats navigating through the canal. Risks include collisions, capsizing of small
boats, and accidental fuel or oil spills. These risks are influenced by site-specific conditions
such as narrow canal sections, intersections, areas with higher boat traffic (including near
residential access points or small ferry landings), and limited traffic control during dredging
operations. Additional contributing factors include reduced visibility during night-time
operations, adverse weather conditions, and operator fatigue. Such incidents may lead to
localized water pollution (e.g. oil spills) and disturbance of bottom sediments. This impact is
assessed as Moderate.
- Increased canal embankment erosion risks due to disturbance of riverbed and embankment
structures from dredging, under the impacts of canal flow dynamics.
j. Impacts on group of households harvesting water hyacinth
Water hyacinth is an invasive aquatic species widely distributed along the project canals. In
recent years, local households have increasingly collected and utilized this biomass for
handicraft production, providing a supplementary source of income, particularly during periods
of low agricultural activity.
It is estimated that approximately 20–30 households are engaged in this activity, primarily
concentrated in Phu Cuong and Truong Xuan communes. Income derived from water hyacinth
accounts for about 5–10% of total household income, indicating that it serves as a secondary
livelihood source (interviews of some households engaging in water hyacinth in Phu Cuong
communes).


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 149
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Dredging activities will require the clearance of aquatic vegetation within the canal corridor,
which may temporarily reduce the availability of water hyacinth for harvesting. As a result,
these households may experience short-term disruptions to their livelihood activities. The
magnitude of impact is expected to vary depending on the extent of reliance on this activity and
the spatial overlap between dredging sections and harvesting areas.
However, these impacts are considered temporary and localized plus (i) water hyacinth
regenerates rapidly under natural conditions, (ii) dredging progresses sequentially along the
canal, allowing continued access to unaffected sections, and (iii) this activity represents only a
supplementary income source, and thus the impact magnitude is considered Moderate. To
minimize impacts of dredging activities on these households, advance notices (at least one
month prior to construction) to affected communities regarding the dredging schedule will
enable them to harvest available biomass in advance.
(B) Construction of erosion control embankment
a. Noise and vibration impact on residential receptors along the canal
Construction of five embankment sections will require relocation of 52 households (35 semi-
permanent, 17 permanent), as addressed in the RAP. Pile installation using vibratory hammer
and water jetting (barge-mounted) will generate continuous noise and vibration, affecting
nearby sensitive receptors, including ~20 households at 15–20 m (Thanh Loi), ~30 households
at 10–20 m (Truong Xuan), and ~100 households along My Hoa 1 (10–20 m on the right bank
and 35–50 m on the left bank).
At 10–20 m, noise may approach or exceed QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT limits (55 dBA daytime),
and vibration may be perceptible with minor cosmetic effects, though below structural damage
thresholds (DIN 4150). Beyond 20–50 m, both noise and vibration are expected to decrease to
acceptable levels. Overall, the impact magnitude is Moderate for receptors within 10 – 20 m of
source, and are temporary, localized, and manageable through standard mitigation measures
(e.g., daytime works, method optimization, and monitoring)

b. Increased canal bank erosions from embankment construction
The proposed design includes parallel embankments along both canal banks (e.g., My Hao 1)
and localized protection at intersections and bends (e.g., Thanh Loi, Truong Xuan), which will


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                150
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

influence flow dynamics and erosion patterns. Bank stabilization and confinement are expected
to reduce erosion and lateral moving; however, localized increases in flow velocity, particularly
during high discharge, may induce bed scouring if not adequately addressed.
At intersections and corner sections, changes in flow direction may generate turbulence and
concentrated hydraulic forces, increasing the risk of localized erosion at embankment toes and
adjacent unprotected areas. These risks are design-dependent and assessed as Moderate. With
appropriate measures (e.g., toe protection, energy dissipation, and smooth hydraulic transitions),
impacts can be effectively controlled, while the overall configuration provides long-term
benefits for canal stability.
c. Interruptions of community access to the canal (navigation and water use)
Field surveys and local reports (2025) confirm that the canals support irrigation, drainage, and
waterway transport, while domestic water is supplied via piped systems (no direct abstraction
from canals). The five proposed embankment sections are not located at ferry crossings or main
access points.
During construction, temporary obstructions (e.g., equipment, materials) may locally affect
navigation and access near work sites; however, impacts on transport, irrigation, and drainage
are expected to be minor, localized, and temporary. Accordingly, impacts are assessed as low
during construction.
(C) Impacts during reconstruction of 4 bridges
a. Traffic disruption and increased road safety risks
Traffic disruptions and safety risks may occur at four construction locations (Kenh 1 Bridge,
Hamlet 7 Kenh Nang Bridge, Canal 5 Bridge, and Bridge No.4), affecting local residents in
Phuoc Tan 3 and Long Hung communes. During construction, risks include vehicle–vehicle,
vehicle–worker, and vehicle–construction zone collisions, as well as pedestrian safety concerns.
The existing bridges across Cho Bung Canal are small, low-capacity rural structures and present
constraints to dredging operations. Interaction between construction vessels/barges and these
bridges may pose risks of collision and structural damage (piers, decks, abutments). Therefore,
impacts are temporary and localized, and overall, the impact magnitude is considered Moderate,
subject to implementation of appropriate traffic and construction safety measures.




  Kenh Nang Bridge           Canal 5 Bridge           Hamlet 7 Kenh            Bridge No.4
                                                       Nang Bridge

b. Community Health and Safety (CHS) and Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) Risks
Bridge construction will generate multiple sources of impact, including removal of existing
piers and canal bed clearance, operation of barge-mounted cranes near medium-voltage power
lines, pile driving for abutments and foundations, and transport of sand and stone from Ho Chi
Minh City, An Giang, and Dong Nai via both road and waterways. These activities will require
temporary closure of canal navigation and diversion of road traffic, thereby affecting key
receptors, including local communities and businesses—particularly in sensitive locations such
as Bridge No. 3 (dense residential area) and Bridge No. 4 (adjacent to a market and DT878)—
as well as approximately 49 project workers involved in construction operations.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                151
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

In terms of spatial extent, direct impacts will be confined to the construction footprint at the
four bridge sites, while indirect impacts will extend to detour routes and material transport
corridors. The frequency and duration of impact will vary, with continuous disruption to traffic
throughout the estimated 18-month construction period, and intermittent impacts associated
with specific activities such as pile driving and girder launching. The magnitude of impact is
considered as Substantial, primarily due to safety risks from crane operations near power lines
(potential electrical flashover), heavy equipment uses in confined spaces (collision and falling
object risks), and increased traffic density on diversion routes. These risks can be effectively
managed through strict safety controls and traffic management measures.
c. Degradation, Damages to Existing Structures and Infrastructure and Interruptions of
related public services
Construction activities including bored piling, pressing of reinforced concrete piles, removal of
old sheet piles/bridge piers, movement of heavy trucks (steel, sand, stone), and crane operations
for girder launching will generate significant mechanical loads and vibrations propagating
through soft soil foundations. These sources of impact may affect residential houses and
structures along the canal (notably near Bridge No. 3), canal-side access roads, medium-voltage
power lines, and buried infrastructure such as water supply pipelines and telecommunication
cables.
The spatial extent of direct impacts is expected within approximately 20–50 m from bridge
foundation works, where vibration may induce minor structural effects (e.g., wall cracking,
localized settlement) and affect road stability. Indirect impacts may extend along material
transport routes connecting highways to bridge sites. Peak impacts will occur during pile
driving/pressing and excavation near bridge footings, with frequency linked to construction
cycles and repeated heavy vehicle movements.
Overall, the magnitude of impact is considered as Moderate, reflecting risks of localized
structural damage to nearby houses, deterioration of rural roads, potential interaction with
power lines, and damage to underground utilities. These impacts are temporary and can be
managed through appropriate engineering controls and monitoring measures.
(3) Subproject 2.3: Completing the water control infrastructure cluster enhances climate
resilience and supports agricultural production transformation in the southwestern
Mekong River region.
a. Impacts and risks of equipment mobilization and construction material transport
Waterborne transport of construction materials and mobilization of heavy equipment for the
Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluices will involve operation of barges (200–800T), tugboats (150–
360 HP), motorboats (23–150 HP), cranes (6T–80T mobile; 10T–50T crawler; 30T floating),
piling equipment (vibratory and impact hammers, piling barges), and earthworks machinery. A
total of 115,748 tons of materials will be transported over the 24-month construction period,
equivalent to approximately 280 barge trips (~12 trips/month). These activities represent the
main sources of impact, generating noise, exhaust emissions, and risks of fuel/oil leakage.
Key receptors include aquatic ecosystems along the Saintard River (Dai Ngai) and Bai Xau
River (My Xuyen), riverside households, and inland waterway users (passenger boats, cargo
barges, and aquaculture/agriculture transport vessels). Given baseline traffic levels of 45–50
vessels/day on the Saintard River and 35–40 vessels/day on the Bai Xau River, the additional
barge movements will temporarily increase vessel density, potentially causing localized
navigation constraints, collision risks, and sediment disturbance leading to short-term turbidity.
Continuous operation of engines and onboard equipment also presents risks of oil spills and
pollution if not properly managed.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                152
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

These impacts will be intermittent over the 24-month construction period, with peaks during
mobilization and major material delivery phases. Overall impact magnitude varies by location:
at Dai Ngai (Saintard River), higher baseline traffic results in a moderate level of impact due to
increased navigation risks, while at My Xuyen (Bai Xau River), lower traffic and sufficient
channel capacity result in low to moderate impacts. All impacts are temporary and can be
effectively managed through standard environmental and traffic management measures.
Overall, the impact magnitude is considered Moderate.
b. Water Quality Reduction
Construction of the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluices will generate multiple sources of impact,
including in-river works (sheet pile driving, foundation excavation, dredging to –5.0 to –5.5 m),
domestic wastewater from workers, and wastewater and risks from concrete batching and heavy
equipment operation. These activities will affect aquatic ecosystems in the Saintard River
(inside the Dai Ngai Bridge) and Bay Xao River and nearby areas, including common species
(e.g., gobies, climbing perch, shrimp, snakes, and eels).
During the 24-month construction period, sediment disturbance will increase turbidity and
suspended solids (SS), potentially releasing accumulated pollutants from bottom sediments
(including organic clay and acid sulfate soils). Dewatering of cofferdams may discharge high-
silt water if not properly treated. Domestic wastewater is estimated at 4.98 m³/day (Dai Ngai,
99 workers) and 3.32 m³/day (My Xuyen, 66 workers), while construction wastewater from
equipment washing is approximately 19.92 m³/day (Dai Ngai) and 12.28 m³/day (My Xuyen).
On-site batching plants (20–60 m³/h) and fuel use also pose risks of high-pH wastewater and
oil/grease leakage. Stormwater runoff is estimated at 1.45 m³/s (Dai Ngai, 23.30 ha) and 4.24
m³/s (My Xuyen, 68.00 ha), with pollutant concentrations (0.5–1.5 mg N/L; 0.004–0.03 mg P/L;
10–20 mg COD/L; 10–20 mg TSS/L) generally below QCVN 40:2025/BNNMT limits.
In terms of spatial extent, turbidity plumes are typically highest within 50–100 m of
construction, decrease significantly beyond 200–300 m, and return to near-baseline conditions
at 300–500 m. The Dai Ngai sluice is located approximately 2.9 km from the Hau River, and
the My Xuyen sluice approximately 29 km away, limiting wider downstream impacts. Overall,
impacts are temporary and localized within the construction zone and adjacent waters, and with
proper sediment control, wastewater treatment, and spill prevention. Therefore, the impact
magnitude is considered as Moderate.
c. Increased Waterway Traffic and Safety Risks
Although the Project applies modular (soft) solutions to minimize the footprint, in-river
construction within steel sheet pile cofferdams, together with intensive use of barges, tugboats
(150 HP), and floating equipment, will narrow the navigation channel and create temporary
disruptions to waterway transport. Baseline traffic of approximately 45–50 vessel trips/day on
the Saintard River and 35–40 trips/day on the Bay Xao River, mainly motorboats, ~100 DWT
self-propelled vessels, and 250–500 DWT barges will be affected.
These sources of impact include cofferdam installation occupying 1/2 of the channel width,
creating bottleneck conditions and localized hydraulic changes (increased currents and vortices),
combined with higher vessel density from construction traffic and risks of submerged hazards
(e.g., anchor lines, falling materials). Affected receptors include commercial barges, local
motorboats, canal banks, and riverside households. Spatially, direct impacts occur within the
construction zone and extend approximately 500–1,000 m upstream/downstream; indirect
effects include congestion, increased travel time, and fuel costs. Impacts will persist throughout
the ~24-month construction period, with peak intensity during cofferdam installation/removal,
high-traffic periods, harvest seasons (Feb–Apr; Jul–Aug), and the dry season. Overall, the
impact magnitude is considered Substantial.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                153
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

d. Increased Bank/Riverbank Erosion Risks
The Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sites are underlain by weak soft clay (11 m to >30 m thick), and
in-river works using steel sheet pile cofferdams will narrow the channel, increase flow velocity,
and generate strong shear forces acting on the riverbed and natural banks. Excavation of
foundation pits (to –5.0 m to –5.5 m) and channel clearing alter slope equilibrium, while
continuous movement of barges (~ 100T), tugboats, and crane barges in confined sections
generates waves that further erode banks. In addition, pile pressing and Larsen sheet pile driving
transmit vibrations through soft soils, reducing shear strength and increasing susceptibility to
erosion and collapse. Rapid dewatering of cofferdams may also disturb hydrostatic balance,
inducing subsidence or slope failure.
These impacts affect along the Saintard and Bai Xau rivers, particularly riverbanks and nearby
households. These direct impacts are greatest opposite the cofferdam and can extend
approximately 200–500 m upstream and downstream, especially downstream where
accelerated flows and turbulence occur. Impacts will persist throughout the ~24-month
construction period, with frequency being continuous while cofferdams are in place and
intensified under semi-diurnal tidal conditions (flow reversal twice daily), as well as during
flood season (May–November), high tides, and strong winds.
At Dai Ngai, larger channel width and proximity (~2.9 km) to the Hau River amplify tidal
exchange and hydraulic forces, affecting approximately 6 households. At My Xuyen, although
the channel is smaller, the higher proportion of cross-section occupied by cofferdams and close
proximity to houses increase risks, affecting approximately 25 households. Without appropriate
stabilization and construction controls, there is a tangible risk of bank erosion, local collapse,
and damage to riverside properties. Overall, the impact magnitude is considered Substantial.
e. Biological Impacts and Risks
The Dai Ngai and the My Xuyen sluice construction involve intensive in-river works that,
although taking place in already modified agricultural and residential landscapes, still pose
localized risks to aquatic and riparian ecosystems. The main impacts arise from dredging and
excavation of riverbed sediments to form foundation pits, which can significantly increase
turbidity and suspended solids, as well as from cofferdam installation, backfilling, pile driving,
and underwater concrete works that directly alter or remove natural habitats. In addition,
continuous operation of heavy equipment generates underwater and airborne noise, while site
clearance for camps and storage areas results in the loss of riverside vegetation. There is also a
risk of water quality degradation from oil leaks, domestic wastewater, and improper waste
management.
These pressures may affect a range of benthic organisms (e.g., worms, mollusks, small
crustaceans), plankton communities, fish and shrimp species, and riparian vegetation such as
coconut and nipa palms. The most direct impacts are confined to construction footprints and
disposal areas, where habitats may be temporarily removed. However, indirect effects,
particularly from sediment plumes can extend roughly 500 to 1,500 m upstream and
downstream, depending on tidal and flow conditions, reducing light penetration and affecting
fish respiration. Noise from piling activities can travel over longer distances, potentially
disturbing fish behavior and causing them to avoid the area.
These impacts will persist throughout the approximately 24-month construction period, with
turbidity-related effects occurring more or less continuously while cofferdams are in place, and
noise and vibration occurring intermittently depending on construction activities. The most
sensitive period is likely at the onset of the rainy season, when many aquatic species are
breeding or migrating, and disturbances may interfere with these natural cycles.
- Loss of riparian habitat:


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                154
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

To establish construction campsites, material storage areas, temporary access roads, and
foundation pits, vegetation clearance along both banks is required.
The subproject will require permanent land acquisition of 132,420 m², resulting in clearance of
approximately 8,763 trees and associated riparian vegetation. Of this, 56,210 m² will be
acquired at the Dai Ngai sluice (including 15,760 m² of agricultural land) and 76,210 m² at the
My Xuyen sluice (including 28,570 m² of agricultural land).
Field surveys (February 2026) indicate that vegetation within the footprint is dominated by
common, salt-tolerant pioneer species such as Sonneratia caseolaris and Nypa fruticans, along
with invasive weeds, with no rare, endemic, or threatened species recorded (per Vietnam Red
Data Book, 2007 and IUCN Red List). Similarly, fauna is limited to common, adaptable species
typical of disturbed landscapes (e.g., frogs, skinks, and common bird species), with no critical
habitats, breeding sites, or species of conservation concern identified. Accordingly, while
vegetation clearance will result in localized habitat loss, the overall impact on biodiversity is
low, as affected species are common, mobile, and able to relocate to nearby similar habitats
- Alteration of riverbed habitat at construction sites:
Excavation of foundation sediments to depths of –5.0 m to –7.0 m will remove the natural
riverbed layer within the construction footprint, effectively replacing it with a temporary
environment of soil, rock, and concrete. This results in direct habitat loss for benthic
communities; low-mobility and sediment-dwelling species (e.g., polychaetes, bivalves) are
unlikely to escape and may experience near-total mortality within the active work zone. At the
same time, pile driving and Larsen vibratory hammering generate strong underwater acoustic
waves, causing fish to avoid the construction area within a radius of several hundred meters.
Hydrodynamic and water quality modeling indicates that increases in TSS and turbidity are
localized and short-lived. The turbidity plume is generally confined within approximately 500
m downstream of active works and dissipates within 2–4 hours after activities cease. Within
this mixing zone, reduced light penetration may temporarily limit phytoplankton productivity
and, consequently, affect food availability for lower trophic levels (e.g., small fish and shrimp).
However, given the limited spatial extent and duration, these impacts are Low, localized, and
reversible following completion of construction.
Available surveys and consultations confirm that the project area, located in estuarine zones
already influenced by long-term human and aquaculture activities does not support rare or
endangered species (per IUCN or Vietnam Red Data Book). Accordingly, although habitat loss
occurs within the construction footprint, surrounding ecosystems are expected to recover
naturally within 1–2 years after completion. Overall, the impact magnitude is assessed as
Moderate.
f. Impacts of noise and vibration on surrounding structures
Noise and vibration during sluice and embankment works are primarily generated by Larsen
sheet pile driving, bored pile drilling, and continuous operation of barges and dredgers. Pile-
driving hammers produce high-intensity impulsive noise, while vibration arises from activities
with strong mechanical impulses, including pile installation, soil compaction (rollers), and
movement of heavy trucks on rural roads. Additional noise sources include excavators, cranes,
drilling rigs, batching plants, and on-site generators.
These impacts may affect nearby receptors, including approximately 25 households at the My
Xuyen sluice and 6 households at the Dai Ngai sluice, particularly vulnerable structures such
as temporary houses and shallow-foundation buildings close to riverbanks, as well as Ba Chua
Xu Temple in the My Xuyen area. Given the soft, waterlogged soils typical of the Mekong
Delta, vibration can propagate effectively, with potential structural effects (e.g., wall cracking,
minor settlement) within 20–50 m of piling locations. Noise may exceed applicable standards

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 155
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

(QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT and WBG EHS Guidelines) within a radius of approximately 200–
500 m due to the open river environment.
Impacts will occur throughout the about 24-month construction period, with peak intensity
during the first 3–6 months when foundation works and cofferdam piling are undertaken.
Vibration is intermittent or cyclical (during piling and compaction), while noise is generally
continuous during working shifts. At Dai Ngai, most houses are located beyond 100 m (except
a small number closer to the site), whereas at some locations distances are only a few to tens of
meters, increasing the risk of structural effects. Overall, noise and vibration impacts are
considered as Moderate and require appropriate mitigation and monitoring measures during the
construction phase.
(4) Subproject 2.4: Nam Mang Thit Irrigation System
a. Water Quality Reduction
In-river works for the Cai Hop sluice, the saltwater prevention sluices (Cai Ca, Sa Rai, Muong
Khai Lon, Tra Ngoa), and in-field sluices (Bung Truong, Say Don) including sheet piling,
cofferdams, foundation excavation with grab dredgers and jet suction, and bed dredging
together with leachate from temporary dredged material disposal sites will impact on surface
water.
These activities affect to the Cai Hop River, Tra Ngoa Canal, Mang Thit River, Muong Khai
Canal, and Bung Truong Canal by disturbing bottom sediments, which increases TSS and
turbidity. Elevated turbidity reduces light penetration and limits photosynthesis, while
oil/grease leaks from equipment may further inhibit oxygen exchange. As a result, dissolved
oxygen (DO) may decrease locally; under low DO conditions, aquatic organisms may be
stressed or experience mortality, and decomposition processes can further deplete oxygen.
These impacts on water quality are expected to be localized and temporary but may be
significant during active works, particularly if sediment control and spill prevention measures
are not effectively implemented.
Regarding to assessment of Acid Sulfate Soil (ASS) risk, based on the assessment of foundation
excavation conditions, the likelihood of encountering acid sulfate soils (ASS) at depths of –5.0
m to –5.5 m is considered negligible. This conclusion is supported by sediment analysis
conducted in February 2026. Accordingly, the risk of pyrite oxidation and subsequent release
of acidic constituents (H⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, SO₄²⁻), which could lead to acidification of
surrounding water bodies during this phase of excavation, is not considered a valid risk for these
structures. Overall, the impact magnitude is considered Substantial.
b. Disturbance to waterway traffic and increased safety risks
Waterway transport is a primary mode of movement for local residents, agricultural
cooperatives, and commercial operators along the Co Chien River, Tra Ngoa Canal, and
connected waterways. Traffic surveys (February 2026) indicate moderate to high vessel density
(approximately 30–50 passages/day), comprising small wooden boats, self-propelled barges
(50–150 DWT), and larger barges (250–500 DWT). Traffic is seasonal, peaking during harvest
periods (April, August) and favorable tidal windows.
Under the proposed construction approach, impacts on road traffic are limited. Most sluice
works are confined to waterways, and at Cai Hop, the HL1 dike road (6 m wide, connecting to
TL907) will remain operational, as navigation locks are constructed using parallel cofferdams
without disrupting the existing road. Accordingly, road traffic impacts are minor, short-term,
and manageable.
By contrast, impacts on waterway traffic are more pronounced. Installation of steel sheet pile
cofferdams will narrow the channel by approximately 40–50%, leaving a restricted navigable


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                156
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

width of about 10–20 m. This creates localized increases in flow velocity and more difficult
navigation, particularly for larger barges (up to 500 DWT) under tidal or peak flow conditions.
At the same time, construction vessels (e.g., 200T barges, crane barges, tugboats) will increase
traffic density within these confined sections, elevating collision risks with passing vessels,
especially under low-visibility conditions. In conclusion, impacts on waterway traffic are
considered Moderate and require strict navigation control and safety management.
c. Impacts due to increased risk of subsidence, erosion, and sedimentation
Riverbank stability risks in the Nam Mang Thit sluice system are closely linked to local
geotechnical conditions, which are characterized by very soft, highly compressible clay layers
(approximately 11 m to >30 m thick, per FS, March 2026). Under these conditions, even
moderate disturbances can trigger instability. In-river construction activities such as installation
of sheet pile cofferdams, dredging, and foundation excavation to depths of –5.4 m to –6.9 m
temporarily narrow the channel and increase flow velocity. This, in turn, raises bed shear stress
and the likelihood of toe scour and bank erosion. At the same time, movements of heavy barges
(~ 200T), tugboats (150 CV), and crane barges generate continuous wake waves that further
weaken unprotected slopes along canals and riverbanks (e.g., Co Chien River, Tra Ngoa, Sa
Rai, Cai Ca, Muong Khai Lon).
Excavation of foundation pits in such weak soils introduces additional risks. Unsupported
slopes are prone to sliding or rotational failure, and rapid dewatering of cofferdams can alter
hydrostatic balance, potentially causing subsidence or lateral movement of adjacent ground,
especially during the rainy season and high-tide conditions when soils are already saturated.
These mechanisms are site-specific and largely driven by the soft soil profile rather than by the
construction method alone.
For components involving simple sluice gate replacement, no significant geotechnical risks are
expected. For new sluice construction, although the inherent soil conditions are weak, the works
are temporary and implemented in controlled phases. Overall, the impact magnitude is
considered Substantial.
d. Impacts on Biodiversity
Construction of the Cai Hop sluice, the saltwater control sluices (Cai Ca, Sa Rai, Muong Khai
Lon, Tra Ngoa), and the in-field sluices (Bung Truong, Say Don) will involve extensive in-
channel works, including sheet piling, cofferdam installation, foundation excavation, and
dredging. These activities, together with concrete mixing and the operation of heavy equipment,
introduce several pathways for water quality degradation. In practice, the main concern is not a
single source, but the combination of different waste streams generated on site—construction
wastewater from dewatering and batching operations (typically high in suspended solids and
alkalinity), domestic wastewater from worker camps, stormwater runoff carrying sediments
from exposed surfaces and stockpiles, and the risk of oil, grease, and fuel leaks from machinery
and vessels operating on the waterways.
These pressures act directly on nearby rivers and canals, by extension, on the aquatic
communities they support. During active construction, sediment disturbance can increase
turbidity and suspended solids, while even small amounts of oil or grease can interfere with
oxygen exchange at the water surface. In combination, these processes may lead to localized
reductions in dissolved oxygen and short-term stress on aquatic organisms, including plankton
communities (cyanobacteria, diatoms, zooplankton) and common fish species through
disruption of the food chain. That said, these effects tend to be intermittent and confined to
active work areas, and the system’s natural dilution and recovery capacity is generally sufficient
to limit impacts to a low level where basic controls are in place.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 157
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Worker-generated wastewater adds a further, more continuous pressure. With an estimated
volume of around 4 m³/day per sluice, untreated discharge could introduce organic matter,
nutrients, and pathogens into receiving waters, causing localized pollution if not properly
managed. Similarly, wastewater from on-site concrete works, often highly alkaline needs to be
controlled to avoid pH shocks. The scale of equipment mobilization (including barges of 250–
400 tons, tugboats, cranes, pumps, and generators) also means that spill risks are real rather
than theoretical, particularly during periods of heavy rainfall when runoff can carry
contaminants directly into canals.
On land, the footprint of construction is relatively modest and largely confined to already
modified environments. Vegetation along canal banks is dominated by common species such
as nipa palm, coconut, shrubs, and scattered melaleuca, with surrounding land use primarily
agricultural (rice and fruit crops within 200–500 m). Faunal assemblages are similarly typical
of disturbed landscapes such as frogs, small reptiles, rodents, water snakes, and common birds,
with no evidence from field surveys of sensitive habitats or species of conservation concern.
As a result, impacts on biodiversity are low, short-term, generally within the 12–24 months and
manageable with standard good practice. Overall, the impact magnitude is considered Moderate.
e. Impacts on production and daily life of residents around the construction area
(i) Impacts on agricultural production
Field surveys conducted in February 2026 indicate that the subproject’s area of influence
includes approximately 5,210 ha of annual cropland and 5,473.0 ha of perennial cropland,
supporting the livelihoods of about 309,494 households. Agricultural production in the area is
closely linked to the local hydrological regime. Seasonal rice cultivation follows two main
cropping cycles including Winter–Spring (February–April) and Summer–Autumn (July–
August) with high water demand during land preparation and flowering stages. In parallel,
perennial orchards and aquaculture systems (fish and shrimp ponds) operate year-round and
rely on stable water exchange and salinity control.
