For new sluice construction, a phased partial-blockage cofferdam approach will be applied.
This method is designed to limit flow disruption, maintain navigation, and ensure continued
water supply for agricultural use. Hydraulic assessments indicate that, although flow is locally
constricted during construction, residual discharge remains sufficient to meet irrigation needs
across the 5,210 ha of annual crops and 5,473.0 ha of perennial crops. In addition, during
periods of saline intrusion, the cofferdams may provide a temporary protective function by
limiting saline water movement upstream. For works involving upgrading or replacement of
existing sluice gates, construction will be scheduled during open-gate periods to avoid
interference with current water supply functions. On this basis, impacts on agricultural
production are expected to be limited, localized, and temporary, and are therefore the impact
magnitude is considered Low.
(ii) Impacts on Domestic Water Supply (Compliance with ESS4)
The project area relies primarily on treated surface water for domestic use, and a key sensitive
receptor has been identified at the Say Don sluice: a water supply intake station located
approximately 250 m far from the construction site, extracting raw water directly from the canal.
Given this proximity, construction activities, particularly dredging, foundation excavation, and
cofferdam installation pose a risk of increased TSS and turbidity at the intake. Local dispersion
analysis indicates that sediment plumes generated during active works could reach the
abstraction point, potentially affecting raw water quality.
To manage this risk, specific and enforceable mitigation measures are required within the 250
m zone. High-disturbance activities (e.g., dredging, pile driving) must be scheduled outside the
intake operation hours through close coordination with the water supply operator. In parallel,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                158
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

physical containment measures such as properly designed silt curtains with sufficient depth
shall be installed around active work areas to limit sediment moving toward the intake. In the
event that turbidity levels exceed applicable thresholds (QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT) or supply is
disrupted, the Contractor will be required to provide a temporary alternative water supply (e.g.,
tanker delivery or temporary upstream abstraction) at no cost to affected users. The impact
magnitude is considered Moderate.
f. Impacts on Cultural, Religious, and Belief Sites during Construction
Some religious and belief structures within the Subproject 2.4 area include: (i) Xuan Hiep
Church, approximately 150 m from the Cai Ca sluice; (ii) Hieu Nhon Parish Church, located
adjacent to the Say Don sluice, about 40 m away. These are places of worship for the Christian
community. Residents typically attend services and pray daily from 5:00–6:00 a.m. and 7:00–
8:00 p.m., on Sundays, and on major Christian holidays, especially Christmas. The number of
worshippers is not large and is spread across different times of the day, mainly in the evenings
and on weekends. Construction activities may disrupt spiritual and religious practices and cause
traffic congestion during major religious holidays. The impact magnitude is considered Low
and can be mitigated. Scheduling of construction activities should be coordinated with church
religious activities.
6.3.3. Impacts and Risks during the Operation Phase
6.3.3.1. Subproject 2.1
a. Alteration of coastal sediment transport dynamics
To assess the impacts of flow regime changes and sediment transport, the Project conducted
hydraulic modelling under multiple scenarios. The results of the hydraulic model indicate that:
Under the baseline condition (without the project), severe erosion occurs at most locations along
the shoreline, with erosion rates ranging from 0.2 to 0.4 m/year and an average value that may
reach up to 0.5 m/year. The volume of sediment loss on the landward side of the dyke is
estimated at 13,684.9 m³/year.
After the construction and operation of the project, erosion on the landward side of the dyke is
eliminated and is replaced by a clear accretion trend, with deposition thickness ranging from
0.1 to 0.4 m/year. The total sediment deposition at Thoi Thuan and Tan Thuy on the landward
side of the dyke reaches 208,625.3 m³/year, while the seaward side records 60,712.7 m³/year.
For Dong Hai and Truong Long Hoa, sediment accumulation reaches 89,534.4 m³ on the
landward side and 22,752.6 m³ on the seaward side.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                159
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    Figure 6. 10: Morphological Evolution Between Baseline and Post-Construction
  Conditions Under a 1-Year Climatic Scenario at the Thoi Thuan Commune and Tan
                           Thuy Commune Coastal Dyke




 Figure 6. 11: Morphological Evolution Between Baseline and Post-Construction
  Conditions Under a 1-Year Climatic Scenario at the Dong Hai Commune and
                    Truong Long Hoa Ward Coastal Dyke
Based on the results of the morphological distribution obtained from the numerical model
(Figures x and y), it can be confirmed that the wave-reduction structures under Subproject 2.1
do not increase the risk of erosion in adjacent areas. The structures not only create favourable
accretion zones on the landward side but also contribute to sediment retention and enhanced
deposition both within and outside the dyke system. The combination of the area’s natural
topographic and geomorphological characteristics and the design of the permeable (gapped)
embankment system helps maintain good stability under the influence of coastal currents. It
does not generate adverse, cascading erosion impacts in the surrounding areas.
In Subproject 2.1, the alignment of the works was carefully selected to ensure continuity,
smoothness, and connection with previously constructed or soon-to-be-constructed structures
using other funding sources to create a closed system. The dike alignment includes wave-
reduction sections placed parallel to the shore and combined with groynes to trap sand and
control nearshore currents. The project area is positioned directly facing the main wave
direction, with gaps between wave-reduction dikes set at 10 m. Additionally, offshore groynes
100 m long are applied as a measure to stabilize the beach. Wave-breaking structures face harsh
environmental conditions such as high waves, strong wind-driven sea levels, strong currents,
and seawater corrosion. Therefore, they must be properly designed and constructed to prevent
damage or corrosion that could lead to incidents such as erosion, subsidence, or cracking.
These wave-reduction structures are small in scale, and most are designed and positioned to
integrate with the local existing dike system, so changes in beach morphology are considered
negligible. The impact magnitude is considered Low.
b. Impacts on water quality
Breakwaters may negatively affect water quality due to reduced water exchange in the protected
area, causing some water stagnation, oxygen depletion during the summer, and a potential
decline in water quality. Nevertheless, it should be noted that the proposed wave-reduction
works under Subproject 2.1 are small in scale; the dike sections are not solid, allowing gaps for
vessels to pass. Moreover, there is almost no dredging or excavation involved, and all works
are located far from the shoreline. Under these conditions, the impact magnitude is considered
Low.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                160
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

c. Impacts on Biodiversity
Positive impacts
The offshore wave-reduction dikes create calm water areas inside, which trap sediment and
promote beach accretion. The resulting deposition area (up to 100 ha in the project area)
provides ideal conditions for mangrove regeneration, protecting and increasing the area of
protective forests within the project zone. Additionally, restored mangroves provide large
amounts of organic detritus as direct and indirect food for fish species, while also creating
“nursery” and safe spawning grounds for most benthic organisms and fish. This helps increase
the richness of the coastal ecosystem, which is currently threatened by erosion.
Negative impacts:
The presence of physical dike structures may alter flow patterns, thereby affecting the dispersal
of larvae (the area currently has high densities of crustacean and mollusk larvae). However, the
wave attenuation of dike alignment does not constitute a continuous or fully enclosed physical
barrier. The design incorporates segmented wave attenuation dikes with gaps ranging from 10
m to 20 m. These openings, in combination with the relatively small scale of the structures and
their offshore placement, ensure that water, sediments, and planktonic organisms can continue
to circulate freely. The dispersal of crustacean larvae as well as the movement of nearshore fish
and shrimp can therefore continue normally through these gaps without obstruction. Therefore,
the impact magnitude is considered Low..
d. Impacts on coastal activities and local businesses
Once the wave-reduction structures are completed, the coastal agricultural land will be
protected, ensuring the continuation of production and cultivation for local households,
especially those in the coastal areas of Tan Thuy and Dong Hai communes.
However, it may make vessels take longer routes around them to reach different desired
locations. This increases transportation costs and fishing activities. The design of the
embankment includes gaps of 10–20 m to allow vessels to pass, and waterway traffic in the
project area is low, so the impact on vessel movement is considered Low.
e. Visual/landscape impacts along coastal zones
The wave-reduction embankments in Tan Thuy and Truong Long Hoa communes are very close
to Con Nhan recreational area and Ba Dong beach. The embankments cause negative
disturbances, such as reduced coastal landscape quality and beach usability. The protruding
embankment blocks make the seascape appear less natural. The structures may also hinder
swimming and recreational activities because strong currents and whirlpools are generated in
the gaps between embankment blocks, increasing the risk of drowning. However, there are
currently no tourism activities along these embankments. Therefore, the impact on the
aesthetics of coastal tourism and recreation is considered as Low.
6.3.3.2. Subproject 2.2
Upon completion of canal dredging, embankment construction, and installation of sluice gates
and pumping stations, the operational phase of Subproject 2.2 is expected to generate
predominantly positive long-term outcomes for flood resilience, navigation, and aquatic
ecosystem recovery across the Dong Thap Muoi region. Residual negative impacts associated
with altered hydrodynamics, sluice gate operation, and disposal site management are of limited
magnitude and fully manageable through standard operating procedures and engineered
maintenance measures.
a. Flood Mitigation and Drainage Capacity — Substantial Positive



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                161
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Extraction of approximately 2.5 million m³ of sediment expands canal cross-sections and
deepens the bed by an average of 1 m along the Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam and Cho Bung
alignments, significantly increasing water conveyance capacity. The integrated operation of
protective embankments and sluice gate systems stabilizes flow, accelerates flood discharge
during the wet season, and reduces the frequency and duration of localized inundation events
across the Dong Thap Muoi region. Coordinated embankment and sluice gate operation also
minimizes turbulent vortex formation at canal intersections, reducing bank erosion risk at key
junctions. This is assessed as a substantial, long-term, large-scale positive impact — the primary
intended outcome of the subproject and directly aligned with the project's climate resilience
objectives.
b. Inland Waterway Navigation and Safety — Substantial Positive
Restoration of design canal depth enables safe navigation by vessels of up to 1,000 tons,
substantially improving freight capacity and navigation safety on the Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam
corridor. Stable, protected embankments prevent re-sedimentation in the short to medium term
and reduce the risk of bank collapse that currently threatens channel geometry and vessel safety.
The improvement in navigation conditions will generate positive economic impacts for
agricultural commodity transport, supply chain efficiency, and regional connectivity —
particularly for communities in the Dong Thap Muoi interior that are primarily canal-dependent
for the movement of goods.
c. Changes in Flow Hydrodynamics and Localized Erosion and Siltation — Moderate
The construction of continuous embankments on both canal banks will narrow the effective
drainage cross-section relative to the natural channel geometry, producing localized increases
in flow velocity during peak wet season discharge conditions. Elevated velocity at embankment
transition points — particularly at corners, sluice gate outlets, and junctions between embanked
and unembanked sections — may generate new erosive vortices, presenting a risk of scour at
structural toe zones and accelerated bank retreat at unprotected downstream reaches. Sediment
transport dynamics will also be altered, with the potential for localized deposition in sections
where velocity drops below the sediment transport threshold, requiring periodic maintenance
dredging to preserve design channel capacity. These risks are well-understood in embankment
canal engineering and are fully manageable through standard technical design measures
including toe protection, riprap armoring at transition zones, and energy dissipation structures
at sluice gate outlets.
d. Sluice Gate and Pumping Station Operations — Low
Operational management of sluice gates for irrigation and drainage will alter internal
hydrological regimes within the managed canal network, requiring close coordination with
local agricultural water use calendars. Poorly timed gate operations risk either water shortages
during dry season cropping periods or localized waterlogging if drainage is impeded during wet
season events. These risks are operational rather than structural, and are manageable through a
formal Water Management Operating Protocol developed in coordination with provincial
irrigation authorities and local agricultural extension services. Noise and vibration from pump
motor operation will be periodic and localized, affecting only immediately adjacent residents;
standard acoustic screening and maintenance scheduling to avoid night-time operation will
address this impact adequately.
e. Aquatic Ecosystem and Fisheries Recovery — Substantial Positive
The operational phase is expected to deliver measurable and sustained improvement in aquatic
habitat conditions relative to the pre-project baseline of siltation and organic enrichment.
Restored channel hydraulics will enhance the canal system's self-purification capacity, increase
dissolved oxygen levels, and improve conditions for fish and other aquatic organisms. Benthic
community recovery — including recolonization by worms, mollusks, and other invertebrates


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                162
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

— is expected within approximately one year of dredging completion, consistent with recovery
timelines observed in comparable Mekong Delta canal rehabilitation projects. The overall
trajectory of the operational aquatic environment is assessed as positive and long-term.
f. Post-Construction Disposal Site Management — Moderate
Upon project completion, all 30 disposal sites (totaling approximately 112.6 ha) will be
transferred to local authorities for long-term management and reuse. Identified reuse options
include public infrastructure development (bus stations, commercial centers in An Phuoc
commune), concentrated residential areas in Thap Muoi commune, and strategic soil banks for
future land-levelling initiatives. However, given that the average thickness of deposited dredged
material across the sites is estimated at 2.2–2.3 m (ranging from 1.5 m to 5.0 m), comprehensive
settlement monitoring is required before any construction activity is permitted at these locations.
Furthermore, given the project's location within an active acid sulfate soil zone, residual acidic
leachate generation and soil pH recovery at disposal sites must be rigorously monitored and
confirmed as within acceptable thresholds before final reuse designation is approved by the
PMU and relevant local authorities.
Operational phase impacts are predominantly positive and long-term, representing the
realization of the subproject's core climate resilience and regional development objectives.
Residual negative impacts — localized hydrodynamic changes, pump noise, and disposal site
residual risks — are of Moderate to Low significance and are fully controllable through
engineered design measures, formal operating protocols, and post-construction monitoring. The
principal management obligations of the operational phase are the sustained implementation of
a Water Management Operating Protocol in coordination with provincial agricultural
authorities, and a Disposal Site Residual Risk Monitoring Programme covering settlement, pH,
and leachate quality prior to any reuse authorization.
6.3.3.3. Subproject 2.3
a. Environmental impacts and risks
Impacts on water quality are the most critical environmental concern for sluices (combined with
lock chambers) throughout their operational period. The scientific basis for pollution control
and sluice operation in river sections regulated by sluices has been demonstrated in published
research studies8, including:
-    Inflow discharge, COD and DO concentrations from upstream, and the sluice gate opening
     size have a negative effect, while upstream turbidity has a positive effect on the rate of
     change in COD concentration.
-    Sluice gate opening size, NH3-N concentration, and upstream water depth have a negative
     effect, while inflow discharge and upstream turbidity have a positive effect on the rate of
     change in NH3-N concentration.
Beyond these factors, it is known that most adverse environmental impacts of an operating
sluice are caused by gate closure. Research has shown that when a sluice is closed, total nitrogen
and phosphorus accumulate upstream, leading to poorer water quality upstream than




8 Dou, Ming; Zhang, Yan; Zuo, Qiting and Mi, Qingbin (2015). Identification of key factors affecting water quality
concentration in the sluice-controlled river reaches of Shaying River in China via statistical analysis methods.
Environmental Science: Processes & Impacts - EM-ART-03-2015-000130.R1

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                        163
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

downstream9. As a result, sluice operation can increase water pollution and the potential for
eutrophication in upstream sections.
Because river and canal flows are frequently blocked by sluices, especially during the dry
season, the water inside the sluice loses its natural tidal fluctuations and daily or biweekly ebb
and flow. With the sluice closed, the river remains full of stagnant water, either not flowing or
flowing very slowly. This causes high accumulation of pollutants. Waste from households,
pesticides, and fertilizers build up densely due to the lack of flushing from seawater.
Consequently, dissolved oxygen (DO) levels in the water are very low, reducing the self-
purification capacity of the water. Contaminated surface water forces residents to switch to
groundwater for domestic use, accelerating land subsidence at a rate much higher than sea-level
rise. Over time, this damages infrastructure and increases the risk of salinization and
groundwater contamination.
The operation regime, particularly the duration of gate closure and opening, is the most
important factor affecting water quality. The longer the gates remain closed (to store freshwater
or control tides and salinity), the higher the level of water pollution upstream.
The impact magnitude is considered as Low.
b. Impacts on Ecosystems and Biodiversity
Survey results and analysis of aquatic samples indicate that the existing ecosystem is in a
degraded condition, showing signs of organic pollution. The area does not host any endemic,
rare, or endangered species listed in the Vietnam Red Data Book or the IUCN Red List, and no
migratory species have been recorded. Some commonly observed aquatic species in the area
include typical fish and shrimp such as snakehead fish, catfish, tilapia, and greasyback shrimp.
Based on the scale of construction of the combined sluice gate and navigation lock, as well as
the gate operation procedures (opening/closing), the impacts on biodiversity and ecosystems
may vary. The operation of the sluice gates may follow seasonal patterns or may change over
time under abnormal conditions or due to the impacts of climate change.
The design of the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluice gate, combined with navigation locks, allows
for the movement of vessels through the lock chamber, which in turn also provides
opportunities for common fish and shrimp species to pass through. When a vessel passes
through the lock, the chamber must be filled or drained to equalize the water level with the
receiving side. This difference in water level generates a flow, and fish and shrimp, which tend
to swim against currents or follow water flow, are attracted into the lock chamber. As the lock
gates are opened to allow vessels to enter or exit slowly, fish and shrimp may also take
advantage of this opportunity to move through the lock to the opposite side.
Flexible operation of sluice gate opening and closing: During safe periods (appropriate salinity
levels and no risk of flooding), the gates will be opened. Fish and shrimp will take advantage
of the natural water flow through the sluice compartments.
When the gates are closed, a large number of suspended sediments and organic matter will settle
to the bottom (particularly in the upstream area), burying the existing benthic communities.
However, since the current benthic community is already very poor (only 4–7 species tolerant
to salinity fluctuations and pollution), the resulting loss in terms of biodiversity is not significant.




9 Feng Lan, Wang Haisen and Yan Yan (2022). Spatial–Temporal Variations of Water Quality in Urban Rivers

after Small Sluices Construction: A Case in Typical Regions of the Taihu Lake Basin. Int. J. Environ. Res. Public
Health 2022, 19, 12453. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19191245


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                        164
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

In coastal and estuarine areas due to the interaction between saltwater and freshwater columns.
The maximum difference between the highest and lowest salinity levels is an important factor,
as this range must correspond to the osmoregulatory capacity of the species.
At the brackish–freshwater interface, there is a zone of maximum turbidity at the river mouth.
This is a zone of high chemical activity caused by two processes: the upstream movement of
the salt wedge during rising tides, which stirs up sediments, and the coagulation of suspended
particles in freshwater when it meets brackish water. Reduced water clarity affects primary
production, benthic biomass, and bird species that rely on sight for foraging. Lower clarity
reduces phytoplankton productivity, which can decrease biomass at the river mouth, making it
more difficult for visually hunting birds to find fish10.
Additionally, in rivers blocked by sluices, there may be a shift in species composition from
flow-adapted aquatic ecosystems to species adapted to standing water ecosystems.
Additionally, the closure of the sluice gates poses a risk of proliferating invasive alien species,
particularly water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes), in the upstream area. This species is highly
tolerant to stagnant and oxygen-depleted water environments. The extensive growth of water
hyacinth will cover the water surface, obstructing waterway navigation, blocking sunlight
penetration (thereby preventing the photosynthesis of submerged flora), and increasing the
anaerobic conditions of the bottom layer. According to the ESS6 classification, the project area
is classified as a Modified Habitat and does not host any endangered species. Therefore, impact
magnitude is considered as Low.
c. Impacts on Inland Waterway Transport
Operating Regime of Dai Ngai and My Xuyen Sluice-Locks: The operation of the sluice-locks
will be strictly governed by seasonal hydrological conditions and real-time monitoring data to
serve the overall needs of the river basin. The general operating principles are:
Dry Season Operation: The primary function is to prevent salinity intrusion and store fresh
water for domestic and agricultural use. The main sluice gates will be closed during periods of
high salinity or required freshwater storage. During this time, all waterway traffic must be
routed through the lock chambers.
Rainy Season/Flood Operation: The system operates to alleviate waterlogging, flush
alum/acidity out to the West Sea, and drain floodwaters. Sluice gates will be opened flexibly,
taking advantage of low tides to maximize discharge efficiency. Waterway traffic can navigate
through the main sluice gates when fully opened, subject to velocity controls
Based on the operational regime defined, the impacts on waterway navigation are assessed
under two primary scenarios: when the sluice gates are closed (Dry Season) and when they are
open (Rainy Season).
Increased travel time during Dry Season operations: As per the operating regime, during periods
of high salinity or freshwater storage, the main sluice gates will be fully closed. Navigation on
the Saintard and Bai Xau rivers will be temporarily restricted from free-flowing passage and
redirected through the lock chambers. This transition requires vessels to wait for lock gate
openings, undergo water level balancing within the chamber, and adhere to signal controls.
While this inherently increases travel time compared to unobstructed river navigation, it is a
planned operational delay rather than a disruption
Ensuring navigation safety through flow velocity control (Rainy Season operations): When the
sluice gates are opened for drainage, flood discharge, or alum flushing during the rainy season,

10 Daan Rijk Gerritse van Wieringen (2019). The impact of sluice management on biodiversity and ecosystem

services in the Haringvliet. MSc Thesis in Environmental Sciences. Wageningen University and Research


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                     165
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

the structural frames and piles naturally reduce the river’s wet cross-section. However, the
hydraulic design of the water passage strictly limits the maximum flow velocity through the
sluices to 1.5 m/s. This controlled velocity serves as a critical safety parameter, ensuring that
vessels passing through the opened main gates will not be destabilized by excessive currents or
face severe collision risks.
Capacity to handle vessel loads and maintain logistics chains: Both the Saintard and Bai Xau
Rivers are classified as Class III national inland waterways. To ensure that the seasonal closure
of the sluice gates does not sever the logistics supply chain, the lock systems have been
dimensioned at a considerable scale (15 m usable width and up to 100 m usable length). These
dimensions are fully capable of accommodating the diverse vessel fleets typical of Class III
waterways, including large self-propelled barges and pushed barge combinations. Furthermore,
the integration of waiting docks, collision bumpers, and visual/audio signaling systems at both
ends of the locks ensures that navigation remains safe and orderly, even during peak lock usage.
Overall, the impact magnitude is considered as Low
d. Impacts on Water Access
Under dry season flow conditions with a calculated frequency of 85%, salinity levels on the
Saintard canal remain above 2g/l for almost the entire season. This results in prolonged salinity
retention within the Saintard River, creating significant difficulties for water intake in
agricultural production areas. Salinity intrudes from two directions: from the Hau River through
the Dai Ngai gateway and from the lower My Xuyen area into the Saintard canal, passing
through the Maspero canal and reaching the Soc Trang - Phung Hiep canal.
For extreme years or typical natural disasters such as those in 2016, both historical events and
calculation results show that salinity concentrations on the Saintard canal exceed 2g/l for almost
the entire season. The primary direction of salinity intrusion is from the Hau River. Salinity
levels reaching the Maspero River have peaked at > 4g/l at times and have intruded as far as
the Soc Trang - Phung Hiep canal. With salinity levels > 4g/l persisting throughout nearly the
entire dry season, the Long Phu - Tiep Nhat project area would be completely isolated. The lack
of water supply for production would lead to significant agricultural losses, and if the region
transitions to fruit tree cultivation, the impacts would be extremely severe.
Considering the climate change period of 2050, sea levels will rise higher, significantly
impacting salinity intrusion in coastal areas. According to calculation results, salinity
concentrations on the Saintard River will exceed 2g/l for almost the entire dry season, creating
difficulties in sourcing fresh water for the Long Phu – Tiep Nhat area.
The level of salinity intrusion from the Hau River and My Thanh River into the Soc Trang City
area is quite high; salinity concentrations on the Soc Trang – Phung Hiep canal can reach 4g/l
and remain stagnant throughout the dry season.
During neap tide periods, combined with the volume of fresh water from the Hau River flowing
through the Saintard canal down to the lower Co Co River, salinity levels fluctuate between 3-
4g/l, which does not ensure adequate saltwater intake for shrimp farming in this area. When the
Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluices are closed, the reduction in water flow from the Hau River
causes salinity levels on the Co Co River to increase to over 7g/l, thereby improving the water
intake process for aquaculture.
Based on the calculation results after operating the closure of the My Xuyen sluice, salinity in
the shrimp farming area increases significantly, covering an area of up to more than 9,815
hectares. Thus, in accordance with the Mekong Delta regional planning, if this area transitions
to aquaculture in the future, salinity levels will be guaranteed to meet the water requirements
for shrimp farming when the My Xuyen sluice is operated to control the freshwater source from
the Hau River into this area.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                166
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)




   Figure 6. 12: Areas affected when operating the My Xuyen sluice to control water
                                             sources
Based on the hydraulic model results, the impact of operating the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen
sluice-locks on water access is analyzed as follows:
The hydraulic model indicates that the project area is currently facing a water access crisis in
both ecological zones:
For agricultural production areas (freshwater): Saltwater intrudes from the Hau River through
the Dai Ngai gateway and the lower My Xuyen area into the Saintard canal. Under normal dry
season conditions (85% frequency), salinity almost always exceeds 2g/l, causing significant
difficulties for freshwater intake in the Long Phu - Tiep Nhat area. Especially in extreme years
like 2016, salinity levels > 4g/l persisted throughout nearly the entire dry season, completely
isolating the project area, cutting off freshwater access, and threatening serious damage to
production, particularly fruit trees. This risk will become even more severe by 2050 due to sea-
level rise.
For aquaculture areas (saltwater/brackish): Conversely, during neap tide periods, massive
volumes of freshwater from the Hau River flow down and dilute the water source in the Co Co
River, causing salinity to fluctuate only between 3-4g/l. This level is too low to meet the salinity
requirements for shrimp farming intake for local residents.
When the two sluices, Dai Ngai and My Xuyen, are operated to close and store water during
the dry season, it will create an effective water storage area in the canal for the project region.
Calculation results show that when operating the sluices along the Saintard canal of the Long
Phu – Tiep Nhat area to open and take in water for agricultural production, the water level in
the Saintard canal decreases by approximately -0.4m. This is negligible compared to the lowest
water level at 85% frequency at Dai Ngai (approximately -1.91m), still ensuring safety for
agricultural water intake in the Long Phu – Tiep Nhat region. Thus, the Long Phu – Tiep Nhat
area will be more proactive in opening sluices to take water from the Saintard canal to serve
internal agricultural production.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 167
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Can Tho City is a coastal-adjacent area and is heavily influenced by tides. Hydraulic calculation
results show that at a 10% frequency, the water level on the Maspero River exceeds 1.4m,
causing a total flooded area of over 7,603 ha across Soc Trang, Phu Loi, and My Xuyen wards.
Specifically, Soc Trang and Phu Loi wards face about 6,768 ha of flooding, while My Xuyen
ward faces approximately 83.6 ha.
When the two sluices, Dai Ngai and My Xuyen, are operated to close during rising tides and
open during falling tides, the water level on the Maspero River is reduced by about 20-25cm
compared to pre-operation levels. The flooded area in Soc Trang, Phu Loi, and My Xuyen wards
decreases significantly by more than 1,043 ha. Specifically, Soc Trang and Phu Loi see a
reduction of about 887 ha, and My Xuyen sees a reduction of about 156 ha.
The commissioning of the sluice-lock system creates positive and proactive changes to the
hydrological regime, resolving water resource conflicts: Improved saltwater access for
aquaculture: When the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen sluices are closed, the freshwater volume from
the Hau River is controlled and reduced. Consequently, salinity levels on the Co Co River
recover and increase to over 7g/l, creating ideal conditions for water intake for aquaculture.
Large scope and scale of benefits: The model shows that operating the My Xuyen sluice alone
significantly increases safe salinity levels for a vast shrimp farming area of over 9,815 ha.
Calculation results indicate that the flooded area in Soc Trang, Phu Loi, and My Xuyen wards
is reduced to only about 4,788 ha. Additionally, the sluice-lock system can perform one-way
transport to create a unidirectional flow out to the Hau River if pollution occurs on the Saintard
route (closing My Xuyen sluice, opening Dai Ngai sluice) to avoid affecting the My Xuyen
shrimp farming area. One-way sluice operation can also create a unidirectional flow out to the
Co Co River if pollution occurs in the My Xuyen shrimp farming area downstream of the My
Xuyen sluice (closing Dai Ngai sluice, opening My Xuyen sluice).
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
e. Water Use Conflict Risks
The operation of the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen lock sluice complex brings significant benefits
in water resource management, but it also generates certain risks of conflicting water use among
different user groups and ecological subzones.
- Conflict between crop cultivation areas and aquaculture areas: In the Long Phu – Tiep Nhat
area, there is a concentration of specialty fruit orchards and rice cultivation that require fresh
water. In the My Xuyen area, industrial shrimp farming is developed, requiring brackish water
(salinity usually maintained above 7 g/l) for healthy shrimp growth. If priority is given to fully
closing the sluices to protect and store freshwater, cutting off the upstream freshwater supply
from the Hau River could raise salinity in the My Xuyen area to excessively high levels
(especially during peak hot months), causing slow shrimp growth or environmental shock.
Conversely, if freshwater is released to dilute the shrimp farming area, the stored freshwater for
fruit orchards upstream may be insufficient. Therefore, the operation of the Dai Ngai and My
Xuyen lock sluices need flexible adjustment of opening and closing schedules to minimize
conflicts between agricultural cultivation and aquaculture areas.
- Conflict between inland waterway transport and the task of preventing salinity intrusion and
storing freshwater: The locks are designed to maintain navigation when the main sluice gates
are closed to block saltwater. Each time a vessel passes through a lock, the lock chamber must
follow the procedure to balance water levels. This process inadvertently allows a certain volume
of saltwater from outside to enter the freshwater buffer zone. During extreme and prolonged
dry seasons, if the waterway traffic flow is high and lock operations occur continuously, the
cumulative saltwater intrusion through the lock chamber may exceed the safe threshold (usually



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                168
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

1 g/l for fruit crops), affecting the availability of water for agricultural use. However, these
impacts are considered minor and infrequent.
- Conflict between salinity prevention/freshwater storage and drainage: During the dry season,
to cope with drought, the operation procedure requires closing the sluice gates to transform the
internal river and canal system into a “freshwater reservoir.” Fully closing the sluices stops
natural water flow, turning rivers and canals into stagnant water. In this situation, discharges
from households, agricultural fertilizer residues, or effluent from shrimp ponds are no longer
naturally flushed by tidal flow, increasing the risk of local pollution, reduced domestic water
quality, and outbreaks of aquaculture diseases in the inland area.
- Conflict regarding local drainage needs: Conflicts may also arise among agricultural users
during seasonal transitions. Farmers in low-lying areas or at harvest time may want to open the
sluices for rapid drainage to prevent flooding and crop damage. Conversely, farmers in higher
or newly planted areas may want to keep sluices closed to maintain stable water levels in canals,
reducing irrigation costs. Therefore, to harmonize these conflicts, a flexible lock operation
procedure should be established based on real-time monitoring data of salinity and water levels.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
f. Alteration of the hydrodynamic regime leading to localized erosion and sedimentation
risks
Sedimentation Risk Assessment: When the sluice gates are closed (for salinity intrusion
prevention or freshwater retention), the flow velocity in the vicinity of the structure will
abruptly decrease to near zero (still water condition). Suspended sediment particles will lose
their kinetic energy and settle to the bottom.
Location and Extent: This primarily occurs upstream of the sluice gates and directly inside the
navigation lock chamber. It may also occur in slack water zones near the downstream banks.
Sedimentation causes the shallowing of navigational channels, hindering waterway traffic
through the lock, and may silt up the agricultural water intake gates of nearby local residents.
Magnitude of Impact: Moderate. Sedimentation will require the Project Owner to allocate a
budget for maintenance of dredging during the operational phase.
Erosion/Scouring Risk Assessment: Erosion induced by flow constriction: The presence of the
structure's piers narrows the river's cross-section. Even when all sluice gates are fully opened,
the water flow is forced through a reduced cross-sectional area, causing a surge in flow velocity.
When the gates are opened after a period of closure, the water level difference (head difference)
between the upstream and downstream creates a high-velocity jet flow with immense energy,
scouring the riverbed and bank structures.
Location and Extent: Local scour occurs most severely downstream of the structure, extending
along both banks of the river/canal within a range of 200m to 500m downstream of the sluice.
Scouring leads to riverbank erosion, land loss, and threatens the safety of households living
close to the water's edge (particularly sensitive in the My Xuyen area).
Magnitude of impact by structure:
For Dai Ngai Navigation Lock/Sluice: Magnitude of Impact: Moderate. The Saintard River is
a large waterway strongly influenced by the semi-diurnal tidal regime from the sea and the
massive water discharge from the Hau River. The kinetic energy of the flow passing through
the sluice here is substantial. The risk of deep scour holes forming downstream poses a direct
threat to the stability of the structure's own foundation and the adjacent embankments.
For My Xuyen Navigation Lock/Sluice: Magnitude of Impact: Moderate. Although the water
discharge is smaller than that of Dai Ngai, the My Xuyen canal is narrow and features a dense


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                169
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

population living directly along its banks. The foundation soil here is very soft/weak; therefore,
an increase in flow velocity poses a high risk of bank failure, leading to the collapse of
residential houses, which subsequently entails complex compensation issues and social
grievances.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
g. Specific Impacts and Risks at the Subproject
During operation of the lock, each time the lock gate is opened for vessels to pass, there is a
risk of saltwater leaking into the lock chamber. When vessels pass through the chamber into
the inland areas, this saltwater will be pushed along the surface into the interior of the lock.
- High concentration of vessels in the waiting area in front of the lock gate may cause collisions,
snapped mooring lines, or damage to the piers or lock chamber gates;
- Sedimentation in the lock chamber: Long-term operation may result in suspended silt and
sand settling inside the chamber, causing sediment accumulation, jamming of the gate rails, or
reduced bottom clearance, increasing the risk of barges grounding;
- Risk of electrical or emergency operation failures: During operation, gates may become stuck
due to branches, large debris, or foreign objects lodged in the gate slot, preventing the gate from
closing fully. If this occurs during peak saline high tide, saltwater could rush into the inland
areas;
- Risk due to power outage or hydraulic system failure: In natural disaster scenarios (storms,
lightning damaging power lines), if the backup generators at the control station do not function,
the gates will become “inoperative.” Inability to open the sluice sluices to drain floodwater
immediately could cause inland flooding;
Sluice gate operation involves controlling water flow via manual or automatic gate lifting,
posing critical Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) risks including entrapment, drowning,
hydraulic injuries, and electrical hazards. Key risks include entrapment by moving machinery,
high-pressure water surges, structural failures, and hazardous atmospheres.
Primary OHS risks: (i) Mechanical Hazards: Entrapment and crushing accidents occur,
particularly with electric gates or when operating mechanisms fail. (ii) Hydraulic
Hazards: Sudden water release, high-pressure leakage, and unexpected movement of the gate
due to water pressure can cause catastrophic failures or worker injuries. (iii) Physical
Hazards: Personnel face hazards from falling equipment, structural collapses during
maintenance,     and      high-noise  levels    from     rushing    water;    (iv) Confined
Spaces/Submersion: Cleaning or repairing gates may require entering confined spaces or
working in shallow, fast-moving water, creating risks of drowning, suffocation, or toxic gas
exposure; (v) Ergonomic Hazards: Manual handling during manual lifting operations can cause
musculoskeletal injuries.
Traffic safety risks are primarily driven by human behavior, including speeding, impaired
driving, distraction, and failure to use safety restraints. Speeding significantly increases both
the likelihood of a crash and its severity. Other major risks include poor road infrastructure,
unsafe vehicles, and inexperience. Key traffic safety risks and contributing factors.
- Occupational safety risks for sluice operation staff;
- Traffic safety risks when operating the sluice/bridge combined with the roadway.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
h. Increased Flooding



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 170
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

According to calculations, operation of the My Xuyen sluice will raise water levels in the area
outside the sluice by approximately 7 cm over a length of about 2.2 km, which will increase
flooding in the residential areas outside the sluice. This impact may create a risk of water
overtopping the dike and flowing into residential areas. At the same time, elevated water levels
could cause environmental pollution as wastewater and contaminated water flow into
residential areas. Aquaculture areas for shrimp and fish outside the lock are at risk of flooding,
causing escape of stock into the river; areas with crops, vegetables, and fruit trees are also at
risk of being submerged.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
i. Risk of waterborne diseases
The closure of sluice gates leads to water stagnation and diminishes the river's self-purification
capacity. The subsequent accumulation of pollutants triggers eutrophication, creating optimal
conditions for the proliferation of bacteria, parasites, and disease vectors (such as mosquitoes
transmitting dengue fever and malaria). These impacts are primarily localized in the slack water
zones upstream of the sluices and within the inland canal network during the peak dry season
(January to May). The risk of disease outbreaks peaks if the sluices remain closed continuously
without periodic flushing. Vulnerable receptors include households residing along the canals—
particularly children, the elderly, and low-income families still reliant on surface water for
domestic use—as well as sluice operation workers. However, the impacts are seasonal, and the
actual risk of exposure remains low given that the coverage rate of centralized clean water
supply exceeds 95%. Furthermore, these risks are highly manageable through local preventive
health programs and the implementation of flexible sluice operation procedures (i.e., periodic
opening and closing to maintain flow). Magnitude of Impact is assessed as Low
j. Impacts on local service infrastructure
Conflicts with and relocation requirements for existing technical infrastructure (ESS1 &
ESS5): The construction of civil works (such as sluices and embankments) will inevitably
intersect with other technical infrastructure. Typically, power lines, telecommunication cables,
or water supply pipelines running along transport routes or crossing the sluice construction sites
will require rearrangement or relocation (e.g., the dismantling and relocation of electric poles
within the sluice construction boundaries). This process not only incurs implementation costs
but also poses risks of localized disruption to essential services (electricity, water supply,
telecommunications) for local communities during the construction period.
Pressure on local infrastructure and services due to labor influx (ESS4 - Community
Health and Safety): Construction activities will entail the mobilization of thousands of non-
local workers. Although this workforce is typically dispersed across multiple construction
sites—which prevents a massive shock in any single area and minimizes the severity of the
impact—their presence still exerts a certain degree of pressure on the existing infrastructure
within host communities. This includes increased demand for electricity, clean water, solid
waste management capacity, and local healthcare facilities.
Degradation of local road networks (ESS4): The transportation of large volumes of
construction materials utilizing heavy trucks and mechanized equipment poses a risk of
degrading and causing subsidence to rural roads and small-scale bridge and sluice systems that
were not originally designed to withstand heavy loads
Magnitude of impact is assessed as Low.
6.3.3.4. Subproject 2.4
a. Positive Impacts of Operating the Nam Mang Thit Irrigation System



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                171
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Changes in Hydrological Regimes: The operation of the Cai Hop system, saltwater prevention
sluices, and in-field regulatory sluices will alter water levels and flow regimes within the river
and canal network. Calculation results indicate that average monthly water levels during the
dry season will reach +0.5m or higher across most of the project area. Specifically, levels in the
Tra Ngoa River will increase by 20–30 cm, and the Cai Hop canal will rise by 34–39 cm,
ensuring water supply requirements are met. After closing the sluice loops, water levels in
internal canals have improved; specifically, monthly water levels in the 19/5 and 3/2 canals
have increased by 26–43 cm and 26–38 cm, respectively, compared to current conditions. This
also reduces flooding duration within the project area.
Salinity Regulation, Tidal Flood Reduction, and Freshwater Supply: Once the project is
completed and operational, it will overcome abnormal saltwater intrusion into freshwater
production areas between February and May. It will also limit flooding during high tides
combined with floods from September to November, allowing residents to take initiative in
securing freshwater sources for production.
Contribution to Economic Development: The completed works will proactively prevent high
tides and respond to sea-level rise. It will actively create water sources and control salinity,
drainage, and acidity/alum leaching for approximately 225,682 ha of land.
Promoting Social Development: The project contributes to improving social conditions in
beneficiary areas: (i) The construction of a bridge over the Tra Ngoa sluice will improve the
road traffic network in Tra Con Commune, connecting the island areas on both sides of the river
with the commune center and PR.907. This enables faster goods transport, reduces shipping
and travel costs (eliminating the need for ferries), and particularly helps disadvantaged
households gain better access to healthcare services due to improved mobility. (ii) The
construction of the Cai Hop navigation lock will improve waterway traffic conditions. (iii) As
communes in the project area primarily rely on surface water for daily activities and drinking,
maintaining freshwater and enhancing its supply during the dry season will improve the
domestic water source for local residents.
b. Impact on access to water
Based on field surveys in the project area, local authorities indicated that the water supply
criterion is met when the average water level (Htb) ≥ +0.5 m. Therefore, to facilitate assessment,
the average water level maps of each specific calculation scenario are used as follows:
For the study scenarios above, representative points (at the beginning, middle, and end of the
project area) were selected, as shown in the figure below, for analysis.

c. Impact on agricultural economy
In the period after 2030 to 2050 and after 2050, according to the sustainable agricultural
development orientation under climate change adaptation conditions, the production model in
the project area will shift from monoculture rice to a rice–aquaculture model (rotation or
integrated).
In this context, the designed and upgraded irrigation system will play a role in regulating water
according to seasonal salinity–freshwater requirements, specifically:


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 175
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

-   Dry season: The sluice system along the Mang Thit River and the Cai Hop lock (on the Co
    Chien River) will operate flexibly to retain salinity or dilute it, providing suitable conditions
    for aquaculture growth (such as shrimp and brackish water fish);
-   Rainy season: The works ensure tidal control, water drainage, and flood mitigation, thereby
    securing rice cultivation conditions and residents’ safety.
Therefore, given the large area of Nam Mang Thit, the transformation of production models
cannot be implemented simultaneously, and multiple production models may coexist.
Accordingly, a specific transition plan is needed along with investment in works to separate
production model zones within the area.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
d. Impacts from Sedimentation, Subsidence, and Erosion
Source of impact: Operation of navigation locks/sluices
Affected receptors: People living along rivers/canals
Level of impact: With the proposed sluice size, the water conveyance area is about 50–60% of
the existing river cross-section. When the channel area is narrowed to this extent, the flow
velocity through the sluice increases, but not excessively. This increase in flow velocity may
slightly enhance the risk of local scouring at the sluice location within a range of approximately
10–20 m. The design has taken this impact into account, including the construction of energy
dissipation structures before and after the sluice gates and reinforced banks on both sides of the
sluice. Additionally, the project area has relatively weak geological conditions. Therefore,
during design and calculations, structural stability must be ensured to minimize risks of
subsidence and erosion of the works. Furthermore, when the sluices operate for salinity control
or to retain water downstream of the project area, water circulation is restricted, flow velocity
in rivers and canals decreases, and some areas may become stagnant, allowing sediments to
settle. However, since these sluices are located upstream near the Mang Thit River, the period
of saline intrusion in this area is limited, so the potential for sedimentation caused by sluice
closure is minor and primarily represents natural sedimentation processes. The Project Owner
will install monitoring equipment to measure water levels, flow velocity, sediment deposition,
and observe subsidence and erosion at each sluice. The impact level from sedimentation,
subsidence, and erosion is not significant and can be mitigated through appropriate technical
measures.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
e. Impact on ecosystems and biodiversity
Survey results and analysis of aquatic samples indicate that the existing ecosystem is in a
degraded condition, showing signs of organic pollution. The area does not host any endemic,
rare, or endangered species listed in the Vietnam Red Data Book or the IUCN Red List, and no
migratory species have been recorded. Some commonly observed aquatic species in the area
include typical fish and shrimp such as snakehead fish, catfish, tilapia, and greasyback shrimp.
The area is also characterized by the presence of several invasive species, including water
hyacinth, Mimosa pigra, suckermouth catfish, and golden apple snail. Sluice gate closure may
increase the proliferation of these invasive species in the upstream area. These species are
highly tolerant of stagnant and oxygen-depleted water environments. The extensive growth of
water hyacinth may cover the water surface, obstruct navigation, block sunlight penetration
(thereby inhibiting photosynthesis of submerged aquatic vegetation), and exacerbate anaerobic
conditions in the bottom layer.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                  176
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Analysis of aquatic samples indicates that the benthic community has been reduced to pollution-
tolerant indicator species such as oligochaete worms and mosquito larvae. Therefore, the
operation of sluice gate opening and closing is not expected to have significant impacts on
benthic organisms.
Impacts in coastal and estuarine areas are also significant due to the exchange between saline
and freshwater columns. The maximum difference between the highest and lowest salinity
levels is an important factor, as this range must correspond to the osmoregulation capacity of
species. At the boundary between brackish and freshwater, there is maximum turbidity in the
estuary. This is a zone of high chemical activity and turbidity caused by two processes: the
upstream movement of salt during rising tides, which stirs sediments, and the flocculation of
dissolved particles in freshwater when it meets brackish water. Reduced water clarity in the
estuary affects primary organisms, benthic biomass, and bird species that rely on vision for
foraging. Decreased clarity reduces phytoplankton productivity, which can reduce filter-feeding
biomass in the estuary. Consequently, birds that rely on sight to hunt are less likely to locate
fish in the estuary11.
The impact magnitude is considered Low.
f. Impact on inland waterway transport
Operating Regime of the Nam Mang Thit Sluice System
The operation of the sluice system—including newly constructed sluices (Muong Khai Lon,
Cai Ca, Sa Rai, and Tra Ngoa) and upgraded sluices (Rach Rum, My Van, and Nang Am)—
adheres to a flexible regime based on real-time hydrological and water quality monitoring data.
Dry Season (Salinity Control and Freshwater Storage): The system is operated to be fully closed
when the river-side salinity exceeds the threshold of S > 1.0 g/l. Freshwater intake for storage
is only performed when water quality is guaranteed. To ensure environmental hygiene, the
sluices will be opened to flush pollutants during low tides if inland pollution is detected above
allowable limits.
Rainy Season and Tidal Peaks (Tidal Peak Clipping and Flood Drainage): The sluices operate
for "tidal peak clipping" by closing during high tides to prevent flooding and for "flood
drainage" by opening during low tides to discharge inland water. Coordinated operation
between the two ends of the sluice system facilitates circulation and prevents water stagnation
and localized pollution.
Control Mechanism: The system utilizes hydraulic cylinders to enable rapid, precise, and
automated operations driven by salinity and water level sensors. A manual operation mode is
maintained to ensure system redundancy and overall safety.
Emergency Response Plan: In the event of a main gate failure, a backup stoplog system will be
deployed to isolate the sluice chamber for repairs without interrupting salinity prevention or
freshwater storage functions. Parameters such as gate deformation and ground subsidence are
continuously monitored via sensors to provide timely warnings and risk alerts
Source of impact: The opening and closing operational mechanism of the sluice gates for
salinity control and flood regulation.
Affected receptors: Freight transport vessels (carrying rice, fruits, and raw materials) and
passenger watercraft navigating the canal network connected to the Mang Thit River.



11 Daan Rijk Gerritse van Wieringen (2019). The impact of sluice management on biodiversity and ecosystem services in the Haringvliet.
MSc Thesis in Environmental Sciences. Wageningen University and Research



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                                  177
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Baseline characteristics: The average inland waterway traffic volume on the main canals is
approximately 30 vessel trips per day.
Continuity of navigation: Given that deep saline intrusion in the South Mang Thit area
typically peaks during March and April, this disruption to navigation is strictly seasonal.
However, the installation of a hydraulic cylinder system enables rapid gate operation. This,
combined with an average lock operation frequency of once every two days for waterway traffic
regulation during the dry season, ensures the continuity of the local logistics supply chain.
Navigational clearance and safety: The designed bottom elevation of the sluice beams ranges
from +3.5 m to +7.3 m (averaging between +4.5 m and +5.5 m), which guarantees sufficient
and safe navigable clearance for barges and watercraft characteristic of the Mekong Delta
region. Furthermore, upgrading from automatic gates to forced-operation gates at the Rach Rum,
My Van, and Nang Am sluices allows for more proactive water regulation. This maintains
stable inland water levels, thereby creating more favorable conditions for watercraft navigation
during the dry season.
Risks of operational incidents: Gate malfunctions (e.g., failure to open or close) could cause
sudden bottlenecks in waterway traffic. However, this risk is effectively managed through a
routine maintenance plan and a contingency manual operation protocol utilizing barges and
crane equipment when necessary.
Impact Assessment: By integrating large-scale lock chambers (with a usable width of 15 m)
and implementing a flexible operating regime driven by real-time data, adverse impacts on
inland waterway transport are significantly mitigated. Therefore, the impact magnitude is
considered Low.
g. Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) and Traffic Risk Impacts
-   Incidents caused by sluice gate damage or jamming may affect sluice operation,
    particularly during peak dry season periods when saline intrusion reaches upstream areas.
-   Waterway traffic accidents may occur when boats/barges pass through sluices or navigation
    locks.
-   Land traffic accidents may occur when vehicles cross bridges (e.g., Tra Ngoa sluice bridge,
    bridge over Cai Hop navigation lock).
-   Negative impacts are mainly associated with structural incidents such as erosion,
    subsidence of sluice structures, etc. During operations, competent authorities and local
    governments will monitor erosion development, record observations, issue warnings, and
    implement mitigation measures for potential incidents. Therefore, the impact level is
    considered low and can be minimized through appropriate technical and operational
    measures.
-   Risk of gate obstruction and emergency operation: During operation, gate valves may
    become jammed by tree branches, large debris, or foreign objects, preventing full closure.
    If this occurs at the peak of a high saline tide, saline water could rapidly flood the inland
    areas.
-   Risk from power loss or hydraulic system failure: In the event of natural disasters (storms,
    tornadoes damaging power lines), if backup generators at the control station fail, the gates
    may become “immobilized.” Inability to open sluices for immediate flood drainage could
    result in inland flooding.
-   Sluice gate operation involves controlling water flow via manual or automatic gate lifting,
    posing critical Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) risks including entrapment,
    drowning, hydraulic injuries, and electrical hazards. Key risks include entrapment by


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                178
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    moving machinery, high-pressure water surges, structural failures, and hazardous
    atmospheres.
-   Primary OHS risks: (i) Mechanical Hazards: Entrapment and crushing accidents occur,
    particularly with electric gates or when operating mechanisms fail. (ii) Hydraulic
    Hazards: Sudden water release, high-pressure leakage, and unexpected movement of the
    gate due to water pressure can cause catastrophic failures or worker injuries. (iii) Physical
    Hazards: Personnel face hazards from falling equipment, structural collapses during
    maintenance, and high-noise levels from rushing water; (iv) Confined
    Spaces/Submersion: Cleaning or repairing gates may require entering confined spaces or
    working in shallow, fast-moving water, creating risks of drowning, suffocation, or toxic
    gas exposure; (v) Ergonomic Hazards: Manual handling during manual lifting operations
    can cause musculoskeletal injuries.
-   Traffic safety risks are primarily driven by human behavior, including speeding, impaired
    driving, distraction, and failure to use safety restraints. Speeding significantly increases
    both the likelihood of a crash and its severity. Other major risks include poor road
    infrastructure, unsafe vehicles, and inexperience. Key traffic safety risks and contributing
    factors.
-   Occupational safety risks for sluice operation personnel.
-   Traffic safety risks when sluice operation is combined with road traffic.
-   The impact magnitude is considered Low.
6.4. Cumulative Impacts Assessment (CIA)
6.4.1. Methodology and Strategic Framework
Under the World Bank Environmental and Social Framework (ESS1), the environmental and
social assessment should evaluate the incremental impact of the project when added to other
past, present, and reasonably foreseeable future developments. Following the approach
established in standard WB ESF and the IFC Good Practice Handbook on Cumulative Impact
Assessment and Management's six-step Rapid Cumulative Impact Assessment (RCIA) process,
this section consolidates the cumulative impact assessment for the entire MERIT project rather
than evaluating subprojects in isolation.
Specifically, this Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) anchors its baseline and cumulative
assessment in the Mekong Delta Master Plan (2021-2030, vision to 2050). The Mekong Delta
Masterplan (MDIRP), approved by Prime Minister Decision No. 287/QD-TTg, establishes the
mandatory strategic foundation for all development and climate adaptation activities in the
region. A core paradigm shift of this Masterplan is the transition from intensive natural resource
exploitation to nature-based adaptation, emphasizing the principle of "living with floods,
brackish water, and salt water". It explicitly advocates for reducing groundwater extraction,
decreasing intensive triple-crop rice production, and encouraging natural and man-made
systems that are resilient to climate change and upstream Mekong mainstream developments.
Importantly, the Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) of the MDIRP—conducted
comprehensively alongside the planning process—identified severe regional environmental
trade-offs associated with the widespread development of large-scale water control
infrastructure. The SEA has already evaluated the system-wide environmental consequences of
large-scale water infrastructure investments across the region, including the creation of
enclosed freshwater zones, sediment trapping, salinity gradient changes, and disruptions to fish
migration. By framing the MERIT interventions within these established SEAs and Master Plan,
this ESIA does not replicate the delta-wide strategic analysis already undertaken but rather
relies directly on the MDIRP SEA's findings to frame the project-level cumulative risks.
Because the MERIT project will construct and simultaneously operate major locks and sluice

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                179
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

gates (e.g., Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop, and the Nam Mang Thit system), it directly triggers
the systemic risks identified in the SEA. Consequently, this CIA acknowledges that water
quality degradation cannot be treated as a minor, localized, or temporary construction issue.
Instead, the cumulative risk of converting open, tidally flushed waterways into stagnant
freshwater zones during the dry season is assessed as a significant, long-term programmatic
impact that requires automated real-time water quality management to resolve the conflicts
identified in the regional SEA.
6.4.2. Spatial and Temporal Boundaries (RCIA Step 1)
Under the IFC Good Practice Handbook on Cumulative Impact Assessment (IFC Handbook),
setting boundaries is an iterative process requiring the geographic and temporal scope to
encompass all impacts from past, present, and predictable future developments that influence
VEC conditions. For a programme of MERIT's scale, these boundaries transcend the traditional
Direct Area of Influence (DAI) and are defined by the ecological and hydrological realities of
the Mekong Delta rather than arbitrary administrative borders or physical distances.
A. Spatial Boundaries (Geographic Scope) Applying the IFC’s rules of thumb for geographic
boundaries, the spatial scope of this CIA is defined by the distance that cumulative effects can
travel and the wider area occupied by the affected VECs. The spatial boundary for the MERIT
project encompasses the administrative territories of Vĩnh Long, Can Tho, Dong Tháp, and Tay
Ninh, but is strictly delineated by three interconnected hydrological zones:
1. The Estuarine and Coastal Zone (Subproject 2.1): The spatial boundary includes the 16.6
   km of coastline where wave breaker and sedimentation structures will be constructed (Tan
   Thuy, Thoi Thuan, Dong Hai, and Truong Long Hoa in Vinh Long Province). This
   boundary extends offshore into the East Sea to capture the cumulative alterations to coastal
   sedimentation patterns and marine benthic habitats.
2. The Interconnected Inland Waterway Network (Subprojects 2.2, 2.3, 2.4): Previous
   assessments dismissing cumulative water quality impacts on the grounds that construction
   sites are physically separated by 10 km or more are scientifically invalid. The spatial
   boundary must encompass the shared, connected river systems. This includes the Saintard
   and Bãi Xàu Rivers (which both feed into the shared Hậu River system), as well as the
   Mang Thit and Co Chien river networks. Because water pollutants and suspended
   sediments from dredging 61 km of canals (Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam and Cho Bung) may
   travel downstream, the entire downstream aquatic corridor is included in the spatial
   boundary.
3. The Enclosed Freshwater Operational Zone: During the operational phase, the cumulative
   spatial boundary is defined by the vast area of natural land whose tidal flushing regime will
   be altered. Upon completion, the MERIT structures (Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop, etc.)
   will integrate with existing WB9-funded sluice gates (Tan Dinh, Bong Bot, Vung Liem)
   and the Southern Hau River Water Control Project to form an enclosed freshwater zone
   covering 226,682 hectares between the Hau and Co Chien Rivers. This entire 226,682-
   hectare zone is the spatial boundary for assessing cumulative risks of eutrophication,
   dissolved oxygen depletion, and aquatic habitat alteration.
B. Temporal Boundaries (Time Frame) Applying the IFC’s rules of thumb for temporal
boundaries, the time frame must encompass the complete life cycle of the proposed
development and any potential effects that extend beyond it. Therefore, the temporal boundaries
for the MERIT CIA are defined in two distinct phases:
1. Construction Phase (Short to Medium-Term): The temporal scope covers a continuous
   period of 2 to 3 years (24 to 36 months). This boundary captures the critical window of
   simultaneous construction activities across the delta, including the concurrent mobilization


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                               180
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    of hundreds of workers, the simultaneous deployment of massive dredging fleets extracting
    over 339,000 m³ of sludge, and the combined traffic congestion on shared road and
    waterway networks.
2. Operational Phase (Long-Term): The temporal scope extends to 50 years after project
   completion (from 2031). This long-term boundary is absolutely critical to accurately assess
   the progressive, cumulative accumulation of impacts that take decades to materialize. A
   50-year timeframe captures:
The long-term trapping of sediment and progressive depletion of inland channels as well as the
surface water quality. In particular, the sediment will be trapped gradually during the period.
The chronic accumulation of domestic and agricultural pollutants within the 226,682-hectare
enclosed freshwater zone during dry-season gate closures.
The synergistic effects of the project structures interacting with long-term climate change, sea-
level rise, and delta-wide subsidence over the next half-century.
6.4.3. Identification of Cumulative Stressors and VECs (RCIA Steps 2 and 3)
Following the IFC Handbook, this step identifies the Valued Environmental and Social
Components (VECs) affected by the project and maps all past, present, and reasonably
predictable future activities—as well as natural stressors—that will cumulatively interact with
these VECs.
A. Identification of VECs: VECs are environmental and social attributes considered to be
important in assessing risks, including physical features, ecosystem services, natural processes,
and social conditions. Based on the strategic findings of the Mekong Delta Plan SEA and the
subproject contexts as well as intensive consultation with the local communities and authorities,
and other project owners, four primary VECs have been identified as highly vulnerable to the
cumulative effects of the MERIT programme:
+   VEC 1: Surface Water Quality and Aquatic Ecosystems: Encompassing the interconnected
    hydrological networks (Hau River, Saintard River, Bay Xau River) and the planned
    enclosed freshwater zone. This VEC is highly sensitive to massive dredging turbidity and
    operational eutrophication due to the loss of tidal flushing.
+   VEC 2: Hydrology, Erosion, and Sedimentation: The regional flow dynamics and sediment
    transport patterns, which are vulnerable to cumulative flow restrictions and sediment
    trapping from multiple regional water control structures.
+   VEC 3: Inland Waterway Traffic and Logistics: The shared navigational routes on the Hau,
    Mang Thit, and Co Chien river networks, which are critical for regional supply chains to
    concurrent construction vessel staging and sequential lock operations.
+   VEC 4: Road infrastructure and air quality: Although the construction of Dai Ngai and My
    Xuyen and other sluice gates (sub-project 2.3 and 2.4), coastal wave breakers (2.1) will
    primarily transport materials via waterways, the risk of impacts on road traffic is still
    medium. It is due to the fact that the regional road infrastructure (provincial and inter-
    commune roads in Vĩnh Long, Cần Thơ, Đồng Tháp, and Tây Ninh) is narrow and degraded
    and frequently passes through densely populated residential clusters. These existing routes
    have a very low carrying capacity and are highly vulnerable to structural degradation,
    traffic safety risks, and dust dispersion caused by the cumulative deployment of heavy
    transport truck fleets across the concurrent subprojects.
+   VEC 5: Social Infrastructure and Labor Influx: Local healthcare, accommodation, and
    social cohesion, which are vulnerable to simultaneous influx of construction workforces
    mobilized from outside the region. In reality, the contractor may hire local workers, the


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                181
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    contractor can set up temporary camps for workers to stay, which will be strictly managed
    by the contractor and local authorities.
+   VEC 6: Regional Livelihoods and Water-Use Trade-offs: the ecological sub-zones
    characterized by conflicting water needs: the Long Phu – Tiep Nhat area (dependent on
    freshwater) and the Mỹ Xuyên area (dependent on brackish water)
B. Identification of Cumulative Stressors (RCIA Step 2) The identified VECs will be
cumulatively impacted by three categories of stressors: internal MERIT activities, external
development projects, and natural environmental drivers.
1. Internal MERIT Stressors (Simultaneous Construction & Operation): The MERIT project
itself acts as a massive regional stressor. The simultaneous implementation of Subprojects 2.1,
2.2, 2.3, and 2.4 involves mobilizing large vessel fleets, establishing multiple worker camps,
and conducting extensive earthworks (such as the 44 km of dredging in Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam
canal and 17km of Cho Bung canal in Dong Thap) concurrently. Operationally, the
simultaneous closure of the Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cái Hóp and external sluice gates locks
drastically alters the Delta's tidal flushing regime in term of the distribution of freshwater and
saline water, triggering shifts in the economic viability of different.
2. External Relevant Projects: Screening of the geographic scope has identified the following
reasonably foreseeable developments that share the same VECs and spatial/temporal
boundaries as MERIT.
