3. Natural and Environmental Stressors: In assessing the predicted future conditions of the
VECs, the CIA must incorporate severe natural stressors defining the Mekong Delta's trajectory.
These include accelerating sea-level rise scenario indicating that an 80 cm rise in sea level could
permanently inundate around 31.94% of the Mekong Delta (MONRE, 2020). For severe dry-
season salinity intrusion, it was observed during the 2019–2020 dry season, the 4 g/L salinity
boundary intruded about 60 km up the Tiền River and 40–50 km along the Hậu River, which is
8–10 km deeper inland than in the severe 2015–2016 event; on the Hàm Luong River, the
affected reach extended 78 km inland, 5 km beyond the 2016 maximum (Hapri, 2024). The
Mekong Delta is increasingly exposed to extreme flood and drought events associated with
climate change induced shifts in rainfall and river flows, while widespread deltaic land
subsidence of 1–5 cm per year—often exceeding the rate of global sea level rise—further
amplifies flood and salinity risks (Le Anh Tuan, 2020). Extreme flooding/drought events driven
by climate change, and widespread deltaic ground subsidence. Over the past 30 years,
groundwater extraction has caused an average land subsidence rate of 1.1-2.5 cm/year across
the region (Rise and Fall project, 2019), with central urban areas (such as Can Tho and Ca Mau)
subsiding 20 to 50 cm over 10 years. These natural drivers fundamentally alter the carrying
capacity and resilience of the identified VECs, exacerbating the infrastructure-induced impacts.
More information will be provided in each VEC if any in the following steps (4&5) to ensure
consistency.
6.4.4. Assessment of Cumulative Impacts on VECs (RCIA Steps 4 and 5)
Following Steps 4 and 5 of the IFC Good Practice Handbook, this section assesses the expected
future conditions of the VECs and evaluates the significance of these predicted cumulative
impacts against established thresholds. The assessment considers the combined effects of the


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                     183
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

MERIT project’s simultaneous construction activities, external developments, and natural
stressors over the defined 50-year temporal boundary.
VEC 1: Surface Water Quality and Aquatic Ecosystems
Present Condition (RCIA Step 3): Extensive field surveys, literature reviews, and community
consultations confirm that the subproject areas (such as the Chợ Bưng canal, Phước Xuyên
canal, Saintard River, and Bãi Xàu River) do not intersect with any Critical Habitats, Key
Biodiversity Areas (KBAs), or protected areas. The screening results indicate that no species
classified as Critically Endangered (CR), Endangered (EN), or Vulnerable (VU) under the
IUCN Red List or the Vietnam Red Data Book were recorded within the project's direct
footprint. Baseline monitoring reveals that the aquatic environment across the project area is
already highly sensitive and degraded. Biological sampling indicates severe organic pollution
and eutrophication, characterized by the dominance of Euglena and cyanobacteria (blue-green
algae) at the surface, and anaerobic benthic zones dominated by pollution-indicator species such
as oligochaete worms (Limnodrilus hoffmeisteri, Branchiura sowerbyi) and chironomid larvae.
The aquatic fauna is dominated by common freshwater and tolerant species widely distributed
in agricultural ecosystem, such as snakehead fish (Channa striata), climbing perch (Anabas
testudineus), and invasive species like the sailfin pleco (Pterygoplichthys spp.). Therefore, the
impacts are evaluated against a heavily modified, yet regionally important, aquatic ecosystem.
Furthermore, soils in the Đồng Tháp Mười area (Subproject 2.2) exhibit acid sulfate
characteristics.
Construction Phase Impacts (RCIA Step 4): The simultaneous dredging of the Phước Xuyên
and Chợ Bưng canals using hydraulic suction dredgers will potentially increase the Total
Suspended Solids (TSS). The concurrent disturbance of potential acid sulfate soils across these
sites risks remobilizing iron (Fe) and aluminum (Al) into the water column, further reducing
Dissolved Oxygen (DO) through the oxidation of Fe²⁺ and impacting already stressed aquatic
habitats.
Operational Phase Impacts (Critical Risk) (RCIA Step 4): The most severe cumulative impact
of the MERIT programme occurs during operation. The simultaneous closure of the Đại Ngãi,
Mỹ Xuyên, and Cái Hóp locks—combined with the external Southern Hậu River sluice
gatesand the completed WB9 (MD-ICRSL) sluice gates will establish a massive enclosed
freshwater zone covering over 226,682 hectares. Without the natural flushing capacity of the
daily tides, the interconnected river and canal network will convert into stagnant freshwater
zones. Domestic waste, agricultural fertilizers, and pesticide residues will accumulate densely,
driving DO levels below the 4 mg/L survival threshold for many native aquatic species and
triggering toxic algal blooms. (Ha Thao Nguyen et al, Journal of Agriculture and Environment,
2025)
Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): The elimination of tidal flushing may push VEC 1
beyond acceptable thresholds. Therefore, the cumulative significance is rated as Medium to
Significant.
VEC 2: Hydrology, Erosion, and Sedimentation
Predicted Future Condition: The operation of multiple large-scale sluice gates fundamentally
alters the natural morpho-dynamic balance of the river-sea system. When gates are closed, the
sudden reduction in flow velocity causes suspended silt and sand to settle upstream. The long-
term operational consequence of sequential lock and gate closures is the severe alteration of
regional sediment transport. Water stagnation and flow restriction will accelerate the deposition
of silt and sand upstream, progressively depleting inland channels over the 50-year lifespan.
Impact of Increased Water Levels Upstream (Backwater Curves): The permanent
operation of the proposed water control infrastructure requires periodic, simultaneous gate


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                184
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

closures during peak dry season high tides to prevent saline intrusion and manage localized
flooding. Based on empirical hydrodynamic modeling (MIKE 11) conducted for the Feasibility
Study, the simultaneous closure of these regional ship locks and sluice gates will form a
backwater effect
Subproject 2.3 (Đại Ngãi & Mỹ Xuyên): To rigorously evaluate the backwater phenomenon,
specific hydrodynamic calculations were conducted comparing two scenarios: Scenario 1
(current baseline topography without sluice gates) and Scenario 2 (closure of the newly
constructed sluice gates during high tide). The empirical results demonstrate that the maximum
backwater effect occurs precisely during the rising tide, just as the gates begin to block the
incoming sea water. At this peak moment, the water level immediately upstream of the
structures increases by only 5 to 9 cm compared to the baseline conditions. It is estimated by
the FS team that the back curve distance from the construction area to upstream is around only
2.2 km.
Subproject 2.4 (Nam Mang Thit): The MIKE 11 model maps the complex canal network
dynamics and mathematically proves the water retention is highly localized. In the Trà Ngoa
canal at node K4+000, the average water level in December increases from a baseline of 0.62
meters to 1.07 meters during operational closure, representing a maximum localized backwater
curve of +0.45 meters. At the Cái Hóp river (node K6+000), the water level increases from 0.55
meters to 0.92 meters, an increase of +0.37 meters. Overall, the maximum regional variations
peak at just 0.39 meters during the dry season.
Ripple Effects Downstream and Upstream under Tidal Influence:
Upstream Ripple Effects and Backwater Distance: The physical distance of this water back
curve is strictly confined. Because the project structures (such as the Mang Thit River canals
and the Bai Xau/Saintard Rivers) are secondary or tertiary branches located in a low-lying
coastal depression near the East Sea, this backwater effect is very weak. Hydraulic models
simulating the Mekong Delta river network provided from the FS team confirm that when all
gates are closed at peak high tide, the absolute maximum extent of upstream water ponding
along the Hậu and Cổ Chiên rivers extends only up to a maximum few tens of kilometers with
only 5-9 cm (Subproject 2.3) and 39cm (Subproject 2.4) operational backwater curves that is
hydrologically impossible to generate sufficient hydraulic energy to push water upstream into
higher-elevation areas, to propagate 150–200 kilometers upstream into transboundary
territories like Cambodia.
Downstream Ripple Effects (Loss of Tidal Flushing): The most severe cumulative impact of
the MERIT programme occurs during operation, where the simultaneous closure of these locks
establishes a massive enclosed freshwater zone covering over 226,682 hectares. Without the
natural flushing capacity of the daily tides, the interconnected river and canal network will
convert into stagnant freshwater zones. In the absence of tidal ebb and flow, domestic waste,
agricultural fertilizers, and pesticide residues will accumulate densely. This will severely
degrade downstream water quality, driving Dissolved Oxygen (DO) levels below the 4 mg/L
survival threshold for many native aquatic species and triggering toxic algal blooms.
Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): Over the 50-year temporal boundary of the
infrastructure, this cumulative sediment trapping will progressively deplete inland channels by
sedimentation, causing long-term reductions in both irrigation capacity and navigability,
diminishing the agricultural irrigation capacity, threatening the project's long-term operational
sustainability. Conversely, sudden gate openings can cause severe downstream scouring. The
cumulative significance is rated as Medium.
VEC 3: Inland Waterway Traffic and Logistics



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                185
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Present Condition and Baseline Vulnerability (RCIA Step 3): The inland waterway network is
the economic lifeline of the Mekong Delta, heavily utilized for transporting agricultural
products, construction materials, and passengers. Based on empirical baseline traffic surveys,
the current waterway traffic density across the subproject areas is highly variable but locally
significant:
Subproject 2.1 (Coastal Coastal Zones and Main Corridors of Cổ Chiên and Hậu rivers): The
main navigation channels on the Cổ Chiên and Hậu rivers (Class I waterways) handle heavy
traffic of approximately 200 to 400 vessel movements per day. Nearshore estuaries experience
intense, localized traffic from 80 to 120 small-scale fishing vessels heavily constrained by tidal
cycles. These existing waterways are currently functioning near capacity in several narrow
pinch-points, making them highly vulnerable to sudden influxes of heavy construction fleets or
navigational obstructions
Subproject 2.2 (Phước Xuyên – Hai Tam canal, Nguyen Van Tiep canal): High density,
averaging 70-100 vessel trips/day, predominantly comprising traditional boats, 50-100 DWT
barges, and five active informal cross-canal passenger ferries.
Subproject 2.3 (Saintard and Bãi Xàu Rivers): Moderate density, with approximately 45-50
trips/day on the Saintard River and 35-40 trips/day on the Bãi Xàu River.
Subprojects 2.4 (Nam Măng Thít and Cổ Chiên River): Moderate to high density, averaging
30-50 vessel movements/day
Predicted Future Condition: The simultaneous execution of the MERIT subprojects will inject
a massive, overlapping fleet of heavy marine construction and transport vessels into this
baseline network over a 24- to 36-month period. The project's specific waterway traffic demand
includes:
Subproject 2.1: Generating immense localized spikes in material transport, requiring an
estimated 60 vessel trips/day at Thới Thuận, 36 trips/day at Trường Long Hòa, and 22 trips/day
at Tân Thủy. Because unloading can only occur during high tides (H ≥ +1.0m, roughly 6
hours/day), this creates severe bottlenecking and collision risks with local fishing boats. When
these concentrated project fleets overlap with regional waterway traffic—and navigate
alongside concurrent external developments like the Southern Hậu River Water Control
Project—vessels will no longer experience free-flowing transit. Instead, they will face physical
channel constrictions, severe collision risks (especially at night or during peak harvest seasons),
and must sequentially wait to pass through multiple active construction zones and lock
chambers.
Subproject 2.2: Simultaneous mobilization of 24 excavators, 36 high-capacity dredging barges,
and 24 tugboats (150 HP), severely constricting the narrow navigable channels of the Phước
Xuyên canal.
Subproject 2.3: Deployment of 8 transport barges (250T–400T), 6 tugboats, 6 floating pile-
driving vessels, and 6 canoes, alongside fixed cofferdams that will encroach upon 50% to 60%
of the natural river cross-section.
Subproject 2.4: Because road transport is intentionally minimized, the massive logistical burden
of Subproject 2.4 is shifted directly onto the inland waterway network. The baseline waterway
traffic along the Nam Mang Thit and Co Chien River axes currently averages 30 to 50 vessel
movements per day. During the concurrent 24- to 36-month construction phase, deploying
material transport barges, tugboats, and floating pile-driving equipment directly into these
active Class IV inland waterways will severely constrict navigable channels. In the long-term
operational phase (RCIA Step 4), the new regional locks will become physical bottlenecks for
interprovincial waterway transport. Instead of free-flowing transit, vessels will be forced to


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 186
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

sequentially wait to pass through the lock chambers, which will cumulatively increase transit
times for regional agricultural supply chains.
As such, during construction, the simultaneous deployment of high-capacity barges (250T–
400T), tugboats (150 HP), suction dredgers, and floating cranes across Class III national inland
waterways (such as the Saintard, Bãi Xàu, and Măng Thít rivers) will constrict navigational
channels. Vessels navigating the regional network will no longer experience free-flowing transit;
instead, they must sequentially wait to pass through multiple lock chambers (e.g., Đại Ngãi lock,
Rạch Mọp lock, Mỹ Xuyên lock), requiring time for water level balancing and signal
coordination. However, the sluice gate system under the Southern Hau River Water Control
Project is located in upstream tributaries of the Hau River, at Rach Cai Con, Rach Cai Cau,
Rach Cai Tram, etc., and is not on the main transport route of the Hau River. Therefore, during
construction, the risk of cumulative impacts on inland waterway traffic and the logistics supply
chain is medium. This compounds also with traffic from external coastal wind power projects
but not severely impact.
Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): Driven by the empirical data showing the massive
injection of project vessels (dozens of barges, tugboats, and pile drivers) into waterways
already handling up to 100 to 400 trips/day, combined with the physical constriction of
channels by cofferdams and dredging pipelines, the cumulative disruption to the regional
logistics supply chain and the elevated risks of vessel collisions are definitively rated as
Substantial.
VEC 4: Road infrastructure and air quality:
Present Condition and Baseline Vulnerability: The current baseline air quality in the project
areas is relatively good and within the allowable limits of QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT. However,
the local road infrastructure (provincial and inter-commune roads in Vĩnh Long, Cần Thơ, Đồng
Tháp, and Tây Ninh) is narrow, often heavily degraded, and frequently passes through densely
populated residential clusters. Current baseline rural traffic is predominantly composed of
motorbikes and light agricultural vehicles, with medium traffic density and localized high-
density peaks during school opening/closing times and local market hours (impacting highly
sensitive receptors located directly on these narrow routes, such as Thanh Lợi 2 Primary School,
Hòa Bình Market, and Xuan Hiep school, Hoa Binh Kindergarten, and local health stations.
These existing roads have a very low carrying capacity for heavy truck traffic and are highly
vulnerable to structural damage and dust dispersion.
Subproject 2.1: Transport routes include National Highway 53/53B, provincial roads, and
communal roads in Dong Hai, Truong Long Hoa, Tan Thuy, and Thoi Thuan. On existing
coastal provincial roads, traffic volume is currently relatively low as it passes mainly through
rural areas with low population density, handling approximately 1,092 trucks, 208 buses, and
1,070 cars per day. However, traffic safety remains a significant concern due to poor local
compliance with traffic laws and over-speeding at night.
Subproject 2.2: The broader regional land transport network connecting to this Subproject
involves National Highways 1, 22, 30, as well as various provincial and inter-communal roads.
However, the localized rural road and bridge infrastructure directly at the project sites has a
very low carrying capacity. For example, the existing bridges crossing the Chợ Bưng canal
currently serve a localized traffic volume predominantly consisting of motorbikes, bicycles,
and pedestrians to support daily living and local agricultural production.
Subproject 2.3: Road transport routes include National Highways 1, 91, 60, 1A, 62, the Ho Chi
Minh City - Trung Luong Expressway, provincial roads 864 and 827B, and communal roads.
While the national highways and expressways are designed for higher capacities, the localized
provincial and communal roads connecting directly to the construction sites are generally
narrow, often degraded, and pass through populated residential clusters. These existing local

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                187
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

routes have a very low carrying capacity. They currently experience localized high-density
peaks during school opening/closing times and local market hours, making them highly
vulnerable to structural degradation and traffic safety risks when subjected to heavy
construction transport.
Subproject 2.4: Road transport routes include National Highways 1 and 53, alongside
communal roads. While the national highways have a higher carrying capacity, the localized
communal roads connecting directly to the sluice and lock construction sites (such as the Cai
Hop navigation lock or riverbank sluices in Tra Con, Hoa Binh, and Trung Hiep communes)
are highly vulnerable to structural degradation if subjected to heavy construction transport.
Predicted Future Condition: during the construction, the simultaneous establishment of multiple
construction sites across the four MERIT subprojects, alongside external regional developments
(such as the coastal wind power projects and the Southern Hậu River Water Control Project),
will trigger a significant cumulative spike in vehicular traffic sharing the same regional
transport corridors in which the traffic density is already rather high if using the road transport
only. To accurately assess the significance of this impact, the projected traffic demand has been
evaluated against the current classification and carrying capacity of the receiving road networks:
Cumulative Traffic Injection: The MERIT project will inject a highly concentrated volume
of 10-to-15-ton heavy diesel transport trucks into this fragile network.
Subproject 2.1 (Coastal Interventions): Material transport will mostly transport by waterway
from the sea, localized land transport will generate approximately 25-28 heavy truck trips/day
at the Tân Thủy and Thới Thuận sites.
Subproject 2.2 (Inland Dredging): Transporting materials and waste will require the
mobilization of approximately 12 dump trucks operating daily for the Phước Xuyên – Hai Tam
canal area, and 1 dump truck daily for the Chợ Bưng area.
Subprojects 2.3 and 2.4 (Hau River and Nam Mang Thit): Because these massive water
control structures (Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, and Nam Mang Thit sluices) are situated along main
river axes, heavy materials will predominantly be transported via waterways. Consequently,
road transport is strictly minimized, generating only 1 to 5 truck trips per day per subproject for
localized waste clearance.
Impact Assessment: When these heavy vehicle movements (totaling hundreds of trips daily
across the delta) overlap with the logistical transport for external infrastructure projects, the
cumulative traffic volume will drastically exceed the structural and operational design capacity
of these Class IV, V, and VI rural roads. Consequently, this simultaneous heavy-duty transport
will not only accelerate rapid structural degradation (subsidence, potholes, cracking of
asphalt/concrete) but will exponentially increase traffic safety risks (collisions) for vulnerable
local road users (students, pedestrians, two-wheelers) who must share these narrow,
unprotected corridors.
+   Cumulative Air Quality Degradation: The simultaneous operations of excavation,
    backfilling, and transportation of dredged materials and construction supplies will generate
    cumulative loads of dust (TSP, PM10, PM2.5) and exhaust emissions (CO, SO2, NO2,
    VOCs). While calculations using the Sutton dispersion model and WHO emission factors
    may show localized dust concentrations within limits for a single site, the simultaneous
    mobilization of hundreds of heavy trucks (e.g., 10-15 ton diesel dump trucks) across the
    delta will create an overlapping, prolonged pollution footprint along shared access roads.
    During dry and windy days, the cumulative dust dispersion will impact roadside residents,
    businesses, and schools.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 188
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

+   Cumulative Traffic Analysis with Concurrent Projects: When evaluating these specific
    traffic volumes against reasonably foreseeable external projects under construction during
    the MERIT implementation phase, the cumulative impacts are highly localized:
Coastal Zone Overlap (Low Impact): In the coastal areas of Subproject 2.1, the waterway
will share with projects of V1-3 (Phase 2), Thăng Long, and Đông Hải 3 offshore wind power
plants (all expected to be under construction and operational by Q4/2026). As such no severe
cumulative congestion of marine/waterway transport and accelerate the structural degradation
of fragile rural access roads.
Southwest Hậu River Overlap (Moderate Impact): For Subprojects 2.3 and 2.4, the
concurrent regional development is the Southern Hậu River Water Control Project. However,
the cumulative impact on road infrastructure is assessed as Moderate. The MERIT ship locks
rely almost entirely on inland waterway transport for heavy materials, and the nearest external
construction sites (such as the Mương Khai Lớn 2 sluice) are located approximately 10
kilometers away, ensuring no overlapping congestion on shared rural road networks however
the regional road network will be possibly cumulatively affected.
WB9 Project (Subproject 3: Improving flood drainage capacity and livelihoods) linked to
Subproject 2.2. This project was completed and entered its operational phase in 2020.
Consequently, there is no overlapping heavy-duty transport congestion on the rural road
networks for Subproject 2.2. The cumulative interactions with regional projects like WB9 are
instead strictly operational and synergistic with positive impacts such as improved surface water
quality, regional flood retention capacity.
+   Cumulative Degradation and Safety Risks: The concurrent deployment of heavy transport
    vehicles will drastically exceed the permitted weight and traffic volume of rural roads. This
    cumulative pressure will rapidly cause road surface subsidence, cracking, and potholing.
    Furthermore, the increased traffic density, combined with the temporary occupation of road
    surfaces for material storage, poses an elevated and simultaneous risk of severe traffic
    accidents and localized traffic jams, directly threatening the safety of local communities
    and vulnerable groups (e.g., pupils).
+   Operational Phase Impacts: primarily rely on the newly built bridges over the locks (such
    as the Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, and Cái Hóp structures) which will improve local connectivity,
    the induced economic development and increased agricultural transport utilizing these new
    crossings will permanently alter local traffic flows. This will lead to a long-term, albeit
    gradual, increase in regional vehicular emissions (GHGs). Waterway will not be freely
    transported as before, instead the open and closure of the sluice gates will be regulated
    during the operation. However, it still has to ensure the no traffic congestion.
+   Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): As mentioned, to reduce the transportation cost
    and take advantage of popular inland waterway transport, MERIT project will prioritize the
    inland waterway transport, the construction will be executed over 2-3 year period.
    Additionally, the Southern Hậu River Water Control Project is also implemented by MAE
    (same project owner with MERIT) and will be coordinated to regulate the construction
    density to avoid peak construction period. As such, this cumulative impact will be rated
    Moderate.
VEC 5: Social Infrastructure and Labor Influx
+   Predicted Future Condition: The simultaneous implementation of the four MERIT
    subprojects, alongside external regional developments, requires a massive influx of
    construction workers mobilized from outside the region. This concurrent labor influx
    places acute, simultaneous stress on local public service provisions, notably water supply,
    electricity, and the localized healthcare system. Furthermore, bringing hundreds of outside


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                189
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    workers into rural communes elevates the risks of communicable disease transmission,
    social conflicts with host communities, and high-consequence incidents of Gender-Based
    Violence (GBV) and Sexual Exploitation and Abuse / Sexual Harassment (SEA/SH).
+   Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): Given the limited absorptive capacity of rural
    health stations and the heightened vulnerability of local communities, the cumulative social
    pressures and labor influx risks are evaluated as Moderate.
VEC 6: Regional Livelihoods and Water-Use:
+   Present Condition (RCIA Step 3): The region is currently defined by a fragile, climate-
    stressed livelihood baseline. Farmers in the Long Phú – Tiếp Nhật sub-region cultivate
    specialty fruit orchards (e.g., longan, pomelo) and rice, which are highly vulnerable to fatal
    saline intrusion during the dry season. Downstream, the Mỹ Xuyên area has developed
    intensive industrial shrimp farming, which requires stable brackish water ideally
    maintained above 7 g/L) to prevent environmental shock and aquatic diseases.
+    Predicted Future Condition: (RCIA Step 4) The operation of the infrastructure complex
    generates a profound dual-impact on regional livelihoods:
+   Negative Impacts (Bottlenecks and Water-Use Conflicts): The locks will inevitably
    become physical bottlenecks for interprovincial waterway transport, increasing transit
    times and costs for agricultural supply chains. More critically, severe water-use conflicts
    will arise. If gates are fully closed to protect upstream fruit orchards from salinity, it cuts
    off the freshwater supply from the Hậu River; during peak hot months, this can cause
    salinity in the Mỹ Xuyên shrimp ponds to spike to lethal levels. Conversely, opening gates
    to dilute shrimp ponds risks destroying the upstream freshwater crops.
+   Significant Positive Impacts (Freshwater Security and Salinity Optimization):
    Cumulatively, the enclosed system will successfully prevent deep saltwater intrusion from
    the Hậu River (especially in extreme drought years), proactively securing the livelihoods
    of farmers across 51,500 hectares of cultivation area in the Long Phú – Tiếp Nhật sub-
    region. Concurrently, by restricting freshwater outflow, the system allows salinity in the
    Cổ Cò River to remain above 7 g/L even during low tides. This is a massive positive
    cumulative impact, as it provides the key shrimp farming areas in My Xuyen with ideal,
    stable saline conditions, drastically reducing the risk of shrimp mortality due to thermal
    shock or disease.
+   Predicted Future Condition: (RCIA Step 4) The construction of the 16.6 km coastal wave-
    reduction embankments (designed 100–150 meters offshore) directly intersects the state-
    leased water surface areas of four active clam farming cooperatives: An Thủy and Tân
    Thủy Cooperatives (Bến Tre province), and Phương Đông and Trường Long Hòa
    Cooperatives (Trà Vinh province). These farming plots are typically located 150m to 300m
    from the shore and extend up to 2km seaward.
+   Short-Term Construction Impacts (Negative):
Water Quality and Habitat Disturbance: Construction activities—specifically the driving of 10-
12m long reinforced concrete piles, riprap placement, and barge movements—will heavily
disturb the seabed and generate localized spikes in water turbidity. This will directly disrupt the
clam habitats and feeding capabilities within the immediate vicinity of the alignment.
Scale of Livelihood Disruption: Because a standard clam farming cycle lasts 16 to 20 months,
the construction schedule will unavoidably disrupt at least one full production cycle.
Magnitude of Spatial Loss: Despite the disruption, the permanent spatial loss is mathematically
minimal. The preliminary Inventory of Loss (IOL) confirms that permanent land acquisition for
the embankments affects only 13 hectares out of the 1,007.5 hectares leased by these four

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                 190
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

cooperatives, accounting for a mere 1.29% of their total aquaculture area. The total temporarily
and permanently affected farming area during construction is estimated at 24 hectares.
+   Long-Term Operational Impacts (Positive): During the operational phase, the wave-
    breakers will effectively dissipate wave energy and stimulate sediment accretion behind
    the structures. This will restore the coastal mangrove belts and ultimately stabilize the
    hydrodynamic environment, creating a highly favorable, climate-resilient baseline for the
    cooperatives to sustain and expand future clam farming operations.
+   Significance Evaluation (RCIA Step 5): The project delivers a Positive cumulative impact
    for regional economic security but carries a Substantial social risk of inter-community
    conflict if the operational trade-offs are poorly managed and communicated.
6.5. Transboundary Impacts Assessment (OP 7.50 International Waterways)
The World Bank's Operational Policy 7.50 (Projects on International Waterways) applies to any
river, canal, lake, or similar body of water that forms a boundary between, or flows through,
two or more states, as well as any tributary or other body of surface water that is a component
of such waterways. To ensure compliance with the OP 7.50 and address the hydrological
complexity of the Mekong Delta, this section comprehensively evaluates the transboundary
implications of the MERIT Project. The assessment is structured to evaluate hydrological
connectivity, project use, hydraulic impacts, and international obligations.
6.5.1 Mapping Hydrological Connections to the Mekong River System
    •   Identification and Characteristics of Relevant Water Bodies:
The project operates within a complex hydro-spatial network of both natural and man-made
systems at the terminal edge of the Mekong Basin:
International Transboundary Waterways: The Mekong River is a transboundary watercourse
that travels approximately 4,750 to 4,900 kilometers from its source on the Tibetan Plateau,
flowing through six riparian states: China, Myanmar, Lao PDR, Thailand, Cambodia, and
Vietnam. As the river enters the downstream delta region near Phnom Penh in Cambodia, it
naturally bifurcates into two major distributaries: the Mekong main branch and the Bassac River
(Mekong River Commission (2017) Transboundary Water Resources Management Issues In
the Mekong Delta).
Domestic Natural Rivers (Intermediate Systems): Upon crossing the Cambodian-Vietnamese
border, these two primary transboundary branches become known as the Tiền River (originating
from the Mekong) and the Hậu River (originating from the Bassac). As they approach the East
Sea, the Tiền River further subdivides into six smaller distributaries (which includes the Cổ
Chiên River), while the Hậu River splits into three. These main branches are interconnected by
a massive, complex network of both natural rivers and approximately 66,000 kilometers of
man-made canals that define the Mekong Delta's hydrology. The Vàm Cỏ Tây River acts as a
major domestic inland waterway.
Domestic Man-Made Canals: The project directly intervenes in heavily regulated, man-made
primary and secondary irrigation canals, specifically the Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam, Cho Bung,
Hồng Ngự, and Nguyen Van Tiep A canals in the Đong Thap Mười area, as well as the 19/5
canal, Tra Ngoa canal, and regulating canals in the Nam Mang Thit and lower Hau River areas.
Hydrological Connectivity Analysis: Project Site → Intermediate Systems → International
Waterway)
The MERIT project infrastructure is situated at the terminal nodes of this international
hydrological network, precisely at the downstream coastal and estuarine zones of the Mekong



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                               191
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

Delta, specifically within Vinh Long, Đong Thap, Tay Ninh, and Can Tho, physically
disconnected from upstream international boundaries:
The proposed infrastructure:
The wave-breaker embankments (Subproject 2.1) are located at the absolute coastal boundary
where these distributaries discharge into the East Sea.
Subprojects 2.3 and 2.4 (Ship Locks and Regional Sluices): Dai Ngai and My Xuyen and the
integrated regional sluice gates (Nam Măng Thít system) are positioned directly on the lower
reaches of the Hau River, the Co Chien River, and their secondary regulating canals. The Hau
and oổ Chien Rivers are the final distributaries of the Mekong River before it discharges into
the East Sea. Upstream, these rivers trace back to the Bassac and Mekong branches at the
Cambodian border.
Subproject 2.2 (Inland Canal Dredging): Dredging interventions occur within the man-made
Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals in the Dong Thap Muoi irrigation system.
Hydrologically, these canals regulate and distribute water within the local area and connect to
main canals (such as Hong Ngu Canal and Nguyen Van Tiep A Canal) before discharging into
the Vam Co Tay River, part of the inland river system. Dredging under this project primarily
aims to maintain and restore the original cross-section of the canals, remove sedimentation, and
improve water conveyance and drainage, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the existing
irrigation system. These interventions do not change flow directions, involve water extraction,
inter-basin transfer, or alter the discharge or hydrological regime of receiving river systems.
Flow Direction, Tidal Influences, and Distance to International Connections
Flow Direction and Tidal Influence: The dominant, gravity-driven hydrological flow of the Tiền,
Hậu, and Co Chien rivers is strictly unidirectional, discharging forcefully from the upstream
basin into the East Sea. While tidal influences from the East Sea push saline water upstream
during the dry season, empirical hydrodynamic modeling (MIKE 11/21) provided from the FS
team, confirms this tidal and salinity intrusion is strictly bounded to a maximum penetration of
65 to 68 kilometers from the river mouths.
Distance to International Connection: The following details the characteristics, hydraulic
connections, and exact waterway distances to the international boundary for each subproject:
+ Subproject 2.1:
Names of Water Bodies: The primary water body is the nearshore coastal waters of the East
Sea (specifically along Thoi Thuan, Tan Thuy, Dong Hai, and Truong Long Hoa), which are
sandwiched between the mouths of major estuaries, namely the Cổ Chiên River, the Hau River,
and the Ham Luong River.
Characteristics: These are natural, open coastal marine and estuarine environments. The waters
here are characterized by a vast surface area heavily influenced by large tidal ranges, wind, and
coastal waves, functioning as the ultimate receiving waters for the inland river networks.
Hydraulic Connectivity: The wave-reduction embankments will be constructed approximately
100 to 150 meters offshore in the East Sea. These coastal waters are dynamically connected to
the adjacent inland river networks through a strong semi-diurnal tidal regime. Specifically,
high-resolution hydrological mapping verifies that the construction sites at Truong Long Hoa
(Vinh Long) are directly connected to the Hậu River system via a 12.4-kilometer coastal-
estuarine path. In Vinh Long, the coastal sites interact directly with the Co Chien, Ham Luong,
and Ba Lai rivers.
Ultimate Connection to Mekong River & Distance to Border: The wave-breaker embankments
are located at the absolute coastal boundary where the main distributaries of the Mekong River


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                                192
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

discharge into the East Sea. The Co Chien and Ham Luong rivers are direct downstream
distributaries of the Tien River (the main Mekong branch), while the Hau River is the
downstream continuation of the Bassac branch. Because these project sites are situated at the
absolute downstream terminus of the basin where it meets the ocean, they are physically located
approximately 150 to 200 kilometers downstream from the Cambodian border (the nearest
upstream connection point to the international waterway).
+ Subproject 2.2:
Names of Water Bodies: Phuoc Xuyen – Hai Tam Canal and Cho Bung Canal.
Characteristics: These are heavily regulated, man-made, domestic primary and secondary
irrigation canals.
Hydraulic Connectivity: These canals regulate and distribute water within the local area,
connecting to main regional canals (such as the Hồng Ngự and Nguyễn Văn Tiếp A canals)
before ultimately discharging into the Vam Co Tay River and the Tien River.
Ultimate Connection to Mekong River & Distance to Border: It originates from the Tien River
(in the vicinity of Hong Ngu Town), traverses Dong Thap province. The distance (As the crow
flies): from the Hai Tam intersection to the Vietnam-Cambodia international border
(specifically the northern boundary of Tan Hong District near the Dinh Ba border gate) is
approximately 20 km. The upstream starting point of this dredging section is located 10
kilometers from its confluence with the Tien River in Hong Ngu. This confluence point is
situated approximately 30 km downstream from the Tan Chau hydrological station (which
marks the entry point of the Tien River at the international border). Therefore, the total
continuous waterway distance from the nearest project intervention site to the international
transboundary watercourse is 40 kilometers.
+ Subproject 2.3:
Names of Water Bodies: Saintard River (location of Đại Ngãi lock) and Bãi Xàu River (location
of Mỹ Xuyên lock).
Characteristics: These are natural, domestic secondary and tertiary estuarine river branches.
Hydraulic Connectivity: The Saintard and Bãi Xàu rivers act as major tidal channels connecting
inland agricultural areas to the main Hậu River.
Ultimate Connection to Mekong River & Distance to Border: Both rivers discharge directly
into the Hau River (the Bassac branch of the Mekong River). The Dai Ngai lock construction
site is located 2.95 kilometers from the Hau River, while the My Xuyen lock is located 29
kilometers from the Hậu River. From these confluence points, the Hau River flows completely
within Vietnamese territory for approximately 150 to 200 kilometers upstream before reaching
the Cambodian border.
+ Subproject 2.4:
Names of Water Bodies: Măng Thít River, Cái Hóp River, and associated internal canals (Tra
Ngoa, Sa Rai, Cai Ca, Muong Khai Lon, Bưng Truong, Say Đon).
Characteristics: A complex network of natural domestic secondary rivers and man-made
tertiary regulating canals.
Hydraulic Connectivity: This dense network connects the inland agricultural zones of Vĩnh
Long province to the surrounding main river boundaries.
Ultimate Connection to Mekong River and Distance to Border: The system connects directly to
both the Co Chien River and the Hau River. The Cai Hop navigation lock is located
approximately 900 meters from the Co Chien River. The internal Nam Mang Thit sluices are
located over 20 kilometers from the Cổ Chiên River and over 10 kilometers from the Hậu River.
From these respective confluence points, the Co Chien and Hau Rivers extend approximately

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)
                                               193
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

150 to 200 kilometers upstream along the main channel before connecting to the transboundary
boundary with Cambodia.
Because the maximum tidal influence is 65-68 km, it is physically and hydrologically
impossible for any project-induced backwater or dredging plume to propagate 150-200 km
upstream against the unidirectional river gradient into Cambodian territory.
A detailed hydro-spatial map (Figure 6.7) has been developed to demonstrate this connectivity,
confirming that all project sites are located at the absolute downstream terminus of the
international basin. It visually proves the project is situated where the unidirectional flow of the
Mekong system reaches the sea, making it physically impossible for construction impacts or
operational water retention to hydraulically propagate hundreds of kilometers upstream into
Cambodian territory.

6.5.2 Assessment of Potential Transboundary Impacts
The MERIT project involves the construction of major Category I water control works,
including sequential ship locks (Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, Cái Hóp), regional sluice gates (Nam
Măng Thít), coastal wave breakers, and the extensive dredging of inland canals (e.g., the Phước
Xuyên – Hai Tâm and Chợ Bưng canals). To determine OP 7.50 applicability, these activities
are evaluated strictly regarding their water extraction and potential to pollute the international
basin.
Identification of Project Activities that May Result in Impacts:
Water extraction and discharge:
+ Construction Phase: With an estimated average workforce of 60 to 80 workers mobilized
  per construction site, the total domestic water demand for workers is strictly limited to
  approximately 3 to 4 m³ per day per site. The total water requirement for direct construction


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               194

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    activities is estimated at approximately 7 m³ per day per site. The project will only extract
    a highly limited volume of water (sourced from local surface water or groundwater) and
    prioritize the market supply to ensure the technical requirement for on-site concrete mixing
    and domestic use for construction workers. And discharge of treated construction
    wastewater into local secondary canals.
+ Operational Phase: The permanent sluice gates and ship locks are designed exclusively to
  regulate local tidal fluctuations, prevent abnormal seawater intrusion, and secure
  freshwater for localized agricultural production within the enclosed deltaic zone. The
  infrastructure does not divert water out of the Mekong basin, nor does it involve the
  consumptive extraction of transboundary river flows. Therefore, the project constitutes no
  transboundary "water use" that would deprive upstream riparians of water quantity.
  Operationally, the project manages water retention but does not abstract water for
  consumptive out-of-basin transfers.
    Dredging and River Training, Earthworks: Extensive inland canal dredging (Subproject
    2.2), specifically the Phước Xuyên – Hai Tâm and Chợ Bưng canals, generating localized
    dredged materials and turbidity
    Construction of Sluices, Embankments, and Salinity/Tidal Control Structures: The
    construction and operation of major regional control nodes, including the Đại Ngãi and Mỹ
    Xuyên ship locks (Subproject 2.3), the Nam Măng Thít sluice gates (Subproject 2.4), and
    coastal wave-breakers/embankments interfacing the East Sea (Subproject 2.1).
Assessment of Potential Pollution (Dredging and River Training, Earthworks):
+   Hydrology: changes in flow, water levels, and flow regime
Although the project is located approximately 150 to 200 km from the Cambodian border, to
meet the rigorous requirements of OP 7.50, this assessment based on the empirical
hydrodynamic evidence by utilizing both the MIKE 11 and MIKE 21 hydraulic models for the
Mekong Delta.
Impact of Increased Water Levels Upstream (Backwater Curves): The permanent operation of
the proposed water control infrastructure (such as the Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, and Cái Hóp locks,
and the Nam Măng Thít system) in Subprojects 2.3 & 2.4 requires periodic gate closures to
prevent saline intrusion and manage localized flooding. The primary hydrological alteration
stems from the simultaneous closure of the regional ship locks and sluice gates to retain
freshwater and prevent salinity intrusion. The FS team utilized the MIKE 11 model to simulate
this backwater effect.
Subproject 2.3: Đại Ngãi & Mỹ Xuyên Ship Locks: To rigorously assessment the backwater
phenomenon, specific hydrodynamic calculations were conducted comparing two scenarios:
Scenario 1 (current baseline topography without sluice gates) and Scenario 2 (closure of the
newly constructed sluice gates during high tide). The empirical results demonstrate that the
maximum backwater effect occurs precisely during the rising tide, just as the gates begin to
close to block the incoming sea water. At this peak moment, the water level immediately
upstream of the structures increases by only 5 to 9 cm compared to the baseline conditions. It
is estimated by the FS team that, the backcurve distance from construction area to upstream is
around only 2.2 km (Mỹ Xuyên) and 2.95km (Đại Ngãi).
Subproject 2.4: Nam Măng Thít Irrigation System: The FS strictly utilized the MIKE 11 model
to map the complex canal network dynamics of the Nam Măng Thít area. The model
mathematically proves the water retention is highly localized. Particularly, in Tra Ngoa canal
at K4+000, the average water level in December increases from a baseline of 0.62m (KB-A3)
to 1.07m during operational closure (KB-A4), representing a maximum localized backwater
curve of +0.45 meters. Similarly, Cái Hóp river at K6+000 shows an increase from 0.55m to

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              195

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
0.92m, an increase of +0.37 meters. Overall, the FS confirms that maximum regional variations
peak at just 0.39 meters during the dry season.
+ Ripple Effects Downstream and Upstream under Tidal Influence:
Upstream Ripple Effects and Backwater Distance: Because the rivers (such as the Mang Thit
River canals and the Bai Xau/Saintard Rivers) are secondary or tertiary branches located in a
low-lying coastal depression near the East Sea, this backwater effect is extremely weak.
Hydraulic models simulating the Mekong Delta River network provided from the FS team
confirm that when all gates are closed at peak high tide, the absolute maximum extent of
upstream water extends only up to a maximum 2.9km (Đại Ngãi), right at the confluence of
Hau River, and 2,2km (Mỹ Xuyên) within Bay Xao River in subproject 2.3 while the distance
from My Xuyen gate to Hau River is 29km. Therefore, it is hydrologically impossible to
generate sufficient hydraulic energy to push water upstream into higher-elevation areas, to
propagate 150–200 kilometers upstream (from the confluence of Hau River) into transboundary
territories like Cambodia.
Downstream Ripple Effects (Loss of Tidal Flushing): The most severe cumulative impact of
the MERIT programme occurs during operation, where the simultaneous closure of these locks
establishes a massive enclosed freshwater zone covering over 226,682 hectares. Without the
natural flushing capacity of the daily tides, the interconnected river and canal network will
convert into stagnant freshwater zones. In the absence of tidal ebb and flow, domestic waste,
agricultural fertilizers, and pesticide residues will accumulate densely. This will severely
degrade downstream water quality, driving Dissolved Oxygen (DO) levels below the 4 mg/L
survival threshold for many native aquatic species and triggering toxic algal blooms.
+   Water quality: pollution and pollutant transport
The most severe acute risk during the construction phase during the 24-to-36-month is water
quality degradation. The project involves the massive dredging of inland canals of Phước
Xuyên – Hai Tam, Chợ Bưng of Subproject 2.2 and estuarine riverbeds to restore original cross-
sections,
-   Dredging Scale and Mechanics: The project will execute extensive canal dredging to
    restore flow capacity of Phước Xuyên – Hai Tam, Chợ Bưng, generating approximately
    2.15 to 2.5 million cubic meters of dredged material consisting of soft mud, clayey silt, and
    fine sediments. The direct excavation of the riverbed will disturb natural bottom sediments,
    causing sudden, localized spikes in Turbidity and TSS in the water column.
-   Chemical Contaminants and Acid Sulfate Soils (ASS): While baseline testing confirms
    heavy metals and pesticide residues are below regulatory thresholds, the soils in the Đồng
    Tháp Mười area are characterized by acid sulfate conditions. The agitation of these
    sediments risks remobilizing iron (Fe) and aluminum (Al) into the water column, which
    can temporarily reduce DO levels through the oxidation of Fe²⁺ and cause localized
    acidification if improperly managed.
-   Hydrological Proof Against Transboundary Pollution: Although the project generates
    substantial localized pollution (TSS, Fe²⁺, and wastewater), it poses zero risk of
    transboundary pollution. According to the contaminant transport models run by the FS
    Consultant, dredging operations will temporarily elevate TSS concentrations above the
    existing baseline by approximately 0.4 to 0.6 mg/L at the exact dredging locations. The
    spatial distribution model for TSS propagation demonstrates that the area of influence from
    the dredging location spreads out to both sides by approximately 300 to 500 meters along
    the canal. Within this defined mixing zone, suspended solids and turbid plumes are strongly
    localized. The model confirms that beyond this 350 to 450-meter radius, the concentration
    of suspended solids decreases significantly due to rapid dilution and deposition, returning


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              196

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    to near-baseline levels, ensuring that pollutant transport does not trigger widespread or
    transboundary water quality degradation. More importantly, the MERIT dredging and
    construction sites are located at the absolute downstream terminus of the Mekong basin.
    Because the dominant hydrological flow of the Hậu and Cổ Chiên rivers is strictly
    unidirectional—discharging forcefully into the East Sea—it is physically and
    hydrologically impossible for construction wastewater, turbidity plumes, or remobilized
    acid sulfate soils to travel hundreds of kilometers upstream against the river gradient into
    Cambodian territory.
-   Tidal Flushing Mechanics: During construction, turbidity can technically propagate both
    downstream and upstream. However, the dominant baseline flow of the Hậu and Cổ Chiên
    rivers is heavily unidirectional, discharging toward the East Sea.Furthermore, the
    construction area operates under a semi-diurnal tidal regime. While flood tides can push
    water upstream, the maximum distance that water (and any associated dredging pollutants)
    can travel within a single 6-hour tidal cycle is only 15–30 km. When the tide ebbs, the flow
    recedes back to the sea over a much longer distance.
+   Sediment: alterations in sediment transport dynamics will occur across both the
    construction and long-term operational phases:
-   Construction Phase: Sediment plume modeling indicates that the main impact zone is
    within 100–500 m of the construction area (Hayter, E.J., 1986, Modeling of dredged
    material plumes). According to international hydraulic sediment transport models (Van
    Rijn 2020, PIANC 2010, and USACE 2015), high-turbidity zones generated by grab and
    suction dredgers are typically confined to 50–100 m from the excavation source. The
    concentration of suspended solids decreases significantly after 200–300 m and returns to
    near-baseline levels after approximately 300–500 m, depending on local sediment grain
    size.
-   Operational Phase: the long-term operation of multiple large-scale sluice gates will
    fundamentally alter the natural morpho-dynamic balance of the river-sea system. When the
    massive gates are closed to prevent salinity, the sudden reduction in flow velocity creates
    water stagnation. This blockage eliminates the natural flushing force of the tides, causing
    suspended silt and sand to settle rapidly upstream of the gates. Over the infrastructure's 30-
    year operational lifespan, this restricted sediment transport will accelerate the continuous
    deposition of silt and sand, progressively deplete the inland channels and necessitate the
    maintenance dredging.
+   Salinity: saltwater intrusion and shifts in salinity gradients
-   Subproject 2.3: The MIKE 11/21 Advection-Dispersion (AD) module simulated extreme
    drought conditions (e.g., 2019-2020) and mathematically calculated that the critical 4 g/l
    salinity boundary penetrates a maximum of 65 km up the Hậu River from the East Sea. As
    such, The Đại Ngãi and Mỹ Xuyên locks operate strictly within this 65 km lower estuarine
    boundary. The concrete calculation result proves that the maximum localized water
    retention generated by this subproject is merely 0.34 meters. It is physically and
    hydraulically impossible for a 34-centimeter localized water head to generate enough
    kinetic energy to push a backwater curve 150 to 200 kilometers upstream against the
    massive natural discharge of the Hậu River into Cambodian territory.
-   Subproject 2.4: The models calculate that the 4 g/l salinity boundary penetrates a maximum
    of 68 km up the Cổ Chiên River.
Analysis of Impact Propagation
+   Direct, Indirect, and Cumulative Impacts:


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               197

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
Cumulatively, the simultaneous closure of the MERIT sluice gates (alongside existing WB9
infrastructure) creates a massive enclosed freshwater zone covering over 226,682 hectares.
While this locally alters aquatic habitats and intra-field water quality due to the loss of tidal
flushing, the cumulative hydraulic energy generated by the +0.45m maximum backwater
elevation is nominal.
+   Spatial Extent of Impact Propagation (Bidirectional):
The regional hydrodynamics are dominated by a massive, gravity-driven unidirectional flow
forcefully discharging into the East Sea. Bidirectional (tidal) propagation and salinity gradients
are strictly limited to the lower estuarine zone. It is physically and hydrologically impossible
for the localized 500-meter dredging plumes or the 45-centimeter operational backwater curves
to propagate upstream against this powerful unidirectional river gradient. Therefore, the project
causes no appreciable alteration to the water quantity or quality reaching upstream riparians.
Conclusion on Extent of Potential Impacts on Riparian Countries
Based on the empirical MIKE 11 and MIKE 21 modeling results, the MERIT project will not
cause appreciable harm to the water quality, water quantity, or sediment transport of upstream
riparian countries, specifically Cambodia.
+   Upstream Riparians (Cambodia):
As formally concluded in the subproject FS hydrodynamic evaluation: the project operates
without conflict over water resources because it is entirely located at the absolute downstream
terminus of the basin. For Subprojects 2.3, the maximum backwater effect is 5-9 cm with the
distance of 2.2km (My Xuyen) and 2.95 km (Dai Ngai) and Subprojects 2.4 with the maximum
backwater effect is only +0.45m (0.39m is average), which dissipates entirely within from a
few tens of kilometers and cannot traverse the 150-200 km distance to the Cambodian border.
For Subproject 2.2, which is located closer to the border, the natural hydrology flows
downstream from Cambodia into Vietnam; therefore, gravity strictly prevents the 500-meter
localized dredging plume from transporting pollutants backward across the international
boundary.
+   Downstream Riparians:
The project infrastructure is situated at the terminal discharge point of the Mekong Basin into
the East Sea. There are no further downstream riparian states. Consequently, the spatial extent
of all direct, indirect, and cumulative impacts is entirely confined to Vietnamese territory,
thereby securing full compliance for the OP 7.50 Paragraph 7(a) transboundary exception
6.5.3 Assessment of Significance and Compliance with World Bank Operational Policy
7.50
Assessment of "Appreciable Harm" to Riparian States (Technical Justification)
Based on the empirical hydrodynamic modeling conducted during the Feasibility Study, the
MERIT project will not cause "appreciable harm" to the water quality or quantity of other
riparian states (Cambodia or otherwise). This determination is technically justified by the
following modeled constraints:
Water Quantity Limits (MIKE 11): The maximum localized water retention (backwater curve)
generated by the simultaneous closure of the regional ship locks and sluice gates (Subprojects
2.3 and 2.4) peaks between +0.34 meters and +0.45 meters. Located at the downstream terminus
of the basin (150-200 km from the border), this micro-elevation lacks the hydraulic energy to
propagate upstream against the massive unidirectional discharge of the Hậu and Cổ Chiên rivers.
Water Quality Limits (MIKE 11/21 & Empirical Data): Dredging-induced turbidity plumes and
construction wastewater (Subproject 2.2) settle entirely within a strict 300 to 500-meter


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               198

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
localized boundary. Because the regional hydrology of the Đồng Tháp Mười area is gravity-
driven with a unidirectional flow from Cambodia downstream into Vietnam, it is hydrologically
impossible for these localized plumes to travel backward across the international border.
Furthermore, tidal and salinity dynamics altered by the project operate exclusively within a
mathematically proven 65-68 km downstream estuarine boundary, preserving the integrity of
the shared estuary.
Determination of Exception under OP 7.50
Under OP 7.50, the World Bank normally requires the beneficiary state to formally notify other
riparians of any proposed project on an international waterway. The Bank allows exceptions to
this notification requirement for projects that involve additions or alterations that require
rehabilitation, construction, or other changes to any ongoing schemes. Based on the technical
justifications above, the MERIT project strictly qualifies for the riparian notification exception
set out in Paragraph 7(a) of OP 7.50 and Paragraph 7(c) of OP 7.50. To qualify for this exception,
the project must not adversely change the quality or quantity of water flows to other riparians,
must not be adversely affected by other riparians' possible water use, and must not alter or
expand the original scheme's scope to make it appear a new or different scheme.
Based on the empirical evidence, the MERIT project strictly qualifies for the Paragraph 7(a)
exception due to the following legal and environmental determinations:
+   This exception applies exclusively to the upgrading, repair, and replacement of the gates
    for the three existing sluices: Nàng Âm, Mỹ Văn, and Rạch Rùm (subproject 2.4). In
    addtion, the dredging of sediment from inland waterways including the Phước Xuyên – Hai
    Tam and Chợ Bưng canals (subproject 2.2) are strictly classified as rehabilitation and long-
    term maintenance works. They are executed solely to remove decades of accumulated silt
    to restore the original design conveyance capacity of the existing canals for local irrigation,
    drainage, and inland waterway transport. The dredging does not create new river branches,
    exceed the original scheme, or change the nature of the basin to make it appears a new or
    different scheme. Under OP 7.50, Paragraph 7(a) allows exceptions for additions or
    alterations to ongoing schemes that require rehabilitation, provided they do not adversely
    change the quality or quantity of water flows to other riparians and will not be adversely
    affected by other riparians' water use. Because these specific components genuinely
    involve the rehabilitation of existing facilities with no change to their original function or
    capacity, they fully qualify under Paragraph 7(a).
Based on the empirical evidence, the MERIT project strictly qualifies for the Paragraph 7(c)
exception due to the following legal and environmental determinations:
+   The proposed new infrastructure (such as the Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, and Cái Hóp ship locks,
    and coastal wave breakers) are designed to integrate into the Mekong Delta's existing
    regional water control matrix (including the previously completed WB9/MD-ICRSL
    structures). They function as necessary additions and alterations to an ongoing regional
    scheme to manage localized tidal surges and saline intrusion. These infrastructure are
    qualified under Paragraph 7(c). Paragraph 7(c) applies to any project that relates to a
    tributary of an international waterway where the tributary runs exclusively in one state and
    that state is the lowest downstream riparian, provided the project will not cause appreciable
    harm to other states:
-   Exclusive Domestic Tributaries: The aforementioned new investments are located on
    secondary or tertiary tributaries (such as the Bãi Xàu, Saintard, and Măng Thít rivers) and
    internal canal networks that run exclusively within Vietnamese territory.
-   Lowest Downstream Riparian: Vietnam is the lowest downstream riparian state of the
    Mekong River basin discharging into the East Sea.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                199

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-     No Appreciable Harm: Empirical hydrodynamic modeling (MIKE 11 and MIKE 21)
      confirms that because the project is located at the downstream terminus of the basin (150-
      200 km from the border) under a gravity-driven flow regime towards the sea, it is physically
      and hydrologically impossible for the localized operational backwater curves (+ 0.34m to
      +0.45m). Therefore, the new infrastructure will not cause appreciable harm to the quantity
      or quality of water in upstream riparian countries such as Cambodia.
Because the MERIT project consists of rehabilitation works and additions to existing schemes
that cause no upstream transboundary water quality or quantity adverse impacts on Cambodia
or Laos, the project unambiguously qualifies for the riparian notification exception set out in
Paragraph 7(a) and Paragraph 7(c) of OP 7.50.
Vietnam’s Obligations under the 1995 Mekong Agreement (MRC)
While the project secures an exception from notification under the World Bank's internal OP
7.50 policy, the Government of Vietnam remains bound by the 1995 Mekong Agreement.
Under the Mekong River Commission's Procedures for Notification, Prior Consultation and
Agreement (PNPCA), mandatory notification or prior consultation is legally triggered by
specific activities, most notably inter-basin diversions or significant consumptive water
abstractions from the mainstream.
Because the MERIT project’s infrastructure consists exclusively of non-consumptive, tidal-
control sluice gates, navigation locks, and rehabilitation dredging located on domestic
secondary and tertiary tributaries, it does not trigger the mandatory PNPCA notification
thresholds. Consequently, there is no legal requirement to submit a formal project dossier to the
MRC Secretariat as a condition of compliance for this project. Any future sharing of project
hydrodynamic data with the MRC will be conducted at Vietnam's discretion to foster voluntary
regional cooperation under the Procedures for Data and Information Exchange and Sharing
(PDIES), and is entirely independent of the World Bank's OP 7.50 clearance process.
6.6. Climate Change Impacts
The updated 2020 Climate Change Scenario issued by the Ministry of Natural Resources and
Environment is used to assess impacts on the MERIT Project in the Mekong Delta. The
differences between the RCP4.5 (medium–low emission) and RCP8.5 (high emission)
scenarios are analyzed in relation to the Project’s objectives and infrastructure components.
6.6.1. Overview of Technical Parameters by Scenario (Mekong Delta Region)
Based on data from the 2020 updated Climate Change Scenario report, by mid-century (2046–
2065), the following key parameters are projected:
