Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                    236
                          Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    ES Issues                                       Mitigation Measures                                         Standards, Regulations/     Supervised
                                                                                                                      Guidelines               by
                  -  A designated person with adequate qualifications and experience shall be responsible       rescue and salvage; and
                     for managing worker accommodation. Where contractors are engaged, clear contract           prevention and control of
                     management responsibilities and supervision and reporting requirements shall be            domestic violence
                     established.                                                                             - Worker Accommodation:
                 - Local community members may be recruited for cleaning, cooking, and general                  Processes and Standards
                     maintenance tasks. Staff shall receive basic training on health and safety.                (IFC and EBRD)
                 - A community relations plan shall be established, including communication
                     mechanisms to enable regular information exchange and consultation with local
                     communities, allowing for timely identification and response to issues and maintaining
                     good working relationships. The site manager shall be responsible for implementing
                     this plan.
                 - Impacts of worker accommodation on local communities shall be assessed and
                     mitigated.
                 Accommodation Fees and Services:
                 - Where fees are charged, workers shall be provided with clear information and detailed
                     descriptions of all payments such as rent, deposits, and other charges. Rental
                     arrangements shall be fair and represent only a small proportion of workers’ income.
                 - Where company-provided housing is considered part of workers’ wages, best practice
                     requires that workers be provided with employment contracts clearly specifying
                     housing arrangements, including rules on payments and fees, facilities and services
                     provided, and notice requirements.
                 - Food and other services shall be either free of charge or reasonably priced, not
                     exceeding local market prices.
                 Health, Safety, and Security Operations:
                 - Health and safety management plans, including electrical safety, equipment operation,
                     disease prevention, and food safety, shall be carefully designed and implemented.
                     Emergency plans for health and safety and fire protection shall be prepared, including
                     additional plans for specific local incidents (e.g., earthquakes, floods, storms).
                 - Fire prevention and firefighting plans shall be established, including staff training,
                     regular inspection and monitoring of firefighting equipment, and periodic drills.
                 - Guidance on the harmful effects of alcohol and drug abuse and risks related to
                     HIV/AIDS and other health risks shall be provided to workers, with clear policies
                     established. Workers shall have access to adequate preventive measures such as
                     contraception and mosquito nets.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                   237
                          Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    ES Issues                                       Mitigation Measures                                           Standards, Regulations/   Supervised
                                                                                                                        Guidelines             by
                  - Workers shall have easy access to medical facilities and healthcare personnel. Where
                    possible, female doctors/nurses should be available for female workers.
                 - Clear measures shall be in place to ensure site and worker security, such as 24/7
                    security personnel. Where appropriate, both male and female security staff shall be
                    employed.
                 - Security personnel shall have clearly defined duties and receive clear instructions on
                    their roles and responsibilities, particularly the obligation not to harass, intimidate, or
                    discriminate against workers. They shall understand the importance of respecting
                    workers’ rights and community rights and behave appropriately.
                 - Body searches shall only be conducted in specific circumstances and by specially
                    trained personnel using the least intrusive methods possible. Searches of female
                    workers shall only be conducted by female security staff.
                 Workers’ Rights and Living Conditions:
                 - The construction site for the worker camps has been reviewed with the local
                    government, and communities adjacent to the proposed housing areas have been
                    notified prior to establishment.
                 - It is ensured that the camp management plan includes specific provisions for female
                    workers, including separate and lockable toilet facilities, access to female medical staff,
                    and confidential reporting channels for harassment or abuse.
                 - Restrictions on workers’ freedom of movement to and from the accommodation shall
                    be limited and applied only where appropriate or required for work purposes, based on
                    fair agreements. Ideally, workers should have 24/7 access to enter and leave the
                    accommodation. Any restrictions for security reasons shall be balanced with respect to
                    workers’ freedom of movement.
                 - Trade union representatives shall be granted access to workers at their accommodation.
                    A worker consultation mechanism shall be designed and implemented; preferably, a
                    committee including elected worker representatives shall be established.
                 - Workers’ gender, religion, cultural, and social practices shall be respected. In
                    particular, workers shall be facilitated to observe holidays and religious practices.
                 - Workers shall be informed of their rights and obligations and provided with copies of
                    internal rules, procedures, and disciplinary mechanisms in a language or format they
                    understand. Designated smoking areas shall be provided.
                 - Fair and non-discriminatory procedures shall be in place for disciplinary actions,
                    including the right of workers to defend themselves.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                      238
                           Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    ES Issues                                        Mitigation Measures                                              Standards, Regulations/   Supervised
                                                                                                                            Guidelines             by
                  Grievance Channel Setup:
                   - Procedures and mechanisms for workers to raise grievances shall be made available.
                   - Workers and surrounding community members shall have channels to raise concerns
                      regarding security and personnel. Community representatives shall be provided with
                      accessible means to express opinions and grievances.
                   - It is confirmed that the Community Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) is publicly
                      posted in Vietnamese around the camp area, with clearly defined contact persons and
                      response timelines. Regular community liaison meetings — at a minimum monthly —
                      are organized between the camp management and local community representatives to
                      identify and resolve emerging concerns before they escalate.
                  3. REMEDIATION
                   - The person in charge of the camp shall report to health authorities any outbreaks of
                      infectious diseases, food poisoning, and other serious incidents.
                   - Workers subject to disciplinary actions arising from behavior at the accommodation
                      shall have access to fair and transparent hearings, with the ability to appeal against
                      decisions and refer disputes to relevant public authorities.
                   - In cases of conflict among workers or between workers and staff, workers shall have
                      easy access to fair conflict resolution mechanisms.
                   - In more serious cases, including severe physical or mental abuse, mechanisms shall be
                      in place to ensure full cooperation with law enforcement authorities (if applicable).
                   - A transparent and effective process shall be in place for addressing community
                      complaints.

                  2. MITIGATION
                   - All contractors operating near recorded cultural heritage sites – including Go Thap and
                      the religious facilities identified in Chapter 6 (Impact Assessment) – receive

                     specialized training on "Chance Find Procedures" for archaeological artifacts prior to
                     the commencement of excavation works.
                  - Pre-construction consultations are conducted with the Go Thap Heritage Management
                     Board and relevant religious institutions to reach an agreement on transportation routes,
                     working hours near construction areas, and emergency communication protocols.
                  - It is ensured that the notification protocol in the event of a discovery includes direct
                     contact with the Commune People’s Committee and the Provincial Department of
                     Culture within 24 hours; this obligation is incorporated into construction contracts as a
                     binding clause.
                  - Provide an initial description of the discovered area or site;
                  - Secure and protect the discovered area. In cases where the find includes movable
                     antiquities or sensitive remains, night-time security arrangements shall be provided
                     until local authorities, the Department of Culture, Sports and Tourism, or the Institute
                     of Archaeology take over;
                  - Notify the Supervision Engineer, who shall immediately inform the Employer, the
                     responsible local authorities, and the Institute of Archaeology (within 24 hours or less);
                 3. REMEDIATION
                  - The competent local authorities shall be responsible for protecting and preserving the
                     site before deciding on appropriate subsequent procedures. This may require the
                     Institute of Archaeology to conduct a preliminary assessment of the findings. The
                     significance and importance of the findings shall be evaluated according to various
                     criteria related to cultural heritage, including aesthetic, historical, scientific/research,
                     social, and economic values;
                  - Decisions on how to handle the findings shall be made by the competent authorities.
                     Such decisions may include changes to the layout (e.g., where the find is an immovable
                     cultural relic or of archaeological significance), preservation, conservation, restoration,
                     and excavation;
                  - Implementation of decisions by the competent authorities regarding the management
                     of the findings shall be communicated in writing by the relevant local authorities.

                 2. MITIGATION                                                                                      Safety in Construction
                 OHS Planning & General Provisions:                                                                 Activities

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                      242
                          Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    ES Issues                                       Mitigation Measures                                            Standards, Regulations/    Supervised
                                                                                                                         Guidelines              by
                  -  The Contractor shall prepare an OHS Management Plan as part of the C-ESMP.                  - WBG EHS Guidelines for
                  -  Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): Use PPE where hazards cannot be eliminated or            Construction         and
                     sufficiently reduced. Identify and provide appropriate PPE. Maintain PPE properly,            Demolition
                     clean when contaminated, and replace when damaged. PPE use shall be part of regular         - ESS2.
                     training.
                 - Structural failure risks: Assess conditions of nearby structures at each construction site
                     to prevent collapse. Ensure all lifting, supporting, and transport systems are stable and
                     safe, and approved by the CSC and CPO before use.
                 Ergonomics, Falls, and Struck-by Hazards:
                 - Heavy manual work: Train workers in lifting and material handling techniques during
                     construction and dismantling, including setting weight limits above which mechanical
                     assistance or multiple workers are required. Provide occupational safety training for
                     workers. Implement administrative controls in work processes, such as job rotation and
                     rest breaks.
                 - Slips, trips and falls: Provide adequate PPE. Regularly clean debris and spilled
                     liquids. Identify and mark electrical cables and ropes in common areas and walkways.
                     Use anti-slip footwear.
                 - Working at height: Install guardrails or other barriers capable of withstanding loads,
                     or use safety harnesses when working at heights of 2 meters or more, or at any height
                     where there is a risk of falling into operating machinery, water or other liquids,
                     hazardous substances, or through openings in working surfaces. Install signage and
                     safety monitoring systems to warn workers of fall hazards, deep pits, and ensure
                     protection, marking, warning signs, and covering floor, roof, or excavation openings
                     along access routes.
                 - Struck-by hazards: Limit movement speed on site. Provide appropriate PPE such as
                     safety glasses with side shields, face shields, hard hats, and safety shoes.
                 Machinery, Electrical & Specialized Operations:
                 - Moving machinery: Plan and manage traffic flow, establish speed limits, and assign
                     trained personnel wearing reflective clothing to direct traffic. Use lifting equipment
                     that is properly inspected, maintained, and suitable for the load.
                 - Confined spaces: Prior to entry, ensure workers are adequately trained in confined
                     space hazard control, atmospheric testing, PPE use, and that rescue plans and
                     equipment are in place. Provide safety measures such as ventilation.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                     243
                          Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

    ES Issues                                       Mitigation Measures                                         Standards, Regulations/   Supervised
                                                                                                                      Guidelines             by
                  -  Electrical safety: Mark all live electrical equipment and lines with warning signs.
                     Inspect all wires, cables, and hand tools for damage and comply with manufacturer's
                     recommendations for voltage limits. Insulate/ground all electrical equipment used in
                     damp conditions and use circuit breakers with leakage protection. Protect cables from
                     damage by shielding or suspending them above traffic areas. Properly label high-
                     voltage service areas (“electrical hazard”).
                  - Eye hazards & Welding: Use machine guards, splash shields, and eye/face protection
                     such as safety glasses, goggles, or face shields. Implement Safe Operating Procedures
                     (SOPs) for grinding, sanding, or working with liquid chemicals. Regularly inspect
                     protective equipment before use. Provide over-glasses for workers who wear
                     prescription lenses. Provide appropriate eye protection such as welding goggles or full-
                     face shields for all personnel involved in welding. Implement fire prevention measures
                     including fire extinguishers and training.
                 Environmental & Chemical Stressors:
                  - Thermal stress: Adjust work/rest cycles. Provide temporary rest areas. Provide
                     protective clothing. Supply adequate drinking water or electrolyte drinks.
                  - Fire and explosion: Storage areas shall have natural or passive ventilation at floor and
                     ceiling levels and use spark-proof equipment. Provide training on handling flammable
                     materials and fire prevention/extinguishing.
                  - Corrosive, oxidizing and reactive chemicals: Workers handling such chemicals shall
                     be specially trained and provided with appropriate PPE (gloves, aprons, protective
                     suits, face shields, goggles).
                 3. REMEDIATION
                  - Confined space rescue: Access for rescue operations shall be provided where feasible.
                     Areas near confined space entries shall allow sufficient room for emergency and rescue
                     activities. Safety measures shall include self-contained breathing apparatus, lifelines,
                     and standby personnel positioned outside confined spaces with rescue and first aid
                     equipment ready.
                  - Chemical & Fire Emergencies: Ensure adequate first aid facilities, including
                     emergency eyewash and showers near workstations. Storage areas (for flammable
                     materials) shall be equipped with firefighting equipment.
                  - Damage Compensation: Establish a basis for compensation if damage occurs (related
                     to structural failure risks).

7.1.3.2. Site-Specific Mitigation Measures for Wave-Reduction Embankment Construction
    (Subproject 2.1)

 This entire section shall be incorporated into the bidding documents for the construction
 package of the wave-reduction (sediment accretion) embankments.

The wave-reduction embankments under Subproject 2.1 in coastal communes of Vinh Long
Province are of small scale, applying spun concrete pile technology, with minimal dredging and
foundation works.
The project uses a large number of materials, it is recommended that the contractor use recycled
materials or reuse materials that meet technical and financial requirements.
Materials need to be purchased from reputable suppliers or licensed mines.
Impacts during the construction phase mainly arise from material transportation, pile driving,
and rock placement. Although the level of impact is not significant, the contractors shall
implement the following measures during construction:
a. Management of material sources and supply chains
-   Verify the validity of mineral extraction licenses of sand and stone quarries prior to entering
    into contracts with suppliers.
-   Materials need to be purchased from reputable suppliers or licensed mines. Encourage the
    use of ready-mix concrete to reduce the demand for clean water at the construction site.
The project uses a large amount of materials, it is recommended that the contractor use recycled
materials or reuse materials that meet technical and financial requirements. Submit to the
Project Owner/Construction Supervision Consultant documentary evidence of the legal origin
for each batch of materials (invoices, delivery notes from licensed quarries) prior to
mobilization to the construction site.
-   Storage of construction materials and wave-reduction embankment components:
    Coordinate with local authorities and communities to agree on locations and areas for
    material stockpiling to avoid encroachment on residential land, commercial land, and
    aquaculture areas. Ensure proper security and safety management of material storage areas.
-   Remove improperly stored materials and restore any affected land to its original condition
    in case of encroachment or mismanagement.
b. Transportation of materials, pile components, and rock by barges or vessels:
-   Ensure environmental safety during transportation: verify that transport vessels meet
    technical standards and prevent fuel leakage; avoid vessel collisions.
-   Strictly manage construction vessels and barges; discharge of oily residues and solid waste
    directly into the sea is strictly prohibited.
-   Establish an emergency response plan for oil spills to prevent the spread of pollutants over
    a wide area. This plan must be in place at the construction site prior to the commencement
    of construction activities, and shall include, at a minimum, the following:
+ Maintain at least 200 m of oil containment booms, 500 oil absorbent pads, and approved
  dispersants at the floating equipment staging area.
+ Establish an emergency communication chain (response time < 30 minutes) with the
  Project Management Unit, the Coast Guard/Border Guard, and the Provincial marine
  environmental incident response authority.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                248

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
+ In the event of an incident, prioritize the deployment of containment booms to protect
  existing mangrove forests, protection forests, and clam farming areas, and establish a no-
  go buffer zone around these sensitive ecosystems.
c. At wave-reduction embankment installation locations:
-   Install clear warning signs and navigation lights at night to ensure visibility for waterway
    traffic and prevent collisions.
-   In case of increased turbidity near aquaculture areas, install floating barriers to contain
    turbidity dispersion.
-   Rock placement and installation of structural components shall be conducted gradually and
    with controlled speed to minimize suspended sediment dispersion.
-   To ensure absolute safety for wind power infrastructure, the Contractor shall implement
    the following measures:
+ Obtain official written confirmation from wind farm developers regarding the as-built
  alignment of submarine cable routes.
+ Conduct surveys using cable detection equipment or specialized scanning devices in areas
  near submarine cables to determine their exact coordinates and burial depth.
+ Establish an Exclusion Zone with a minimum radius of 50 m around the identified cable
  routes.
+ No piling, anchoring, or any seabed-intrusive activities shall be carried out within this
  exclusion zone.
d. Implementation of biodiversity impact mitigation measures
-   Establish strict construction boundaries to limit the extent of seabed disturbance beyond
    the designed footprint of the dike body.
-   Sequence the construction of wave-reduction structures and groynes to optimize sediment
    trapping during construction:
+ Construct parallel breakwaters sections first: prioritize building sections parallel to the
  shoreline in areas experiencing the highest erosion rates to reduce incoming wave energy.
+ Subsequently construct groynes: 100 m-long groynes perpendicular to the shoreline shall
  be constructed in an alternating pattern to “lock” longshore currents and create sediment
  traps.
+ Construct in the direction of sediment transport: based on hydrodynamic modeling,
  construction should commence from the upstream side of sediment transport and progress
  downstream so that sediments carried by currents are retained as each new section is formed.
-   Once the dike section has created a sheltered water zone behind it, material transport barges
    shall operate on the seaward side of the dike, avoiding entry into accretion zones to prevent
    disturbance of newly deposited sediments.
e. During construction: There may be risks to worker health and safety and potential structural
damage due to sea waves. To mitigate these impacts, the contractors shall:
-   Provide adequate personal protective equipment (PPE), including life jackets.
-   Train workers on safety measures for marine construction activities.
-   Ensure first aid equipment is always available on site.
-   Construction shall not be carried out during natural disasters or hazardous weather
    conditions (in accordance with Subsection 2.1, Section 2 of the National Technical

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              249

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    Regulation on Safety in Construction issued together with Circular No.16/2021/TT-BXD),
    specifically:
+ During tropical depressions, storms, or strong winds of Beaufort force 5 or higher (as
  defined in QCVN 02:2009/BXD);
+ During thunderstorms, tornadoes, hail, or lightning;
+ When the air temperature exceeds 35°C or falls below 0°C without appropriate specialized
  personal protective equipment to ensure safety;
+ ring heavy rainfall with precipitation of 51 mm/24 hours or 26 mm/12 hours or more;
+ During rough sea conditions (wave height of 2.0 m or higher) or strong currents.
+ In case of incidents, provide immediate first aid and emergency response.
+ Investigate incidents and implement corrective actions to prevent recurrence.
f. Upon completion of wave-reduction embankment installation, all surplus materials and solid
    waste shall be cleared from the site prior to handover to the Project Owner.
7.1.3.3. Site-Specific Mitigation Measures for Dredging Activities (Subprojects 2.2)
At the detailed design stage, the Dredging and Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP)
attached in the Appendix 9 shall be updated and incorporated into the bidding documents. After
contract award, the contractors shall prepare a Contractor’s DMMP based on the Project DMMP
and submit it to the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) and the Project Owner for
review and approval prior to commencement of dredging. The DMMP shall present the volumes
of dredged and excavated materials generated, the physical and biological characteristics of
such materials, a description of dredging methods and procedures, temporary storage solutions,
transportation, and final disposal. Additional mitigation measures are listed below. These shall
also be incorporated into the bidding and contract documents.
(i) Contractor’s management measures for dredging activities:
To manage potential environmental and social impacts, as well as occupational health and
safety risks associated with dredging, including transportation and disposal of dredged
materials during contract implementation, the contractors shall implement and comply with the
following requirements:
        (a) Comply with mitigation measures specified in the detailed design and technical
specifications of the bidding documents, including dredging methods, transportation procedures,
and final disposal. The contractors shall not adopt alternative dredging methods, particularly
hydraulic pumping to discharge slurry directly from channels to the banks, which may result in
greater environmental and social risks and impacts.
       (b) Comply with all Environmental, Social, Health and Safety (ESHS) provisions in the
bidding documents/contract.
       (c) Appoint ESHS personnel, including those responsible for managing the Grievance
Redress Mechanism (GRM) and the dredging plan, and submit for approval by the Project
Owner.
       (d) Conduct consultations with affected communities and local authorities, including:
Hoa Binh Domestic Water Supply Plant in Phu Cuong Commune; households living along the
canal; aquaculture households using water from Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam Canal (Tay Ninh
Province side); households using water for agricultural purposes in the Cho Bung canal area;
individuals harvesting water hyacinth for handicrafts; river users traveling by boat or ferry, etc.
The Contractor shall avoid or suspend dredging during certain hours of the day to allow local
people to access water from rivers and canals as needed.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                250

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
      (e) Prepare a Contractor’s Emergency Response Plan for incidents such as inland
waterway traffic accidents.
        (g) Register transportation routes, equipment, and construction schedules with relevant
authorities to facilitate inspection and monitoring of dredging, transport, and disposal activities.
Operators of dredging vessels and barges shall be trained and hold valid certifications.
       (h) Provide adequate and appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) to workers
and enforce its use, especially life jackets when working on water. Assign personnel to monitor
each work shift for a timely emergency response.
       (i) Conduct training for workers on ESHS rules prior to commencing work under the
contract.
       (k) Install and maintain warning signs during construction, including floating buoys on
water and signage along riverbanks and canal banks in accordance with the approved plan.
        (l) Establish and demarcate dredging corridors using buoys when dredging vessels and
barges move to new sections of rivers or canals, to minimize risks to inland waterway traffic,
particularly collisions with local boats and fishing gear.
       (m) Install signal lights on dredging vessels and barges.
        (n) Oils and lubricants stored on barges and dredging vessels shall be kept to a minimum,
stored in sealed containers under covered areas, and must not leak into water bodies. Oil-
contaminated materials shall be stored in secure containers for collection by licensed hazardous
waste handlers.
        (o) Inspect and maintain equipment and machinery to ensure they are in good condition
and renew registration before expiry. Major maintenance or repair of excavators shall not be
carried out at dredging sites but at designated maintenance workshops.
         (p) Overloading dredged materials onto barges, compartments, or any transport vehicles
is strictly prohibited.
       (q) Safely remove all equipment and materials introduced to dredging sites (in water
and along riverbanks) upon completion of dredging works.
      (r) Maintain logs and records of ESHS performance and submit monthly ESHS
monitoring reports.
        (s) Notify local authorities and businesses along the dredging routes of the dredging
plan at least one (1) month in advance.
(ii) Air Pollution and Noise Mitigation Measures
Air pollution and noise will be mitigated in accordance with the measures specified in the
ESCOP. In addition, the following measures shall be given due attention:
       (a) If dredging is conducted near residential areas: operations shall be restricted between
10:00 PM and 6:00 AM to avoid causing disturbance to local residents.
        (b) Avoid excessive horn use during dredging. Do not use outdated dredging equipment
that generates high noise levels.
       (c) At dry dredged material disposal sites: materials shall be covered with appropriate
materials to minimize dust dispersion into the environment.
       (d) Do not dispose of foul-smelling dredged sludge near residential areas. In
unavoidable cases, appropriate odor treatment measures and covering shall be implemented to
prevent odor dispersion.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 251

                                                                                      Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
       (e) The contractors shall comply with QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT: National Technical
Regulation on Ambient Air Quality; and QCVN 26:2025/BNNMT: National Technical
Regulation on Noise.
(iii) Water Pollution Mitigation Measures:
In general, water pollution will be mitigated through the measures specified in the ESCOP. In
addition, the following measures shall be given due attention:
       (a) Contractors shall comply with QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT: National Technical
Regulation on Surface Water Quality; and QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT: National Technical
Regulation on Sediment Quality through the monitoring program.
       (b) Contractors shall comply with QCVN 14:2025/BTNMT: National Technical
Regulation on Domestic Wastewater and Urban and Concentrated Residential Area Wastewater;
IFC Guidelines on Domestic Wastewater (World Bank EHS Standards); and QCVN
40:2025/BNNMT: National Technical Regulation on Industrial Wastewater through the
monitoring program.
        (c) Silt curtains or other appropriate sediment control measures shall be used to limit
the dispersion of sediments released during dredging.
       (d) The contractors’ selection of dredging equipment (cutter, suction/hopper, etc.) shall
be appropriate to the depth and type of dredged material.
        (e) Appropriate timing for dredging shall be selected, preferably during the dry season
and at low tide.
        (g) Advanced and low-pollution dredging technologies shall be applied to minimize
disturbance. Dredging shall be carried out from downstream to upstream. Dredging vessels shall
be technically and administratively registered with the Vietnam Register in accordance with
Circular No.16/2023/TT-BGTVT dated June 30, 2023, amending and supplementing
regulations on inland waterway vessel registration. All barges participating in dredging works
shall meet the technical requirements specified in QCVN 17:2011/BGTVT: National Technical
Regulation on Pollution Prevention by Inland Waterway Vessels, particularly with respect to
control of domestic wastewater, solid waste, and oily wastewater.
        (h) Dredged sludge in the initial stage is in liquid form and becomes solid after 24 to 48
hours of settling. All leachate from disposal sites shall be directed to drainage systems and
discharged back into the canal. To minimize environmental impacts from dredged sludge and
to protect canal water quality, dredged materials shall be transported to designated disposal
areas with appropriate design capacity. The dredged material shall be pumped into containment
areas, with overflow directed to a settling pond for suspended solids removal. After a period of
settling, wastewater shall be recirculated back to the canal. Depending on site-specific
conditions, the standard design of containment dikes includes: height of 2 m, base width of 5
m, and crest width of 1 m.
        (i) Prior to dredging, the construction team shall anchor vessels in accordance with
regulations. In adverse weather conditions, such as nighttime, fog, or rainfall with visibility less
than 300 m, the contractors shall install signal lights in compliance with regulations.
        (k) Prior to construction, the Project Owner, in coordination with the contractors, shall
notify the inland waterway management authority, local communities, and local authorities at
least one week in advance to enable them to arrange water abstraction and plan transportation
and daily activities accordingly.
        (l) Excavator buckets shall be operated in accordance with proper procedures, with
controlled lifting speed to reduce sediment dispersion.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 252

                                                                                      Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
       (m) Appropriate modeling shall be applied to simulate hydrodynamic and sediment
transport conditions.
       (n) Storage of used fuel containers on dredging vessels shall comply with the
requirements of the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment regarding hazardous waste
storage.
         (o) Discharge of solid waste, hazardous waste, and wastewater into canals or rivers is
strictly prohibited. Engine oil, used oil, and other hazardous substances shall be collected and
stored in containers at construction sites. Containers must be leak-proof, properly labeled,
stored appropriately, and returned to the supplier.
       (p) Maintenance of dredging vessels and oil replacement shall only be conducted at
designated facilities. Such activities shall not be carried out at dredging locations.
        (q) Dredging vessels shall be equipped with wastewater collection and oil-water
separation systems. Wastewater shall only be discharged when it meets the applicable
Vietnamese technical regulations on industrial wastewater.
       (r) Adequate sanitation facilities shall be provided for workers on board; direct
discharge of human waste into rivers and canals is strictly prohibited.
       (s) Maintenance, oil replacement, and lubrication of vehicles and equipment shall only
be conducted in designated areas. Measures shall be taken to prevent leakage of chemicals, fuel,
and lubricants into soil and water sources. Drip trays shall be used for collecting rags and
maintenance materials. Waste shall be collected and managed in accordance with hazardous
waste management requirements.
        (t) Regular internal environmental monitoring (by the dredging contractors) and
independent environmental monitoring (by an independent environmental consultant engaged
by CPO/Ministry of Agriculture and Environment or the World Bank) shall be conducted to
assess the contractors’ compliance with the World Bank ESS and Vietnamese regulations.
        (u) Regular environmental quality monitoring (including surface water and air quality)
shall be conducted during dredging and disposal activities to assess compliance with the World
Bank ESS and national technical regulations. In cases where water quality does not meet
standards, dredging activities shall be suspended to allow sedimentation until suspended solids
concentrations comply with permissible limits.
       (v) Construction activities shall not be carried out under adverse weather conditions,
such as heavy rain, which may pose risks to navigation safety.
(iv) Mitigation Measures for Impacts on Aquatic Ecosystems Caused by Dredging
       (a) Contractors shall prepare appropriate dredging procedures to minimize disturbance
to aquatic ecosystems.
       (b) Dredging activities shall be carried out at locations specified in the approved design,
applying construction methods that avoid prolonged disturbance and turbidity which may affect
the aquatic ecosystems of rivers/canals.
        (c) Dredging contractors shall comply with all water pollution mitigation measures
specified above. If rivers/canals are not polluted by dredging activities, adverse impacts on
aquatic organisms will be reduced.
         (d) Consider temporary flow diversion measures or installation of silt curtains during
dredging to reduce impacts from turbidity and suspended solids at sensitive sections, such as
water intake points of Hoa Binh Domestic Water Supply Plant and sections with aquaculture
facilities along rivers/canals, in order to avoid negative impacts on aquatic ecosystems, fisheries,
and aquaculture areas. Net enclosure methods may significantly reduce turbidity.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                253

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)




              Figure 7. 1: Method of Using Enclosure Nets During Dredging
       (e) Manage, collect, and treat wastewater in accordance with current regulations on
discharge. Store fuel in sealed containers, keeping the containers closed in a confined area to
prevent fuel leakage into rivers.
       (g) Do not clear the decks of vessels or barges in dredging areas to avoid discharging
wastewater into rivers/canals, which could increase water pollution and negatively affect
aquatic life.
      (h) Pay attention to extreme weather conditions such as strong winds, storms, currents,
and water levels to be able to temporarily suspend dredging activities, thereby minimizing
damage, impacts on water quality, and aquatic life.
(v) Mitigation Measures for Impacts on the Raw Water Pumping Station of Hoa Binh Water
Supply Plant
     (a) Establish a protection zone around the pumping station, coinciding with the regulated
protection area for drinking water sources (800 m upstream and 200 m downstream of the
pumping station).
     (a) Install silt curtains around the dredging area to limit the dispersion of sludge and
suspended solids into the surrounding environment, especially when working near the water
supply facility.
       (c) Monitor water quality in the pumping station protection zone when dredging near the
facility’s protection boundary (500 m from the protection boundary, corresponding to 1,300 m
upstream and 700 m downstream of the pumping station). Minimum monitoring parameters
shall include turbidity/TSS, pH, and DO to promptly detect any abnormal fluctuations in water
quality.
      (d) Before dredging within the pumping station protection zone, the Project Owner and
contractors shall notify Viettech Environment Co., Ltd. of the planned dredging schedule so
that the operator can temporarily stop or adjust water abstraction and increase water storage in
the reserve reservoir to ensure stable water supply to the local area during construction. The
plant’s reserve reservoir can meet water supply demand for approximately seven days.
     (e) Response measures in case of potential water supply impacts: If monitoring results
show unusually high turbidity or TSS, or indicate an impact on the plant’s water intake
operations, the contractors shall suspend dredging, review sludge control measures, and only
resume construction after water quality parameters have returned to stable levels.
(vi) Mitigation of Impacts on Aquaculture Households
     For aquaculture households using water from Phuoc Xuyen and Cho Bung canals:


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             254

                                                                                  Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
      (a) For households drawing water from Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals, the
CPO shall closely coordinate with the contractors, local authorities, and potentially affected
households to optimize dredging activities—balancing technical dredging requirements while
minimizing adverse impacts on aquaculture. Contractors shall also implement safety measures,
such as using buoys to mark the construction area, to prevent potential collisions with vessels
operating dredging equipment on the river.
      (b) Prior to construction, the contractors shall coordinate with local authorities and
residents to inform them of the dredging schedule so that households can plan water abstraction
appropriately, avoid water intake during periods of turbidity, or collect water in advance. The
Project shall agree with local residents on the timing for water intake into their aquaculture
ponds and the release of shrimp into the ponds. The aquaculture season shall be arranged with
local households to ensure construction activities do not occur in ways that negatively affect
their shrimp farming, for example, by ensuring canal water used for aquaculture does not
become turbid due to dredging. The National Technical Regulation on Brackish Water Shrimp
Farming (QCVN 02-19:2014/BNNPTNT on veterinary hygiene, environmental protection, and
food safety) shall be applied to monitor this arrangement.
       (c) During dredging, contractors shall apply appropriate methods, such as phased
dredging and minimizing the use of dredging equipment, to reduce risks and potential impacts
on normal aquaculture operations and vessel navigation. The phased dredging approach is
reflected in the contractor's construction schedule submitted to the CSC, and that any deviation
from the agreed phasing that could affect aquaculture operations triggers automatic notification
to affected households
      (d) Consultations shall also be conducted with affected persons based on the final
technical design of dredging activities. In cases where dredging causes damage to aquaculture
ponds, the dredging contractors shall compensate households in accordance with regulations.
The compensation commitment in cases of dredging-caused damage to aquaculture ponds is
elaborated into a clear, accessible procedure — specifying how households can report damage,
who assesses the claim, what evidence is required, what the compensation standard is, and
within what timeframe payment will be made. this procedure is communicated to all affected
aquaculture households before dredging begins, and that households are aware they do not need
to pay any fees to access the compensation process.
(vii) Mitigation measures for residential areas along canals
        (a) The contractors shall apply appropriate construction methods at canal sections where
houses are located close to the banks in order to avoid bank instability and impacts on house
structures. The "appropriate construction methods" to be applied at sections with houses close
to canal banks are specified in detail in the contractor's construction plan submitted to the CSC,
rather than left to contractor discretion. Households living immediately adjacent to the canal
bank are individually informed of the specific methods that will be used near their properties
before works commence in their section.
      (b) Dredging activities shall be carried out in short sections, limiting dredging close to the
bank toe and maintaining a safe distance in accordance with the design. The safe distance from
the bank toe maintained during dredging is clearly defined in the technical specifications and
disclosed to affected households, so that residents understand the basis for decisions affecting
the stability of their properties.
      (c) During construction, silt curtains shall be installed to limit the dispersion of sediments
and turbidity toward residential areas; controlled dredging equipment shall be used to reduce
vibration and noise; and strict control of dredged material transportation shall be implemented
to avoid spillage into the canal. The installation of silt curtains near residential areas is verified


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  255

                                                                                       Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
by the CSC before dredging commences in those sections, and that records of silt curtain
installation and condition are maintained throughout construction.
      (d) The contractors shall conduct surveys and prepare baseline condition records of houses
along the canal prior to construction, and shall install warning signs, safety buoys, and assign
personnel for waterway traffic control to ensure community safety throughout the construction
period. The pre-construction baseline condition survey of houses along the canal is conducted
jointly with — or at minimum in the presence of — the affected household, and that a signed
copy of the baseline record is provided to the household. Households are informed of their right
to request a post-construction comparison survey if they believe their property has been
damaged, and that the process for doing so is clearly explained before works begin. Ensure that
warning signs, safety buoys, and waterway traffic control personnel are in place before any
dredging commences in residential sections, not installed reactively after safety concerns are
raised.
(viii) Mitigation Measures for Infrastructure and Waterway Traffic (Bridges and Local Ferry
Crossings)
      To minimize negative impacts on waterway and land transportation infrastructure (bridges
and ferry docks) during dredging, contractors shall implement the following measures:
     (a) Survey and develop detailed construction plans: Conduct surveys of the current
condition of all bridges, ferry docks, temporary and permanent structures crossing the canal
before construction; determine clearance heights, pier foundations, and areas of influence to
develop suitable dredging plans that avoid scour or structural instability.
     (b) Establish a construction protection corridor: Define safe distances when working near
bridges and ferry docks; limit or avoid placing heavy equipment, mooring vessels, or laying
dredge pipelines within sensitive areas around bridge piers and docking zones.
     (c) Proper waterway traffic management: Plan construction in segments, avoiding
simultaneous work in high-traffic areas; assign traffic controllers or use buoys and signage to
ensure safe passage for vessels, particularly at ferry crossings.
      (d) Advance notice of construction schedule: contractors shall notify ferry operators and
local authorities of the timing and scope of dredging so operators can adjust transportation
activities accordingly.
     (e) Vibration and erosion control: Apply appropriate construction methods (reduce
equipment power, work in layers, avoid strong agitation) near bridge foundations to limit local
scour, settlement, or impacts on structural stability.
     (g) Install warning systems and ensure safety: Provide adequate signage, signal lights, and
buoys in construction areas near bridges and ferry docks; particularly increase warnings at night
or under adverse weather conditions.
      (h) Regular monitoring and inspection: Continuously monitor the condition of bridges and
ferry docks throughout construction; promptly detect abnormal signs such as cracks, settlement,
or foundation erosion to take immediate corrective action.
      (i) Restoration and compensation (if any): contractors are responsible for repairing,
restoring, or compensating for any damage to bridges, ferry docks, and related transportation
infrastructure caused by construction activities.
(ix) Mitigation Measures for Impacts During Installation and Operation of Dredge Pump
Pipelines Across Roads
     (a) Pipeline management: Arrange the pipeline route reasonably to minimize crossing
roads; prioritize road shoulders or technical corridors. Where road crossings are unavoidable,


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              256

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
select suitable locations and install adequate signage, barriers, and warning lights. Communities
and road users along proposed pipeline routes — including residents, market vendors, and
school commuters — are notified of the pipeline installation schedule and the location of road
crossings at least one week in advance. Pipeline routes crossing roads near schools, health
facilities, and markets are identified in advance and that the CSC reviews and approves crossing
arrangements at these locations before installation.
      (b) Pipeline protection at crossings: Apply measures such as burying pipes with protective
layers, providing temporary bridges/speed bumps across the road, or reinforcing structures to
withstand vehicle loads and prevent damage.
       (c) Ensure traffic safety: Coordinate traffic flow management as needed; assign traffic
controllers in sensitive areas or during peak hours. Traffic management during pipeline road
crossings specifically accounts for peak school hours and market activity periods, and that
traffic controllers are assigned at these times at crossings near sensitive facilities.
      (d) Leak and incident control: Conduct regular inspections, especially at joints; provide
emergency materials (sandbags, backup pumps) and promptly collect any spilled material in
case of leaks.
      (e) Road cleaning and restoration: Immediately remove spilled sludge; restore the road
surface after construction to ensure safety and aesthetics.
     (g) Construction timing: Prefer working outside peak traffic hours to reduce traffic
impacts.
      (h) Monitoring and compliance: Conduct regular monitoring and comply with regulations
on traffic, labor safety, and environmental protection.
(x) Mitigation Measures for the Use and Management of Dredged Material
       (a) According to current Vietnamese regulations, dredged material with a primary
composition of clay mixed with silt, if used for land reclamation, may be considered a mineral
resource (Group IV) managed by the State under Articles 2 and 6 of the 2024 Law on Geology
and Minerals, and Appendix 1 of Decree 193/2025/ND-CP. Therefore, its use and disposal must
follow a plan approved by the competent authority.
        (b) To minimize environmental impacts, the Project has coordinated with local
authorities to identify dredged material receiving areas, primarily former soil extraction pits
that are no longer in operation, to use the dredged material for land reclamation and site
restoration. This approach both accommodates the volume of dredged sludge from the Project
and contributes to terrain rehabilitation while reducing safety risks at abandoned extraction pits.
        (c) Before dredging, dredged material must be quality-checked to ensure it does not
exceed national technical regulations (QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT) on sediment quality.
Transportation and disposal shall follow the Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP) to
control environmental risks such as sludge dispersion, leachate, and impacts on nearby
residential areas.
        (d) In addition, if dredged material meets environmental standards and there is local
demand, a portion may be reused for land filling, soil improvement, or small infrastructure
works, thereby reducing the need for sludge disposal sites and minimizing environmental
impacts of the Project.
        (e) The application of new technologies to convert dredged sludge into construction
material is encouraged. For example, a method proposed by MCIC shows that cement–dredged
sludge reinforcement technology can produce material with strength ranging from 1 kg/cm² to
3 kg/cm². With a test ratio of 100 kg of cement per 1 m³ of sludge, the product achieves higher



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                257

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
results than cement–sand mixtures: 28-day UCS compression strength reaches 2.5–3.0 kg/cm²;
Cone penetration index > 800 kPa13.
(xi) Mitigation Measures for Storage and Handling of Dredged Material
(a) Based on the list of dredged material storage sites proposed by the People’s Committees of
the communes, the Project Owner shall submit an official request to the Provincial People’s
Committee for approval of the area and location for receiving dredged material (in accordance
with Decree No.57/2024/ND-CP on managing dredging activities in seaport waters and inland
waterway areas).
(b) Before construction, the dredging contractors shall prepare an Environmental Protection
Commitment, which must be appraised by the Provincial Department of Agriculture and
Environment.
(c) If the dredged material does not contain heavy metals or pesticides exceeding the limits
specified in QCVN 43:2025/BNNMT on sediment quality, and has a pH above 5.5 (with no
risk of acid leakage from potential pyrite), the material shall be classified as regular industrial
solid waste and managed in accordance with Article 81 of the 2020 Law on Environmental
Protection. It may be transferred to disposal sites designed as follows:
 + The base and walls of the disposal site shall be compacted; the bottom and walls lined with
     geotextile or equivalent impermeable material to prevent leakage to groundwater and
     surrounding areas; a treated wastewater outlet shall be installed to discharge into the canal
     or river.
 - This type of dredged material disposal site mainly functions as a settling basin: sediment is
     allowed to settle, and the water layer, after quality verification meeting QCVN
     40:2025/BNNMT for industrial effluent, shall be discharged back into rivers or canals.
 + After water removal, only dry sludge remains in the disposal site. This can be considered a
     “Soil Bank,” serving as a material source for construction works or for local residents to
     use as building material or for land improvement.
(d) In the case of dredged material containing acidic sulfidic soil (pH below 5.5), it is necessary
to design and construct a storage site with three compartments:




 Figure 7.2: Design of Dredged Material Figure 7.3: Design of Dredged Material Storage
       Storage Site with pH > 5.5       Site Contaminated with Acid Sulfate Soil (pH ≤
                                                              5.5)

-   The first compartment contains the dredged material.
-   The second compartment contains water drained from the first compartment that is highly
    acidic; the acidic water is treated with lime in this compartment.
