                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
 -   The third compartment contains treated water, which can be discharged into rivers or
     canals after analysis confirms compliance with the Vietnamese National Technical
     Regulation on Industrial Wastewater.
 (e) To avoid any risks caused by the disposal site, the following measures must be implemented:
 -   Install warning signs, including inland waterway signals (for river- or canal-side disposal
     sites), such as buoys, signage, and signal lights.
 -   Design and construct the disposal sites as described above.
 -   Establish and maintain a drainage system around the disposal area to channel any leachate
     back to the canal or river.
 (f) Conduct periodic environmental monitoring (quarterly), specifically monitoring water
 quality and aquatic organisms, carried out by an independent environmental monitoring
 consultant hired by the Project Owner.
 -   (xii) Erosion Control
 -   Monitor bank erosion during dredging activities.
 -   Assign staff to conduct daily erosion monitoring.
 -   Do not carry out dredging on days with heavy rainfall or during the flood season.
 -   Ensure that slope construction follows the dredging design specifications.
7.1.3.4. Site-specific Mitigation Measures for New Bridges/Locks/Boat Docks construction
    (Subprojects 2.2, 2.3, 2.4)
(i) Environmental and Health Safety Measures During Construction
Construction of locks/boat docks should avoid periods of high river water levels;
Implement measures to prevent spills, control sedimentation, divert surface water flow, and
 restore affected areas.
 Local authorities and the community shall be notified of the construction of existing locks/boat
  docks at least two weeks in advance.
 -   Life jackets, safety harnesses, and ear protection shall be provided to workers during bridge
     construction over rivers and streams.
 -   Adequate lighting shall be ensured for nighttime works.
 -   Life jackets and appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) shall be provided to
     workers, and their use shall be mandatory when working in or above water, especially
     during construction of bridge abutments (typically 2–3 meters above the water surface).
 -   During sluice/lock construction, waste shall be strictly controlled to prevent any discharge
     or dumping of wastewater, sludge, waste materials, fuel, or waste oil into water bodies. All
     such materials shall be collected and treated on land along the riverbank. Sludge and
     sediments shall also be pumped ashore for treatment, and direct discharge into rivers shall
     not be permitted.
 -   Upon completion of bridge construction, the construction area shall be restored.
 -   Mixing concrete directly on the ground shall not be permitted and shall be carried out only
     on impermeable surfaces.
 -   All runoff from mixing areas shall be strictly controlled, and cement-contaminated water
     shall be collected, stored, and treated at approved locations.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               259

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Unused cement bags shall be stored in a manner that protects them from rain and runoff.
    Used (empty) cement bags shall be collected and stored in weather-resistant containers to
    prevent cement dust from dispersion by wind and water pollution.
-   All surplus concrete shall be removed from the site upon completion of concrete works and
    properly disposed of. Washing surplus concrete onto the ground shall not be permitted. All
    excess aggregates shall also be removed.
-   During bored pile construction, the use of bentonite shall be carried out within earth or steel
    cofferdams to prevent spillage into the environment. All soil–bentonite mixtures and
    spilled bentonite shall be collected, and the following treatment measures are
    recommended:
-   Construction of bridge piers/abutments on land: Liquid soil–bentonite mixtures and
    bentonite slurry shall be collected into drainage channels, tanks, or containers to prevent
    direct discharge on site. The slurry shall then be settled, partially dried, and transported to
    designated locations for treatment, recycling, or recovery of bentonite.
-   Construction of piers adjacent to flowing water: Soil mixed with bentonite, including
    liquefied mixtures and spilled bentonite, shall be transported to onshore storage areas or
    placed in containers for settling or drying, then transported to disposal or recycling sites
    for bentonite recovery.
-   For water supply works associated with bridge construction, strict waste control plans shall
    be implemented to prevent direct discharge or dumping of wastewater, sludge, waste
    materials, fuel, or waste oil into water bodies. All such materials shall be collected and
    treated on the riverbank. Sludge and sediments shall be pumped ashore for treatment, and
    direct discharge into rivers shall not be permitted.
-   Restoration of water crossings shall be undertaken, including general methods applicable
    to all water crossings and site-specific method statements for critical or sensitive
    crossings.
-   After bridge construction, the construction area, diversion channels, sedimentation ponds,
    and temporary access routes shall be restored to the satisfaction of Contractor’s
    Environmental Officer and Social Staff.
(ii) Management of Excavation, Cutting, and Slope Filling Works
Excavation, cutting, and slope filling works shall be carefully managed to minimize adverse
environmental impacts.
-   All excavation and earthworks shall be properly controlled, particularly during the rainy
    season.
-   The Contractor shall maintain the stability of cut and fill slopes and shall minimize
    disturbance to areas outside the approved construction limits.
-   The Contractor shall complete final cut and fill profiles at any location as soon as possible,
    preferably in a single continuous operation, to avoid partially completed earthworks,
    especially during the rainy season.
To protect all cut or filled slopes from erosion in accordance with the design drawings, drainage
channels have been cut and provided at the top and bottom of the slopes. Cut drainage channels
should be installed above high cuts to minimize surface runoff and slope erosion.
The contractors shall maximize the use of excavated material that meets the standards for
embankment material within the project area.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                260

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
Any unsuitable excavated or rejected materials shall be disposed of at designated disposal areas
as agreed with the CSC.
(iii) Site-specific Mitigation Measures for Dredging, Excavation, and Filling Activities in
Acid Sulfate Soil (ASS) Areas - (Subprojects 2.2)
Subproject 2.2 traverses the Plains of Reeds, an area underlain by Potential Acid Sulfate Soils
(PASS). Soil pH values recorded within the project area range from 3.8 to 5.5, confirming active
acid sulfate conditions. The exposure of anaerobic, sulfide-bearing sediments to atmospheric
oxygen through dredging and open disposal creates a risk of sulfuric acid generation, iron and
aluminum mobilization, and acidic leachate discharge to the canal network. All disposal site
design, operational management, monitoring, and emergency response procedures shall be
governed by the Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP), prepared by the contractor,
reviewed by the CSC, and approved by the PMU Environmental Specialist prior to the
commencement of any dredging or disposal activity.
The following mitigation measures shall be applied by contractors:
Pre-Construction
   •   Conduct soil quality analysis across all SP 2.2 construction and disposal sites to
       delineate active and potential ASS zones and prepare an ASS map as part of the
       Contractor's DMMP Soil Management section.
   •   Analyze the physico-chemical properties of sediment across excavation depths to
       determine the PASS stratigraphy and inform excavation sequencing and disposal site
       design.
   •   All disposal sites receiving material from PASS-confirmed zones shall be designed as
       three-compartment systems comprising: (i) a receiving cell for raw dredged material;
       (ii) an active lime (CaCO₃) neutralization cell with continuous pH monitoring and
       dosing capability; and (iii) a final settling and verification cell confirming that pH is
       between 6.5 and 8.5 and TSS is below the QCVN 40:2025/BNNMT discharge threshold
       before any decant water is returned to the canal network.
   •   Containment embankments at all disposal sites shall be designed and certified by a
       qualified geotechnical engineer prior to pumping commencing. Embankments shall
       maintain a minimum freeboard of 0.5 m above the projected maximum water level at
       all times. Lined overflow channels and contingency bunding shall be installed at all sites
       before hydraulic pumping commences.
   •   Disposal sites shall not be located within 100 m of active aquaculture operations,
       drinking water intakes, or tidal exchange zones without additional engineered
       containment measures confirmed in the DMMP.
During Construction
   •   Continuous pH monitoring shall be maintained within the neutralization cell. Lime
       dosing shall be triggered automatically or manually when pH falls below 6.0 — not 5.5
       — to intervene before iron and aluminum mobilization is initiated.
   •   Decant water shall not be discharged to the canal network unless pH is confirmed
       between 6.5 and 8.5 and TSS meets the applicable discharge standard at the point of
       release. Discharge without confirmed compliance is prohibited.
   •   Where highly acidic material (pH below 5.5) is stockpiled along construction routes,
       lime treatment shall be applied to exposed surfaces, particularly in proximity to
       aquaculture zones and drinking water intake areas.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              261

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    •   In cases where large volumes of ASS must be excavated for embankment or dyke
        construction, exposed surfaces shall be covered with non-acid-forming soil or clay to
        minimize oxidation.
    •   Exposed dredged material surfaces at disposal sites shall be progressively covered or
        revegetated to reduce the oxidation surface area and limit leachate generation.
    •   Disposal shall not commence at any site during periods of forecast heavy rainfall where
        containment capacity cannot be guaranteed.
    •   In the event of embankment failure, blocked drainage outlets, or uncontrolled leachate
        discharge, the contractor shall cease pumping immediately, implement the emergency
        spill response procedure documented in the DMMP, and notify the PMU Environmental
        Specialist within two hours. Operations shall not resume until containment is restored
        and independently verified.
Monitoring
    •   Water quality monitoring shall be conducted weekly at the canal immediately
        downstream of each SP 2.2 disposal site during active dredging and decanting,
        measuring pH, TSS, DO, dissolved Fe, and Al against thresholds in the DMMP and
        QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT.
    •   A disposal site operational and inspection log shall be maintained by the contractor and
        reviewed by the CSC and PMU Environmental Specialist fortnightly, with findings
        included in the quarterly DMMP compliance report submitted to the World Bank.
Contractor Awareness and Liability
    •   Contractors shall receive training on legal obligations, geochemical risks, and
        operational best practices for working in ASS environments prior to mobilization. Any
        contractor causing soil or water acidification resulting in fish, shrimp, or crop mortality
        shall be liable for fines and compensation in accordance with Vietnamese law.
(iv) Concrete Batching Plants
Focus on sourcing ready-mix cement concrete and asphalt concrete from legally authorized
suppliers in the area. In cases where cement or asphalt batching plants must be constructed on-
site, attention shall be paid to the following issues:
-   Construction facilities, including concrete batching plants and asphalt mixing plants, shall
    minimize land take by being located at intersections or areas where relatively larger land
    areas will ultimately be required.
-   Concrete mixing shall be carried out on impermeable surfaces. Cement-containing waste
    and wastewater shall be collected via drainage systems with on-site sedimentation pits
    before discharge to receiving bodies.
-   Concrete batching plants shall be located at least 200 meters away from residential houses
    and other sensitive receptors such as pagodas, churches, communal houses, school gates,
    healthcare facilities, and public offices.
(v) Prevention of Groundwater Pollution
    Bridge construction will involve bored pile drilling.
-   During drilling, groundwater or rainwater runoff may enter the foundation pits. The
    following measures shall be implemented to prevent groundwater pollution:




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                262

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Heavy rainfall during excavation: Cover excavation openings to minimize rainwater
    inflow. At the same time, pump accumulated rainwater out of the pits to prevent sidewall
    collapse, soil saturation, and difficulties in excavation and material transport.
-   In cases where a significant volume of groundwater arises within the Project area, the
    Contractors shall use pumps to discharge water from the foundations into settling
    tanks/ponds, from where it will be collected and discharged into the regulation basin.
(vi) Mitigation measures for Dismantling of bridges (Subprojects 2.2)
-   Local authorities and communities shall be notified of the demolition schedule at least one
    (1) month prior to commencement of demolition works.
-   Warning signs for bridge demolition shall be installed and maintained for both road and
    waterway traffic.
-   Solid fences shall be installed and maintained to block access at both ends of the bridge at
    least one (1) week prior to mobilization of machinery and demolition works. Security
    personnel shall be deployed on a 24-hour basis. Adequate lighting shall be ensured at night.
-   Traffic diversion signs shall be installed at least 600 meters in advance of the bridge closure
    for demolition and reconstruction works.
-   Demolition of existing bridges shall commence only after the new bridge has been opened
    to traffic and uninterrupted access has been ensured.
-   Metal sheet hoardings with a minimum height of 2.5 meters shall be installed at both ends
    of the bridge near residential areas to ensure the safety of local residents during demolition
    activities
(vii) Disposal of Materials
If the Contractor proposes any new disposal or spoil storage sites during the construction phase,
such sites shall be subject to approval by the CPO and the relevant local authorities. The
Contractor shall ensure that these sites: (a) are not located within designated agricultural areas,
or any other private or public properties; (b) do not interfere with natural drainage systems; (c)
do not create potential future problems; and (d) do not affect endangered or rare flora. Under
no circumstances shall the Contractor dispose of any materials in environmentally sensitive
areas. The final intended use of disposal sites shall be subject to approval by local authorities.
In addition to the siting requirements for disposal areas, the following actions shall be
implemented:
-   Landowners shall be compensated where agricultural land is occupied for use as disposal
    sites.
-   Prior to commencement of disposal activities, the top 30 cm of natural topsoil shall be
    stripped and stockpiled on site. This material shall be reserved and used as cover material
    for site rehabilitation upon completion of disposal activities.
-   Where disposal sites are located near rivers or watercourses, retaining walls and/or
    interceptor drains or settling ponds shall be constructed prior to the start of construction
    activities. Surface runoff shall be retained and allowed to settle before being discharged
    into receiving water bodies.
-   To ensure the stability of disposal sites, stone-pitched surfaces and stone protection at the
    toe of slopes shall be applied to prevent erosion and maintain stability.
-   Peripheral drainage ditches shall be constructed around disposal sites to control surface
    runoff.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                263

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Construction of disposal sites and transport of spoil materials at night near residential areas
    is strictly prohibited. Disposal sites shall be watered during operation to suppress dust.
-   Disposal sites located near agricultural land shall be limited in size to avoid damage to
    crops.
7.1.3.5. Site-specific Mitigation Measures for Sluice/Lock Construction (Subprojects 2.3, 2.4)
(i) Mitigation of Water Quality Degradation
-   Conduct a preliminary analysis of water quality, soil, and ecology prior to construction
    works, and select appropriate methods for disposal of dredged sludge.
-   Select temporary stockpile locations along canals/rivers before disposal, taking into
    account the potential for water stagnation in temporary settling pits and channels (if
    necessary) leading back to the river/canal.
-   If there is a high risk of water pollution, wastewater treatment systems and contingency
    plans for chemical or oil spills must be included as part of environmental protection
    measures.
-   Maintain settling pits inside or around the construction site, and dredge them regularly to
    preserve their settling function.
-   Organize construction materials and excavation wastes neatly, in quantities corresponding
    to the construction schedule, to limit materials being washed away by surface runoff.
-   Construction material stockpiles, excavation and fill areas, and disposal sites must be
    located at least 10 m from the river/canal to minimize sediment, soil, or debris entering the
    waterway, which could cause siltation or blockages.
(ii) Mitigation of Impacts on Aquatic Life and Biodiversity
Survey results of the aquatic ecosystem in the area indicate that the species present at the
sluice/lock construction sites are common to the region. Construction activities are not
expected to be destructive to these species, but may affect their moving and the re-
establishment of their habitats after construction is completed. To minimize adverse impacts
on aquatic species and facilitate the early restoration of stable habitats, the Project Owner shall
implement the following measures:
-   Comply with the construction schedule and plan, and follow the approved construction
    procedures for locks and sluices.
-   Do not discharge construction waste, domestic waste, or wastewater into the environment.
-   Develop monitoring and contingency plans: (i) establish a plan to monitor bank changes
    during construction; (ii) prepare ready-to-use measures and materials to respond promptly
    to any signs of subsidence or erosion.
-   Strictly adhere to approved designs: construct locks and sluices within the specified scope,
    depth, and slope.
-   Before completing construction, the contractors must clean the site and restore the
    environment to its original condition, including: (i) removing all unused materials from the
    construction site; (ii) dismantling and relocating all machinery, equipment, navigation
    buoys, temporary facilities, and workstations used during construction; (iii) restoring the
    site to its pre-construction condition and handing over a clean area to the local authorities.
(iii) Mitigation of Impacts from Disruption or Damage to Infrastructure
-   Comply with inland waterway traffic safety laws.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                264

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Adhere to the prescribed speed limits; do not transport oversize or overloaded cargo.
-   At construction sites, the contractors shall install adequate navigation and traffic signs to
    guide vessels safely. During peak construction periods, personnel must be assigned to direct
    vessel traffic within the construction area.
-   Construction vessels must display signal lights to alert local boats and prevent collisions at
    night. In adverse weather conditions, such as fog or rain reducing visibility beyond 300 m,
    the contractor shall illuminate signal lights in accordance with regulations.
-   Obtain permission and notify the waterway management authorities regarding construction
    activities and the transportation routes of materials via public information channels.
-   Cooperate closely with local authorities throughout the project implementation.
-   Inform local authorities and communities about the construction schedule of the works.
-   All mooring lines and safety equipment must be monitored 24/7. Submerged cables must
    be equipped with clearly visible buoys to ensure they are easily avoided and do not pose a
    hazard to passing vessels.
-   Strictly comply with the Inland Waterway Traffic Law.
(iv) Mitigation of Impacts from Increased Risk of Subsidence, Erosion, and Sedimentation
For the construction area:
-   Construction material stockpiles and excavation waste must be placed at least 10 m away
    from drainage channels to minimize the entry of soil and debris into canals, ditches, or
    rivers, which could cause sedimentation and blockage.
-   Maintain sedimentation pits inside or around the construction site and dredge them
    regularly to preserve their settling function.
-   Transport waste away from the construction site in the shortest time possible.
-   Neatly stockpile materials according to the construction schedule to reduce the risk of
    materials being washed away by surface runoff.
-   Level and compact disposal areas for excavation waste and dredged sludge to reduce the
    risk of subsidence and erosion.
-   In the event of bank erosion, the contractor shall suspend construction activities at that
    location and coordinate with competent authorities to address the incident.
-   During the construction of locks/sluices and auxiliary structures, the Project Owner shall
    assign staff to monitor bank erosion and settlement to promptly detect signs of collapse and
    implement remedial measures.
(v) Mitigation of Impacts on Local Livelihoods and Daily Activities
    ▪   Prior to construction, contractors must coordinate with local authorities and residents to
        announce the construction schedule and plan via local communication channels so that
        residents can plan water usage accordingly.
    ▪   Construction activities should be concentrated in the dry season when canal/river flow
        is low, in order to limit the spread of turbidity from the construction site to adjacent
        areas.
    ▪   The Project Owner, in coordination with contractors, shall regularly inspect temporary
        storage areas and material stockpiles, particularly after heavy rain, to assess any damage
        caused by surface runoff, soil erosion, sedimentation, or acid sulfate soils (if present).
        Simultaneously, site leveling must be restored before the completion of works.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               265

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
(vi) Mitigation of Impacts on Local Livelihoods During Construction
   ▪   Notify local residents and authorities at least two weeks prior to construction.
   ▪   Implement dredging in segmented, well-planned sections: divide into short segments
       for sequential construction to minimize the area of banks affected simultaneously.
   ▪   Ensure minimal flow diversion for construction; do not block water flow, allowing safe
       passage for boats.
   ▪   Restore all affected areas and access routes to their original condition immediately after
       construction.
   ▪   Assign staff to coordinate traffic in densely populated areas where boats frequently
       transport passengers, goods, or services along the route.
   ▪   Keep the construction site orderly and cleared as work progresses, allowing residents to
       maintain their livelihoods and production.
   ▪   Maintain close coordination with relevant authorities throughout the construction
       process.
(vii) Mitigation of Impacts on Cultural, Religious, and Heritage Sites
   ▪   Notify local residents and authorities of construction plans and schedules at least two
       weeks in advance.
   ▪   Avoid construction during local religious or spiritual activities.
   ▪   Respect local religious beliefs, customs, and practices, maintaining harmonious social
       relations at the construction site and in the surrounding community.
(viii) Mitigation of Impacts on Sensitive Structures
   ▪   Notify local residents and authorities of construction plans, schedules for each contract
       package, and transport/disposal routes at least two weeks in advance; clearly delineate
       and separate the construction area from surrounding areas.
   ▪   Ensure minimal flow diversion for construction; do not block water flow, allowing boats
       to navigate safely.
   ▪   Assign staff to coordinate traffic in densely populated areas where boats frequently
       transport passengers, goods, or services.
   ▪   Avoid construction, material transport, and disposal during peak periods of public
       activity, such as at local markets, school drop-off/pick-up times, or along student
       commute routes.
   ▪   Install warning signs, directional signs, nighttime lighting, and other indicators to
       separate the construction site from surrounding areas.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              266

                                                                                   Official Use Only
                            Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)

7.1.4. Mitigation Measures During Operation Phase
Potential impacts that may occur during the operation phase were considered during the project
preparation stage. Therefore, the Proposal includes measures to address potential impacts and
risks during operation, along with environmentally friendly solutions and opportunities for
green coverage. Costs associated with mitigation measures, minimized as much as possible,
have been included in the project cost estimate. Meanwhile, some mitigation measures will be
specified in detailed design drawings.
(i.) Mitigation measures during operation for the canal system after dredging
 -      Environmental restoration: Conduct environmental restoration activities after dredging,
        such as rehabilitating affected areas, planting trees along the canal banks, and restoring
        degraded ecosystems.
 -      Regular maintenance: Carry out regular maintenance of canals to ensure stable operation
        and minimize the need for future dredging.
Table 7. 6: Mitigation Measures During Operation Phase for Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam and

(ii) Mitigation measures for sluices and navigation locks
(ii.1) Reducing impacts on water quality during operation
Waste management from vessels:
-      Comply strictly with regulations on discharges (domestic wastewater, oil, garbage) from
       waterway transport vehicles while navigating and mooring in navigation locks and sluice
       areas.
-      Establish waste collection stations at navigation locks and adjacent areas so that vessels
       can properly dispose of waste according to regulations.
-      Supervision and enforcement: Strengthen inspection and monitoring of compliance with
       discharge regulations and impose strict penalties on violations.
-      Awareness raising: Educate crew members and local residents about the impacts of
       indiscriminate discharges on water quality.
-      Wastewater collection and treatment: Wastewater generated from maintenance and
       cleaning of navigation locks and sluices (wash water, residues) must be collected and
       treated to meet standards before discharge into the environment.
-      Solid waste control: Solid waste generated during maintenance must be sorted, collected,
       and treated according to hazardous waste management regulations.
Control of oil and grease leakage from operating and maintenance equipment:
-      Regular maintenance: Conduct routine maintenance of mechanical and hydraulic
       equipment of navigation locks and sluices to prevent oil and grease leakage.
-      Use of environmentally friendly materials: Prioritize the use of biodegradable lubricants
       that are less harmful to water environments.
-      Installation of spill collection systems: Equip locations at risk of leakage with containment
       devices and materials (e.g., oil booms, absorbent pads) to enable rapid response in case of
       spills.
-      Emergency response procedures: Develop and practice spill response procedures, including
       containment, collection, and proper treatment of spilled oil.
(ii.2) Mitigation of impacts on ecosystems and biodiversity
-      Comply with the general operation procedures of the Nam Mang Thit irrigation system
       under Decision No.829/QD-BNN-TCTL dated 04 March 2021 by the Ministry of
       Agriculture and Rural Development on issuing the operation procedures for the Nam Mang
       Thit system; and the detailed operation procedures for sluices within the project scope once
       approved.
-      Restoration and recreation of riparian habitats: (i) plant native trees along embankments:
       select native species with deep roots, flood tolerance, and the ability to provide shade; (ii)
       create artificial or semi-natural wetland areas: in suitable locations, small ponds or pools


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                         277

                                                                                              Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    can be created connected to canals/rivers to serve as spawning and nursery sites for native
    fish species, while also providing feeding grounds for water birds; (iii) use environmentally
    friendly embankment materials: research and apply embankment materials with high
    roughness and porosity to allow plant root attachment and habitat for small animals.
-   Maintain ecological connectivity: retain sections of natural shoreline to preserve diverse
    habitats and provide access for terrestrial animals to the water. Ensure continuous green
    belts along embankments connected to nearby natural ecological areas to facilitate wildlife
    movement.
(ii.3) Mitigation of impacts on waterway traffic
To minimize impacts on waterway traffic when passing through sluices and navigation locks,
the public should be encouraged to apply the following measures:
-   Comply with the general operation procedures of the Nam Mang Thit irrigation system
    under Decision No.829/QD-BNN-TCTL dated 04 March 2021 by the Ministry of
    Agriculture and Rural Development on issuing the operation procedures for the Nam Mang
    Thit system; and the detailed operation procedures for sluices within the project scope once
    approved.
-   Comply with legal regulations on waterway traffic safety: TCVN 5664-1992; TCVN 5664-
    2009; sector standard 22TCN-269-2000; Decision No.23/2011/TT-BGTVT dated 31
    March 2011 on the management of inland waterways by the Minister of Transport; Circular
    42/2021/TT-BGTVT dated 31 December 2021 on regulating traffic control, collision
    prevention, and limiting inland waterway traffic; and Decree No.30/2017/ND-CP dated 21
    March 2017 on the organization and operation of emergency response, natural disaster
    response, and search and rescue.
-   Coordinate waterway traffic according to reasonable channels.
-   Install warning signs and signals at sluices and navigation locks to prevent accidents when
    passing through the structures.
    To reduce impacts on river traffic, the public should also be encouraged to:
-   Install warning signs and lights at traffic bridges to prevent accidents in the project area.
-   Strengthen state management of waterway traffic: (i) Technical inspection of vessels:
    ensure vessels operating on the route are registered, equipped with safety devices, and
    properly maintained; (ii) Enhance patrol and control: strictly handle violations such as
    overloading, navigating outside designated channels, and operating without licenses.
-   Promote awareness and compliance with traffic laws among waterway users.
During the operation of navigation locks, for traffic bridges over the locks:
-   Raise public awareness of regulations and proper use of roads.
-   Monitor and supervise vehicle speed and driver behavior.
-   When traffic density is high, emissions, noise, and vibrations may be issues, but impacts
    can be mitigated through long-term management plans.
(ii.4) Mitigation of impacts from sedimentation, subsidence, and erosion
-   Mitigation of sluice erosion: To reduce erosion impacts during sluice operation, all sluice
    structures are reinforced at the upstream and downstream sides as follows: (i)
    Reinforcement length: varies depending on each sluice, with the river side L = 10–30 m,
    and the field side L = 10–30 m. (ii) Channel banks are reinforced with a 30 cm thick layer



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                278

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    of riprap on a filter fabric. (iii) Channel bed is reinforced with a 50 cm thick gabion or
    riprap layer on filter fabric.
-   Mitigation of sedimentation: Regular dredging is conducted in the sluice area to minimize
    sediment accumulation at the sluice location.
-   For canal sections with embankments and dikes: after construction is completed, local
    authorities and relevant agencies should regularly monitor and inspect erosion and
    sedimentation trends in the project area and surrounding zones. Potentially hazardous
    erosion points should be detected in time to issue warnings and implement remedial
    measures promptly.
-   Maintenance and upkeep of embankments, dikes, and sluices: Ensure water levels do not
    flood residential areas and agricultural land and allow effective flood discharge.
-   Maintain and upgrade erosion and subsidence warning systems on dikes and embankments:
    warning signs, guidance signs, signal lights, etc. Ensure the warning system is complete,
    clear, and easily recognizable so that residents can plan vehicle use with appropriate load
    limits.
-   Management of embankments, dikes, and sluices: Monitor erosion and subsidence during
    operation to plan maintenance, control transport activities, and manage construction works
    that violate the protection corridor of embankments and dikes.
-   Plant greenery along embankments and dikes: Create green corridors, stabilize banks,
    reduce erosion, and absorb certain pollutants.
(ii.5) Measures to Reduce Impacts from Incidents during Lock/Sluice Operation
-   Periodic maintenance of machinery and equipment at locks and sluices during operation:
    Regularly inspect and lubricate moving parts, remove obstructions (waste, silt).
-   Measures to prevent lock/sluice gate jamming:(i) Control system and backup power: Install
    systems to operate the lock gates in case the main system fails. (ii) Sensors and alarms:
    Install sensors to detect early signs of gate jamming (increased motor load, abnormal noises)
    and issue alerts. (iii) Emergency response team: Train technical staff to handle gate
    jamming promptly, with plans and specialized equipment to free the gates. (iv) Prevent
    leakage and seepage: Inspect joints, water-tight seals, and ensure backup pumping systems
    can remove water in case of leaks; maintain ready supplies and personnel for rapid
    emergency response, with regular training.
-   Measures to prevent vessel collisions: (i) Guidance and warning system: Install buoys and
    clear signage at lock entrances and exits. (ii) Traffic control system: Assign personnel to
    direct vessels safely into and out of locks. (iii) Protective equipment: Install bumpers along
    lock walls and gates. (iv) Response procedures: Establish procedures for handling
    collisions (damage assessment, rescue, notification).
-   Mitigation of impacts from extreme weather and climate change: (i) Operation response
    procedures: Develop procedures for operating sluices during extreme weather (open/close
    gates according to forecasted water levels). (ii) Close monitoring: Continuously monitor
    water levels and flows during the rainy season, floods, and high tides. (iii) Reliable
    communication: Ensure smooth communication between operational units and authorities.
-   Compliance with general operation procedures of the Nam Mang Thit irrigation system
    under Decision No.829/QD-BNN-TCTL dated 4 March 2021 of the Ministry of
    Agriculture and Environment on issuing the operation procedures of the Nam Mang Thit
    irrigation system; and the detailed operation procedures for sluices within the project scope
    once approved.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               279

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
(ii.6) Measures to Ensure Worker Safety during Lock Operation
-   Equip 100% of workers with mandatory protective gear (standard life jackets, helmets,
    anti-slip shoes, etc.) when working along lock chambers, on bridges, or near gate
    opening/closing areas. Position lifebuoys with attached ropes along both sides of the lock
    chamber walls and on top of sluices.
-   During maintenance of cable hoists, hydraulic cylinders for gate operation, or backup
    generators, disconnect power, lock control panels, and hang warning tags to prevent
    accidental startup.
-   When large barges enter lock chambers and mooring lines are secured while waiting to
    balance water levels, the lines are under high tension. Shore-based operators must stay
    outside the "cable snap hazard zone" to avoid serious accidents if lines break.
-   Conduct regular emergency drills for situations such as rescuing people who fall into water,
    handling transformer station fires, and emergency flood discharge procedures when gates
    are jammed.
(ii.7) Coordination Mechanism among Sluice Operators for Water Quality Monitoring
and Sluice Gate Operation
Simultaneous operation of large-scale saltwater control sluices in the area may cause
cumulative impacts on upstream water quality, such as pollutant accumulation, eutrophication
and algal blooms, and dissolved oxygen depletion. Therefore, an operation and water quality
monitoring mechanism should follow these principles:
Centralized monitoring and management system: Sluices in the area must be equipped with
automated monitoring networks (salinity, pH, turbidity, etc.) transmitting real-time data to a
central operations center.
Inter-sluice synchronized operation procedures: Establish coordinated procedures specifying
closure thresholds (e.g., when the external river salinity boundary reaches 4 g/L) and
emergency opening thresholds.
Clear institutional responsibilities: In case of conflicting objectives (e.g., one area wants to
close gates to retain freshwater for rice irrigation, another wants to open gates to save shrimp
from hypoxia due to pollution), final decision-making authority must rest with the Provincial
People’s Committee Chairman or the regional Disaster Prevention Command, based on
monitoring data.
(ii.8) Mitigating the risk of waterborne diseases caused by stagnant water during sluice
closure
Develop sluice operation scenarios that incorporate "flushing" the canal system. Leverage high
tides or water level differentials to periodically open sluices, creating flow to facilitate water
circulation and reduce pollutant concentrations.
Monitor microbiological parameters (Coliform, E. coli) at critical stagnant water points
throughout the dry season to provide early warnings to the community.
Coordinate with local health authorities to advise residents against bathing or washing directly
in stagnant canal sections and to implement larval and mosquito control measures at the
household level.
Encourage and support residents in constructing septic tanks, while restricting the direct
discharge of livestock waste into canals to minimize the nutrient sources that allow bacteria and
vectors to thrive.
7.1.5. Mitigation Measures of Cumulative Impacts


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               280

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
During the operation of the Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop locks and the longitudinal sluice
system along the Nam Mang Thit River, measures to mitigate cumulative impacts on Valued
Environmental Components (VECs) include:
VEC 1: Interior surface water quality:
-   Develop inter-regional operation procedures, connected with the Nam Hau River sluices,
    Ninh Quoi lock, and others.
-   Install an automated monitoring network to measure salinity, water levels, and water
    quality in real time at upstream, downstream, and interior locations. Monitoring data are
    transmitted to the operations center for computer analysis, enabling precise hourly
    decisions on gate opening/closing. This optimizes freshwater intake, prevents extreme
    salinity intrusion, and avoids flooding in interior areas.
-   Even during dry seasons, the operation procedure must account for opening gates to create
    ecological flows. These flows help reduce residual fertilizers, pesticides, and stagnant
    domestic wastewater, preventing eutrophication and dissolved oxygen depletion that could
    cause mass fish kills.
-   Local authorities in the area must commit to strict management of urban and agricultural
    wastewater discharged into the Nam Mang Thit and Long Phu–Tiep Nhat systems before
    gates are closed.
VEC 2: Biodiversity and fisheries resources:
-   Gate opening/closing schedules must integrate biological data on the reproductive cycles
    and moving seasons of characteristic native fish species in the Bay Xao and Saintard rivers.
    Use periods of suitable salinity to open locks or bottom sluice gates to create channels for
    natural movement of shrimp and fish between open river areas and interior regions,
    maintaining genetic diversity and natural food chains.
-   During periods without salinity intrusion threats, prioritize fully opening gates to ensure
    natural water circulation, aquatic larvae movement, and sediment transport.
VEC 3: Hydrology, erosion, and sedimentation:
-   Gates must be operated gradually, with stepwise opening and closing. Sudden full opening
    or slamming of gates is strictly prohibited to prevent shock waves that could damage
    embankments or cause erosion of adjacent riverbeds.
-   Conduct periodic riverbed surveys every six months (using echo sounding) at both ends of
    the sluices (particularly Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, and Cai Hop). Timely place ripraps or
    gabions in energy dissipation zones if scour holes are detected, and plan dredging of
    sediment bars obstructing flow.
VEC 4: Waterway traffic and livelihood conflicts:
-   Enhance the capacity of operational staff and use smart waterway traffic management
    systems (cameras, signal lights) to minimize barge waiting times and ensure the continuity
    of commodity supply chains (rice, construction materials). Publicize lock opening/closing
    schedules via mobile apps or local radio.
-   Maintain close coordination between authorities and local communities to balance water
    levels. If prolonged gate closure causes shrimp mortality due to lack of salinity, or improper
    gate opening leads to crop loss from salinity intrusion, residents must have access to a
    grievance mechanism (GRM) for prompt resolution and livelihood compensation if the
    damage is caused by operational errors.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               281

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
7.1.6. Mitigation Measures to Climate Change Issues
(i) For the Phuoc Xuyen–Hai Tam and Cho Bung canal system:
-   Implement adaptation measures: Climate change increases the frequency of short-duration,
    high-intensity rainfall events. To prevent flooding, the management unit must regularly
    monitor sedimentation levels and plan routine maintenance dredging to ensure the canals
    maintain sufficient cross-sectional area for rapid drainage.
(ii) For bridges along Cho Bung canal after new construction:
Implement adaptation measures: Inspect for scour under bridge piers caused by strong flows
during flood seasons and promptly place ripraps to reinforce foundations and ensure structural
stability.
(iii) For the Dai Ngai and My Xuyen locks:
-   To mitigate climate change impacts during operation, implement adaptation measures:
    Operate gates flexibly according to extreme scenarios. Climate change has made tidal
    patterns and salinity intrusion irregular. Gates are not operated on fixed schedules but are
    fully controlled by the sensor system. When abnormal sea-level rise or deep salinity
    intrusion is detected, the system automatically closes gates to protect interior areas;
    conversely, gates are opened urgently during localized heavy rainfall to discharge
    floodwater.
-   Rising temperatures and increased salinity accelerate metal corrosion and concrete erosion.
    Operators must periodically inspect and apply epoxy anti-corrosion coatings on steel gates,
    lubricate hydraulic cylinder systems, and protect the 22kV outdoor electrical network from
    salt deposition damage.
-   Implement mitigation measures: Regularly maintain motors, cable hoists, and transformer
    stations to ensure electromechanical systems operate at peak efficiency, minimizing energy
    loss. Prioritize use of grid electricity and minimize the operating time of diesel backup
    generators to reduce emissions.
7.2. Role and responsibilities in implementing the ESMP
7.2.1. Institutional Arrangements
The tables and figures below summarize the roles and responsibilities of key stakeholders and
their relationships in the implementation of the Environmental and Social Management Plan
(ESMP).
-   The Contractor is responsible for implementing mitigation measures. These measures will
    be included in the bidding documents, and the associated costs will be incorporated into
    the construction contract packages.
-   The Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) is responsible for daily supervision of the
    implementation of mitigation measures, with costs included in the service contract with the
    CSC.
-   The Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) is responsible
    for overall environmental monitoring, including assisting the CPO in environmental
    supervision and monitoring, and reporting on the implementation of the ESMP through
    monitoring reports.

7.3. Environmental compliance framework
7.3.1. Environmental and Social Responsibilities of the CPO/Detailed Design Consultants
During the preparation of the Terms of Reference for consultancy services and construction
bidding documents, the CPO will closely coordinate with the consulting experts to ensure that:
(i) contract packages and cost estimates include the implementation of the ESMP, independent
safeguard monitoring services, environmental sampling/monitoring and compliance
supervision, reporting, etc.; (ii) ESCOP and relevant general and specific mitigation measures
are incorporated into the bidding documents; and (iii) environmental monitoring and training
are included within the scope of work assigned to the construction supervision consultants. At
the feasibility study/detailed design stage, the CPO will closely coordinate with the feasibility
study consultants and detailed design engineers to ensure that green/landscape solutions,
environmentally friendly measures, and relevant mitigation measures proposed in the
ESIA/ESMP are reviewed and incorporated into the technical design where appropriate. During
construction, the CPO will closely coordinate with the supervision consultants to monitor
contractor compliance and report to the relevant authorities. The CPO will also direct the
supervision consultants and contractors on actions to be taken in the event of issues, incidents,
or accidents.
The CPO is mandatory required to establish a dedicated Environmental and Social Safety Unit
or Department comprising at least three full-time specialists for the entire project lifecycle,
including an Environmental Specialist responsible for ecology, water quality, acid sulfate soils,
and waste; a Social and Resettlement Specialist overseeing livelihoods, land acquisition, the
Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM), and community affairs; and an OHS/CHS Specialist
focused on occupational and community safety at major maritime or riverine construction sites.
These professionals must hold at least a university degree—with a preference for a Master’s—
in relevant fields such as Environmental Science, Water Resources Management, Sociology,
Anthropology, Occupational Safety, or Civil/Hydraulic Engineering, and they must


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  285

                                                                                       Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
demonstrate mastery of the World Bank’s ten Environmental and Social Standards (ESS).
Furthermore, candidates are required to possess 3 to 5 years of practical experience in ODA
projects, particularly those funded by the WB or ADB, alongside English proficiency sufficient
for reviewing international ESIA/ESMP reports and coordinating directly with World Bank
supervision missions.
The EO will monitor environmental issues and supervise subproject safety compliance. The EO
will be supported by the independent environmental supervision consultant, dedicated ES staff
at each subproject, environmental staff of the construction supervision team, and the
contractors. The SO will supervise and coordinate the social/resettlement aspects of the project.
The SO will be supported by the independent resettlement supervision consultant, social staff
of the construction supervision team, and contractors.
7.3.2. Environmental Responsibilities of the Contractors
The contractors are responsible for implementing measures to control and minimize negative
impacts arising from construction activities. Mitigation measures specified in the ESMP must
be fully applied to prevent adverse effects on local communities and the environment
throughout the construction phase. When mitigation measures cannot be effectively
implemented during construction, they must be carried out during the completion phase (and
before the issuance of the completion of acceptance certificate). The contractors’
responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following tasks:
-   Comply with relevant legislation on environmental management, community health, and
    safety;
-   Carry out work within the scope of the contract requirements and other bidding conditions;
-   Appoint representatives from the construction team to participate in on-site supervision
    organized by the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC;
-   Implement corrective actions under the guidance of the Environmental Officer (EO), Social
    Officer (SO) of the CPO, and the ES of the CSC;
-   In cases of non-compliance or deviations, investigate and propose mitigation measures, and
    implement corrective actions to minimize environmental/social impacts;
-   Halt construction activities when these generate adverse impacts exceeding the guidance
    provided by the EO and ES. Propose and implement remedial actions or alternative
    construction methods if necessary to reduce environmental impacts. Failure to comply may
    result in temporary suspension of construction or other penalties until the issue is resolved
    to the satisfaction of the EO and ES;
-   If the contractors propose to use raw material sources not included in the project ESIA, the
    contractors must report to the CSC and CPO and coordinate with them to carry out an
    environmental assessment of these sources to verify compliance with national
    environmental requirements. Only compliant sources may be used for this project;
-   Contractor shall conduct (i) surveying structures likely in affected areas and record status
    before construction; (ii) doing pile driving tests to identify areas of influence; (iii) control
    driving intensity; (iv) relocating and support rents for temporary relocation, or relocate
    permanently if at safety risks; and (v) compensating to those not relocated, not supported
    by affected;
-   The contractors are responsible for carrying out all corrective measures at its own cost. The
    contractors are also responsible for covering any costs or damages arising from non-
    compliance with the ESMP and/or applicable environmental regulations.
7.3.3. Contractors’ Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                286

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
After signing one contract, the contractor will be required to prepare and submit a specific
Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) for each contract package
to the CSC and CPO for review and approval. The deadline for preparing and submitting the
C-ESMP is within 28 to 30 days from the date of contract signing or the date of receiving the
Letter of Acceptance, depending on the specific provisions in the Particular Conditions of
Contract (PCC) and the Bidding Documents.
The objective of the Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) is to
provide guidance for environmental management of the proposed works/activities on the
project construction sites. This ensures that the contractor (and any subcontractors) minimizes
environmental impacts and has a plan to manage environmental issues on site. The C-ESMP
will detail how the contractor will mitigate impacts from construction activities and record the
contractor’s responses for monitoring, supervision, verification, internal audits, and corrective
or improvement actions for environmental conditions to be complied with. The C-ESMP must
be site-specific and include detailed control measures to be implemented on-site to mitigate any
potential environmental impacts from the proposed works/activities. If the proposed
works/activities in the C-ESMP change under the contract, the contractor will be required to
revise the C-ESMP to reflect these changes or modifications. The content of the C-ESMP must
include the following:
(i) Applicable policies/regulations, providing a definition of the contractor’s environmental
policy and guidance on the commitment to implement the on-site Environmental Management
Plan;
(ii) A brief description of documentation, including issue date, revision status, distribution list,
and detailed preparation of personnel and access;
(iii) Applicable laws and regulations related to the requirements in the project ESMP. Identify
contractor permits, licenses, and approvals related to the C-ESMP;
(iv) Details on how the environmental and social impacts identified in the ESIA will be
managed on-site, including: Specific on-site mitigation measures during construction; ESCOP;
Contractor ESMP to be developed after the contractor is selected and before construction begins;
Contractor dredging management plan that the contractor is required to prepare;
(v) Detailed environmental and social training that all contractor staff (including subcontractors)
must undertake. At minimum, all contractor staff working at project sites must: i) familiarize
themselves with and understand the C-ESMP for the works; ii) be aware of their environmental
responsibilities and legal obligations on site; iii) undergo health, safety, and emergency
response training;
(viii) Specific capabilities, support mechanisms, and resources required to implement the C-
ESMP satisfactorily. Detailed environmental responsibilities of all contractor staff, including
subcontractors on site, with appropriate knowledge, skills, and training for specific tasks, will
be identified;
(ix) The contractor is responsible for preparing monthly environmental reports, as part of the
progress reports required in the tender documents, including ad hoc reports, if necessary, to
submit to the project owner. The content of these reports may include:
-   Contractor implementation of the C-ESMP in accordance with the agreed schedule;
-   Identification of any difficulties in implementing the C-ESMP and recommendations to
    address them in the future;
-   Identification of non-compliance issues and proposed corrective actions;




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 287

                                                                                      Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Reports from subcontractors regarding C-ESMP implementation, including minutes of
    meetings and discussions organized by the contractor.
7.3.4. Responsibilities of Contractors’ Safety and Environmental Officer (C-ES)
Contractors are required to appoint a qualified individual as the Contractors’ Safety and
Environmental Officer (C-ES) on site. The C-ES must be trained in environmental management
and possess the necessary skills to convey environmental management knowledge to all
relevant personnel under the contract. The C-ES is responsible for supervising compliance with
the contractors’ Environmental Management Plan (C-ESMP) nd technical environmental
instructions. The responsibilities of the C-ES include:
-   Conduct on-site environmental supervision to assess the contractors’ compliance with
    ESHS requirements, including the use of tools and construction methods related to
    pollution control and the adequacy of implementing environmental mitigation measures;
-   Monitor compliance with environmental protection solutions, pollution prevention, control
    methods, and contractual requirements;
-   Supervise the implementation of environmental mitigation measures;
-   Prepare audit reports on environmental monitoring data and site environmental conditions;
-   Investigate complaints and propose corrective actions as required;
-   Advise the contractors on environmental improvement, awareness, and proactive
    implementation of pollution prevention measures;
-   Propose appropriate mitigation measures for the contractors in case of violations, conduct
    additional monitoring if violations occur, following guidance from the EO/SO of the CPO
    and CSC;
-   Notify the contractors, and the EO/SO (CPO and CSC) of environmental issues, submit the
    contractors’ ESMP implementation reports to the EO/SO (CPO and CSC), and relevant
    authorities if needed;
-   Maintain detailed records of on-site activities related to environmental management;
-   Utilize or assign qualified personnel to implement actions and measures necessary to
    address labor-related issues, including occupational health and safety (OHS) experts;
-   Prepare the contractors’ Labor Management Plan (LMP) and C-ESMP, including
    applicable OHS requirements for workers under contract on the project;
-   Submit the contractors’ LMP and C-ESMP to the CPO/CSC for review and approval before
    mobilizing staff to the project site for assigned tasks;
-   Supervise the construction work of subcontractors, including implementation of the
    contractors’ LMP and C-ESMP;
-   Maintain records of recruitment and employment for contract workers (including
    subcontractors), including documentation verifying minimum working age as required
    under the LMP;
-   Provide regular training for contract workers on issues including, but not limited to,
    occupational health and safety and other social risks such as GBV, SEA/SH, and codes of
    conduct to maintain good relationships with local communities;
-   Require main suppliers to identify and address risks related to child labor, forced labor,
    GBV, SEA/SH, and occupational health and safety for their workers;




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                           288

                                                                                Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Establish and implement a grievance mechanism based on the GRM set out in the LMP for
    contract workers, ensuring that grievances from contract workers are addressed in a timely
    manner, and report on the status of grievance handling to the CSC and CPO. This grievance
    mechanism will form part of the contractors’ LMP;
-   Ensure that all contractors’ and subcontractors’ workers understand and sign the Codes of
    Conduct before starting work;
-   Implement all necessary measures to address risks of exploitation and sexual abuse
    (SEA)/sexual harassment (SH) as specified in the contractors’ LMP/C-ESMP and ensure
    full compliance with these measures;
-   Report promptly to the CPO any incidents occurring at their construction site and on the
    implementation of labor, occupational health, and safety measures.
Incident Reporting
Contractors must notify the Construction Supervision and the Project Owner of any of the
following incidents within the agreed timeframe:
-   Any violations of laws, national regulations, or international agreements;
-   Any serious accidents or fatalities;
-   Significant impacts causing loss of personal property, such as traffic accidents, damage to
    houses/roads, and other incidents;
-   Serious surface water or groundwater pollution;
-   Failures of bunds at storage sites causing significant environmental contamination;
-   Forest fires related to actions of workers/laborers;
-   Any complaints related to sexual harassment, child abuse, or any other incident involving
    children;
-   Receipt of complaints regarding pollution or damage.
7.3.5. Responsibilities of Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant
(ISEMC)
The Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (ISEMC) will be contracted
by the CPO to provide environmental protection training for CPO staff and conduct
environmental inspections. ISEMC will perform monitoring, including:
-   Providing support to the CPO to establish and operate the environmental and social
    management system, coordinating with the CSC team to train contractors on the project’s
    environmental management requirements;
-   Organizing training times and implementing capacity-building activities for the CPO, CSC,
    contractors, and stakeholders on ESHS during project implementation. This includes
    conducting gender/labor awareness training for contractors’ staff, CSC staff, and CPO
    personnel;
-   Conducting random field inspections to verify compliance or recommend timely
    corrective/improvement actions;
-   Assessing environmental quality in areas affected by construction activities (including site
    observation, reviewing environmental quality data provided by the CSC, examining other
    available documents, and collecting additional samples if necessary);
-   Reviewing contractors’ environmental compliance, including the implementation of
    mitigation measures and related documentation;


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             289

                                                                                  Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Reviewing CPO’s and CSC’s compliance with the ESMP;
-   Reviewing CPO’s compliance with the RP and EMDP;
-   Providing guidance and technical support to the CPO and Environmental Officer (EO) on
    environmental and social issues.
Conduct ad-hoc field visits when:
Environmental incidents or serious accidents occur: When reports are received regarding
incidents such as oil spills, hazardous chemical leaks, severe riverbank/canal erosion, or
cofferdam failures that cause sudden changes to water quality and the ecosystem; when serious
occupational accidents (fatalities or severe injuries) occur on-site; or when incidents pose a
direct threat to the lives or property of local residents (e.g., a crane hitting power lines or
construction materials falling onto residential houses).
Urgent community complaints arise: Through the Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM), if
there are emergency reports or strong complaints from local residents regarding construction
activities severely impacting their lives: structural cracking or collapse of houses due to pile-
driving vibrations, noisy nighttime construction disturbing sleep, blocking access routes
without providing alternatives, or causing the loss of domestic/irrigation water sources.
Signs of serious violations of the Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP): When
the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC), local authorities, or the press discover and
report that the contractor is continuously and intentionally ignoring environmental protection
measures: unauthorized dumping of dredged sludge or construction waste in non-designated
areas, discharging untreated wastewater directly into canals, or failing to provide Personal
Protective Equipment (PPE) for workers in hazardous tasks.
Risks following natural disasters or extreme weather: Immediately after major storms, floods,
or abnormal high tides, the Independent Consultant may conduct ad-hoc field visits to assess
the integrity of temporary environmental protection works (such as embankments, silt curtains,
and material stockpile areas) and to review risks of secondary erosion.
Direct requests from the Client or the World Bank (WB): When the Client (Project Management
Unit/CPO) or WB missions identify potential risks that have not been fully assessed or need to
independently verify sensitive environmental and social information before making decisions
on disbursement or suspension of works.
7.3.6. Responsibilities of Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC)
During the construction phase, the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC), qualified as
required by the CPO, will conduct environmental monitoring. CSC will assign Environmental
and Social (ES) staff, who will be responsible for inspecting and supervising all construction
activities to ensure that mitigation measures set out in the ESMP are properly implemented and
that the Project’s negative environmental and social impacts are minimized. CSC will employ
a sufficient number of Environmental Supervision Engineers with adequate knowledge of
environmental protection and construction project management to carry out necessary tasks and
monitor the contractors’ performance. Specifically, CSC’s ES staff will:
-   Review and assess, on behalf of the CPO, whether the construction design meets the
    requirements of the mitigation measures and management of impacts in the ESMP;
-   Review and approve contractors’ Environmental and Social Management Plans (CESMP);
-   Coordinate with the CPO Environmental Officer (EO) to review environmental compliance
    at material and new quarry sites proposed and advise the CPO on whether these extraction
    sites meet the criteria for use in the Project;



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              290

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Verify and confirm monitoring parameters, locations, equipment, and results in accordance
    with the CPO’s environmental monitoring procedures;
-   Monitor the implementation of contractors’ CESMPs, including enforcement, timely
    recommendations for additional measures to complete mitigation actions, and providing
    guidance on corrective actions. If the contractor commits a serious violation (e.g., fatal
    labor accidents, large-scale chemical leaks or oil spills, or repeatedly ignoring Non-
    Compliance Notices), the CSC has the authority to suspend that specific construction item
    until the risk is fully mitigated. Furthermore, the CSC is entitled to propose that the Client
    withhold a portion of the contractor's payment until the violation has been resolved.
-   Organize and conduct awareness training on HIV/AIDS for all workers, CSC staff, and
    CPO personnel;
-   Conduct environmental quality monitoring, collect samples, and prepare periodic
    environmental monitoring reports, including reports on CESMP implementation status for
    the CPO, and prepare environmental monitoring reports during the construction phase;
-   Review payment requests related to environmental mitigation costs, if any.
7.3.7. Compliance with Laws and Contractual Requirements
Construction activities shall comply not only with the pollution control and environmental
protection requirements specified in the contract but also with the laws on pollution control and
environmental protection of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.
All reports on construction measures submitted by contractors to the CSC for approval shall
also be forwarded to the ES staff to review whether pollution control and environmental
protection measures have been fully addressed.
The ES staff will also review the progress of work items to check for any violations of
applicable environmental laws, so that potential legal non-compliance risks can be prevented.
Contractors shall periodically copy relevant documents to the CPO’s and CSC’s environmental
and social officers. These documents shall include at a minimum: construction progress reports,
updated construction measures, applications/registrations under environmental protection laws,
and all valid permits/registrations. The contractors’ on-site Environmental and Social Officer
(C-ES) and the CSC’s ES staff shall also have the right to access the site diary upon request.
Upon receiving these documents, the Senior Environmental Officer (SEO) and ES staff shall
advise the CPO Environmental Officer (EO) and the contractors on cases of non-compliance
with laws and contractual requirements for environmental protection and pollution control, to
guide subsequent actions. If the SEO and ES conclude that any permit/registration application
or any preparatory work for pollution control and environmental protection may not comply
with the construction measures or may result in potential non-compliance with pollution control
and environmental protection requirements, they shall provide advice to the contractors and the
CPO.
7.3.8. Environmental Complaints and Penalty System
The bidding documents will require contractors to arrange an Environmental, Social, Health,
and Safety (ESHS) Performance Security equivalent to 1–3% of the contract value. In the event
of serious non-compliance, environmental pollution, or social issues, and the contractor fails to
remediate them within the allowed timeframe, the funds from the ESHS Performance Security
shall be used to pay a third party engaged by the CPO to address these issues.
Furthermore, if the CSC/ES/ISEMC/CPO detects contractor for non-compliance with the
contractor’s ESMP or environmental regulations during field supervision. The contractor's
monthly interim payments may be withheld depending on the severity of the violation through


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               291

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
a penalty system classified into four levels: (i) Level 1 includes minor violations with small-
scale, localized impacts that are easily remediated immediately and leave no long-term
consequences for the environment or community, such as workers failing to wear full PPE in
low-risk areas, littering domestic waste within camp premises, or trucks spilling small amounts
of soil without immediate cleanup; (ii) Level 2 consists of moderate violations where impacts
extend beyond a narrow boundary, causing reversible environmental effects or community
nuisance, or repeated Level 1 violations despite reminders, such as oil leaks from excavators,
night construction noise resulting in complaints, or failing to suppress dust; (iii) Level 3 covers
serious violations causing distinct and widespread environmental damage, directly threatening
the health and safety of the community or workers, leading to severe complaints, or repeated
Level 2 violations, such as serious labor accidents involving fractures or disability, causing
structural cracks in local houses due to vibration without timely compensation, illegal dumping
of dredged sludge, or discharging toxic chemicals into rivers; and (iv) Level 4 represents
especially serious violations involving catastrophic incidents, irreversible environmental
damage, serious breaches of national law, or total disregard for suspension orders, such as fatal
accidents, the collapse of bridge spans under construction, causing local disease outbreaks, or
the use of child or forced labor. For Level 1 and 2 violations, administrative fines may be
applied and deducted directly from the payment for that month, while for Level 3 and 4
violations, the immediate suspension of construction activities at the specific items or areas
where the violation occurred may be enforced.
The contractor will be granted an extension (as determined by the CSC/CPO) to remedy the
non-compliance. If the contractor satisfactorily completes the corrective actions within the
extension period (as confirmed by the CSC/CPO), no penalty shall apply, and the remediation
costs shall be reimbursed to the contractor. However, if the contractor fails to successfully
complete the necessary corrective actions within the extension period, the contractor shall bear
the costs of engaging a third party to remedy the damages (deducted from their payment).
In cases where the ISEMC/CSC/CPO fails to detect the contractor’s non-compliance with
environmental requirements, these entities shall be responsible for covering the costs to remedy
the violations.
Based on this Environmental and Social Compliance Framework, including the penalty system,
the CSC shall develop a detailed penalty system for environmental and social non-compliance
and submit it to the CPO for approval and implementation.
7.3.9. Reporting Regime
The Project Management Office (CPO) is responsible for conducting internal monitoring of the
reporting regime. In addition, the CPO will engage an independent monitoring agency to carry
out independent supervision of the implementation of the Resettlement Plan (RP) and to assess
the living standards of affected persons during and after resettlement completion.
Both internal and independent monitoring will be conducted regularly during the construction
phase (on a monthly basis for internal monitoring and every six months for independent
monitoring). A final project evaluation of the implementation of the Resettlement Plan is
mandatory, and a report will be prepared to confirm whether the objectives of OP4.12 have
been achieved.
The tools for monitoring and reporting on the implementation of the ESMP are summarized in
the table below.

7.4. Environmental monitoring program
7.4.1. Construction Phase
Environmental management and monitoring will be led by the Project Owner (Central Project
Management Unit for Irrigation Projects) with the support of consulting or service units.
            Table 7. 9: Environmental monitoring during construction phase
  Monitoring content                              Construction stage
 1. Coastal seawater monitoring of Subproject 2.1
 Parameters monitored pH, TSS, DO, N-NH4+, oil and grease, coliform.
 Monitoring frequency quarterly during construction.
 Reference standard:    QCVN 10:2023/BTNMT – National technical regulation on seawater
                        quality.
