Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
7.4.2. Operation Phase
7.4.2.1. Management and Monitoring of Cumulative Impacts (RCIA Step 6)
A. Programmatic Management Approach
Following Step 6 of the IFC Good Practice Handbook, the management of cumulative impacts
requires shifting from isolated, subproject-specific mitigation to a coordinated, programme-
level strategy. Because the MERIT project generates significant internal cumulative stressors
across the Mekong Delta, the CPO/PMU cannot rely solely on individual contractors to manage
regional risks. The PMU will implement centralized, inter-regional management plans, backed
by robust monitoring indicators and effective supervision mechanisms, to ensure no VEC
exceeds it carrying capacity.
Establishment of a Multi-Stakeholder Coordination Mechanism:
CPO/PMUs will facilitate the Inter regional Management plan prior to the commencement of
construction. This mechanism will ensure synchronized action across jurisdictional boundaries
and concurrent projects:
+   Coordinated Water Infrastructure Operation (Subprojects 2.3 & 2.4): The simultaneous
    closure of the Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, and Nam Măng Thít regional sluice gates, combined
    with external water control projects (e.g., Southern Hậu River), could cause cumulative
    pollutant accumulation and severe oxygen depletion within the enclosed freshwater zones.
    The plan will facilitae the implementation of synchronized operation procedures based on
    shared, real-time automated monitoring data (salinity, pH, DO). Clear institutional
    responsibilities will be defined so that in the event of conflicting water-use demands (e.g.,
    retaining freshwater for agriculture vs. flushing polluted water for aquaculture), emergency
    opening/closure thresholds are managed collectively at the regional level, with final
    decision-making authority resting with the Provincial People’s Committee Chairmen.
+   Participatory Monitoring and Data Sharing: In accordance with WB ESS10, the PMU will
    mandate that project-level environmental monitoring data (water quality, sediment, and
    traffic incident logs) be openly shared with government agencies, external developers, and
    local communities. Community-based monitoring boards—facilitated by local Women's
    Unions and Fatherland Fronts—will be integrated into this coordination mechanism,
    providing a direct grievance pathway for local residents to report cumulative nuisances
    (e.g., overlapping noise, dust, or localized flooding) so that the co-management committee
    can mandate immediate, multi-party corrective actions.
B. Specific Management and Monitoring Strategies per VEC
VEC 1: Surface Water Quality and Aquatic Ecosystems
+   Management Strategy: To mitigate the severe cumulative risk of creating a 226,682-hectare
    stagnant freshwater zone, the PMU will implement a Formal Inter-Regional Water Quality
    Management Plan. General aspirations toward "flexible scheduling" are prohibited. The
    operating procedures for the Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop, and Nam Mang Thit structures
    (with already operational sluice gates of Tan Dinh, Bong Bot, Vung Liem invested by WB9
    project) must be synchronized and prioritize water quality safety on par with salinity
    control. The plan mandates periodic gate openings (ecological flushing flows) even during
    the dry season to dilute accumulated agricultural chemicals and domestic wastewater,
    preventing eutrophication and mass fish kills.
+   Monitoring & Supervision:




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              296

                                                                                   Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
+   Real-time Automated Network: Sluice gates must be equipped with automated monitoring
    networks measuring salinity, pH, and turbidity, transmitting data in real-time to a central
    command center to trigger automated gate operations.
+   Defined Thresholds: Dissolved Oxygen (DO) must strictly not fall below 4 mg/L (the
    critical survival threshold for aquatic life).
Periodic Sampling: Quarterly sampling of surface water, bottom sediment, must be conducted
to assess pollutants that automated systems cannot measure, such as heavy metals and pesticide
residues. ESMP will be more specific on this.
VEC 2: Hydrology, Erosion, and Sedimentation
+   Management Strategy: To address the long-term cumulative depletion of inland channels
    caused by sediment trapping behind the newly installed locks and sluice gates, the PMU
    will develop a 50-year Maintenance Dredging Strategy. This must be integrated into the
    project's long-term operational sustainability and budget plan to ensure regional irrigation
    capacity and navigability are maintained over the infrastructure's lifespan. Furthermore,
    sudden, full openings or slamming of gates are strictly prohibited to prevent hydrodynamic
    shock waves that cause severe downstream scouring.
+   Monitoring & Supervision: Operation unit (Vinh Long and Can Tho water resouces
    company) will conduct periodic riverbed bathymetric surveys (using echo-sounding) every
    six months at both ends of the major sluice gates. If scour holes or abnormal sediment bars
    are detected, timely remediation (e.g., placing riprap/gabions or maintenance dredging)
    must be initiated. The state budget will secure for this.
VEC 3: Inland Waterway Traffic and Logistics
+   Management Strategy: To prevent the simultaneous collapse of regional waterway
    transport during the 24–36-month construction window, the PMU will shift from localized
    contractor-led traffic management to a Delta-Wide Coordinated Waterway Traffic
    Management Plan. This includes utilizing smart waterway traffic systems (cameras, signal
    lights) to minimize barge waiting times at successive locks, ensuring the continuity of
    regional commodity supply chains.
+   Under construction, n narrow waterway pinch-points (such as the 34m-wide sections of the
    Phước Xuyên – Hai Tam canal), contractors are strictly prohibited from conducting
    simultaneous earthworks on both banks. Dredging must be executed progressively (section-
    by-section), legally mandating that at least 50% of the navigable channel width remains
    unobstructed for local traffic at all times.
+   Managing the Social Dimension of Waterway Disruption: Addressing the direct livelihoods
    of communities dependent on these waterways, the PMU and contractors must implement
    targeted social protections:
Mandatory Advance Notification: Contractors must notify local ferry operators, fish traders,
and aquaculture households at least 15 days (two weeks) in advance of dredging fleets reaching
their specific canal sections. This notification must be delivered directly to the affected groups
and broadcast via local radio, not merely posted at the Commune People's Committees (CPCs).
Protection of Cross-Canal Transit: Dredging pipelines and steel ramp bridges must be
designed to avoid obstructing informal passenger ferry ramps. If dredging temporarily severs
a ferry crossing, the project must provide temporary bridging or alternative transit support to
ensure students and market vendors can cross safely.
+   Standardized Navigation Safety Infrastructure: The PMU will mandate uniform
    deployment of high-visibility navigation buoys, reflective signage, and signal lights across


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               297

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    all subprojects. During peak construction hours or in dense residential zones, dedicated
    traffic controllers in motorized patrol boats must be deployed to guide 400-ton material
    barges safely past local wooden boats.
+   Under operation: To manage the permanent transition of the Hậu and Cổ Chiên river
    tributaries into a regulated, multi-lock transit corridor, the operating unit will implement
    regional logistical synchronization to prevent severe supply chain delays.
+   The operating procedures for the MERIT locks (Đại Ngãi, Mỹ Xuyên, Cái Hóp) must be
    digitally integrated with external structures (e.g., the Southern Hậu River sluice gates).
    Lock chambers must operate on a synchronized schedule to allow interprovincial barges a
    "green wave" transit, minimizing idle waiting times between successive locks.
+   Monitoring & Supervision: The Independent Environmental Monitoring Consultant (IEMC)
    and the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC) will monitor specific elements of zero
    severe vessel collisions within the project AoI, maximum wait times at operational lock
    chambers (e.g., tracking whether vessels are delayed beyond acceptable economic
    thresholds, 100% compliance with the 15-day advance notification rule for dredging blocks,
    100% functionality of nighttime navigation lights on anchored construction vessels and
    cofferdams…During operation, lock opening, closing, and water-level balancing schedules
    must be publicized in real-time via mobile applications and local waterway radio
    frequencies, allowing barge captains and local fishers to adjust their transit times efficiently.
VEC 4: Road Infrastructure and Local Air Quality
+   Management Strategy: The simultaneous mobilization of heavy transport fleets requires a
    Traffic Management Plan. Contractors are strictly prohibited from transporting materials
    during peak community hours (e.g., school opening/closing times). Stringent dust
    suppression protocols are mandatory, including covering all transport trucks and watering
    unpaved routes and disposal sites at least 3-4 times daily during the dry season.
+   Monitoring & Supervision: Included in the bidding document of all contractors, any
    structural damages, subsidence, or potholing on local provincial and inter-commune roads
    caused cumulatively by project transport must be fully reinstated by the contractors
    immediately following construction. Ambient air quality (TSP, PM10) will be monitored
    periodically at highly sensitive receptors, such as local schools and clinics by the
    Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) of the project
    during the construction.
VEC 5: Social Infrastructure and Labor Influx
Management Strategy: the PMU will ensure that a Worker Influx Management Plan as part of
the Contractor’s Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) prior to the
commencement of any civil works will be required to manage the risks relating to Worker
Influx & Social Pressures. To avoid overburdening local social infrastructure:
Healthcare Capacity: Prior to mobilization, the PMU and contractors must consult with local
health authorities in each project commune to assess existing healthcare capacity. Contractors
are also required to provide on-site first aid and basic primary healthcare for workers at the
camps to ensure local health stations are not overburdened.
Water Supply Pressure: To prevent resource competition with local residents, contractors must
assess whether the local water supply infrastructure in affected communes has sufficient
capacity to serve both the existing population and the incoming workforce. Where community
supply is constrained, contractor camps are strictly required to independently source their water
(e.g., via authorized commercial water tankers) rather than drawing from community supply
systems


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 298

                                                                                      Official Use Only
    Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    Disease Transmission: Contractors are required to implement strict health screenings and
    vaccination programs for workers to prevent the transmission of vector-borne diseases (such
    as dengue fever and malaria), waterborne diseases, and communicable infections (including
    STDs, and HIV/AIDS) that thrive in crowded accommodations or through interaction with
    local communities. Mandatory hygiene, sanitation, and waste management standards must be
    continuously maintained at all worker camps
    GBV and SEA/SH: The influx of male construction workers poses disproportionate risks to
    women and girls in nearby communities, including increased risks of SEA/SH. The Worker
    Influx Management Plan will include targeted measures to address these risks, including the
    establishment of confidential, survivor-centered reporting channels specifically for GBV and
    SEA/SH incidents. Crucially, the PMU and contractors will engage local Women's Union
    representatives and community leaders in the continuous monitoring of GBV/SEA/SH risks
    and the effectiveness of the implemented mitigation measures
    Social Conflict Risk and Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM): Differences in culture,
    behavior, and the influx of external workers can lead to tensions with host communities. To
    manage this, the project’s Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) will be made fully accessible
    to local community members—not just workers—and will explicitly cover complaints related
    to worker behavior, camp nuisances, and social conflicts. The PMU must ensure that
    community members are fully informed of the GRM, including contact points and procedures,
    before workers arrive on site.
+   Monitoring & Supervision: The project Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) will be made
    accessible to local community members, specifically covering complaints related to worker
    behavior and social conflicts. Local Women's Union representatives will be engaged to
    continuously monitor GBV/SEA/SH risks.
    VEC 6: Regional Livelihoods and Water-Use:
+   Management Strategy: To manage the complex water-use between freshwater agriculture
    (orchards/rice) and brackish aquaculture (shrimp) within the massive, enclosed zone, the PMU
    will shift from localized mitigation to a programmatic, participatory water management
    framework. In particular, the PMU and local authorities must formally capture both the positive
    benefits and the unavoidable bottlenecks in all community-level communications.
+   Prior to the dry season, the operating agency should hold public meetings to transparently
    explain the operational trade-offs to affected communities. This ensures that both upstream
    fruit farmers and downstream shrimp farmers understand the dual benefits the system is
    designed to deliver—specifically, freshwater security for 51,500 hectares of cultivation and
    stabilized, improved salinity conditions for shrimp farming in Mỹ Xuyên. Transparent
    communication of these trade-offs is legally essential for maintaining the social acceptance of
    the infrastructure. Secondly, the decisions on gate opening and closing will not rest solely with
    technical operators. The operation unit will establish zones and flexible management plans for
    freshwater and brackish aquaculture using a highly participatory approach for determining the
    operating schedules of the water control infrastructure. This Formal Multi-Stakeholder Water
    User Committee will include the representatives from both farming and aquaculture
    communities, ensuring affected livelihood groups have a structured, democratic channel to
    raise concerns and provide input on operational adjustments. Thirdly, the time for closing the
    sluice gates will be consulted closely with the local community and informed to the local
    people at least 1 week (to 15 days) in advance. A detailed, mutually agreed-upon sluice gate
    operation schedule will be developed and distributed to the community through locally
    appropriate channels (e.g., commune loudspeakers, local radio, and mobile applications). Last
    but not least, acknowledging that farmers transitioning from freshwater crops to climate-
    resilient brackish aquaculture face high initial risks, the project will implement step-by-step


    Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              299

                                                                                       Official Use Only
    Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    livelihood support programs, including agricultural extension, capacity building, and market
    connectivity, ensuring vulnerable households (e.g., ethnic minorities, elderly) are not left
    behind during this regional ecological transition. In this regard, the PMU will maximize
    component 3 of the MERIT project to support affected households if any.
+   Monitoring & Supervision: In compliance with ESS10 and specified in Stakeholder
    Engagement Plan (SEP), the project will maintain a robust, accessible GRM specifically
    tailored to water-use conflicts. If improper gate operations (e.g., prolonged closure causing
    shrimp mortality from hypoxia, or premature opening causing crop loss from sudden salinity
    intrusion) result in direct economic damage, residents must have access to this GRM for prompt
    investigation, resolution, and livelihood compensation if the damage is verified as an
    operational error. In addition to that, monitoring by local community boards will be conducted
    following Government practices, with technical support from the PMU and WB (via
    consultants if required). Local mass organizations (e.g. Farmers' Union, Women's Union) will
    be empowered to continuously monitor the actual implementation of the agreed gate operation
    schedules and report any unannounced deviations that threaten local harvests. Importantly, the
    operation unit will track programmatic livelihood indicators over the first years of operation,
    including: (i) 100% compliance with the advance notification requirement for gate closures,
    (ii) Documented minutes of the Multi-Stakeholder Water User Committee meetings proving
    community input was integrated into seasonal water management plans, (iii) Grievances
    registered related to water-use conflicts that are responded to and resolved within the stipulated
    service standards.
    7.4.2.2 Environmental quality monitoring
    As no waste is generated during the operation phase, the environmental monitoring program
    does not propose environmental sampling and analysis during this stage.
    For the dredged canal routes of Phuoc Xuyen Hai Tam Canal and Cho Bung Canal, monitoring
    of sedimentation rates shall be conducted to support periodic maintenance planning; and the
    effectiveness of flood drainage, waterlogging control, and freshwater conveyance shall be
    assessed against the original project objectives.
    At locations with sluices/navigation locks, real-time salinity monitoring shall be implemented
    to adjust opening and closing schedules flexibly and appropriately, thereby minimizing
    conflicts in water use. In addition, automatic and periodic (sampling-based) monitoring of
    upstream water quality when gates are closed shall be conducted. If signs of “cumulative
    impacts” are detected, such as a sharp decrease in DO (dissolved oxygen), algal blooms
    (eutrophication), or localized salinity increases due to leakage through the lock chamber,
    immediate reporting is required to activate gate opening and flushing operations.
    Monitoring of barge navigation through navigation locks (Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, Cai Hop) shall
    be conducted, particularly for incidents such as gate jamming, mooring line breakage, or saline
    intrusion into inland areas due to improper gate operation.
    Monitoring of periodic maintenance shall be carried out, especially the collection of waste oil
    and oil-contaminated rags from hydraulic cylinders and backup generators, ensuring no leakage
    into the river.
    Waterway traffic safety monitoring: operation of navigation signal systems (green/red lights)
    at both ends of the navigation lock and public announcement systems shall be monitored.
    Operators must directly supervise via cameras and radio communication to guide vessels and
    barges to designated berthing locations for water level balancing, ensuring safe distances to
    avoid collisions with lock gates. Periodic inspection of navigation buoys, clearance signage,
    navigation channels, and anti-drift barriers outside the sluices shall be conducted.


    Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               300

                                                                                        Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
For road traffic (on bridge decks): monitoring of load limit signs, speed limit signs, and bridge
lighting systems shall be conducted. Periodic coordination with local traffic inspection
authorities shall be undertaken to check overloading violations by heavy trucks crossing the
bridge, in order to protect bridge structures.
Management of water pollution from stormwater runoff on bridge decks: regularly inspect deck
drainage inlets and downpipes to ensure they are not clogged by plastic waste, leaves, or
sediment. Periodic (especially before the rainy season) inspection and dredging of accumulated
sludge and debris in sedimentation pits along access roads and beneath bridge abutments shall
be carried out.
Emergency response procedures (operational risks, natural disasters, occupational accidents):
Operational incidents (gate jamming, mooring line breakage): If a main gate is jammed due to
debris and cannot be opened/closed, immediately activate the alarm procedure and mobilize
divers or specialized cranes to remove the obstruction. If a vessel’s mooring line breaks within
the lock chamber, issue public announcements to evacuate people from the danger zone and
deploy tugboats/rescue boats to control and tow the vessel.
Natural disaster incidents (storms, extreme floods): Closely monitor weather forecasts. Upon
instruction from the Provincial Disaster Prevention and Control Committee, all personnel must
be mobilized for standby duty. Activate operational procedures to fully open gates for inland
flood discharge or fully close gates to prevent storm surge intrusion. Materials (sandbags,
gabions) shall be prepared to reinforce weak embankment sections on both sides of the sluice.
Occupational accidents (drowning, electric shock): At navigation lock areas, adequate life
buoys with throw lines, stretchers, and first aid kits must always be available. In the event of a
person falling into the water, immediately deploy life buoys, lower rescue ladders, and activate
station-wide alarms. In cases of electrical faults or mechanical accidents, the first responder
must immediately press the “Emergency Stop” button at the nearest control panel, disconnect
power, and provide first aid before contacting emergency medical services.
7.5. Training and capacity building program on environmental management
The table below provides a typical training program on safeguard policies. The training
programs will be developed and implemented by a Technical Assistance team to support the
implementation of safeguard policies for the CPO. The CPO/IESMC, with the support of the
Technical Assistance team, will provide training to environmental and social staff of contractors,
the CSC, and other relevant groups.
-   Trainees: Management staff and environmental and social staff of the CPO, Construction
    Supervision Consultants (CSC), contractors, procurement staff of the CPO; representatives
    of stakeholders and local communities in the project area. Contractors are responsible for
    training workers and drivers.
-   Training schedule: Training will be conducted at least one month prior to the
    commencement of the first construction contract. Subsequent training times may be
    adjusted to align with the construction schedule of project components.
-   Training frequency: The basic training programs outlined in the table below will be
    delivered every six months, with content updated to reflect the specific activities being
    implemented. Training programs for CPO staff are expected to continue during the initial
    years of the Project. Three-day training times for CSC and contractors are also planned to
    be conducted twice per year for at least two years.
 Table 7. 10: Training and Capacity Building Program on Environmental Management


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               301

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
                             Module 1: Training for E&S Staffs of CPOs
 Training        - The project will provide CPOs’ E&S staff with training as a part of CPOs
 course             capacity building on aspects of ESF requirements and relevant instruments.
                 - E&S (Environmental & Social) training for CPO staff is crucial for managing
                    project risks, focusing on World Bank Environmental & Social Standards
                    (ESS), grievance mechanisms, stakeholder engagement, labor and working
                    conditions and standards, and contractor oversight. Training covers
                    implementing E&S instruments, managing ESHS performance, codes of
                    conduct (like SEA/SH), and preparing reports, with plans developed post-
                    project effectiveness.
 Trainee         - Designated E&S Staff and other relevant CPO Staff
 Timeframe       - Initial training shall be provided within 3 months after the Effectiveness Date.
                 - Periodic trainings shall be provided at least once a year, and as needed, during
                    project implementation.
 Duration       2 days
 Content         - E&S Risk Management: Understanding and applying the World Bank's
                    Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) and Standards (ESSs)
                 - Project-Specific Instruments: Implementing the Environmental and Social
                    Management Plan (ESMP), Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), and Labor
                    Management Procedures (LMP) and RP.
                 - Grievance Mechanisms: Handling complaints and feedback effectively
                 - Stakeholder Engagement: Mapping stakeholders and conducting meaningful
                    engagement
                 - Contractor Management: Ensuring contractors implement E&S requirements
                 - Codes of Conduct: Training on codes related to Sexual Exploitation and
                    Abuse/Sexual Harassment (SEA/SH)
                 - Reporting & Monitoring: Preparing E&S performance reports and incident

7.6.4. Summary of ESMP implementation costs
The table below provides an estimated cost for the implementation of the Environmental and
Social Management Plan (ESMP). The ESMP14 implementation cost will include: (i) cost of
implementing mitigation measures by contractors, (ii) supervision cost by CSC, (iii) cost for
the Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC), (iv)
environmental quality monitoring cost, and (v) training costs on safeguard management for
CPO, including technical assistance for implementation of safeguard policies and training
programs. The costs for implementing mitigation measures during construction will be included
in construction contract costs, while the costs for supervision of the Contractor’s Environmental
and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) by the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC)
will be covered under construction supervision contracts. Costs for CPO’s activities related to
ESMP will be drawn from the CPO’s project management budget, including basic safeguard
training programs and allowances for participants in monitoring activities. After project
completion, environmental monitoring costs for constructed works will be covered by
provincial operation and maintenance budgets.
It should be noted that community participation in ESMP implementation is entirely voluntary,
for the benefit of the community and their households. Therefore, community members
participating in ESMP monitoring will not receive wages.

IESMC (in line with national practices) for reference purposes. However, final costs will be
updated during the detailed design stage.

7.7. Project-level Grievance Redress Mechanism


14Excluding costs for the implementation of the Resettlement Plan (RP) and independent monitoring of RP implementation .


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                                    305

                                                                                                        Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
Within the legal framework of Vietnam, citizens lodging complaints are entitled to protection.
As part of overall project implementation, a grievance redress mechanism (GRM) will be
overseen by the EO and SO of the CPO, including procedures, responsible persons, and contact
details. It will be easily accessible to ensure that complaints are addressed and resolved at the
lowest possible level as quickly as possible. This mechanism will provide a framework within
which complaints related to environmental and safety issues can be received, addressed, and
disputes resolved in a timely manner. The GRM will be established prior to the commencement
of construction.
During construction, the GRM will also be managed by contractors under the supervision of
CSC. Contractors will inform affected communities and communes about the availability of the
GRM to address project-related complaints and concerns. This will be carried out through
community consultation and information disclosure processes, whereby contractors will
regularly engage with affected communities and relevant authorities. Meetings will be held at
least quarterly and monthly, project information leaflets will be distributed, announcements will
be made through local media, and notices of upcoming activities will be posted. Contractors
should provide contact information for submitting complaints when communicating
construction progress to local communities.
All complaints and corresponding actions taken by contractors will be recorded in subproject
monitoring reports.
Project-affected households will thus be able to submit complaints directly to contractors or
through local government authorities via the following channels:
-   Verbal: directly to CSC, contractor security staff or representatives at site offices or
    Commune/Ward People’s Committee.
-   Written: by hand delivery or submission of complaint letters to designated addresses.
-   Telephone, fax, or email: to CSC, security staff, contractor representatives or
    Commune/Ward People’s Committee.
Upon receipt of a complaint, CSC, the Contractor’s safeguard officer/ representative or the
Commune/Ward People’s Committee will register the complaint in the grievance log and record
all relevant events until the complaint is resolved. Immediately after receiving a complaint, it
shall be copied into four sets. The original will be kept on file; one copy will be retained by the
receiving officer; one copy will be sent to CSC; and the final copy will be submitted to CPO
within 24 hours of receipt.
Information recorded in the complaint form will include:
-   Date and time of the complaint
-   Name, address, and contact details of the complainant
-   Brief description of the complaint
-   Actions taken to resolve the complaint, including contacts and findings at each stage of the
    resolution process
-   Date and time the complainant was contacted during the resolution process
-   Final decision on the complaint
-   Date, time, and method by which the complainant was informed
-   Signature of the complainant upon decision




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                306

                                                                                     Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
Minor complaints will be resolved within one week. Within two weeks (and weekly thereafter),
a written response will be provided to the complainant (by hand, post, fax, or email), detailing
the procedures undertaken and a timeline of actions.
The main objective is to resolve issues as quickly as possible using the simplest means,
involving as few parties as necessary, and at the lowest possible level. Only when issues cannot
be resolved at this level and/or within 15 days will other competent authorities be involved.
Such cases may arise, for example, where compensation claims cannot be agreed upon or where
the causes of damage need to be determined.
The grievance procedures and resolution process are presented in the table below.
                       Table 7. 14: Grievance Redress Mechanism
First Level        Affected households submit their complaints to the contractor
CSC/Contractor or representative or CSC directly, or one of the officials of the
Commune/Ward        Commune/Ward People’s Committee, either through the village/hamlet
People’s            head or directly to the Commune/Ward People’s Committee, in written
Committee.          or verbal form. The Commune/Ward officials or village head will
                    inform the Commune/Ward People’s Committee of the complaint, or in
                    the case of the contractor, they will ensure the complaints are handled
                    and coordinated by the contractor’s E&S staff. The Commune/Ward
                    People’s Committee or Contractor will work directly with the affected
                    household and provide a solution within 5 working days after receiving
                    the complaint. The secretariat of the Commune/Ward People’s
                    Committee is responsible for recording and maintaining all complaint
                    files under resolution; the equivalent is the E&S officer of the
                    contractor. After the Commune/Ward People’s Committee or
                    contractor issues a decision, the household may lodge a further
                    complaint within 30 working days. If a second decision has been issued
                    and the household is still not satisfied, the household may escalate the
                    complaint to the Provincial People’s Committee or CPO directly.
Second Level       Upon receiving complaints from households, the Provincial People’s
Provincial People’s Committee or CPO will have 30 working days to resolve the case. The
Committee or CPO Provincial People’s Committee or CPO is responsible for recording and
                    maintaining all complaint files submitted. After the Provincial People’s
                    Committee or CPO issues a decision, the household may lodge a
                    complaint within 45 working days. If a second decision has been issued
                    and the household is still not satisfied, they have the right to bring the
                    case to the People’s Court within 45 working days. Subsequently, the
                    Provincial People’s Committee will, if relevant, transfer the
                    compensation amount into an account.
Third Level         In case the complainant files a lawsuit with the People’s Court at
Court.              commune or provincial level and the Court rules in favor of the
                    complainant, the decision must be sent to the complainant and relevant
                    parties and publicly disclosed at the office of the relevant People’s
                    Committee. The complainant will receive the decision within 3 days
                    from the date of issuance at commune/ward level and within 7 working
                    days at commune/provincial level.
The EO and SO of the CPO will be tasked with routine follow up of grievances, whether they
have been submitted through the contractor or local government.

direct workers, contracted workers, and primary supply workers) may directly register their

handling workers’ grievances, including workplace complaints, in an effective and appropriate
manner to comply with national labor regulations and the World Bank (ESS2). Refer to the

World Bank Grievance Redress Mechanism: Individuals and communities who believe that a
project supported by the World Bank has adversely affected their lives may submit their
complaints to the existing project-level grievance redress mechanism or to the World Bank’s
Grievance Redress Service (GRS). This service ensures that submitted complaints are promptly
reviewed in order to address project-related concerns. Project-affected individuals or
communities may also submit their complaints to the World Bank’s independent Inspection
Panel to determine whether harm has occurred or could occur as a result of non-compliance
with the World Bank’s policies and procedures. Complaints may be submitted at any time after
concerns have been brought to the World Bank’s attention and Bank Management has been
given an opportunity to respond. For more information on how to submit complaints to the
World Bank’s Grievance Redress Service (GRS), please visit www.worldbank.org/grs. For
more information on how to submit complaints to the World Bank Inspection Panel, please visit
www.inspectionpanel.org.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                308

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
    CHAPTER 8: PUBLIC CONSULTATION AND INFORMATION DISCLOSURE


The World Bank’s Social and Environmental Framework (ESF), ESS1 (Assessment and
Management of Environmental and Social Risks) and Environmental and Social Standard 10
(ESS10) require consultation and notification of affected people and local authorities regarding
environmental and social issues during project preparation. Community consultation for the
ESIA report must also comply with the requirements of Point a, Clause 3, Article 26 of Decree
08/2022/ND-CP of the Government detailing certain provisions of the Law on Environmental
Protection, as amended by Decree 05/2025/ND-CP dated January 6, 2025.
The purpose of consultation includes: Consultation activities involving the participation of local
authorities and local communities in the project areas during the preparation and
implementation of the ESMP and ESIA aim to provide necessary information to better
understand the project, its potential impacts, and the possible mitigation measures; To clarify
issues discussed during the initial phase of the project; To inform stakeholders of the benefits
to be achieved from project implementation; To encourage community participation in
identifying the project’s environmental impacts; and To collect information on the needs and
feedback of local people and authorities for developing recommendations to mitigate
environmental impacts or to consider adjustments during the detailed engineering design stage.
Information disclosure and community consultation on environmental issues ensure the
acceptance of local authorities, local non-governmental organizations, and affected local
residents within the subproject area. Community participation is one of the fundamental
conditions for securing the support of local authorities and communities for the project and
taking their perspectives into account. Through community consultation, previously
unidentified adverse environmental impacts and potential mitigation measures can be recorded
and incorporated into the ESIA report. In practice, if the community participates early in the
project preparation process, the relationship between the community and project staff becomes
closer. Subsequently, the community can continue to provide feedback and raise any concerns
during project implementation.
8.1. Stakeholder Identification and Analysis
Project stakeholders are defined as individuals, groups, or other entities that: (i) are impacted
or likely to be affected directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the Project; or (ii) may
have an interest in the project. These include individuals or interest groups who may be affected
by the project and those who have the potential to influence the Project's outcomes in any
manner. Based on the above definition, the project stakeholders include:
-   People physically or economically displaced;
-   Land users;
-   Fishing communities in area where works in water are planned (e.g,some clams or
    aquacultural cooperatives and water hyacinth farmers.
-   Owners of fishing boats, local big boats and ferries used for transport of
    goods/agricultural products where new ship lock/sluices gates are built;
-   Water/water way users
-   Project beneficiaries
-   Local community around the project area;
-   Women (particularly women-headed households or single mothers with underage/
    dependent children);


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 309

                                                                                      Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Ethnic groups (especially Khmer, Hoa);
-   Other vulnerable groups (disabled, poor/near poor, landless households, unemployed
    persons)
-   Elderly people and/or social policy householders (invalid soldiers, martyrs, heroic
    mothers);
-   Mass organizations, such as the Fatherland Front, the Women's Union, and the Farmers'
    Union.
-   Households and business households living in or around the project construction area.
-   Other organizations or individuals with interests related to the outcomes and/or impacts
    of the subproject, including: (i) the Provincial People's Committee (PPC), and (ii) the
    Ward/Commune People's Committees (W/CPCs).
8.2. Consultation Process and Methods
The consultation process was conducted using face to face interview methods and direct
community consultations, the face to face interview method is used for the group of officials
from department of agriculture and environment. The community consultation is used for
residents in the project area.
Face to face interview:
+ In May 2025, direct interviews were conducted with provincial- and district-level departments
and agencies, as well as households within the project area. The purpose of this phase of
consultation was to introduce the proposed investment components, including: presentation of
the project; preliminary identification of the potential positive and negative environmental and
social impacts of each proposed option; identification of sensitive receptors and affected areas;
initial assessment of the extent of impacts, including land acquisition, and impacts on structures
and vegetation associated with the implementation of project components; and to discuss and
solicit feedback from representatives of departments, agencies, and participating households.
+ Direct interviews were conducted with officials from the Departments of Agriculture and
Environment of the provinces at the department headquarters from February 2–6, 2026. The
purpose is to inform relevant departments and agencies of the project’s development policy,
present its positive, negative, and potential impacts during the construction phase, and collect
other relevant data and information. The specific consultation results are presented in Table 8.2.
Community consultation meeting: Direct community consultation meetings were organized at
the headquarters of the Commune People's Committees from February 2–6, 2026. The purpose
is to disseminate basic information about the project, present potential positive and negative
impacts during project implementation, and seek consensus from local authorities and residents.
The main contents of the community consultation meetings are:
-   Overview of the project, its investment components, and identification of hamlets/villages
    within the project area.
-   Introduction to the basic requirements of the World Bank’s Social and Environmental
    Framework.
-   Current status of environmental and social issues related to the investment works within
    the communes and outstanding issues.
-   Potential environmental and social impacts of the project on local communities including
    scope of impacts on land acquisition



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               310

                                                                                    Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Community-contributed solutions to limit and mitigate environmental and social impacts
    during project implementation.
-   Feedback on the construction of project works.
Participants in the community consultation meetings included:
-   Representatives of the Project Management Unit (CPO);
-   Representatives of consultants preparing the ESIA/EIA, RP, EMDP, and Feasibility Study;
-   Leaders of the Commune People’s Committee;
-   Representatives of local departments, agencies, and mass organizations;
-   Village/hamlet heads or deputies;
-   Representatives of affected households.

The specific grievance resolution process for land acquisition has already been outlined in the RP. The concerns or questions on land acquisition,
the grievance resolution process will be handled in accordance with the grievance redress mechanism as presented in the RP. For grievances
related to construction impacts, affected persons may submit their grievances directly to the contractor before escalating the grievance to local
authorities... The project's grievance mechanism has already been specifically described in the community consultations during the ESIA
preparation.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                324
                       Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
8.4. Public Consultation during Project Implementation
Supplementary consultations will be conducted during project implementation. The CPO will coordinate with local authorities to publicly
disclose project information, including objectives, locations, and project components. Additionally, the CPO, with support from the loan
implementation consultant, will be responsible for conducting consultations with local residents and affected stakeholders during the
construction process.
                                            Table 8. 3: Consultation Plan for the Next Phase
 Implementi       Form of
                                          Frequency                          Content                                  Participants

8.5. Information Disclosure
The first drafts of the ESIA, SEP, LMP, and ESCP in Vietnamese are expected to be disclosed locally at the communes within the project area
and at the Project Management Board in 04 May year 2026 for community consultation. Based on the contents of the ESIA, local people could
access information about the Project and provide their comments/feedback on environmental and social issues.
The final drafts of the ESIA, RP, SEP, LMP, and ESCP in English are expected to be disclosed on the WB external website in 08 May 2026
for public review. The final ESIA, in both Vietnamese and English, will also be disclosed locally at project locations and on the WB website.
Following the community consultations conducted across 29 wards and communes in the 04 provinces, the key feedback received includes:
Notification to local authorities and residents regarding construction schedules, water cutoff periods, and sluice gate operation times (if any)
must be provided at least 1–2 weeks in advance.
+ During project implementation, compensation and assistance policies for impacts on residential land, garden land, crops, or loss of
livelihoods must comply with the regulations of both the Government and the Donor.
+ Several households in Subproject 2.2 requested the use of hard embankments instead of soft embankments to reinforce both banks. They
also requested a re-evaluation of dredging methods, as these could potentially cause erosion to house or gardens within the project area.
+ An oil spill response plan is required to prevent widespread contamination in the event of an incident.
+ Transportation routes: The Project Owner is requested to select appropriate material transport methods. If transporting by road, vehicles
must comply with load capacity limits, and stone/gravel should be spread to prevent damage to rural roads.
The Project Owner acknowledges all information and feedback from local authorities and residents within the project area. These concerns
will be relayed to the Design Consultant for review and further survey to ensure appropriate design adjustments that minimize losses for
affected households to the lowest possible level.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                   326
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
                CHAPTER 9: CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATION
9.1. Conclusion
The Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT)
encompass major physical interventions including extensive canal dredging, embankment
fortification, and the construction of regional sluice gates and coastal wave breakers. The
construction areas are primarily located within artificial canals and modified secondary
tributaries of the Hau and Tien Rivers. No rare or priority conservation species listed in the
Vietnam Red Data Book or the IUCN Red List have been recorded in these areas. In addition,
no seasonally migratory fish or shrimp species are present in the project implementation area.
While the project is essential for the region's long-term climate adaptation, it carries Substantial
environmental and social risks:
OP 7.50 (International Waterways) Application: The locations of the Dai Ngai, My Xuyen, and
Cai Hop as well as other regional sluices gates of Nam Mang Thit are situated on secondary
tributaries (such as the Saintard, Bãi Xào, and Mang Thít rivers) of the Hau and Tien Rivers,
which are main distributaries of the international Mekong River. However, advanced
hydrodynamic modeling (MIKE 11/MIKE 21) confirms that all hydrological alterations during
the operations of the sluice gates (designed strictly to cut peak tidal surges from the East Sea
and prevent localized salinity intrusion) and construction and dredging impacts (such as
turbidity plumes and flow alterations) are strictly localized and only affect the local hydraulic
regime within the Vietnamese delta. Because the hydrological flow in this downstream
estuarine zone is unidirectional toward the East Sea, it is physically impossible for flow
alterations, water quantity reductions, or dredging-induced turbidity plumes to propagate
upstream and cause "appreciable harm" to Cambodia or any other upstream riparian. The
extensive dredging of the Phước Xuyên – Hai Tam and Cho Bung canals is strictly classified
as long-term maintenance. The works are executed solely to extract decades of accumulated silt
and restore the canals to their original design conveyance capacity for local irrigation and inland
waterway transport. The dredging does not create new river branches, alter the nature of the
basin, or expand the original scheme's scope to make it appear a new or different scheme,
thereby strictly satisfying the criteria of Paragraph 7(a). Furthermore, the regional locks and
coastal wave breakers serve as necessary additions integrated into the Delta's existing regional
water control matrix (directly complementing the completed WB9/MD-ICRSL structures).
Dredged Material and Acid Sulfate Soil Risks: While sediment analysis indicates that
concentrations of heavy metals (Cu, Zn, As, Cd, Pb, Hg, Cr) and pesticide residues are
significantly below the permissible limits of QCVN 43:2025/BTNMT, it is incorrect to
universally conclude that all dredged material is immediately suitable for unrestricted reuse.
The ESIA baseline data confirms the presence of high organic content, anaerobic sediment
conditions, and acid sulfate soils (ASS) with pH values dropping as low as 3.8 in specific
subproject areas. Therefore, the reuse of dredged materials for leveling or agricultural backfill
poses a Moderate to Substantial risk of generating acidic leachate. Reuse is strictly conditional
and must be governed by rigorous pre-treatment, neutralization, and continuous monitoring as
dictated by the Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP).
Aquatic Environment Vulnerability: Baseline biological sampling indicates that the aquatic
environment across the project area is already highly degraded and biologically stressed,
characterized by eutrophication (dense Rotifera), high dissolved organic matter, and anaerobic
benthic zones dominated by pollution-indicator species. Because this ecosystem is already
operating near its ecological threshold, it is highly vulnerable to construction disturbances.
Massive dredging and pile driving will cause severe, localized spikes in turbidity, further
deplete dissolved oxygen (DO), and generate underwater acoustic shockwaves. The impacts on
this stressed aquatic environment during active construction are assessed as Moderate to
Substantial, requiring strict enforcement of "soft-start" piling and targeted dredging schedules.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 327

                                                                                      Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
Summary of Residual Impacts and High-Consequence Risks: While standard construction
impacts—such as dust, emissions, traffic disruption, and general waste generation—can be
effectively mitigated to a Low or Moderate level using standard Environmental and Social
Codes of Practice (ESCOP), the MERIT project presents several high-consequence risks that
demand intensive management. These include: (i) Water Security: Severe turbidity risks
threatening the operational intake of the Hoa Binh Water Supply Plant, which serves thousands
of local households. (ii) Livelihoods: Disruption of offshore clam farming cooperatives and
localized aquaculture ponds due to suspended sediment plumes. (iii) Community Safety:
Structural vibration risks to fragile residential households and masonry structures (e.g., Hieu
Nhon Parish Church) located immediately adjacent to heavy pile-driving zones. (iv)
Cumulative Regional Impacts: The long-term operational risk of creating stagnant, eutrophic
enclosed freshwater zones due to the simultaneous closure of multiple regional sluice gates.
Ultimately, the environmental and social viability of the MERIT project relies entirely on the
strict, legally binding execution of the site-specific Resettlement Plans (RPs), the
Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP), and the Dredged Material Management
Plan (DMMP). Provided that the high-consequence risks are managed through the targeted,
explicitly priced interventions outlined in these safeguard instruments, the project's long-term
climate resilience benefits will significantly outweigh its temporary construction impacts.
9.2. Recommendations
To ensure the MERIT project achieves its climate resilience objectives while strictly complying
   with the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Framework (ESF), the following
   mandatory actions are recommended for the Central Project Office (CPO)/Project
   Management Unit (PMU) and designated contractors:
Contractual and Institutional Mainstreaming (ESS1 / ESS2 / ESCP)
 -   Bidding Integration: The PMU must legally embed the ESMP, DMMP, and RP directly
     into all civil works bidding documents. Contractors must budget for and prepare their own
     site-specific Contractor Environmental and Social Management Plans (C-ESMP) prior to
     mobilization.
 -   Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) Alignment: As mandated by the WB
     review, the PMU must develop a consolidated tabular matrix summarizing all key measures,
     actions, and timeframes required to meet ESS requirements, which will serve as the
     foundation for the project's legally binding ESCP.
 -   Capacity Building: Dedicated Environmental and Social Specialists must be formally
     assigned within the CPO/PMU to supervise C-ESMPs, manage grievance redress
     mechanisms, and enforce compliance on the ground. Organize periodic training sessions
     for contractors and workers on occupational health and safety (OHS), prevention of gender-
     based violence (GBV), and sexual exploitation, abuse, and harassment (SEA/SH). Develop
     a roadmap for livelihood transition training for affected households, focusing on 'nature-
     based' models to ensure sustainable income under the impacts of extreme climate change.
Targeted Management of High-Risk Receptors (ESS4 / ESS5 / ESS8)
 -   Livelihood Protection & Compensation: The CPO/PMU prioritize Unexploded Ordnance
     (UXO) clearance and finalize and ensure transparent, prompt compensation all
     compensation for all affected households. prior to the commencement of any work item.
     For economic displacement—specifically the offshore clam farming cooperatives,
     aquaculture ponds, and the Cai Hop boat workshop—the CPO/PMU must enforce the RP
     to compensate for unavoidable income losses and provide safe waterway bypasses.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             328

                                                                                  Official Use Only
Mekong Delta Climate Resilience and Integrated Transformation Project (MERIT) (P515255)
-   Structural Integrity and Acoustic Management: Mandatory pre-construction structural
    baseline surveys must be conducted for all fragile Level 4 houses and religious sites (e.g.,
    Hieu Nhon Parish Church) within a 50m radius of piling zones to safeguard against
    vibration damage. Noisy works must be strictly limited to daytime hours.
-   Physical Cultural Heritage: For the nationally recognized Gò Tháp archaeological site, the
    CPO/PMU must proactively coordinate with the local cultural heritage department.
    Transport routes must be strictly monitored to prevent material spillage, and formal Chance
    Find Procedures must be enforced.
Strict Control of Dredged Material and Acid Sulfate Soils (ESS3): Contractors must comply
with the DMMP to avoid dropping sludge during transport. Due to the high risk of Acid Sulfate
Soils (ASS), no dredged material may be distributed to local communities for agricultural
backfill without prior neutralization, rigorous chemical testing, and formal CPO/PMU
clearance.
Effective collaboration for management of cumulative impacts
World Bank Operational Policy of OP 7.50. and Vietnam’s Obligations under the 1995 Mekong
Agreement (MRC):
Because the MERIT project consists of rehabilitation works and additions to existing schemes
that cause no upstream transboundary water quality or quantity adverse impacts on Cambodia
or Laos, the project unambiguously qualifies for the riparian notification exception set out in
Paragraph 7(a) and Paragraph 7(c) of OP 7.50.
While the project secures an exception from notification under the World Bank's internal OP
7.50 policy, the Government of Vietnam remains bound by the 1995 Mekong Agreement.
Under the Mekong River Commission's Procedures for Notification, Prior Consultation and
Agreement (PNPCA), mandatory notification or prior consultation is legally triggered by
specific activities, most notably inter-basin diversions or significant consumptive water
abstractions from the mainstream. Because the MERIT project’s infrastructure consists
exclusively of non-consumptive, tidal-control sluice gates, navigation locks, and rehabilitation
dredging located on domestic secondary and tertiary tributaries, it does not trigger the
mandatory PNPCA notification thresholds. Consequently, there is no legal requirement to
submit a formal project dossier to the MRC Secretariat as a condition of compliance for this
project. Any future sharing of project hydrodynamic data with the MRC will be conducted at
Vietnam's discretion to foster voluntary regional cooperation under the Procedures for Data and
Information Exchange and Sharing (PDIES), and is entirely independent of the World Bank's
OP 7.50 clearance process.
